topics.title: Process of Emergency Evacuation: Special Features of Personnel Movement and Parameter Characteristics of the Movement of People
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process of Emergency Evacuation: Special Features of Personnel Movement and Parameter Characteristics of the Movement of People

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Emergency Evacuation

According to the **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering** and the **Tribune-IFEG (International Fire Engineering Guidelines)** referenced in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, **Emergency Evacuation** is defined as:

> The **controlled, time-bounded, and orderly process** of relocating occupants from a fire-affected or threatened zone to a place of relative safety (refuge area, exit staircase, or open ground) through designated egress paths, achieved before untenable conditions develop.

Mathematically, evacuation is deemed **safe** when:

$$\text{ASET} \;>\; \text{RSET}$$

Where:
- **ASET** = Available Safe Egress Time
- **RSET** = Required Safe Egress Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The difference between *Total Evacuation* (whole building emptied) and *Phased Evacuation* (floors emptied in sequence, used in high-rise design) is a frequently examined distinction.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Personnel Movement

**Personnel Movement** in the context of fire safety engineering is the **macro-level, aggregate description of how groups of human beings physically translate through the built environment** under both normal and emergency (alarm) conditions. It is treated as a **fluid-dynamic continuum** in many engineering models, where individual variations are averaged into measurable flow parameters.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Parameter Characteristics of Movement

These are the **quantifiable descriptors** of the moving crowd that an engineer must determine to size exits, stairs, corridors, and refuge areas. The **four master parameters** recognised by the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

1. **Density (D)** — persons per unit floor area (persons/m²).
2. **Velocity (V)** — walking speed of the crowd (m/s or m/min).
3. **Flow (F)** — number of persons passing a reference line per unit time.
4. **Specific Flow (f)** — flow per unit effective width (persons/m/min).

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Why This Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Cricket Stadium Exit**
>
> Imagine 50,000 spectators leaving Eden Gardens or the Wankhede Stadium after a T20 final. The gates (exits) become a **funnel**; you cannot leave faster than the gate allows. People near the gate are squeezed (high **density**), they shuffle (low **velocity**), but the **flow** through the gate is constant once a jam forms.
>
> Now imagine the same crowd, but with **smoke pouring in** from one side. They will:
> - Hesitate at the start (long **pre-movement time**).
> - Run initially, then slow at stair landings.
> - Push toward familiar exits (avoiding unknown stairs).
> - Move *faster* in clear corridors but *slower* in smoke-logged ones.
>
> Emergency evacuation is **stadium exit under smoke and fear**. Our job is to design the building so the "gate" is wide enough to empty the stadium *before the smoke arrives at the stands*.

---

## 1.5 Special Features of Personnel Movement During Fire

These are the **behavioural and physical peculiarities** that distinguish fire evacuation from normal crowd movement:

| # | Special Feature | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pre-movement delay** | Occupants do not move immediately; recognition + reaction time. |
| 2 | **Panic and over-speed initially, then congestion** | Speed is not monotonic with density. |
| 3 | **Group / Affiliative behaviour** | Families, colleagues, or friends delay evacuation to regroup. |
| 4 | **Route familiarity bias** | Occupants prefer known exits (often ignoring safer alternative exits). |
| 5 | **Counter-flow** | Fire-fighters entering while occupants leave, halving the effective width. |
| 6 | **Stairwell compression** | Density on stairs is 2–3× higher than corridors. |
| 7 | **Reduced visibility (smoke)** | Walking speed drops by 30–70% in dense smoke. |
| 8 | **Bottlenecking at doors, landings, turnstiles** | Creates the "arching" crowd effect (Predtechenskii-Milinskii). |
| 9 | **Slower movement on stairs (downward)** | Downward speed ≈ **0.5 m/s** vs flat speed of **1.4 m/s**. |
| 10 | **Herding / following behaviour** | A few informed leaders can dramatically change flow direction. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** Standard codes (NBC 2016, IS 14435) assume **steady, uniform, unimpeded flow** — but real emergency flow is *unsteady, heterogeneous, and biphasic* (walking + queuing). This is a **14-mark favourite** in the Part B questions.

---

## 1.6 Parameter Characteristics of Movement of People

The four primary **measurable characteristics** governing the movement of people, all of which appear in KTU numerical problems, are summarised below:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (KTU Exam Value) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | D | 0.1 – 3.8 (max jam) | persons/m² |
| Velocity (flat) | V_f | 0.5 – 1.4 | m/s |
| Velocity (stairs-down) | V_s | 0.4 – 0.7 | m/s |
| Flow | F | 40 – 80 | persons/min |
| Specific Flow | f | 60 – 90 (max ≈ 1.3) | persons/m/s |
| Pre-movement time | t_pre | 0.5 – 5+ | min |
| Boundary layer (Pauls) | b | **0.15** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of the Evacuation Process

The complete **Process of Emergency Evacuation** is divided into three sequential phases. This sequence is **non-overlapping in the conservative engineering model** (for a worst-case RSET calculation), although in reality they may overlap.

### Phase 1 — Detection & Alarm Phase
- The fire is detected (either automatically by a smoke/heat detector, or manually by an occupant).
- The fire alarm is raised, transmitting the signal to the fire alarm panel and broadcasting audible/visual signals.
- **Duration:** t_det (typically 30 s to 2 min for automatic detection; up to 5 min for manual).

### Phase 2 — Pre-Movement (Recognition / Reaction) Phase
- Occupants **hear/see** the alarm, interpret it, and decide what to do.
- This is dominated by **human behaviour**: investigation, denial, helping others, gathering belongings, waiting for confirmation.
- **Duration:** t_pre (typically **0.5 to 5 minutes**, can exceed 10 min in public buildings).
- **KTU note:** This is often the **largest single contributor** to RSET in office and public buildings.

### Phase 3 — Travel (Movement) Phase
- Occupants physically traverse the egress path: room → corridor → stair → discharge → assembly point.
- **Duration:** t_travel (calculated from flow equations).
- Ends when the last person reaches a place of safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RSET = t_det + t_alarm + t_pre + t_travel**
> KTU numericals frequently ask students to compute RSET and compare it with ASET.

---

## 2.2 The Four Master Parameters — Theoretical Decomposition

### (a) Density (D) — persons/m²
- Defined as the number of persons occupying a unit floor area of the egress component (corridor, stair, lobby).
- **At jam density (D_max ≈ 3.8 persons/m²)** — physical contact, V → 0.
- **At low density (D < 0.5 persons/m²)** — free-flow walking, V ≈ V_max.

### (b) Velocity (V) — m/s
- Mean horizontal walking speed of the crowd.
- Depends on density, slope, smoke obscuration, and the **Fundamental Diagram** (V–D curve).
- For **flat unobstructed paths**: V_max ≈ **1.4 m/s** (Pauls / Fruin).
- For **down-stairs**: V_max ≈ **0.5 m/s** (NFPA empirical data).
- For **up-stairs**: V_max ≈ **0.4 m/s**.

### (c) Flow (F) — persons per minute
- The number of persons crossing a reference line (e.g., a doorway) per unit time.
- Measured at a **bottleneck** because the bottleneck governs the system.
- F = N / t

### (d) Specific Flow (f) — persons per metre-width per second (or per minute)
- Normalised flow per unit effective width, used to compare components.
- The **maximum specific flow** through a doorway is empirically about **1.3 persons/m/s** (≈ 80 persons/m/min).

---

## 2.3 The Fundamental Equation of Crowd Movement

The **continuum (hydrodynamic) model** of crowd flow, foundational to the **Predtechenskii-Milinskii (PM) model** and the **SFPE Handbook**, treats people as a compressible "fluid" and expresses:

$$F \;=\; V \,\times\, D \,\times\, W$$

Dividing both sides by W gives the **Specific Flow**:

$$f_s \;=\; \dfrac{F}{W} \;=\; V \,\times\, D$$

This equation is **the single most important relationship** in evacuation engineering and is examinable in every KTU question paper on this module.

---

## 2.4 Speed-Density Relationship (V–D Curve)

The empirical V–D relationship used in engineering models is **monotonically decreasing** — as density rises, walking speed falls. A simple linear form (used in the KTU 2024 Scheme):

$$V \;=\; V_{\max} \,\bigl(1 - a\,D\bigr)$$

Where:
- V_max = free-flow speed (1.4 m/s flat, 0.5 m/s down-stairs).
- a = empirical constant (≈ **0.25** for corridors, **0.33** for stairs in PM model).
- D = density in persons/m².

> Substituting this V into f_s = V × D gives a **parabolic** specific-flow-vs-density curve whose **maximum** corresponds to the bottleneck capacity.

---

## 2.5 Effective Width Model (Pauls' Boundary Layer)

**John Pauls' Boundary Layer Theory** (1970s) recognises that people keep a psychological buffer from walls and railings. The **effective width** is:

$$W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; W_{\text{clear}} \;-\; n \cdot b$$

Where:
- n = number of boundary edges (e.g., 2 for a stair between two walls).
- b = boundary-layer width = **0.15 m** (for stairs, 0.15 m on each side; total reduction = **0.3 m**).

> [!NOTE]
> **For doors:** b = 0 (people walk straight through), so W_eff = W_clear.
> **For stairs:** W_eff = W_clear − 0.30 m.
> **For corridors:** b = 0.15 m on each side ⇒ W_eff = W_clear − 0.30 m.

---

## 2.6 Capacity of an Egress Component

The maximum flow capacity of any egress element (door, corridor, stair) is:

$$F_{\max} \;=\; f_{\max} \,\times\, W_{\text{eff}}$$

Where f_max is the **maximum specific flow**, an empirical constant for that component type.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F = N / t | Flow rate (definition) | — |
| 2 | F = V × D × W | Fundamental flow equation | — |
| 3 | f_s = V × D | Specific flow | 0 – 1.3 persons/m/s |
| 4 | V = V_max (1 − aD) | Speed–density (linear) | a = 0.25–0.33 |
| 5 | W_eff = W_clear − n·b | Pauls' effective width | b = 0.15 m |
| 6 | F_max = f_max × W_eff | Component capacity | f_max = 1.3 persons/m/s |
| 7 | t_travel = L / V | Travel time (free flow) | — |
| 8 | t_travel = N / F_max | Travel time (queued) | — |
| 9 | RSET = t_det + t_alarm + t_pre + t_travel | Total evacuation time | — |
| 10 | Safety criterion: ASET > RSET | Design verification | — |
| 11 | V_flat (max) = 1.4 m/s | Free flow on horizontal | — |
| 12 | V_stair (down) = 0.5 m/s | Free flow on down-stair | — |
| 13 | D_jam ≈ 3.8 persons/m² | Jam (max) density | — |
| 14 | D_optimal ≈ 1.9 persons/m² | Density at max specific flow | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All numerical problems in the KTU 2024 scheme on this topic are solvable with the above 14 equations.** Memorise the symbols and the constant values shown in **bold** in this table.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

This parameter framework is used by every practicing Fire & Life Safety Consultant in India to:

- **Size exit staircases** in high-rise buildings (NBC 2016, Part 4).
- **Determine the number and width of fire-exit doors** in a floor plate.
- **Validate the RSET** in performance-based design (e.g., a 30-storey IT park in Trivandrum).
- **Conduct crowd simulations** using Pathfinder, STEPS, or Building EXODUS.
- **Design metro-rail evacuation** (Kochi Metro, Delhi Metro) where counter-flow and bottlenecking are critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Flow Equation (F = V × D × W)

### Conceptual Starting Point
Consider a rectangular corridor segment of length L, width W, occupied uniformly by N people. The people are walking in a single direction at speed V with density D.

**Step 1 — Definition of density:**
The number of persons in the segment is

$$N \;=\; D \cdot A \;=\; D \cdot (L \cdot W)$$

This is a direct restatement of "density = persons per unit area" multiplied by the area of the segment.

**Step 2 — Definition of flow rate:**
Flow is the number of persons crossing a reference line at the downstream end per unit time. Since the crowd advances at velocity V, in time t every person within a distance V·t behind the line crosses it:

$$\text{Number crossing in time } t \;=\; D \cdot (V \cdot t) \cdot W$$

**Step 3 — Form the flow rate:**

$$F \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Number crossing in time } t}{t} \;=\; D \cdot V \cdot W$$

Therefore, **the fundamental flow equation** is:

$$\boxed{\,F \;=\; V \cdot D \cdot W\,}$$

This derivation is **directly examinable as a 7-mark derivation question** in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Flow and the Bottleneck Capacity

Starting from the fundamental equation and dividing both sides by W (the effective width):

$$f_s \;=\; \dfrac{F}{W} \;=\; V \cdot D$$

Substituting the linear V–D relationship $V = V_{\max}(1 - aD)$:

$$f_s(D) \;=\; V_{\max}(1 - aD) \cdot D \;=\; V_{\max}\bigl(D - aD^2\bigr)$$

To find the **density that maximises specific flow**, differentiate with respect to D and set equal to zero:

$$\dfrac{df_s}{dD} \;=\; V_{\max}(1 - 2aD) \;=\; 0$$

Solving:

$$1 - 2aD^* \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^* \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2a}$$

**For a corridor (a = 0.25):**

$$D^* \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2 \times 0.25} \;=\; 2.0 \;\text{persons/m}^2$$

**Maximum specific flow (corridor):**

$$f_{s,\max} \;=\; V_{\max}\Bigl(D^* - a(D^*)^2\Bigr) \;=\; V_{\max}\Bigl(\dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{4a}\Bigr) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\max}}{4a}$$

Substituting V_max = 1.4 m/s and a = 0.25:

$$f_{s,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{1.4}{4 \times 0.25} \;=\; \dfrac{1.4}{1.0} \;=\; 1.4 \;\text{persons/m/s}$$

This matches the **empirical SFPE value of 1.3 persons/m/s** for doors (lower due to arching and squeezing).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Travel Time under Bottleneck (Queued) Flow

If N persons must pass a bottleneck of maximum capacity F_max, the travel time is:

**Step 1 — Steady-state approximation:**
Assume the crowd forms a queue and discharges at the bottleneck rate F_max.

**Step 2 — Integrate the flow:**

$$t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; \dfrac{N}{F_{\max}} \;=\; \dfrac{N}{f_{s,\max} \cdot W_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Pauls' effective width:**
For a stair (n = 2, b = 0.15 m):

$$W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; W_{\text{clear}} - 0.30$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; \dfrac{N}{f_{s,\max} \cdot (W_{\text{clear}} - 0.30)}\,}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula for stair travel time** and is the basis of most 14-mark numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Numerical Problem (Worked Solution)

**KTU Sample Problem:**
> A high-rise office floor has 240 occupants. They evacuate through a single stair of clear width 1.2 m. Given V_max = 0.5 m/s on the down-stair, a = 0.33, f_s,max = 0.85 persons/m/s, and pre-movement time t_pre = 2 min. Calculate the travel time, RSET, and the number of stairs required to meet a target RSET of 6 min.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute effective width of the stair (Pauls' model, n = 2):**

$$W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; W_{\text{clear}} - n \cdot b \;=\; 1.2 - 2(0.15) \;=\; 1.2 - 0.30 \;=\; 0.90\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum flow capacity of the single stair:**

$$F_{\max} \;=\; f_{s,\max} \cdot W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.85 \times 0.90 \;=\; 0.765\;\text{persons/s}$$

Convert to persons/min:

$$F_{\max} \;=\; 0.765 \times 60 \;\approx\; 45.9\;\text{persons/min}$$

**Step 3 — Travel time for 240 persons through one stair:**

$$t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; \dfrac{N}{F_{\max}} \;=\; \dfrac{240}{45.9} \;\approx\; 5.23\;\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute RSET (assuming t_det = t_alarm = 0 for simplicity):**

$$RSET \;=\; t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre}} + t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; 0 + 0 + 2.0 + 5.23 \;=\; 7.23\;\text{min}$$

This **exceeds the target of 6 min**, so the design is unsafe.

**Step 5 — Number of stairs required:**
For a target travel time of 4 min (so that RSET = 2 + 4 = 6 min):

$$F_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{N}{t_{\text{travel,target}}} \;=\; \dfrac{240}{4 \times 60} \;=\; 1.0\;\text{persons/s}$$

$$\text{Number of stairs} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{required}}}{F_{\text{max, per stair}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.0}{0.765} \;\approx\; 1.31$$

Rounding **up** for safety, we need **2 stairs**. This is the standard engineering practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round up** the number of stairs in KTU design problems; never round down. This is a frequent mark-loss error in valuation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Formula

| Situation Given in Question | Use This Formula |
|---|---|
| "Find the number of persons that can pass" | N = F × t |
| "Find flow through a doorway of width W" | F = f_s × W_eff |
| "Find travel time through a stair" | t = N / (f_s,max × W_eff) |
| "Find density for maximum specific flow" | D* = 1 / (2a) |
| "Compare ASET and RSET" | Compute RSET and check ASET > RSET |
| "Stair is jammed, what is the max flow?" | Use f_s,max = V_max / (4a) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Three Phases of Emergency Evacuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FIRE IGNITION] --> B[PHASE 1: DETECTION AND ALARM]
    B --> B1[Smoke or Heat Detector Activates]
    B --> B2[Manual Call Point Pressed]
    B --> B3[Audible and Visual Alarm Broadcast]
    B3 --> C[PHASE 2: PRE MOVEMENT PHASE]
    C --> C1[Occupant Hears Alarm]
    C --> C2[Recognition and Interpretation]
    C --> C3[Decision to Evacuate]
    C --> C4[Group Formation and Belongings Collection]
    C4 --> D[PHASE 3: TRAVEL PHASE]
    D --> D1[Exit the Room]
    D --> D2[Traverse the Corridor]
    D --> D3[Enter Stairwell and Descend]
    D --> D4[Pass Through Staircase Discharge Door]
    D --> D5[Reach Assembly Point or Place of Safety]
    D5 --> E[EVACUATION COMPLETE]
    E --> F{Check ASET versus RSET}
    F -->|Safe| G[DESIGN ACCEPTED]
    F -->|Unsafe| H[REDESIGN: ADD EXITS, WIDEN STAIRS, IMPROVE ALARMS]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents an engineered or behavioural milestone. The total time from `A` to `E` is the **RSET**. The time from `A` to untenable conditions is the **ASET**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Architecture of Personnel Movement (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        I1[Occupant Load N]
        I2[Stair Clear Width W]
        I3[Floor to Floor Height H]
        I4[Pre Movement Time tpre]
        I5[Free Flow Speed Vmax]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING[CALCULATION ENGINE]
        P1[Compute Effective Width Weff]
        P2[Compute Max Specific Flow fs max]
        P3[Compute Stair Capacity Fmax]
        P4[Compute Travel Time ttravel]
        P5[Assemble RSET]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[ENGINEERING OUTPUTS]
        O1[Required Safe Egress Time RSET]
        O2[Number of Stairs Needed]
        O3[Travel Time per Stair]
        O4[Pass or Fail Safety Verdict]
    end

    I1 --> P3
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P3
    I4 --> P5
    I5 --> P2

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P1 --> O4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The "calculation engine" mirrors the sequence of equations a fire engineer executes by hand or in software like **Pathfinder** or **STEPS**. Each input parameter feeds exactly one calculation block.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Speed-Density Relationship Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LOW DENSITY D LESS THAN 0.5] --> A1[Free Flow Regime]
    A1 --> A2[Velocity is near Vmax of 1.4 m/s flat]
    A2 --> A3[Walking is comfortable, no queuing]

    B[MEDIUM DENSITY D BETWEEN 0.5 AND 1.9] --> B1[Transition Regime]
    B1 --> B2[Velocity decreases linearly with D]
    B2 --> B3[Some shuffling and queuing begins]

    C[HIGH DENSITY D BETWEEN 1.9 AND 3.8] --> C1[Jammed Regime]
    C1 --> C2[Velocity is very low or zero]
    C2 --> C3[People are shoulder to shoulder, cannot move]

    D[MAXIMUM SPECIFIC FLOW] --> D1[Occurs at D star equals 1 over 2a]
    D1 --> D2[This is the optimal density for maximum throughput]
    D2 --> D3[At D star, flow is maximised not the speed]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a *conceptual* representation of the **Fundamental Diagram of Pedestrian Traffic**. KTU students are expected to draw or describe the V–D curve in the 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Bottleneck Geometry (Doorway Arching)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[People Approaching a Doorway or Stair Landing] --> B[Formation of Semicircular Arch]
    B --> C[Front Row Cannot Pass Due to Squeezing]
    C --> D[Pressure Builds Behind Front Row]
    D --> E[Temporary Stall of Flow]
    E --> F[One Person Breaks Through]
    F --> G[Rest Follow in Sequence]
    G --> H[Resumption of Steady State Flow]
    H --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The "arching" effect (first described by **Predtechenskii & Milinskii, 1969**) is why empirical f_s,max is lower than the theoretical parabolic peak.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "Pre-Movement Time" in emergency evacuation. List any two factors that affect it.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Pre-movement time (t_pre) is the interval between the **activation of the fire alarm** and the **commencement of physical movement** of the occupant toward an exit. It encompasses recognition of the alarm, interpretation of the threat, decision-making, and preparatory actions (collecting belongings, helping others).

**Two key factors affecting pre-movement time:**

1. **Alarm audibility and clarity** — vague or unfamiliar alarm signals cause confusion, lengthening t_pre.
2. **Occupant training and prior drills** — trained occupants respond in 0.5–1 min; untrained occupants may take 3–5 min or more.
3. *Additional credit point:* Building complexity, occupant age, group affiliations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing only "delay" without mentioning the alarm as the **start point** leads to 1-mark loss. Always specify the *start* (alarm) and *end* (start of movement).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is "Specific Flow" in evacuation engineering? State its standard units and the typical maximum value for a doorway.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Specific flow (f_s) is the **flow rate of occupants per unit effective width** of an egress component. It is a normalised measure that allows comparison of different doors, corridors, and stairs.

$$f_s \;=\; \dfrac{F}{W_{\text{eff}}} \quad\text{[persons/m/s or persons/m/min]}$$

**Typical maximum value (for a doorway under emergency conditions):**

$$f_{s,\max} \;\approx\; 1.3\;\text{persons/m/s} \;\approx\; 80\;\text{persons/m/min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing "flow" (F, persons/min) with "specific flow" (f_s, persons/m/min). Examiners strictly check the unit conversion. A wrong unit = **0 marks for the numerical part**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme provides **internal choice** for 14-mark questions. Below are **TWO alternative questions** to give the student a practice choice.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `Choose either A or B`

**(a)** Explain the **three phases of emergency evacuation** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **special features of personnel movement** that distinguish fire evacuation from normal crowd flow. **(7 marks)**
`(b)` A fire exit door has a clear width of 1.1 m. During a drill, 80 occupants passed through it in 50 seconds. Calculate the **flow rate**, **specific flow**, and verify if the doorway is operating at its **maximum specific flow capacity** (f_s,max = 1.3 persons/m/s). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The three phases of emergency evacuation:**

1. **Phase 1 — Detection and Alarm Phase:** Begins with the occurrence of detectable fire phenomena (smoke, heat, flame) and ends when the alarm is broadcast. Duration: t_det + t_alarm.
2. **Phase 2 — Pre-Movement Phase:** From alarm onset to the start of physical movement. Includes recognition, interpretation, decision-making. Duration: t_pre.
3. **Phase 3 — Travel Phase:** From initiation of movement to arrival at a place of safety. Calculated from flow equations. Duration: t_travel.

**Sketch (represent in text):**
*(A standard timeline diagram is expected: a horizontal line with three segments marked t_det, t_pre, t_travel, all summing to RSET, with a parallel line showing ASET. See Diagram A in Section 4.)*

**Special features of personnel movement:**

- **Pre-movement delay** due to disbelief, investigation, information-seeking.
- **Panic-driven initial over-speed**, followed by congestion at bottlenecks.
- **Affiliative / group behaviour** (families do not separate easily).
- **Route familiarity bias** — occupants prefer known exits.
- **Counter-flow** between evacuating occupants and fire-fighters entering.
- **Stairwell compression** — density on stairs is 2–3× that of corridors.
- **Smoke-induced speed reduction** — speed drops by 30–70% in dense smoke.
- **Bottleneck arching** at doorways and landings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Listing features *without tying them to the evacuation phase* is a 2-mark deduction. Each feature should be **linked** to the phase where it manifests (e.g., panic → travel phase; pre-movement delay → pre-movement phase).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Clear width W = 1.1 m
- Number of persons N = 80
- Time t = 50 s
- f_s,max = 1.3 persons/m/s (doorway)

**Step 1 — Flow rate:**

$$F \;=\; \dfrac{N}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{80}{50} \;=\; 1.6\;\text{persons/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective width of door (n = 0, b = 0 for doors):**

$$W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 1.1\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Specific flow:**

$$f_s \;=\; \dfrac{F}{W_{\text{eff}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.6}{1.1} \;\approx\; 1.45\;\text{persons/m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verification against maximum:**

$$f_s \;=\; 1.45\;\text{persons/m/s} \;>\; f_{s,\max} \;=\; 1.3\;\text{persons/m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The doorway is **operating above** the empirical maximum. This is physically impossible in steady state and indicates either (i) transient peak in the initial 5–10 seconds, or (ii) measurement error / rushed crowd (e.g., push). In a real evacuation, the doorway will **not sustain** 1.45 persons/m/s — it will settle to ≈ 1.3 persons/m/s with queuing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Computing flow as 80/50 = 1.6 without converting to **persons/min** when the f_s,max is given in persons/m/s is a common dimensional error. Always **match units** before comparing.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `Alternative Choice`

**(a)** Define the following with units and one engineering use of each:
(i) Density, (ii) Velocity, (iii) Specific Flow, (iv) Effective Width. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 12-storey hostel has 30 occupants per floor. Each floor is connected to a single stair of clear width 1.0 m. Given V_max = 0.5 m/s (down-stairs), a = 0.33, f_s,max = 0.85 persons/m/s, t_pre = 1.5 min, t_det = 0.5 min, and stair length per floor = 4 m. Calculate the **RSET** and the **number of stairs required** for a target RSET of 8 min. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Term | Definition | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (D)** | Number of persons per unit floor area of the egress component | persons/m² | To design floor-area-based occupant load (NBC 2016) |
| **Velocity (V)** | Mean walking speed of the crowd along the egress path | m/s | To compute travel time t = L / V |
| **Specific Flow (f_s)** | Flow rate per unit effective width | persons/m/s | To size door width: W = F / f_s,max |
| **Effective Width (W_eff)** | Clear width minus the boundary-layer buffer (Pauls' model) | m | To compute actual flow capacity of a stair or corridor |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- N_total = 30 × 12 = 360 persons
- W_clear = 1.0 m
- t_pre = 1.5 min, t_det = 0.5 min
- Stair length per floor = 4 m
- f_s,max = 0.85 persons/m/s

**Step 1 — Effective width (Pauls' model, n = 2):**

$$W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 1.0 - 2(0.15) \;=\; 0.70\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity of one stair:**

$$F_{\max,\text{per stair}} \;=\; f_{s,\max} \cdot W_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 0.85 \times 0.70 \;=\; 0.595\;\text{persons/s}$$

**Step 3 — Travel time through one stair (worst case: ground floor sees all 360 persons):**

$$t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; \dfrac{360}{0.595} \;\approx\; 605\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 10.08\;\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — RSET:**

$$RSET \;=\; t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{pre}} + t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; 0.5 + 1.5 + 10.08 \;=\; 12.08\;\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Required travel time for RSET = 8 min:**

$$t_{\text{travel,target}} \;=\; 8 - 0.5 - 1.5 \;=\; 6\;\text{min} \;=\; 360\;\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Required flow capacity:**

$$F_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{360}{360} \;=\; 1.0\;\text{persons/s}$$

**Step 7 — Number of stairs:**

$$\text{Number} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{required}}}{F_{\max,\text{per stair}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.0}{0.595} \;\approx\; 1.68$$

Rounded **up**: **2 stairs** are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Rounding **1.68 down to 1** is the single most common error. A safety factor of 1.5 is implied, and KTU valuation strictly requires **round-up** logic.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — mixing m/s with persons/min in flow calculations.
> 2. **Using clear width instead of effective width** for stairs (forgetting Pauls' 0.30 m boundary).
> 3. **Omitting the pre-movement time** when computing RSET.
> 4. **Rounding down** the number of stairs (must always round up).
> 5. **Confusing "flow" (F) and "specific flow" (f_s)** — they differ by a factor of W_eff.
> 6. **Forgetting the safety criterion** — always end with "ASET > RSET ⇒ Design is Safe."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

- ✅ Emergency evacuation = orderly relocation from danger to safety; **safe when ASET > RSET**.
- ✅ Three phases: **Detection/Alarm → Pre-movement → Travel**.
- ✅ Four master parameters: **Density, Velocity, Flow, Specific Flow**.
- ✅ Fundamental equation: **F = V × D × W**; Specific flow **f_s = V × D**.
- ✅ Maximum specific flow occurs at **D\* = 1 / (2a)**; f_s,max ≈ **1.3 persons/m/s** (door), **0.85 persons/m/s** (stair).
- ✅ Effective width (Pauls' model): **W_eff = W_clear − 0.30 m** for stairs and corridors.
- ✅ Free-flow walking speed: **1.4 m/s** (flat), **0.5 m/s** (down-stairs), **0.4 m/s** (up-stairs).
- ✅ Jam density ≈ **3.8 persons/m²**; Optimal density for max throughput ≈ **1.9 persons/m²**.
- ✅ Travel time at bottleneck: **t_travel = N / F_max**.
- ✅ Pre-movement time typically **0.5 to 5 min** — often the **largest** RSET contributor.
- ✅ Special features: panic, group behaviour, route familiarity, counter-flow, stairwell compression, smoke-induced slow-down, arching.
- ✅ **Always round UP** when determining the number of exits or stairs.
- ✅ **Always check units** — convert m/s to persons/min before computing f_s in persons/m/min.
- ✅ **Always verify** with the safety criterion **ASET > RSET** at the end of every numerical.
- ✅ Refer to: **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering**, **NBC 2016 Part 4**, **IS 14435**, **Tribune-IFEG** for the underlying theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evacuation Exits and Routes: Stages of Evacuation, Importance of Different Stages of Evacuation, Planning and Design of Evacuation Routes, Passages, and Exits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evacuation Exits and Routes: Stages of Evacuation, Importance of Different Stages of Evacuation, Planning and Design of Evacuation Routes, Passages, and Exits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evacuation** is the systematic, time-bound, and orderly movement of occupants from a fire-affected or threatened zone of a building to a predetermined place of safety, achieved through engineered **Exits** and **Evacuation Routes** designed in accordance with **NBC 2016 (India)**, **IS 14435:1997**, and **NFPA 101** Life Safety Code.

**Key KTU Syllabus Definitions:**

- **Exit:** That portion of a means of egress which is separated from other building spaces by construction or equipment required to provide a protected path of travel to the exit discharge. (NBC 2016, Clause 2.43)
- **Exit Discharge:** The portion of an exit that leads from the termination of the exit to a public way, refuge area, or open space.
- **Evacuation Route:** The continuous, unobstructed path of travel from any point in a building to a place of safety.
- **Means of Egress:** The complete path comprising three parts — **Exit Access → Exit → Exit Discharge**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Highway Analogy"** — Think of a fire evacuation route exactly like a **multi-lane highway system**:
> - The **building** is the **origin city**.
> - The **refuge area** is the **destination city**.
> - **Exits** are the **toll booths / exit ramps** (limited number, fixed capacity).
> - **Corridors/passageways** are the **highway lanes** (with finite width).
> - **Occupants** are the **vehicles** (each needing space and time).
> - **Smoke/heat** acts like a **road accident** that blocks lanes.
>
> Just as traffic engineers calculate lane-width, ramp capacity, and signal timing, fire engineers calculate **exit width, occupant load, travel distance, and flow rate** to prevent a "human traffic jam" during fire emergencies.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Kerala/KTU Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Walking speed (horizontal)**: **1.0 m/s** (38 m/min)
> - **Walking speed (stairs – downward)**: **0.5 m/s** (30 m/min)
> - **Specific Flow (Stairs, downward)**: **60 persons/min/m** of effective width
> - **Specific Flow (Level/Corridor)**: **80 persons/min/m** of effective width
> - **Maximum Travel Distance (Residential)**: **22.5 m** (unsprinklered)
> - **Maximum Travel Distance (Office/Assembly)**: **30 m**
> - **Minimum Corridor Width**: **1.5 m** (clear of obstructions)
> - **Minimum Door Width**: **1.0 m** for assembly, **0.9 m** for residential

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stages of Evacuation — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

A complete evacuation is **NOT instantaneous**. KTU and NBC 2016 divide it into **four critical stages** that determine the total Required Safe Egress Time (RSET).

#### **Stage 1: Detection Stage (t_det)**
- The interval between **ignition of fire** and **detection** (by smoke detector, heat detector, sprinkler activation, or human observation).
- **Goal:** Reduce this by installing **automatic detection systems** (addressable smoke detectors, aspirating systems).
- **Why it matters:** Every second of delayed detection = more smoke, more heat, lower visibility, fewer people can evacuate.

#### **Stage 2: Alarm/Notification Stage (t_alarm)**
- The interval between detection and **general occupant awareness** of the emergency.
- Achieved via **voice evacuation systems, sirens, PA announcements**.
- **Critical KTU Point:** A **voice message** ("Fire on 3rd floor, please evacuate through Staircase B") is **far more effective** than a generic alarm bell — it reduces pre-movement time by 30-50%.

#### **Stage 3: Pre-Movement / Recognition Stage (t_pre)**
- The **most variable and psychologically complex** stage.
- Occupants must: **recognize the alarm → interpret it as real → decide to act → begin movement**.
- Behavioral delays include: denial, finishing phone calls, gathering belongings, helping disabled, waiting for confirmation.
- **Pre-movement time (t_pre):** typically **30 seconds to 5 minutes** depending on occupant training, alarm type, and building use.

#### **Stage 4: Travel/Movement Stage (t_trav)**
- The actual physical movement of occupants from their location to a place of safety.
- Depends on: **travel distance, occupant density, exit width, walking speed, stair geometry**.
- This is the **engineered, calculable** portion — covered by formulas in Section 2.3.

#### **Stage 5: Safe Area / Assembly Stage (t_assembly)**
- Occupants reach the **refuge area / assembly point / public way**.
- **Headcount is performed** to verify all occupants are accounted for.
- **Re-entry to the building is strictly prohibited** until fire brigade declares "ALL CLEAR".

### 2.2 Importance of Each Stage (Why KTU Asks This)

| Stage | Risk if Poorly Managed | Engineering/Control Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection** | Delayed awareness → entrapment | Smoke/heat detectors, sprinklers |
| **Alarm** | Occupants unaware → mass casualty | Voice evacuation, flashing beacons |
| **Pre-movement** | Behavioral freeze → congestion | Drills, training, clear signage |
| **Travel** | Stampede, smoke inhalation | Multiple exits, protected stairs |
| **Assembly** | Missing persons miscounted | Mustering points, headcount system |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in PESFT416 — Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Evacuation Time (RSET)** | $RSET = t_{det} + t_{alarm} + t_{pre} + t_{trav}$ | seconds / minutes | Sum of all four stages |
| 2 | **Travel Time** | $t_{trav} = \dfrac{L}{v}$ | seconds | L = travel distance, v = walking speed |
| 3 | **Occupant Load (O)** | $O = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area}}{\text{Occupant Load Factor (OLF)}}$ | persons | OLF from NBC Table 3 (e.g., office: 8 m²/p, assembly: 1.5 m²/p) |
| 4 | **Exit Width Required (W)** | $W = \dfrac{O}{C}$ | meters | C = Capacity factor (persons/min/m) |
| 5 | **Flow Rate (F)** | $F = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | persons/min | N = number of persons, T = time |
| 6 | **Specific Flow (F_s)** | $F_s = v \times D$ | persons/min/m | v = speed, D = density (persons/m²) |
| 7 | **Available Safe Egress Time** | $ASET > RSET$ | seconds | Fundamental Life Safety Inequality |
| 8 | **Total Exit Width** | $W_{total} = \sum W_i$ | meters | Sum of widths of all exits serving a floor |
| 9 | **Travel Distance Check** | $L_{actual} \le L_{permitted}$ | meters | L_permitted from NBC Table 5 |
| 10 | **Number of Exits (N_e)** | $N_e \ge 2$ for O > 50 | integer | NBC 4.4.2 — minimum 2 remote exits |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut):** Evacuation modeling using **Pathfinder, STEPS, or FDS+Evac** software for KTU-validated designs.
- **Shopping malls (LuLu Mall, Centre Square):** Crowd simulation to prevent stampede on sale days.
- **Hospitals (horizontal evacuation)**: Bed-ridden patients moved to adjacent fire compartment in **< 90 seconds**.
- **Educational institutions (KTU campuses):** Assembly point design for 5,000+ students within 6 minutes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Travel Time from Fundamental Kinematics

**Starting Principle:** Travel time is distance divided by velocity (basic Newtonian kinematics applied to pedestrian flow).

**Given:** Occupant on the **5th floor** must travel **L = 30 m** along corridor, then descend **4 flights of stairs** (each flight = 3 m vertical = 4.5 m along slope), then travel **15 m** at ground level to assembly point.

**Step 1: Decompose the total path into homogeneous segments.**

$$L_{total} = L_{corridor} + L_{stairs} + L_{ground}$$

$$L_{total} = 30 + (4 \times 4.5) + 15$$

**Step 2: Identify the walking speed for each segment (Kerala climate adjustment).**

Horizontal corridor: $v_1 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$

Stairs (downward, dense crowd): $v_2 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$

Ground level (outdoor): $v_3 = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$ (open space, less congestion)

**Step 3: Compute individual segment times.**

$$t_1 = \dfrac{30}{1.0} = 30 \text{ seconds}$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{18}{0.5} = 36 \text{ seconds}$$

$$t_3 = \dfrac{15}{1.2} = 12.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4: Sum the segment times (linear superposition for serial paths).**

$$t_{trav} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3$$

$$t_{trav} = 30 + 36 + 12.5$$

$$t_{trav} = 78.5 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.31 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> The **stairs dominate the evacuation time** (36 of 78.5 s ≈ 46%). This justifies why **protected fire stairs** with **separate pressurization** and **wider landings** are mandatory for KTU high-rise designs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Exit Width from Occupant Load

**Given:** A **4th-floor office** with carpet area = **400 m²**.

**Step 1: Determine Occupant Load Factor (OLF) from NBC 2016 Table 3.**

For Business occupancy (office): $OLF = 8 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$

**Step 2: Calculate the total number of occupants on the floor.**

$$O = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area}}{OLF}$$

$$O = \dfrac{400}{8} = 50 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 3: Identify the capacity factor for stairs (downward).**

$$C = 60 \text{ persons/min/m}$$

**Step 4: Set the target evacuation time.**

For KTU/IS 14435, office buildings: $T_{target} = 2.5 \text{ minutes}$

**Step 5: Calculate the total exit width required.**

$$W_{required} = \dfrac{O}{C \times T_{target}}$$

$$W_{required} = \dfrac{50}{60 \times 2.5}$$

$$W_{required} = \dfrac{50}{150} = 0.333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Apply minimum code width.**

Per NBC 4.4.3.1, **minimum effective width of any exit = 1.0 m**, so:

$$W_{provided} = 1.0 \text{ m} \ge W_{required} = 0.333 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Verify number of exits.**

Per NBC 4.4.2, $O \le 50 \Rightarrow N_e \ge 1$ is acceptable, but **KTU best practice** recommends $N_e \ge 2$ for redundancy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Safe Egress Time (RSET) — Complete Worked Example

**Scenario:** A seminar hall in a KTU engineering college with 200 occupants. Calculate the **total evacuation time** from ignition to assembly.

**Step 1: Tabulate stage times based on engineering judgment + IS 14435 norms.**

| Stage | Symbol | Assumed Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection | $t_{det}$ | 30 s | Addressable smoke detector |
| Alarm | $t_{alarm}$ | 10 s | Voice evacuation, pre-recorded |
| Pre-movement | $t_{pre}$ | 60 s | Trained occupants, prior drills |
| Travel (horizontal) | $t_{horiz}$ | 45 s | 45 m at 1.0 m/s |
| Travel (stairs down 2 floors) | $t_{stair}$ | 36 s | 18 m stair path at 0.5 m/s |
| Travel (outdoor) | $t_{out}$ | 20 s | 24 m at 1.2 m/s |

**Step 2: Apply the RSET equation.**

$$RSET = t_{det} + t_{alarm} + t_{pre} + t_{horiz} + t_{stair} + t_{out}$$

$$RSET = 30 + 10 + 60 + 45 + 36 + 20$$

$$RSET = 201 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.35 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Compare with ASET (Available Safe Egress Time).**

For a sprinklered seminar hall: $ASET = 6 \text{ minutes} = 360 \text{ s}$

$$ASET = 360 \text{ s} > RSET = 201 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE DESIGN}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the safety margin.**

$$\text{Margin} = ASET - RSET = 360 - 201 = 159 \text{ s}$$

A positive margin indicates the **design is code-compliant and safe**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Exit Provision Rules (NBC 2016 Summary)

| Occupant Load (O) | Minimum Number of Exits | Min. Exit Width | Min. Stair Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| $O \le 50$ | 1 | 1.0 m | 1.25 m |
| $51 \le O \le 300$ | 2 | 1.0 m each | 1.25 m each |
| $301 \le O \le 500$ | 2 | 1.5 m each | 1.5 m each |
| $O > 500$ | 3 (remote) | 1.5 m each | 1.5 m each |
| High-hazard occupancy | 2 (independent) | 1.5 m | 1.5 m |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Sequential Stages of Evacuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Ignition] --> B[Stage 1: Detection]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Alarm Notification]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Pre-Movement Recognition]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Travel Movement]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Safe Assembly Area]
    F --> G[Headcount Verification]
    G --> H[All Clear Declared]
    B -.->|Faster via sprinklers| B
    C -.->|Faster via voice evac| C
    D -.->|Faster via training| D
    E -.->|Faster via wider exits| E
    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#ff6600
    style C fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style D fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600
    style F fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#003300
    style G fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc
    style H fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Evacuation Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Occupancy Zone]
        A1[Occupant Location]
    end
    subgraph B[Exit Access]
        B1[Room Door]
        B2[Internal Corridor]
        B3[Directional Signage]
    end
    subgraph C[Exit]
        C1[Protected Lobby]
        C2[Fire Staircase]
        C3[Pressurized Shaft]
    end
    subgraph D[Exit Discharge]
        D1[Ground Floor Lobby]
        D2[External Pathway]
    end
    subgraph E[Place of Safety]
        E1[Assembly Point]
        E2[Headcount Zone]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    style A fill:#ffeecc,stroke:#cc6600
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399
    style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style D fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600
    style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flow: Exit Number & Width Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Calculate Occupant Load O] --> Q1{O exceeds 50?}
    Q1 -->|No| E1[Provide 1 Exit, Min 1.0m width]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{O exceeds 300?}
    Q2 -->|No| E2[Provide 2 Exits, Min 1.0m each]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{O exceeds 500?}
    Q3 -->|No| E3[Provide 2 Exits, Min 1.5m each]
    Q3 -->|Yes| E4[Provide 3 Remote Exits, Min 1.5m each]
    E1 --> Check[Verify Travel Distance L actual less than L permitted]
    E2 --> Check
    E3 --> Check
    E4 --> Check
    Check --> Q4{ASET greater than RSET?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Safe[Design is SAFE]
    Q4 -->|No| Revise[Increase width or add exits]
    Revise --> Start
    style Start fill:#fff5cc
    style Safe fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style Revise fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Physical Layout of Staircase-Based Evacuation

> [!NOTE]
> **Building Cross-Section (Text Schematic):**
>
> ```
> ROOF LEVEL
> |------- Roof Slab -------|
> |  5F |  5F |  5F |  5F |
> |-----|  Fire Door 1  ----|   <-- Self-closing fire door (2hr rating)
> |  4F |=====|=====|  4F |   <-- Protected Lobby
> |-----|  Fire Door 2  ----|   <-- 1hr fire door
> |  3F |=====|=====|  3F |
> |-----|  Fire Door 3  ----|
> |  2F |=====|=====|  2F |
> |-----|  Fire Door 4  ----|
> |  1F |=====|=====|  1F |   <-- Exit Discharge Lobby
> |-----   Staircase Well  --|   <-- Pressurized (Pa = 50 Pa for high-rise)
> |  GF  |  EXIT TO OUTSIDE |
> ============================
>     ===>  ASSEMBLY POINT (30m away)
> ```
>
> **Key features labeled:**
> - **Fire-rated self-closing doors** at every floor level
> - **Pressurized staircase** (50 Pa positive pressure for >15m buildings)
> - **Protected lobby** to prevent smoke entry
> - **Independent exit discharge** to open air

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Evacuation" as per NBC 2016. List the four stages of evacuation in chronological order.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Evacuation is the systematic, time-bound, and orderly movement of occupants from a fire-affected zone of a building to a predetermined place of safety through engineered exits and evacuation routes, designed as per NBC 2016.
>
> **Four Stages (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Detection Stage** — ignition to fire detection
> 2. **Alarm/Notification Stage** — detection to occupant awareness
> 3. **Pre-Movement/Recognition Stage** — awareness to initiation of movement
> 4. **Travel/Movement Stage** — physical movement to place of safety
> (5th stage — Assembly/Headcount is often included as a 5th stage in KTU papers)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
*State the importance of the "Pre-Movement Stage" in evacuation. Mention two engineering measures to reduce pre-movement time.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Importance (1 Mark):** Pre-movement is the most **variable and psychologically complex** stage; it accounts for 30-50% of total evacuation time and is the leading cause of crowd congestion at exits.
>
> **Engineering Measures (2 Marks — any two):**
> 1. **Voice evacuation systems** with clear, location-specific instructions.
> 2. **Regular fire drills** (minimum twice a year, IS 14435).
> 3. **Photoluminescent signage** and way-finding lights.
> 4. **Visual alarm devices** (flashing beacons) for hearing-impaired.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

A **5-storey office building** in Kochi has the following data:
- Floor-to-floor height = **3.5 m**
- Carpet area per floor = **500 m²**
- Occupant Load Factor for office = **8 m²/person**
- Travel distance from farthest point to nearest stair = **35 m**
- Walking speed on stairs (downward) = **0.5 m/s**
- Capacity factor for stairs = **60 persons/min/m**
- Target evacuation time = **4 minutes**

**(a)** Calculate the **occupant load per floor** and the **total exit width required per floor**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the **travel time** for the occupant on the **5th floor** and verify if the design meets the **travel distance criterion** as per NBC 2016. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Occupant load per floor (2 Marks).**

$$O = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area}}{OLF} = \dfrac{500}{8} = 62.5 \approx 63 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 2: Number of exits required (1 Mark).**

Since $O = 63 > 50$, minimum **2 exits** are required per NBC 4.4.2.

**Step 3: Total exit width required (3 Marks).**

$$W_{total} = \dfrac{O}{C \times T}$$

$$W_{total} = \dfrac{63}{60 \times 4} = \dfrac{63}{240} = 0.2625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Apply code minimum (1 Mark).**

Minimum per-exit width = 1.0 m, so provide **2 exits × 1.0 m = 2.0 m total** (which is far in excess of 0.2625 m → very safe design).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate total travel path (2 Marks).**

Horizontal corridor: $L_h = 35 \text{ m}$

Staircase descent (4 inter-floor flights to reach ground): $L_s = 4 \times 4.5 = 18 \text{ m}$

Ground floor travel to exit discharge: $L_g = 15 \text{ m}$

$$L_{total} = 35 + 18 + 15 = 68 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Travel time calculation (3 Marks).**

$$t_{trav} = \dfrac{L_h}{v_{h}} + \dfrac{L_s}{v_{s}} + \dfrac{L_g}{v_{g}}$$

$$t_{trav} = \dfrac{35}{1.0} + \dfrac{18}{0.5} + \dfrac{15}{1.2}$$

$$t_{trav} = 35 + 36 + 12.5 = 83.5 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.39 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Travel distance check (2 Marks).**

Per NBC Table 5, for office (unsprinklered): $L_{permitted} = 30 \text{ m}$

Actual horizontal travel: $L_{actual} = 35 \text{ m} > 30 \text{ m}$ ❌ **NON-COMPLIANT**

**Step 4: Recommendation (included in marks).**

> **Remedial Action:** Either (i) install **automatic sprinklers** to extend $L_{permitted}$ to 45 m, or (ii) provide an **additional staircase** to reduce the horizontal travel distance to ≤ 30 m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **four stages of evacuation**. Why is the **pre-movement stage considered the most critical** stage in high-rise buildings? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **3-storey hospital building** has 60 patients on the top floor, all requiring **horizontal evacuation** to the adjacent fire compartment. Calculate the **time required for horizontal evacuation** if the compartment is 20 m away and patients are moved on wheelchairs at 0.6 m/s. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Flowchart (4 Marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. The four stages are:
1. **Detection** (t_det)
2. **Alarm** (t_alarm)
3. **Pre-Movement** (t_pre)
4. **Travel** (t_trav)

Each stage with its time interval should be labeled.

**Why pre-movement is critical in high-rises (3 Marks):**

- High-rise buildings have **large populations** (200-2000 occupants).
- Occupants are **unfamiliar with stair locations** (visitors, patients, students).
- Vertical travel is **physically demanding** → hesitation, especially for elderly and disabled.
- Smoke can enter staircases if **not properly pressurized** → occupants may turn back.
- **Denial behavior** is amplified in tall buildings ("it can't happen on my floor").
- Therefore, **t_pre** can be 3-5 minutes in high-rises, often exceeding t_trav itself.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters (1 Mark).**

$N = 60 \text{ patients}$, $L = 20 \text{ m}$, $v = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2: Calculate travel time per patient (2 Marks).**

$$t_{travel} = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{20}{0.6} = 33.33 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Account for queuing at the compartment door (3 Marks).**

Assume 1 wheelchair can pass through a 1.2 m door every 3 seconds:

$$\text{Service rate} = \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{3 \text{ s/wheelchair}} = 20 \text{ wheelchairs/min}$$

**Step 4: Total queue + service time (1 Mark):**

$$t_{queue} = \dfrac{N}{\text{Service rate}} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3 \text{ minutes}$$

$$t_{total} = t_{travel} + t_{queue} = 0.55 + 3.0 = 3.55 \text{ minutes}$$

**Conclusion:** Horizontal evacuation of 60 patients in **3.55 minutes** is achievable, well within the **6-minute ASET** for a sprinklered hospital.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PESFT416:**
> 1. **Confusing "Exit" with "Means of Egress"** — Exit is only the protected portion; means of egress = exit access + exit + exit discharge.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in formulas — always write "m", "s", "persons/min/m" explicitly.
> 3. **Using wrong OLF** — office = 8, assembly (concentrated) = 1.5, assembly (less concentrated) = 2.5, hospital ward = 8. NBC 2016 Table 3 must be quoted.
> 4. **Mixing units in $W = O/(C \times T)$** — convert everything to consistent units (e.g., O in persons, C in persons/min/m, T in minutes → W in meters).
> 5. **Ignoring NBC minimum widths** — even if calculated width is 0.3 m, code mandates ≥ 1.0 m.
> 6. **Not verifying $ASET > RSET$** — this is the **fundamental life safety check**; loss of 2 marks if omitted.
> 7. **Saying "two exits" without specifying "remote"** — NBC 4.4.2.3 requires exits to be placed at **≥ 1/3 of the diagonal distance** apart.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024):**
>
> - ☐ **Evacuation** = systematic movement to safety, governed by NBC 2016, IS 14435, NFPA 101.
> - ☐ **Four/Five Stages**: Detection → Alarm → Pre-Movement → Travel → Assembly.
> - ☐ **Pre-movement stage is the most unpredictable** (30 s to 5 min).
> - ☐ **RSET** = $t_{det} + t_{alarm} + t_{pre} + t_{trav}$ — must be less than ASET.
> - ☐ **Occupant Load** $O = \text{Area} / \text{OLF}$ (OLF from NBC Table 3).
> - ☐ **Exit Width** $W = O / (C \times T)$ — minimum 1.0 m.
> - ☐ **Capacity factor (stairs down)** = 60 persons/min/m; corridor = 80 persons/min/m.
> - ☐ **Walking speed**: horizontal = 1.0 m/s, stairs down = 0.5 m/s.
> - ☐ **Travel distance limits**: 22.5 m residential, 30 m office (unsprinklered).
> - ☐ **Number of exits**: ≥ 2 if O > 50, exits must be **remote** (≥ 1/3 diagonal apart).
> - ☐ **Means of Egress** = Exit Access + Exit + Exit Discharge.
> - ☐ **Staircase pressurization** = 50 Pa for high-rise (>15 m in Kerala).
> - ☐ **Voice evacuation** reduces pre-movement time by 30-50%.
> - ☐ **Life Safety Inequality**: $ASET > RSET$ — always verify in design problems.
> - ☐ **Assembly point** must be at least 30 m from the building.
> - ☐ **Horizontal evacuation** is preferred in hospitals (bed-ridden patients).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Building Evacuation Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Building Evacuation Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Building Evacuation Time**, also known as the **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)**, is the total time interval between the moment of fire ignition (or the initiating hazardous event) and the moment when the **last occupant reaches a place of safety** (typically an exit discharge, refuge floor, or designated assembly point).

Mathematically, the total evacuation time is the **summation of four sequential time intervals**, as defined by **SFPE (Society of Fire Protection Engineers)** and adopted by the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)**:

$$
T_{RSET} \;=\; t_d \;+\; t_n \;+\; t_{pre} \;+\; t_t
$$

Where each component represents a distinct phase of the human escape process during a fire emergency.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚒

Imagine a school fire drill as a **four-stage water pipe system**:

- **Stage 1 – Detection (t_d):** The "smoke detector" — like a security guard first noticing smoke. Until someone sees/smells it, the alarm cannot ring. In a theatre, this could be minutes before anyone reacts.
- **Stage 2 – Notification (t_n):** The "alarm bell ringing" — once smoke is detected, the alarm must sound. In a manual system, someone must press the call point; in an automatic system, this is almost instant.
- **Stage 3 – Pre-movement (t_pre):** The "thinking & decision time" — occupants must believe the alarm, abandon their work, gather belongings, decide a route, and START moving. This is the **most variable and least predictable** stage (30 seconds to 10+ minutes!).
- **Stage 4 – Travel (t_t):** The "running to the exit" — actual walking/running/crawling through corridors, down stairs, and out the door.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Fire Engineering:** A building is **SAFE** if and only if the evacuation time $T_{RSET}$ is **less than** the Available Safe Egress Time ($T_{ASET}$), which is the time until smoke, heat, or toxic gases make escape impossible. Designers aim for: $\;T_{RSET} \;<\; T_{ASET}$.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravitational acceleration | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | Universal |
| Body elliptical width (human body) | **W_p = 0.45–0.50 m** | SFPE Handbook |
| Shoulder width of adult | **W_s ≈ 0.50 m** | Fruin/Pauls |
| Maximum walking speed (horizontal) | **S_max = 1.30–1.40 m/s** | Unimpeded |
| Maximum specific flow (exit door) | **f_max ≈ 80–82 persons/min·m** | SFPE empirical |
| Critical density (choking point) | **D_c ≈ 1.9 persons/m²** | Fruin (1971) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Time Components — Detailed Logic

### 🔹 2.1.1 Detection Time (t_d)

**Definition:** Interval between fire ignition and the moment of **first detection** (by automatic detector or human senses).

**Sub-components:**
- **Smoke transport time:** Time for smoke to travel from fire seat to detector location.
- **Detector activation time:** Time for smoke concentration / temperature rise to cross detector's threshold sensitivity.

**Typical values (NBC 2016 / SFPE):**
- Automatic smoke detector: **30 s – 2 min**
- Automatic heat detector: **1 – 5 min**
- Human detection (sleeping occupants): **> 5 min**
- Scented/visible smoke: **0.5 – 2 min**

### 🔹 2.1.2 Notification Time (t_n)

**Definition:** Interval between detection and the **broadcast of a general evacuation alarm** to all occupants.

**Logic chain:** Detector → Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) → Sounders/PA System → Occupants' ears.

**Typical values:**
- Voice alarm / PA system: **< 30 seconds**
- Manual call point: **60 – 120 seconds**
- Unannounced (no alarm system): **NOT ACCEPTABLE** in modern codes.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Pre-Movement Time (t_pre) — THE MOST CRITICAL VARIABLE

**Definition:** Interval between the alarm sounding and the moment occupants **begin to move** towards an exit.

**Sub-phases (Sime / Canter models):**
1. **Recognition** — hearing/believing the alarm (10 – 60 s).
2. **Interpretation** — deciding if it is real (10 – 60 s).
3. **Action decision** — choosing to evacuate (10 – 60 s).
4. **Behavioral sequence** — shutting down work, collecting belongings, helping others (0 – 5 min).

**Why it matters:** This is the **single biggest source of unpredictability** in evacuation modeling. KTU examiners love this question!

**Typical values:**
- Offices with trained staff: **1 – 3 minutes**
- Shopping malls with public: **2 – 5 minutes**
- Hospitals (immobile patients): **> 8 minutes**
- Sleeping occupants (hotels, residences): **> 20 minutes**

### 🔹 2.1.4 Travel/Movement Time (t_t) — THE CALCULABLE PHASE

**Definition:** Interval between the start of movement and the **last occupant reaching safety**.

This is the only phase computed deterministically via engineering models. It has two sub-components:

$$
t_t \;=\; t_{walk} \;+\; t_{queue}
$$

- **t_walk** = walking time along unobstructed paths = **Distance / Walking Speed**
- **t_queue** = queuing time at bottlenecks (doors, stairs, corridors)

## 2.2 Hydraulic Flow Model — The Core Mathematical Engine

The **Hydraulic Flow Model**, originated by **John Fruin (1971)** and formalized in the **SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection Engineering**, treats occupants as a **continuous fluid** flowing through a network of building corridors and exits.

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Variables

| Variable | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Occupant Load | N | Total number of persons in the population | persons |
| Density | D | Number of persons per unit area | persons/m² |
| Walking Speed | S | Speed of the pedestrian flow | m/s |
| Flow Rate | F | Number of persons crossing a point per unit time | persons/s or persons/min |
| Specific Flow | f | Flow per unit effective width | persons/(min·m) |
| Effective Width | W_e | Exit width minus boundary layers | m |

### 2.2.2 Key Empirical Equations

**Fruin's Walking Speed Equation (Horizontal):**

$$
S \;=\; 1.4 \;-\; 0.37\,D \qquad \text{[m/s], for } 0 \le D \le 3.5 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

**Fruin's Walking Speed Equation (Stairs, descent):**

$$
S_{stairs} \;=\; 0.62 \;-\; 0.23\,D \qquad \text{[m/s]}
$$

**Fundamental Flow Equation (continuity):**

$$
F \;=\; S \,\times\, D \,\times\, W_e \qquad \text{[persons/s]}
$$

**Specific Flow:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{F}{W_e} \;=\; S \,\times\, D \qquad \text{[persons/(s·m)]}
$$

**Travel Time Through a Path Segment:**

$$
t_{walk} \;=\; \frac{L}{S}
$$

**Travel Time Through a Door (queue + passage):**

$$
t_{door} \;=\; \frac{N}{f_{door} \,\times\, W_e}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{RSET} = t_d + t_n + t_{pre} + t_t$ | Total Required Safe Egress Time | All fire scenarios |
| 2 | $S = 1.4 - 0.37D$ | Fruin horizontal speed | $0 \le D \le 3.5$ p/m² |
| 3 | $S_{stair} = 0.62 - 0.23D$ | Fruin stair speed | $0 \le D \le 3.5$ p/m² |
| 4 | $F = S \cdot D \cdot W_e$ | Flow rate | Steady-state |
| 5 | $f = S \cdot D$ | Specific flow | Unit: persons/(s·m) |
| 6 | $t_{walk} = L / S$ | Walking time | Free flow |
| 7 | $t_{queue} = N / (f \cdot W_e)$ | Queue time at bottleneck | Dense crowd |
| 8 | $T = P / (f_c \cdot W_e)$ | Capacity flow method | Total evacuation |
| 9 | $f_{max} \approx 1.33$ persons/(s·m) | Maximum specific flow (door) | At D ≈ 1.9 p/m² |
| 10 | $T_{RSET} < T_{ASET}$ | Safety criterion | Design constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Layer Deduction (SFPE):** The **effective width** $W_e$ is calculated by subtracting the **boundary layer** ($b \approx 0.15$ m on each side) from the **clear width** $W_c$: $\;\;W_e = W_c - 2b$. For a **single-side boundary**, deduct only **one boundary layer**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hydraulic model is used by:
- **Fire Safety Consultants** designing egress systems for high-rises (BIS/NBC compliance)
- **Tunnel & Metro engineers** for crowd safety
- **Stadium architects** ensuring spectator evacuation
- **Kerala State Fire & Rescue Services** for building plan approvals

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles of Crowd Flow

We begin by considering a stream of $N$ persons moving along a corridor of effective width $W_e$ at density $D$ and walking speed $S$.

### Step 1 — Define the Cross-Sectional Population

At any instant, the **number of persons in a length element $dx$** of the corridor is:

$$
dN \;=\; D \,\times\, (W_e \,\cdot\, dx)
$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Flow Through a Cross-Section

The flow rate (persons per unit time crossing a line) equals the number of persons in a unit length multiplied by their velocity:

$$
F \;=\; \frac{dN}{dt} \bigg/ dx \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F \;=\; D \,\cdot\, W_e \,\cdot\, S \qquad \text{[persons/s]}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive Specific Flow

Dividing by effective width gives flow per unit width — a corridor property independent of geometry:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{F}{W_e} \;=\; D \,\cdot\, S
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Fruin's Empirical Speed Equation

$$
f(D) \;=\; D \,\times\, (1.4 - 0.37D) \;=\; 1.4D - 0.37D^2
$$

### Step 5 — Find Maximum Specific Flow (differentiate)

To find peak capacity, take derivative and set to zero:

$$
\frac{df}{dD} \;=\; 1.4 \;-\; 0.74D \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D^* \;=\; \frac{1.4}{0.74} \;\approx\; 1.89 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Back to Get Maximum Specific Flow

$$
f_{max} \;=\; 1.4 \times 1.89 \;-\; 0.37 \times (1.89)^2 \;=\; 2.646 \;-\; 1.322 \;\approx\; 1.32 \text{ persons/(s·m)}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
f_{max} \;\approx\; 1.32 \times 60 \;\approx\; 79.2 \text{ persons/(min·m)} \;\;\approx\; \mathbf{80 \;persons/(min\cdot m)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This matches the empirical **SFPE standard exit capacity** used in NBC 2016 Part 4 — verification that theoretical derivation matches real measurement!

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (Numerically Exhaustive)

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A 5-storey office building in Trivandrum has **50 occupants** on the **third floor**. They must evacuate via a **1.5 m wide corridor** (30 m long), then descend a **1.2 m wide stair** (15 m vertical height) and exit through a **door of clear width 1.0 m**.
> **Given:** Detection time = 30 s, Notification time = 15 s, Pre-movement time = 90 s.
> **Calculate:** Total evacuation time $T_{RSET}$.

### Step 1 — Determine Boundary-Layer-Adjusted Effective Widths

For a corridor with **two boundaries** (both sides walled), $b = 0.15$ m on each side:

$$
W_{e,\text{corr}} \;=\; 1.5 \;-\; 2(0.15) \;=\; 1.20 \text{ m}
$$

For a stair with **one boundary** (one side has handrail/banister), deduct only one boundary:

$$
W_{e,\text{stair}} \;=\; 1.2 \;-\; 0.15 \;=\; 1.05 \text{ m}
$$

For the door (two boundaries):

$$
W_{e,\text{door}} \;=\; 1.0 \;-\; 2(0.15) \;=\; 0.70 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Density in Corridor

$$
D_{\text{corr}} \;=\; \frac{N}{L_{\text{corr}} \times W_{e,\text{corr}}} \;=\; \frac{50}{30 \times 1.20} \;=\; \frac{50}{36} \;\approx\; 1.389 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Walking Speed in Corridor (Fruin's equation)

$$
S_{\text{corr}} \;=\; 1.4 \;-\; 0.37 \times 1.389 \;=\; 1.4 \;-\; 0.514 \;\approx\; 0.886 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Walking Time Through Corridor

$$
t_{\text{corr}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{corr}}}{S_{\text{corr}}} \;=\; \frac{30}{0.886} \;\approx\; 33.86 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Density on Stair

$$
D_{\text{stair}} \;=\; \frac{50}{15 \times 1.05} \;=\; \frac{50}{15.75} \;\approx\; 3.175 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Walking Speed on Stair (Fruin's stair equation)

$$
S_{\text{stair}} \;=\; 0.62 \;-\; 0.23 \times 3.175 \;=\; 0.62 \;-\; 0.730 \;\approx\; -0.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative speed!** Density **exceeds the model's validity range** (D ≤ 3.5). This indicates a **jam condition** — occupants will queue. When $D > 3.5$, set $S = 0$ and apply **queuing time** model only.

**Practical correction:** Use $D \approx 3.5$ as ceiling:

$$
S_{\text{stair}} \;=\; 0.62 \;-\; 0.23 \times 3.5 \;=\; 0.62 \;-\; 0.805 \;\approx\; -0.185 \;\rightarrow\; 0 \text{ m/s (jam)}
$$

### Step 7 — Calculate Door Capacity and Queue Time

Using maximum specific flow $f_{max} = 1.32$ persons/(s·m):

$$
F_{\text{door}} \;=\; f_{max} \times W_{e,\text{door}} \;=\; 1.32 \times 0.70 \;\approx\; 0.924 \text{ persons/s}
$$

$$
t_{\text{door}} \;=\; \frac{N}{F_{\text{door}}} \;=\; \frac{50}{0.924} \;\approx\; 54.11 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 8 — Sum Total Travel Time

$$
t_t \;=\; t_{\text{corr}} \;+\; t_{\text{door}} \;\approx\; 33.86 \;+\; 54.11 \;\approx\; 87.97 \text{ s}
$$

(Taking conservatively $t_t \approx 90$ s)

### Step 9 — Compute Total RSET

$$
T_{RSET} \;=\; t_d + t_n + t_{pre} + t_t \;=\; 30 \;+\; 15 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 90 \;\approx\; 225 \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting:

$$
\boxed{T_{RSET} \;\approx\; 3.75 \text{ minutes}}
$$

### Step 10 — Safety Verification (if ASET is given)

If, for example, $T_{ASET} = 6$ minutes (from smoke/heat modelling), then:

$$
T_{RSET} \;=\; 3.75 \text{ min} \;<\; T_{ASET} \;=\; 6 \text{ min} \;\;\checkmark \text{ SAFE}
$$

Safety margin: $\;(T_{ASET} - T_{RSET})/T_{RSET} = 60\%$ — comfortable margin per NBC guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Evacuation Time Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FIRE IGNITION] --> B[t_d : DETECTION TIME]
    B --> C[t_n : NOTIFICATION TIME]
    C --> D[t_pre : PRE-MOVEMENT TIME]
    D --> E[t_walk : WALKING TIME]
    E --> F[t_queue : QUEUE TIME AT EXIT]
    F --> G[LAST OCCUPANT REACHES SAFETY]
    G --> H[T_RSET : TOTAL EVACUATION TIME]

    subgraph DETECTION_BLOCK
        B
    end

    subgraph BEHAVIOR_BLOCK
        D
    end

    subgraph MOVEMENT_BLOCK
        E
        F
    end

    H --> I{T_RSET less than T_ASET ?}
    I -->|YES| J[BUILDING IS SAFE : ACCEPTABLE DESIGN]
    I -->|NO| K[UNSAFE : REDESIGN REQUIRED]

    nodeA[Stage 1]:::stageStyle -.-> B
    nodeB[Stage 2]:::stageStyle -.-> C
    nodeC[Stage 3]:::stageStyle -.-> D
    nodeD[Stage 4]:::stageStage4 -.-> E

    classDef stageStyle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef stageStage4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hydraulic Flow Model — Network Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    FIRE[Fire Ignition Event] -->|t_d| DET[Detection Phase]
    DET -->|t_n| NOT[Notification Phase]
    NOT -->|t_pre| PRE[Pre-movement Phase]
    PRE -->|Movement starts| COR[Corridor Segment L1 W1]
    COR -->|t_walk1| STAIR[Stair Segment L2 W2]
    STAIR -->|t_walk2| DOOR[Exit Door W3]
    DOOR -->|t_queue| SAFE[Place of Safety]
    SAFE --> END[T_RSET Computed]

    COR -.->|Fruin S = 1.4 minus 0.37D| SF1[Walking Speed]
    STAIR -.->|Fruin S = 0.62 minus 0.23D| SF2[Stair Speed]
    DOOR -.->|f_max approx 80 per min m| SF3[Door Capacity]

    SF1 -.-> CALC[Hydraulic Flow Calculator]
    SF2 -.-> CALC
    SF3 -.-> CALC
    CALC -.-> END

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    classDef flowStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    class DET,NOT,PRE phaseStyle
    class SF1,SF2,SF3,CALC flowStyle
```

## 4.3 Density vs Specific Flow — Conceptual Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Density less than 0.5 per m2] -->|Free Flow| B[S approx 1.3 m/s]
    B --> C[D increases]
    C --> D[Medium Density 1.0 to 1.9 per m2]
    D -->|Peak Flow| E[f_max approx 1.32 per s m at D approx 1.89]
    E --> F[D exceeds 3.5 per m2]
    F -->|Jam| G[S approx 0 : Crowd Collapse]

    classDef safeStyle fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    classDef peakStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    classDef dangerStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    class A,B safeStyle
    class C,D,E peakStyle
    class F,G dangerStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Required Safe Egress Time (RSET) and list its four components. Which component typically contributes the maximum variability in evacuation time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RSET** is the total time required for the last occupant to evacuate a building from the moment of fire ignition.

$$
T_{RSET} = t_d + t_n + t_{pre} + t_t
$$

The four components are: **(1) Detection time (t_d)** — fire ignition to first detection; **(2) Notification time (t_n)** — detection to general alarm; **(3) Pre-movement time (t_pre)** — alarm to start of movement; **(4) Travel time (t_t)** — start of movement to safety.

> The **pre-movement time (t_pre)** contributes the maximum variability, ranging from 30 seconds (trained staff) to over 20 minutes (sleeping occupants) **[1 mark]**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**State Fruin's walking speed equation for horizontal movement. What is the critical density at which the specific flow becomes maximum, and what is that maximum flow value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fruin's empirical walking speed equation for horizontal corridors:

$$
S = 1.4 - 0.37D \qquad \text{[m/s]}
$$

The **critical density** at which specific flow is maximum:

$$
D^* = 1.4 / 0.74 \approx 1.89 \text{ persons/m}^2 \approx 1.9 \text{ persons/m}^2 \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

Maximum specific flow (theoretical):

$$
f_{max} \approx 1.32 \text{ persons/(s·m)} \approx 80 \text{ persons/(min·m)} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**A high-rise office floor in Kochi has 120 occupants. The evacuation path consists of:**
- **Corridor:** Length 40 m, clear width 2.0 m (two boundaries)
- **Stair:** Length 18 m (vertical), clear width 1.4 m (one boundary)
- **Exit door:** Clear width 1.2 m (two boundaries)

**Given:** Detection time = 45 s, Notification time = 30 s, Pre-movement time = 120 s.

**Calculate:**
- **(a)** Effective widths of corridor, stair, and door. Walking time through corridor and stair, assuming density-based Fruin's equations. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Queue time at door and total RSET. Comment on safety if ASET = 8 minutes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Solution A(a) — Effective Widths & Walking Times **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Effective widths with boundary layer b = 0.15 m**

$$
W_{e,\text{corr}} = 2.0 - 2(0.15) = 1.70 \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_{e,\text{stair}} = 1.4 - 1(0.15) = 1.25 \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_{e,\text{door}} = 1.2 - 2(0.15) = 0.90 \text{ m}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 2: Corridor density**

$$
D_{\text{corr}} = \frac{N}{L_{\text{corr}} \cdot W_{e,\text{corr}}} = \frac{120}{40 \times 1.70} = \frac{120}{68} \approx 1.765 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 3: Walking speed in corridor (Fruin)**

$$
S_{\text{corr}} = 1.4 - 0.37 \times 1.765 = 1.4 - 0.653 = 0.747 \text{ m/s}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 4: Walking time in corridor**

$$
t_{\text{corr}} = \frac{L}{S} = \frac{40}{0.747} \approx 53.55 \text{ s}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 5: Stair density**

$$
D_{\text{stair}} = \frac{120}{18 \times 1.25} = \frac{120}{22.5} \approx 5.33 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

Since $D > 3.5$, **jam condition** — set $S_{\text{stair}} = 0$ and treat as queue. **[1 mark]**

**Step 6: Stair walking time → treated as queue contribution**

$$
t_{\text{stair}} \to t_{\text{queue,stair}} \quad \text{[contributes to door queue]} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

---

#### 📖 Solution A(b) — Door Queue Time & Total RSET **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Door capacity (max specific flow)**

$$
F_{\text{door}} = f_{max} \times W_{e,\text{door}} = 1.32 \times 0.90 = 1.188 \text{ persons/s}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 2: Door queue time**

$$
t_{\text{door}} = \frac{N}{F_{\text{door}}} = \frac{120}{1.188} \approx 101.0 \text{ s}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 3: Total travel time**

$$
t_t = t_{\text{corr}} + t_{\text{door}} = 53.55 + 101.0 = 154.55 \text{ s}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 4: Compute total RSET**

$$
T_{RSET} = t_d + t_n + t_{pre} + t_t = 45 + 30 + 120 + 154.55 \approx 349.55 \text{ s} \approx 5.83 \text{ min}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 5: Safety verification**

$$
T_{RSET} = 5.83 \text{ min} \;<\; T_{ASET} = 8 \text{ min} \;\;\checkmark
$$

Safety margin = $(8 - 5.83)/5.83 = 37.2\%$ → **Building is SAFE with adequate margin** **[1 mark]**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**Explain the Hydraulic Flow Model for evacuation. Derive Fruin's walking speed equation's maximum specific flow. A conference hall of 200 occupants has a single exit door of effective width 1.5 m. Using the capacity flow method, calculate the door egress time and identify what happens if density exceeds 3.5 persons/m².**

**(a)** Explain the hydraulic flow model with assumptions, and derive the maximum specific flow mathematically. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Solve the conference hall problem and discuss jam condition behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Solution B(a) — Hydraulic Flow Model & Derivation **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Model assumptions** **[2 marks]**
1. Occupants are uniformly distributed.
2. All occupants start evacuation simultaneously (worst-case).
3. Movement is steady-state (homogeneous, continuous fluid).
4. Flow follows Fruin's empirical speed-density relation.
5. Building geometry is converted into a network of corridor/stair/door segments.

**Step 2: Set up flow equation** **[1 mark]**

$$
f = D \cdot S = D(1.4 - 0.37D) = 1.4D - 0.37D^2
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate and solve** **[2 marks]**

$$
