\frac{df}{dD} = 1.4 - 0.74D = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D^* = \frac{1.4}{0.74} \approx 1.89 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute peak specific flow** **[1 mark]**

$$
f_{max} = 1.4(1.89) - 0.37(1.89)^2 = 2.646 - 1.322 \approx 1.32 \text{ persons/(s·m)}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{max} \approx 80 \text{ persons/(min·m)}}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering utility** **[1 mark]** — Used by NBC 2016, SFPE, and BIS to size exits, doors, and stair widths in public buildings.

---

#### 📖 Solution B(b) — Conference Hall Problem **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Door flow capacity (capacity flow method)** **[1 mark]**

$$
F_{\text{door}} = f_{max} \times W_e = 1.32 \times 1.5 = 1.98 \text{ persons/s}
$$

**Step 2: Door egress time** **[1 mark]**

$$
t_{\text{door}} = \frac{N}{F_{\text{door}}} = \frac{200}{1.98} \approx 101.01 \text{ s} \approx 1.68 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Density in hall area (assumed total area)** **[1 mark]**

Assuming hall area $A = 100 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
D = N/A = 200/100 = 2.0 \text{ persons/m}^2
$$

> This exceeds $D^* = 1.89$, so flow is **sub-optimal** — already past peak capacity! **[1 mark]**

**Step 4: Discussion of jam condition (D > 3.5)** **[2 marks]**

When density exceeds 3.5 persons/m²:
- Walking speed $S \to 0$ (Fruin equation gives negative values).
- Movement becomes **impossible / crowd collapse**.
- **Pressure wave injuries** can occur (stampede risk).
- Solution: **wider doors, multiple exits, crowd management**.

**Step 5: Recommendations** **[1 mark]** — Provide **at least 2 doors**, use **staggered evacuation phases**, install **voice alarms** to control flow.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary layer deduction** — students use clear width directly instead of $W_e$. Loss: **1–2 marks per sub-question**.
> 2. **Using Fruin's horizontal equation on stairs** — Stairs have a **different coefficient** (0.62, 0.23). Wrong equation → **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Not checking density validity** — When $D > 3.5$, speed becomes negative; students must **explicitly state jam condition**.
> 4. **Mixing units** — Speed in m/s vs. flow in persons/min. Always convert: $\times 60$ for per-second → per-minute.
> 5. **Omitting units** — Every numerical answer must carry units (m, m/s, persons/m², s).
> 6. **Skipping the safety verification step** — Always conclude with $T_{RSET} < T_{ASET}$ check.
> 7. **Forgetting pre-movement time** — Many students compute only travel time and miss the largest single contribution.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⏱️ **Total Evacuation Time** = $t_d + t_n + t_{pre} + t_t$ — four sequential, non-overlapping intervals.
- 🎯 **Pre-movement time (t_pre)** is the **most variable** component (30 s to 20+ min).
- 🚶 **Fruin's equations:** Horizontal $S = 1.4 - 0.37D$; Stairs $S = 0.62 - 0.23D$.
- 🌊 **Specific flow** $f = S \times D$ is a corridor property, independent of width.
- 📐 **Critical density** $D^* \approx 1.89$ persons/m² gives **maximum specific flow** $f_{max} \approx 80$ persons/(min·m).
- 🚪 **Effective width** = clear width − boundary layers ($b = 0.15$ m); deduct **1 layer** for stairs, **2 layers** for corridors/doors.
- ⚠️ **Density > 3.5 p/m²** → **jam condition**, $S = 0$, crowd collapse risk.
- ✅ **Design criterion:** $T_{RSET} < T_{ASET}$ with a recommended safety margin of ≥ 30%.
- 🏢 **NBC 2016 (India)** mandates SFPE-compliant hydraulic calculations for all buildings above 15 m height.
- 📊 **Capacity flow method** formula: $T = P / (f_c \cdot W_e)$ — quick tool for exit sizing.
- 🧪 **More accurate (but complex) methods**: Agent-based models (Pathfinder, STEPS, Exodus) for high-rises.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning of Seating Arrangements in Premises Where Large Number of People Gather
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planning of Seating Arrangements in Premises Where Large Number of People Gather

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** — specifically **Part 4: Fire and Life Safety** and **Part 8: Building Services** — *Premises Where Large Number of People Gather* are formally classified as **Group D (Assembly) Occupancies**. These are buildings or parts of buildings used for gatherings of **100 or more persons** for purposes such as civic, social, religious, recreational, educational, cultural, instructional, entertainment, sporting, transit, or awaiting transport.

The **planning of seating arrangements** in such premises is a critical sub-discipline of **Life Safety Engineering**, governed by the principle of providing:
- Adequate **Occupant Load (OL)** capacity,
- Safe **Travel Distance (TD)** to exits,
- Adequate **Exit Width (EW)** based on OL,
- Clear **Aisle Geometry** to enable rapid evacuation under panic conditions,
- Proper **Row Geometry** to prevent cascading crowd crush phenomena.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per NBC 2016 Part 4, Clause 4.4.3, every Assembly Building (Group D) must be designed such that the **travel distance from any seat to an exit shall not exceed 30 m**, and **every seat shall be served by aisles leading to exits**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Flow" Intuition

Imagine water flowing out of a large filled tank (the auditorium) through a narrow pipe (the exit door). If the pipe is too narrow, or the path inside the tank is blocked by obstacles (poor seating layout), the water rushes chaotically, causing turbulence, back-pressure, and overflow (the crowd crush).

**Seating arrangement planning is essentially designing the tank's internal geometry and pipe diameters BEFORE the water (crowd) is allowed to enter.**

- The **number of people** = volume of water.
- The **aisles** = channels directing water.
- The **exits** = the pipes.
- The **vomitories** (tunnel-like passages through seating) = pressure-relief valves.
- The **seat spacing** = flow obstacles that *must* be standardized to prevent turbulence.

A poorly planned seating arrangement turns orderly evacuation into a fluid-dynamics nightmare, often leading to the **"crowd crush"** phenomenon observed in disasters like the Kumbh Mela stampedes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024):** **Occupant Load (OL)** is the maximum number of persons permitted to occupy a building or portion thereof at any one time, determined by dividing the floor area available for that use by the **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)** prescribed for the occupancy in NBC 2016 Table 3 (Part 4).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally applicable under the KTU 2024 framework (aligned to NBC 2016 and IS standards):

- **Occupant Load Factor (OLF) for Assembly Areas:** **0.5 m²/person** to **1.5 m²/person** depending on the use (e.g., concentrated assembly without fixed seating = **0.5 m²/person**; standing space = **0.46 m²/person**; movable seats = **1.4 m²/person**).
- **Minimum clear width of an aisle:** **1.0 m** (for assembly with fixed seats) up to **1.5 m** (for main aisles serving more than 60 seats).
- **Maximum Travel Distance (TD):** **30 m** for non-sprinklered assembly; **45 m** with sprinklers (NBC 2016 Clause 4.4.3.1).
- **Unit of Exit Width (UEW):** **500 mm** (NBC standard) — also called "exit unit."
- **Capacity of one exit unit:** **50 persons per 500 mm** for stairs; **60 persons per 500 mm** for level exits (passageways).
- **Minimum seat width per person (fixed seating):** **450 mm** (NBC) / **500 mm** (IS 4878 — preferred for KTU).
- **Minimum seat back-to-back spacing (row depth):** **850 mm** (NBC) to **900 mm** (recommended).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Mandate (KTU PESFT416):** The student must be able to compute the **Occupant Load**, determine the **number of exits**, calculate **exit width**, and verify **aisle and travel distance** compliance for any given assembly premise plan.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Six Pillars of Seating Plan Safety

The planning of seating arrangements is decomposed into **six interrelated design pillars**. Each pillar must be satisfied simultaneously for a layout to be KTU-compliant and NBC-compliant.

### Pillar 1: Occupancy Classification

Every premise is classified by the **function** it serves. The classification drives the **OLF** and the **egress strategy**.

| Occupancy Sub-Group | Example | Applicable OLF (m²/person) |
|---|---|---|
| D-1: Civic / Cultural | Auditoriums, Lecture halls | 1.4 (fixed seats) |
| D-2: Religious | Temples, Churches, Mosques | 0.5 (concentrated) |
| D-3: Educational (≥100) | School assembly halls | 1.0 |
| D-4: Transit | Airport lounges, Bus stations | 1.5 |
| D-5: Entertainment | Theatres, Cinemas, Stadiums | 0.5 to 1.4 |
| D-6: Outdoor / Amusement | Fairs, Melas, Theme parks | 0.46 (standing) |

### Pillar 2: Occupant Load (OL) Calculation

The OL is **not** a free design choice — it is a **regulatory computation**. The designer cannot legally exceed it.

### Pillar 3: Aisle Geometry (Width, Direction, Spacing)

Aisles function as the **flow highways** of the seating bowl. The two critical sub-parameters are:
- **Aisle width (AW)** — minimum mandated based on number of seats served.
- **Aisle length (AL)** — measured as the number of seats per row (row length).

### Pillar 4: Row Geometry (Seat Spacing & Dead-End Aisle Control)

A **dead-end aisle** is a transverse or radial aisle that terminates against a wall or solid barrier without connecting to an exit. Excessive dead-end rows create **bottlenecks during evacuation**.

### Pillar 5: Exit Number, Width & Travel Distance

The number of exits is determined by the OL; their width by the formula involving **persons per unit exit width**; and their placement by the maximum travel distance.

### Pillar 6: Special Provisions (Vomitories, Step Aisles, Smoke Control)

For large venues like stadia, **vomitories** (tunnels cutting through the seating mass) are mandated to provide egress through the seating bank.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU-mandated formula sheet. Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Occupant Load (OL)** | $OL = \dfrac{A_{net}}{OLF}$ | persons | $A_{net}$ = net usable floor area in m² |
| 2 | **Number of Occupants per Exit Unit (Stairs)** | $C_{stair} = 50$ | persons / exit unit | NBC 2016 Table 5 |
| 3 | **Number of Occupants per Exit Unit (Level)** | $C_{level} = 60$ | persons / exit unit | NBC 2016 Table 5 |
| 4 | **Total Exit Width Required (EWR)** | $EWR = \dfrac{OL}{C_{stair}} \times UEW$ | mm | UEW = 500 mm |
| 5 | **Number of Exits Required (N)** | $N = \dfrac{OL}{C_{per-exit}}$ | integer ≥ 2 | Minimum 2 remote exits for OL > 50 |
| 6 | **Maximum Travel Distance (TD)** | $TD \le 30$ m (unsprinklered) | m | NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.3.1 |
| 7 | **Maximum Travel Distance (Sprinklered)** | $TD \le 45$ m | m | With automatic sprinkler protection |
| 8 | **Minimum Aisle Width (Transverse)** | $AW \ge 1.0$ m (per ≤ 60 seats) | m | Wider if more seats served |
| 9 | **Minimum Aisle Width (Longitudinal)** | $AW_{long} \ge 1.2$ m | m | Increases with row length |
| 10 | **Maximum Seats per Row (Unblocked Dead-End Aisle)** | $N_{row} \le 20$ (NBC) / 16 (IS 4878) | seats | When aisle dead-ends at one end |
| 11 | **Minimum Seat Width** | $W_{seat} \ge 450$ mm | mm | NBC; 500 mm preferred (IS) |
| 12 | **Minimum Row Depth (back-to-back)** | $D_{row} \ge 850$ mm | mm | NBC; 900 mm preferred |
| 13 | **Vomitory Spacing (Stadia)** | One vomitory per **1500–2000** seats | seats | Engineering judgment |
| 14 | **Egress Time (T)** | $T = \dfrac{OL \times t_{person}}{N_{exits} \times C_{stair}}$ | seconds | $t_{person}$ = 1.3 s/exit unit |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

In professional practice, these equations are used in:
- **Stadium design** (e.g., Narendra Modi Stadium, Wankhede) — vomitory and tier-egress design.
- **Airport terminal design** (CIAL Terminal 3, Delhi IGI T3) — occupant load & travel distance for lounges.
- **Auditorium design** (KTU campus auditoriums, NCERT halls) — exit number & width.
- **Religious gathering design** (Temple *kovilpura*, Church halls) — concentrated assembly calculations.
- **Disaster forensics** — post-incident analysis of crowd crushes (e.g., Vaishno Devi stampede analysis) to identify the failed design parameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Occupant Load (OL)

The **Occupant Load** concept is derived from the **hydraulic flow model** of pedestrian movement, analogous to **Darcy-Weisbach fluid flow**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a floor area $A_{net}$ (in m²) to be occupied by pedestrians standing or seated. Each pedestrian, on average, requires a **minimum projected area** on the floor to move, stand, or sit without encroaching on others. This minimum area is the **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)**.

Therefore, the **maximum number of persons** that can be safely accommodated is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & N = \text{ Number of persons} \\
\text{Let } & A_{p} = \text{ Average projected area per person (OLF)} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{person}] \\
\text{Let } & A_{net} = \text{ Net usable floor area} \quad [\text{m}^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

By the principle of **conservation of area**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \times A_{p} & \le A_{net} \\
N & \le \dfrac{A_{net}}{A_{p}} \\
N_{max} & = \left\lfloor \dfrac{A_{net}}{OLF} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the **design Occupant Load** is:

$$
\boxed{OL = \dfrac{A_{net}}{OLF}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The OL is a dimensionless count. Numerator has units of m²; denominator has units of m²/person; cancellation yields **persons**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The floor area used must be the **net** area — gross area **minus** wall thicknesses, fixed obstructions, stage areas, and circulation aisles NOT serving the occupant zone. Examiners often deduct 1–2 marks if "net" is not explicitly stated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exit Width Requirement (EWR)

The exit width is derived from the **flow rate analogy**, where the crowd is treated as a continuous fluid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & Q = \text{ Peak flow rate of evacuees} \quad [\text{persons / second}] \\
\text{Let } & v = \text{ Walking speed of a pedestrian under panic} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\text{Let } & w = \text{ Effective width of an exit} \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Let } & \rho = \text{ Crowd density at the exit} \quad [\text{persons / m}^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

The fundamental **flow equation** is:

$$
Q = \rho \times v \times w
$$

NBC 2016 empirically encapsulates the right-hand side into a single coefficient $C$, expressed as **persons per unit exit width per minute**. The unit exit width (UEW) is fixed at **500 mm** (or 0.5 m).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{stair} & = 50 \quad \text{[persons per exit unit for stairs]} \\
C_{level} & = 60 \quad \text{[persons per exit unit for level exits]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a given total Occupant Load $OL$, the number of exit units required is:

$$
N_{units} = \dfrac{OL}{C_{stair}} \quad \text{(for stairs)}
$$

The total exit width required is therefore:

$$
\boxed{EWR = N_{units} \times UEW = \dfrac{OL}{C_{stair}} \times 500 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Example numerical evaluation:**

For $OL = 1200$ persons evacuating via a level exit (door):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{units} & = \dfrac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ exit units} \\
EWR & = 20 \times 500 \text{ mm} = 10{,}000 \text{ mm} = 10.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This could be provided as **20 doors of 0.5 m each**, or **10 doors of 1.0 m each**, or any combination satisfying the total 10.0 m.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Number of Seats per Row (Dead-End Aisle)

This is derived from the **maximum time-of-occupancy of a dead-end row** before the crowd reverses direction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & t_{max} = \text{ Maximum tolerable reverse-flow time} \quad [\text{seconds}] \\
\text{Let } & v_{reverse} = \text{ Reverse walking speed} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\text{Let } & W_{row} = \text{ Width of one seat} \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Let } & N_{row} = \text{ Maximum number of seats per row} \quad [-]
\end{aligned}
$$

The total distance to be traversed by the last person in the row to reverse out and reach the working aisle is $L = N_{row} \times W_{row}$.

Equating to the time constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{max} & = \dfrac{L}{v_{reverse}} = \dfrac{N_{row} \times W_{seat}}{v_{reverse}} \\
N_{row} & = \dfrac{t_{max} \times v_{reverse}}{W_{seat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting empirical values from NBC / Fruin's pedestrian data:
- $t_{max} = 30$ s (evacuation window)
- $v_{reverse} = 0.5$ m/s (slower in reverse, panic)
- $W_{seat} = 0.5$ m

$$
N_{row} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.5}{0.5} = 30
$$

NBC conservatively rounds this down to **20 seats per row** for unsprinklered assembly, and IS 4878 further limits it to **16 seats per row** in high-occupancy venues.

$$
\boxed{N_{row}^{max} = 20 \text{ (NBC) / } 16 \text{ (IS 4878)}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Checking a Seating Plan

A complete KTU-compliant seating plan must satisfy **all** the following checks simultaneously. Use this matrix as a checklist during design:

| Check # | Parameter | Symbol | Constraint | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Occupant Load | $OL$ | $\le A_{net}/OLF$ | NBC Cl. 4.4.1 |
| 2 | Min number of exits | $N$ | $\ge 2$ for $OL > 50$ | NBC Cl. 4.4.3 |
| 3 | Exit remoteness | — | $\ge \frac{1}{3} \times$ longest diagonal | NBC Cl. 4.4.3.2 |
| 4 | Total exit width | $EWR$ | $\ge (OL/C) \times 500$ mm | NBC Table 5 |
| 5 | Travel distance | $TD$ | $\le 30$ m (45 m sprinklered) | NBC Cl. 4.4.3.1 |
| 6 | Aisle width | $AW$ | $\ge 1.0$ m (main), $\ge 0.9$ m (cross) | NBC Cl. 4.4.6 |
| 7 | Seats per dead-end row | $N_{row}$ | $\le 20$ | NBC Cl. 4.4.6 |
| 8 | Seat width | $W_{seat}$ | $\ge 450$ mm | NBC |
| 9 | Row depth | $D_{row}$ | $\ge 850$ mm | NBC |
| 10 | Vomitory provision | — | 1 per 1500–2000 seats | IS 4878 |

> [!NOTE]
> **A common KTU valuation pitfall:** Students often compute exit width correctly but forget to verify the **exit remoteness rule** — exits must be placed at a distance of at least **one-third of the longest diagonal** of the occupant area apart. This rule prevents both exits being blocked by a single localized hazard (e.g., a fire near one exit).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Seating Arrangement Design Workflow

This Mermaid diagram captures the **complete engineering workflow** for designing seating arrangements in premises where large numbers of people gather, mapped block-by-block as a functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Initiation] --> A1[Step 1: Identify Occupancy Group and Subgroup per NBC 2016]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Determine Net Usable Floor Area A_net]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Select Occupant Load Factor OLF from NBC Table 3]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Compute Occupant Load OL = A_net divided by OLF]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Determine Minimum Number of Exits N based on OL]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Compute Total Exit Width Required EWR = OL divided by C times 500 mm]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Layout Seating Grid with Seat Width and Row Depth]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Design Aisle Network Transverse and Longitudinal]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Verify Dead End Row Seats Limit equals 20 NBC or 16 IS]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Verify Maximum Travel Distance TD equals 30 m unsprinklered]
    A10 --> A11[Step 11: Check Exit Remoteness equals one third Diagonal]
    A11 --> A12[Step 12: Add Vomitories and Special Provisions for Stadia]
    A12 --> A13[Step 13: Fire Officer Approval and KTU NBC 2016 Compliance Sign Off]
    A13 --> A14[Final Approved Seating Plan]

    style A0 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style A14 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style A6 fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style A10 fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
    style A11 fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Aisle and Vomitory Architecture in a Stadium

This diagram illustrates the **hierarchical flow** of pedestrians from their seats through transverse aisles, then longitudinal aisles, then vomitories, and finally to the concourse level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Seat occupied by a spectator] --> T1[Step A: Reverse out into transverse aisle AW = 1.0 m min]
    T1 --> T2[Step B: Walk along transverse aisle toward longitudinal aisle]
    T2 --> L1[Step C: Merge into longitudinal main aisle AW = 1.2 m min]
    L1 --> V1[Step D: Enter Vomitory tunnel through seating bank]
    V1 --> C1[Step E: Descend step aisle or stair to Concourse level]
    C1 --> E1[Step F: Reach Designated Exit Door 0.5 m to 1.0 m wide]
    E1 --> E2[Step G: Discharge to Safe Assembly Point at grade]

    style S0 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style V1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style E2 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematics of Row Geometry

The following two block-level schematics contrast the **permitted** versus **prohibited** seating configurations based on the dead-end aisle rule.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Permitted: 16 seats per dead end row with cross aisle midway] --> P2[Row 1 has 16 seats dead ended at left, served by working aisle at right]
    P1 --> P3[Row 2 has 16 seats dead ended at right, served by working aisle at left]

    X1[Prohibited: 30 seats per dead end row exceeding NBC limit] --> X2[Single very long row with no cross aisle]
    X1 --> X3[Exceeds maximum travel within row and reverse flow time]

    style P1 fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000

    style X2 fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
    style X3 fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Occupant Load" as applied to assembly buildings. State the Occupant Load Factor (OLF) for (i) a cinema hall with fixed seats, and (ii) a religious congregation hall.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** *Occupant Load (OL)* is the maximum number of persons permitted to occupy a building or portion thereof at any one time, computed as the net usable floor area divided by the prescribed **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)** as per NBC 2016 Part 4.

**OLF values (1.5 marks):**
- (i) Cinema hall with fixed seats: **OLF = 1.4 m²/person**
- (ii) Religious congregation hall (concentrated assembly): **OLF = 0.5 m²/person**

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** The 1.5 marks for OLF are split into 0.75 marks each. Writing only the OLF for one of the two cases costs 0.75 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the maximum permitted travel distance in an unsprinklered assembly building? What is the unit of exit width as per NBC 2016?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Maximum travel distance (1.5 marks):** **30 m** for an unsprinklered assembly building (NBC 2016 Clause 4.4.3.1). With automatic sprinklers, this is extended to **45 m**.
- **Unit of exit width (1.5 marks):** **500 mm** (also called "exit unit"). It is the standard module for sizing exits; one exit unit serves 50 persons for stairs and 60 persons for level exits.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Module question in Part B carries an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below; each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

A multi-purpose auditorium is proposed with the following design data:
- Net usable floor area = **600 m²**
- Occupancy = Concentrated assembly (no fixed seats)
- Staircase-based exits only
- Building is **NOT** sprinklered.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **Occupant Load (OL)**, the **minimum number of exits**, and the **total required exit width**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the longest diagonal of the occupant area is **42 m**, determine the **minimum separation required between the two most remote exits** for compliance with NBC 2016.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & A_{net} = 600 \text{ m}^2, \quad OLF = 0.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{person (concentrated, no fixed seats)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1: Occupant Load} \quad & OL = \dfrac{A_{net}}{OLF} = \dfrac{600}{0.5} = 1200 \text{ persons} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Minimum number of exits} \quad & \text{Since } OL > 50, \text{ minimum 2 exits are required.} \\[4pt]
& \text{For } OL = 1200, \text{ NBC mandates at least 3 exits when OL exceeds 1000.} \\[6pt]
& \text{Hence, minimum } N = 3 \text{ exits} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Total exit width} \quad & C_{stair} = 50 \text{ persons per exit unit (staircase)} \\[4pt]
& N_{units} = \dfrac{OL}{C_{stair}} = \dfrac{1200}{50} = 24 \text{ exit units} \\[6pt]
& EWR = 24 \times 500 \text{ mm} = 12{,}000 \text{ mm} = 12.0 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Distribution:} \quad & \text{Could be 3 staircases of 4.0 m each, or any combination.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (Part a):** $OL = 1200$ persons, $N_{min} = 3$ exits, $EWR = 12.0$ m.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & \text{Longest diagonal } D = 42 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{NBC 2016 Rule:} \quad & \text{Exit remoteness} \ge \dfrac{1}{3} \times D \\[6pt]
\text{Computation:} \quad & \text{Minimum separation} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 42 = 14 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
\text{Verification:} \quad & \text{The two most remote exits must be at least 14 m apart.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation for Part (b):** Formula statement (2 marks), substitution (2 marks), final numerical answer with units (2 marks), and conclusion sentence (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

A school assembly hall of dimensions **30 m × 20 m** has fixed seats arranged in rows. The hall is sprinklered. The following data is given:
- Seat width = **0.5 m** per person
- Row depth (back-to-back) = **0.9 m**
- Aisle width (transverse) = **1.0 m**
- Number of transverse aisles = **3** (creating 4 row blocks)
- Aisle at both ends of each row is a dead-end aisle.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the **maximum number of seats per row** as per NBC and IS 4878 standards. State the limiting rule.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the hall accommodates 12 rows per block, calculate the **maximum seats per block** and the **total seats in the hall**. Verify whether the occupant load falls within the prescribed OLF for an educational assembly of 1.0 m²/person.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.6):} \quad & \text{Max seats per dead-end row} \le 20 \\[4pt]
\text{Rule (IS 4878, more stringent):} \quad & \text{Max seats per dead-end row} \le 16 \\[6pt]
\text{Governing rule:} \quad & \text{IS 4878 governs for stricter compliance.} \\[4pt]
\text{Limiting Rule Statement:} \quad & \text{Both ends of the row are dead-end aisles;} \\
& \text{hence the rule for double-dead-end rows applies:} \\
& \text{IS 4878 limits this to } \mathbf{12 \text{ seats per row}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For double-dead-end rows (dead-end at both ends), IS 4878 further restricts seats to 12. NBC 2016 allows up to 20 only for single dead-end rows.

**Final Answer (Part a):** Maximum = **12 seats per row** (IS 4878, double-dead-end).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Seats per block:} \quad & = \text{Rows per block} \times \text{Seats per row} \\[4pt]
& = 12 \times 12 = 144 \text{ seats/block} \\[6pt]
\text{Total blocks:} \quad & = 4 \text{ (since 3 transverse aisles create 4 blocks)} \\[4pt]
\text{Total seats in hall:} \quad & = 144 \times 4 = 576 \text{ seats} \\[6pt]
\text{Net floor area (estimated):} \quad & A_{net} = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Actual area per person:} \quad & = \dfrac{600}{576} = 1.04 \text{ m}^2/\text{person} \\[6pt]
\text{Prescribed OLF (educational assembly):} \quad & = 1.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person} \\[4pt]
\text{Verification:} \quad & 1.04 \text{ m}^2/\text{person} > 1.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person} \quad \checkmark \\[4pt]
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \text{Occupant load is within the prescribed OLF; layout is compliant.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (Part b):** 144 seats/block, total 576 seats, area per person = 1.04 m²/person > 1.0 m²/person, **COMPLIANT**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN SEATING ARRANGEMENT QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Using GROSS area instead of NET area (loss: 1–2 marks):** Always deduct stage, walls, fixed obstructions.
> 2. **Forgetting the "minimum 2 exits" rule for OL > 50 (loss: 1 mark):** This is a hard rule, not a calculated one.
> 3. **Mixing up OLF values for different occupancies (loss: 1–2 marks):** Memorize the NBC Table 3 values — cinema (1.4), religious (0.5), standing (0.46), educational (1.0).
> 4. **Neglecting the exit remoteness rule of D/3 (loss: 2 marks):** Even with correct exit width, exits placed too close fail compliance.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify sprinklered vs unsprinklered TD (loss: 1 mark):** Always state the case in your answer; 30 m vs 45 m.
> 6. **Not stating units in final answers (loss: 0.5–1 mark):** KTU examiners deduct for "12" without "m" or "mm" units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ☐ **Occupant Load (OL)** = $A_{net} / OLF$ — always use **net** area, never gross.
- ☐ **OLF values** to remember: Standing = 0.46; Religious/Crowded = 0.5; Educational = 1.0; Cinema/Fixed seats = 1.4.
- ☐ **Unit Exit Width (UEW)** = **500 mm**.
- ☐ **Persons per exit unit**: 50 (stairs), 60 (level/door).
- ☐ **Minimum 2 exits** for any assembly with OL > 50; minimum 3 exits for OL > 1000.
- ☐ **Maximum Travel Distance**: **30 m** unsprinklered, **45 m** sprinklered.
- ☐ **Exit remoteness** ≥ **1/3 of longest diagonal** of the occupant area.
- ☐ **Dead-end row limit**: 20 (NBC), 16 (IS 4878), 12 (IS 4878 double-dead-end).
- ☐ **Minimum aisle width**: 1.0 m transverse, 1.2 m longitudinal.
- ☐ **Minimum seat width**: 450 mm (NBC) / 500 mm (IS 4878).
- ☐ **Minimum row depth**: 850 mm (NBC) / 900 mm (IS 4878).
- ☐ **Vomitory spacing** in stadia: 1 per **1500–2000** seats.
- ☐ **Occupancy Group D (Assembly)** includes any venue with **100+ persons** for civic, religious, entertainment, transit, or educational purposes.
- ☐ **Egress time** depends on the slowest-moving cohort — design for **all** categories (children, elderly, differently-abled).
- ☐ **Cross aisles** must be provided if rows exceed 20 seats to break dead-end pockets.
- ☐ **Seats must be FIXED to the floor** in all assembly venues with OL > 50 to prevent the "domino effect" during panic.
- ☐ **Emergency lighting** and **exit signage** with battery backup ≥ 90 minutes is mandatory for all assembly buildings (NBC 2016 Part 8).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Buildings Based on Occupancy and Type of Construction According to Fire Resistance as per NBC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Buildings Based on Occupancy and Type of Construction According to Fire Resistance as per NBC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (As per NBC of India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **National Building Code of India (NBC)**, published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, classifies buildings into distinct groups based on two primary criteria:
> 1. **Occupancy Classification** — The purpose for which a building (or part of it) is used or proposed to be used.
> 2. **Construction Classification** — The type of structural materials and the **fire resistance rating** of the building elements (walls, columns, beams, floors, and roofs).

As per **NBC 2016 (Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)**, every building must be classified in **both** categories simultaneously. The classification drives the design of egress routes, fire detection systems, suppression systems, compartmentation, and allowable floor-area ratios.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Think of a building like a **hospital patient**. Doctors (fire engineers) classify a patient in two ways:
> 1. **Why are they admitted?** (Occupancy) — Is it for a heart issue, a broken bone, or a routine check-up? Each "why" demands a different ward, equipment, and supervision.
> 2. **How strong is their body?** (Fire Resistance) — A frail patient (Type 3 timber) needs very different care than a robust athlete (Type 1 reinforced concrete with 4-hour fire resistance).
> Just as a hospital cannot treat all patients the same way, a fire engineer cannot apply identical safety rules to a **school, a warehouse, and a high-rise office tower**. The **NBC** is the **"triage protocol"** of the built environment.

### 1.3 Why This Classification Matters

- **Life Safety:** Determines the number, width, and location of exits, staircases, and refuge areas.
- **Property Protection:** Dictates the permissible area, height, and fire compartment size.
- **Fire-Fighter Access:** Governs approaches, ventilation openings, and fire-lift provisions.
- **Insurance & Legal Compliance:** Buildings not classified correctly are **non-compliant** and may be denied insurance or occupancy certificates.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** Expressed in **hours** — typically **1, 2, 3, or 4 hours**.
- **Floor Area Ratio (FAR / FSI):** Expressed as a dimensionless ratio.
- **Occupant Load:** Expressed in **persons per square meter (p/m²)**.
- **Travel Distance:** Expressed in **meters (m)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Occupancy (NBC Group A–N)

The NBC classifies buildings into **NINE major occupancy groups**, each subdivided by hazard and population characteristics. This forms the **first layer** of classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for NBC Occupancy Groups:** *" **A Big Group Has Huts, Jamming Kangaroos Leaping Morbidly** "*
> A, B, G, H, J, K, L, M (and **I** for Institutional, **N** for Hazardous, **C** for Assembly replaced by residential sub-classes)

The official list per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 3.1**:

| Group | Occupancy Type | Description | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | **Residential** | Dwellings, hostels, hotels, apartments | Apartments, dormitories, hostels |
| **B** | **Educational** | Schools, colleges, training institutes | Schools, universities, tuition centres |
| **C** | **Institutional** | Hospitals, sanatoria, custodial | Hospitals, mental asylums, prisons |
| **D** | **Assembly** | Gatherings of 50+ persons | Theatres, cinemas, auditoriums, malls |
| **E** | **Business** | Offices, banks, professional services | Office towers, IT parks, banks |
| **F** | **Mercantile** | Sale of goods/merchandise | Shops, stores, supermarkets, malls |
| **G** | **Industrial** | Manufacturing, assembly, processing | Factories, workshops, power plants |
| **H** | **Storage** | Warehousing of goods | Godowns, cold storages, fuel depots |
| **J** | **Hazardous** | Storage/use of explosive/flammable materials | LPG godowns, firework factories, refineries |
| **K** | **Mixed Use** | Two or more occupancies in single building | Mall + Hotel + Office tower |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard Sub-Classes:** Groups **G** (Industrial) and **J** (Hazardous) are further divided into:
> - **G-1:** Low hazard industrial (e.g., electronics assembly)
> - **G-2:** Moderate hazard (e.g., garment factory)
> - **G-3:** High hazard (e.g., paint manufacturing)
> - **J-1, J-2, J-3, J-4:** Based on explosion/flammability severity.

### 2.2 Classification Based on Type of Construction (Fire Resistance)

The **second layer** of NBC classification is based on the **fire resistance of structural elements**. NBC recognizes **four types**:

| Type | Structural Material | Minimum FRR | Fire Survival | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type 1** | Reinforced Concrete / Steel with fire cladding | **3 to 4 hours** | Highest | High-rises, hospitals, public buildings |
| **Type 2** | Reinforced Concrete (lighter) | **2 hours** | High | Offices, apartments up to moderate height |
| **Type 3** | Load-bearing masonry walls + timber/steel roof | **1 hour** | Moderate | Small residential, low-rise commercial |
| **Type 4** | Timber / Combustible construction | **Less than 1 hour** | Lowest | Temporary structures, rural buildings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule:** *Higher the FRR, larger the permissible floor area and height for any given occupancy group.* A Group D (Assembly) building in Type 1 construction can be much taller and larger than the same Group D in Type 3.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While classification itself is largely **tabular**, the following derived formulas govern the design parameters once a building is classified:

$$
\text{Exit Width} = \frac{\text{Occupant Load (P)} \times \text{Unit Exit Factor}}{\text{Flow Capacity per meter of exit}}
$$

$$
\text{Occupant Load (P)} = \frac{\text{Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}{ \text{Occupancy Load Factor (m}^2\text{/person)} }
$$

$$
\text{Travel Distance (d)} \leq \text{d}_{\max} \quad \text{(where } d_{\max} \text{ depends on Occupancy + Type)}
$$

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Dependency on Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Occupant Load Factor | $OLF$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{person}$ | Function of Occupancy Group |
| Travel Distance Limit | $d_{\max}$ | $\text{m}$ | Function of Group + Type |
| Max Floor Area per Compartment | $A_{\max}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Function of Type (FRR) |
| Minimum FRR of Elements | $FRR_{\min}$ | $\text{hours}$ | Function of Type (1 to 4) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Load Calculation:** FRR helps in computing the **fire load density** (MJ/m²) of contents vs. structural capacity.
- **Egress Design:** Occupant load directly determines **number of exits, staircase width, and refuge floor requirements** in high-rises.
- **Insurance Underwriting:** Insurance premium is directly proportional to hazard class and inversely to fire resistance.
- **Smart City / BIM Integration:** Modern **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** software uses NBC classification as the basis for automated fire-safety design checks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Occupancy × Construction Type Interaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NBC matrix links the **Occupancy Group (A–K)** with the **Construction Type (1–4)** to determine the **maximum permissible height, area, and compartment size**. The exhaustive logic is laid out below.

#### Step 1: Identify the Occupancy Group

The first step in any fire-safety design is to read the architectural brief and assign **one (or more) of the nine occupancy groups** to the building.

$$
G_{\text{building}} \in \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, J, K\}
$$

**Logic Transition:** Each group has an **inherent fire risk profile** determined by the *type of people* (sleeping, awake, immobile, mobile) and the *type of activity* (cooking, manufacturing, sleeping, healing).

#### Step 2: Identify the Type of Construction

Determine the **dominant structural material** and assign one of the four construction types.

$$
T_{\text{building}} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}
$$

$$
FRR(T) =
\begin{cases}
4 \text{ h} & \text{if } T = 1 \text{ (heavy RC)} \\
2 \text{ h} & \text{if } T = 2 \text{ (light RC)} \\
1 \text{ h} & \text{if } T = 3 \text{ (masonry + timber)} \\
< 1 \text{ h} & \text{if } T = 4 \text{ (timber/combustible)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic Transition:** Higher FRR implies the structure **holds up longer** under fire, giving occupants more **time to evacuate (ASET)** and fire services more **time to respond (RSET compatibility)**.

#### Step 3: Compute the Permissible Floor Area and Height

The maximum allowable floor area for a given compartment is:

$$
A_{\max} = f(G_{\text{building}}, T_{\text{building}})
$$

where $f$ is a tabulated function in NBC. A representative excerpt (illustrative, not exhaustive) is:

| Occupancy Group | Type 1 (4h) | Type 2 (2h) | Type 3 (1h) | Type 4 (<1h) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A (Residential)** | Unlimited | 2000 m² | 1000 m² | 500 m² |
| **D (Assembly)** | 4000 m² | 2000 m² | Not allowed | Not allowed |
| **G-3 (High Hazard Industrial)** | 2000 m² | 1000 m² | 500 m² | Not allowed |
| **J (Hazardous)** | 1000 m² | Not allowed | Not allowed | Not allowed |

**Logic Transition:** As the **hazard increases** (left to right and top to bottom in the matrix), the **permissible area shrinks dramatically** for the same construction type, because a fire in a hazardous occupancy is far more likely to escalate.

#### Step 4: Derive the Occupant Load

$$
P = \frac{A_{\text{floor}}}{OLF}
$$

where $OLF$ is the **Occupancy Load Factor** from NBC Table 3 (Part 4).

| Occupancy | $OLF$ (m²/person) | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Office (E) | **10** | Loose seating, desks, movement |
| Restaurant (D) | **1.5** | Dense seating, tables |
| Cinema (D) | **0.5** | Fixed seats, very dense |
| Hospital Ward (C) | **8** | Beds, equipment, circulation |
| Classroom (B) | **1.5** | Fixed desks |
| Warehouse (H) | **30** | Sparse human presence |
| Hazardous (J) | **10** | Workers + equipment |

**Logic Transition:** Lower OLF means **higher occupant density**, which in turn demands **wider exits** and **shorter travel distances**.

#### Step 5: Compute Total Exit Width Required

$$
W_{\text{exit}} = \frac{P}{C_{\text{per meter}}}
$$

where $C_{\text{per meter}}$ is the flow capacity of an exit (typically **50–60 persons per meter of effective width per minute** for stairs).

**Logic Transition:** This is the **direct quantitative link** between occupancy classification and physical fire-safety design.

#### Step 6: Verify Travel Distance Compliance

$$
d_{\text{actual}} \leq d_{\max}(G, T)
$$

| Occupancy | $d_{\max}$ for Type 1 | $d_{\max}$ for Type 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Residential (A) | 30 m | 22.5 m |
| Assembly (D) | 30 m | 30 m |
| Educational (B) | 22.5 m | 22.5 m |
| Hazardous (J) | 22.5 m | Not permitted |

**Logic Transition:** Travel distance is **inversely proportional to fire resistance** because a more combustible building must be evacuated faster.

### 3.2 Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A proposed **5-storey office building** (Group E) has a typical floor plate of **1200 m²**. Construction is **reinforced concrete with 2-hour fire resistance** (Type 2). Determine the **occupant load** and **minimum number of exits** required per floor.

**Step 1 — Occupant Load:**

$$
P = \frac{1200 \text{ m}^2}{10 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}} = 120 \text{ persons per floor}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Exit Width Required (using 60 persons/m/min):**

$$
W_{\text{exit}} = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ m per floor}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Exits:** At least **two independent exits** are mandatory (one of width ≥ 1.0 m each).

**Step 4 — Travel Distance Check:** $d_{\max}$ for Group E, Type 2 is **30 m** — must be verified from the farthest point on the floor to the nearest exit.

**Step 5 — Compartment Area Check:** Per the matrix, Group E in Type 2 allows compartments up to **2000 m²** — the 1200 m² floor is **within limits**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Building is **NBC-compliant** for the given parameters. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Buildings (NBC)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Building Classification as per NBC] --> startB{Occupancy-Based}
    startA --> startC{Construction-Based}

    startB --> grpA[A - Residential]
    startB --> grpB[B - Educational]
    startB --> grpC[C - Institutional]
    startB --> grpD[D - Assembly]
    startB --> grpE[E - Business]
    startB --> grpF[F - Mercantile]
    startB --> grpG[G - Industrial]
    startB --> grpH[H - Storage]
    startB --> grpJ[J - Hazardous]
    startB --> grpK[K - Mixed Use]

    startC --> typ1[Type 1 - RC Heavy - FRR 4h]
    startC --> typ2[Type 2 - RC Light - FRR 2h]
    startC --> typ3[Type 3 - Masonry - FRR 1h]
    startC --> typ4[Type 4 - Timber - FRR less than 1h]

    grpG --> g1[G1 - Low Hazard]
    grpG --> g2[G2 - Moderate Hazard]
    grpG --> g3[G3 - High Hazard]

    typ1 --> rule1[Largest Permissible Area and Height]
    typ4 --> rule4[Smallest Permissible Area and Height]

    grpJ --> j1[J1 - Mild Explosion Risk]
    grpJ --> j2[J2 - Moderate Explosion Risk]
    grpJ --> j3[J3 - Severe Explosion Risk]
    grpJ --> j4[J4 - Mass Detonation Risk]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Fire-Safety Design Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    arch[Architectural Brief] --> cls1[Step 1 - Identify Occupancy Group A to J]
    cls1 --> cls2[Step 2 - Identify Construction Type 1 to 4]
    cls2 --> calc1[Step 3 - Compute Occupant Load P]
    calc1 --> calc2[Step 4 - Compute Required Exit Width W]
    calc2 --> calc3[Step 5 - Verify Travel Distance d]
    calc3 --> calc4[Step 6 - Check Compartment Area Limit]
    calc4 --> frr[Step 7 - Assign Fire Resistance Rating FRR to Elements]
    frr --> sys1[Active Fire Protection - Detection and Suppression]
    frr --> sys2[Passive Fire Protection - Compartmentation and FRR]
    frr --> egress[Egress Design - Stairs and Exits]
    sys1 --> comp[Compile NBC Compliance Report]
    sys2 --> comp
    egress --> comp
    comp --> cert[Issue Occupancy Certificate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Fire Resistance vs. Construction Type Spectrum

```mermaid
graph LR
    h4[Type 1 - 4 hour FRR - Highest Safety] --> h2[Type 2 - 2 hour FRR]
    h2 --> h1[Type 3 - 1 hour FRR]
    h1 --> l1[Type 4 - Less than 1 hour FRR - Lowest Safety]

    h4 --> use1[Used in High-rises, Hospitals, Hazardous Industries]
    h2 --> use2[Used in Mid-rise Offices, Apartments]
    h1 --> use3[Used in Low-rise Residential, Small Commercial]
    l1 --> use4[Used in Temporary, Rural, and Single-Storey Structures]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. List any three major occupancy groups as classified by the National Building Code of India, with one example each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The NBC classifies buildings into nine major occupancy groups. Three examples are:

1. **Group A – Residential:** Apartments, hostels, hotels — used for dwelling and sleeping.
2. **Group D – Assembly:** Theatres, cinemas, malls — used for gathering of 50 or more persons.
3. **Group J – Hazardous:** LPG godowns, firework factories — used for storage/handling of explosive or flammable materials.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is meant by 'Fire Resistance Rating' of a building element? How is it expressed?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** is the duration (in hours) for which a building element (wall, column, beam, floor, or roof) **maintains its structural stability, integrity, and insulation** when exposed to a standard fire test (as per **IS 3809 / BS 476 / ASTM E119**). It is expressed in **hours — typically 1, 2, 3, or 4 hours**. A higher FRR means the element can withstand fire longer, allowing more time for evacuation and fire-fighting.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Full 14 Marks]**

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **NBC classification of buildings based on occupancy**. List all major groups with at least two examples for each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **classification of buildings based on type of construction** (fire resistance). State the minimum FRR and suitable examples for each type. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)** classifies buildings based on the purpose for which they are used. The classification is essential because each occupancy has unique fire risks, occupant characteristics, and evacuation needs. The major groups are:

- **Group A – Residential:** Buildings used for dwelling purposes. Examples: Apartments, hostels, dormitories, hotels, boarding houses.
- **Group B – Educational:** Buildings used for instruction. Examples: Schools, colleges, universities, training centres (up to 12th standard and above).
- **Group C – Institutional:** Buildings used for medical or custodial care. Examples: Hospitals, sanatoria, mental health facilities, prisons.
- **Group D – Assembly:** Buildings where 50 or more persons gather for recreation, worship, or consumption. Examples: Theatres, cinemas, auditoriums, malls, restaurants.
- **Group E – Business:** Buildings used for transaction of business. Examples: Offices, banks, IT parks, professional offices.
- **Group F – Mercantile:** Buildings used for display and sale of merchandise. Examples: Shops, stores, supermarkets, showrooms.
- **Group G – Industrial:** Buildings used for manufacturing, processing, or assembly. Sub-classes G1 (low hazard), G2 (moderate), G3 (high hazard). Examples: Garment factory, paint manufacturing, power plant.
- **Group H – Storage:** Buildings used primarily for storage of goods. Examples: Warehouses, cold storage, fuel depots.
- **Group J – Hazardous:** Buildings used for storage/handling of explosive or flammable materials. Examples: LPG godowns, firework factories, refineries.

The classification is used to determine **occupant load factor, exit width, travel distance, compartmentation, and active fire protection requirements**.

**Model Answer (b):**

The NBC classifies buildings based on the **fire resistance of structural elements** into four types:

- **Type 1 Construction (Heavy Reinforced Concrete / Protected Steel):** Minimum FRR of **3 to 4 hours** for all load-bearing elements. Used in high-rise buildings, hospitals, and high-hazard industrial facilities.
- **Type 2 Construction (Light Reinforced Concrete):** Minimum FRR of **2 hours**. Used in mid-rise office buildings, apartments, and institutional buildings.
- **Type 3 Construction (Load-bearing Masonry + Timber/Steel Roof):** Minimum FRR of **1 hour**. Used in low-rise residential buildings, small commercial shops.
- **Type 4 Construction (Timber / Combustible):** FRR **less than 1 hour**. Used in temporary structures, rural dwellings, and single-storey sheds.

The higher the construction type (1 being highest), the **larger the permissible floor area, height, and compartment size** for the same occupancy group. This classification ensures that buildings with greater hazard or higher occupant load are built with materials that can **resist fire long enough for safe evacuation**.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between **Group G (Industrial)** and **Group J (Hazardous)** buildings with examples. Why is Group J given special restrictions? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A proposed **3-storey school building** (Group B) has a typical floor area of **800 m²**. Construction is **Type 2 (2-hour FRR)**. Compute the **occupant load per floor**, the **minimum exit width required**, and verify the **compartment area compliance**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**

| Feature | Group G – Industrial | Group J – Hazardous |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Manufacturing, assembly, processing | Storage/handling of explosives, flammables |
| **Examples** | Garment factory, auto workshop, power plant | LPG godown, firework factory, refinery |
| **Sub-classes** | G1 (low), G2 (moderate), G3 (high) | J1, J2, J3, J4 (based on explosion severity) |
| **Fire Risk** | Moderate to high, but predictable | Extreme; potential for mass detonation |
| **Egress Design** | Standard, but larger exits in G3 | Special exits, blast walls, fire-fighting lobbies |
| **NBC Restrictions** | Compartmentation required | Often requires Type 1 construction, restricted floor area, large setbacks |

Group J buildings are given **special restrictions** because the materials stored or handled pose a **risk of explosion, mass conflagration, or toxic release**, which endangers not only the occupants but also the surrounding neighbourhood. Therefore, NBC mandates **higher construction type, smaller compartment area, blast-resistant design, and dedicated fire-fighting systems** for Group J.

**Model Answer (b):**

**Step 1 – Occupant Load Factor:** For Group B (Educational), $OLF = 1.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$.

**Step 2 – Occupant Load per Floor:**

$$
P = \frac{A}{OLF} = \frac{800}{1.5} = 533.33 \approx 534 \text{ persons per floor}
$$

**Step 3 – Exit Width (using 60 persons/m/min):**

$$
W = \frac{P}{60} = \frac{534}{60} = 8.9 \text{ m per floor}
$$

**Step 4 – Number of Exits:** At least **three independent exits** are required (since width > 5 m).

**Step 5 – Compartment Area Check:** For Group B in Type 2, $A_{\max} = 2000 \text{ m}^2$. The 800 m² floor is **well within limits**. ✅

**Step 6 – Conclusion:** Each floor needs **3 exits of cumulative width ≈ 8.9 m** and the compartmentation is compliant.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Occupancy vs. Construction Type:** Many students mix up the two classifications. *Occupancy* is about *use*; *Type* is about *material & FRR*.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** FRR is in **hours**, occupant load in **persons**, area in **m²**. Writing FRR as "4" without "hours" loses a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the OLF table:** In numerical problems, always **state the OLF value** before computing occupant load.
> 4. **Ignoring sub-classes (G1/G2/G3, J1–J4):** Examiners expect at least one mention in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Not verifying the matrix:** Always cross-check *occupancy × type* against the **maximum permissible area** before concluding.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - ✅ NBC classifies buildings on **two axes**: **Occupancy (A–J, K)** and **Construction Type (1–4)**.
> - ✅ **9 Occupancy Groups:** A-Residential, B-Educational, C-Institutional, D-Assembly, E-Business, F-Mercantile, G-Industrial, H-Storage, J-Hazardous, K-Mixed.
> - ✅ **4 Construction Types:** Type 1 (4h FRR) → Type 4 (<1h FRR).
> - ✅ **Type 1 = safest, largest area allowed; Type 4 = least safe, smallest area allowed.**
> - ✅ **Group G and Group J have sub-classes** (G1–G3, J1–J4).
> - ✅ **Occupant Load** = Floor Area ÷ Occupancy Load Factor (OLF).
> - ✅ **Exit Width** = Occupant Load ÷ Flow Capacity (≈60 persons/m/min for stairs).
> - ✅ **Higher hazard + Lower FRR = Smaller permissible compartment area.**
> - ✅ **Travel distance** must be checked against the matrix for the given (Occupancy, Type) pair.
> - ✅ **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** is tested per **IS 3809 / BS 476 / ASTM E119**.
> - ✅ NBC 2016 Part 4 is the **authoritative reference** for fire and life safety in India.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fire Zone ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fire Zone** is a legally and physically demarcated geographical area (at city/town level) **or** a compartmentalized section within a building, in which uniform fire-safety regulations, construction standards, and occupancy restrictions are enforced to control the risk of fire and prevent its propagation to adjacent areas. According to the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) – Part 4: Fire and Life Safety**, the competent authority (Local Municipality / Fire Department) shall divide the city or town into four distinct fire zones based on the predominant character of the buildings and the type of occupancy permitted within them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition – NBC 2016 (Part 4, Cl. 3.1.1)** > *"Fire Zone" means the area delimited by the authority having jurisdiction, within which the type of construction, occupancy, and fire-protection measures are governed by uniform regulations to limit the spread and severity of fire.* ### 1.2 Classification of Fire Zones (Per NBC 2016, Part 4) The NBC classifies a city's built environment into the following four Fire Zones: | Fire Zone | Designation | Permitted Occupancy | Hazard Level | |-----------|-------------|---------------------|--------------| | **Zone 1** | Low Hazard Zone | Residential, Educational, Institutional, Office (low rise) | **Low** | | **Zone 2** | Moderate Hazard Zone | Business, Mercantile, Assembly, Mixed Residential-Business | **Moderate** | | **Zone 3** | High Hazard Zone | Industrial buildings handling non-flammable materials | **High** | | **Zone 4** | Special Hazard Zone | Buildings storing / handling highly flammable / explosive materials (petroleum, chemicals, LPG, fireworks) | **Very High** | > [!NOTE] > The classification is **mandatory** under Section 3 of NBC Part 4 and is binding on all municipal authorities for town planning, layout approval, and building permission stages. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large apartment complex as a **chocolate bar divided into small squares**. If one square melts (catches fire), the foil between squares prevents the melt from reaching the others. Similarly: - A **city** is divided into **Fire Zones** (the chocolate bar). - Each Fire Zone is separated from the next by **natural or artificial barriers** (roads, open spaces, fire-resistant walls — the foil). - Within a **single building**, floors and fire-rated walls create smaller **fire compartments** (the individual chocolate squares). The goal of zoning is **containment**: a fire originating in Zone 3 (an industrial godown) must NOT be allowed to leap across the boundary into Zone 1 (a residential colony). > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** Think of fire zones like the compartments in a **ship's hull** — even if one fills with water (fire), the ship stays afloat because water (fire) cannot cross to other compartments. ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Minimum Open Space around a Fire Zone (perimeter):** **3 m** (low-rise) to **9 m** (high-rise). - **Fire Wall Minimum Rating:** **2 hours (120 min)** for separating two fire zones. - **Standard Fire (ASTM E119 / IS 3809):** Temperature–time curve where furnace reaches **1000 °C at 4 hours**. - **Maximum Fire Compartment Area (residential):** **2500 m²** per fire zone. - **Travel Distance to Exit:** **22.5 m** (without sprinklers), **45 m** (with sprinklers). - **Ambient Reference Temperature:** **27 °C** (used in some fire load calculations). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Fire Zoning Fire zoning is founded on **three engineering principles**: 1. **Compartmentalization:** Physically isolate the fire source. 2. **Limitation of Damage:** Confine property loss to one zone. 3. **Safe Egress:** Provide occupants of adjacent zones adequate time to evacuate. #### Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — Hazard Assessment:** The municipal authority evaluates the **occupancy type**, **fire load (MJ/m²)**, and **population density** of an area. - **Step 2 — Classification:** Based on the assessment, the area is assigned to Fire Zone 1, 2, 3, or 4. - **Step 3 — Regulation Enforcement:** Specific rules on construction materials, building height, setback distances, and storage of flammables are applied. - **Step 4 — Internal Compartmentation:** Within each building, fire-rated walls/floors subdivide the structure into smaller fire compartments. - **Step 5 — Maintenance:** Periodic inspections ensure that fire barriers, doors, and seals remain intact (Fire Stop systems). ### 2.2 Why Fire Zones Are Necessary — The Engineering 'Why' | Without Fire Zones | With Fire Zones | |--------------------|-----------------| | Fire propagates freely via radiation, convection, conduction | Fire confined to a defined compartment | | Loss of entire city block possible | Damage localized to one fire zone | | Inadequate egress time | Safe egress time extended due to compartmentation | | Mixed occupancies increase toxic exposure risk | Separation reduces cross-occupancy hazard | ### 2.3 Fire Zone Area & Height Restrictions (NBC 2016) | Fire Zone | Permitted Max Height | Max Floor Area per Fire Compartment | Construction Type Allowed | |-----------|---------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------| | Zone 1 (Low) | No restriction (subject to structural safety) | **2500 m²** | Type 1, 2, 3, 4 | | Zone 2 (Moderate) | Up to **15 m** without sprinklers | **1500 m²** | Type 1, 2, 3 | | Zone 3 (High) | **15 m** (single storey industrial) | **1000 m²** | Type 1, 2 only | | Zone 4 (Special) | **Restricted** (subject to license) | **500 m²** | Type 1 only (heavy fire-resistant) | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use **\vert** instead of **\mid** in tables for absolute-value bars to keep markdown table integrity intact. | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | |----------------------|-------------|-------| | $A_{fz} \le A_{max}$ | Fire zone floor area must not exceed maximum permitted area | m² | | $F_{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot H_i)$ | Fire Load = sum of (mass $\times$ calorific value) of all combustible contents | MJ | | $q = \dfrac{F_{L}}{A_{f}}$ | Fire Load Density — energy per unit floor area | MJ/m² | | $t_{egress} = \dfrac{L_{travel}}{v_{walking}}$ | Egress time = travel distance divided by walking speed | seconds | | $L_{max} = 22.5 + \alpha \cdot N_{sprinklers}$ | Maximum travel distance modified by sprinkler factor $\alpha$ | metres | | $O_{L} = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{A_{person}}$ | Occupant Load = floor area divided by per-person area factor | persons | | $W_{exit} = \dfrac{O_{L}}{F_{flow}}$ | Required Exit Width = occupant load divided by flow factor | metres | | $F_{flow} = 50 \;\text{persons/m/min}$ (stair) | Flow factor for staircases (NBC 2016) | persons/m/min | | $H_{min} = \dfrac{F_{L}}{R_{wall} \cdot k}$ | Minimum wall thickness for fire resistance | mm | | $R_{wall} \ge 2 \; \text{h}$ | Required fire-resistance rating for separating wall | hours | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Urban Planning:** Town planners use fire zones to allocate industrial corridors away from residential sectors. - **Insurance Underwriting:** Insurance premium is directly proportional to the **Fire Zone classification** and **Fire Load Density**. - **Building Design:** Structural engineers size fire-rated walls and slabs based on the zone-specific compartment area. - **Fire Service Strategy:** Fire tenders and stations are located based on Fire Zone boundaries for optimal response. - **Code Compliance:** Building permissions and occupancy certificates cannot be issued if fire zone restrictions are violated. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Load Density of a Fire Compartment **Given:** A small office room (Fire Zone 2 occupancy) contains: - Wooden furniture: mass $m_1 = 200$ kg, calorific value $H_1 = 17$ MJ/kg - Paper records: $m_2 = 50$ kg, $H_2 = 16$ MJ/kg - PVC cable insulation: $m_3 = 10$ kg, $H_3 = 25$ MJ/kg - Floor area $A_f = 30$ m² **Step 1 — Compute individual contributions.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_1 &= m_1 \cdot H_1 = 200 \times 17 = 3400 \;\text{MJ} \\[4pt] E_2 &= m_2 \cdot H_2 = 50 \times 16 = 800 \;\text{MJ} \\[4pt] E_3 &= m_3 \cdot H_3 = 10 \times 25 = 250 \;\text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sum total fire load.** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{L} &= E_1 + E_2 + E_3 \\[4pt] &= 3400 + 800 + 250 \\[4pt] &= 4450 \;\text{MJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Fire Load Density.** $$ \begin{aligned} q &= \dfrac{F_{L}}{A_{f}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{4450}{30} \\[4pt] &= 148.33 \;\text{MJ/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Classify the fire hazard.** $$ \begin{aligned} q \;=\; 148.33 \;\text{MJ/m}^2 \;\in\; [121,\; 244)\; \Rightarrow\; \text{Moderate Hazard (NFPA Classification)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A fire load density below **122 MJ/m²** is classified as **Low Hazard**, between **122–244 MJ/m²** as **Moderate**, and above **1220 MJ/m²** as **High Hazard** (per NFPA 13 / IS 14435). ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Fire Compartment Area using Travel Distance **Given:** An office building (Fire Zone 2) has a corridor length $L_{corridor} = 35$ m with **2 sprinkler heads** in the fire compartment. The sprinkler effectiveness factor $\alpha = 11$ m per sprinkler head. **Step 1 — Apply the travel distance formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= 22.5 + \alpha \cdot N_{sprinklers} \\[4pt] &= 22.5 + 11 \times 2 \\[4pt] &= 22.5 + 22 \\[4pt] &= 44.5 \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with corridor length.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{corridor} \;=\; 35 \;\text{m} \;\le\; L_{max} \;=\; 44.5 \;\text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{SAFE} \end{aligned} $$ The compartment satisfies the travel-distance criterion; hence the area may be permitted up to the NBC-mandated $1500 \;\text{m}^2$ cap for Zone 2. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Exit Width from Occupant Load **Given:** An assembly hall (Fire Zone 2) has $A_{floor} = 900 \;\text{m}^2$. The occupant load factor for assembly is $A_{person} = 1.0 \;\text{m}^2/\text{person}$. **Step 1 — Compute occupant load.** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{L} &= \dfrac{A_{floor}}{A_{person}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{900}{1.0} \\[4pt] &= 900 \;\text{persons} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute required exit width.** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{exit} &= \dfrac{O_{L}}{F_{flow}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{900}{50} \\[4pt] &= 18 \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Distribute across multiple exits.** If the design provides **3 exit staircases**, then per-stair width is: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{stair} &= \dfrac{W_{exit}}{N_{exits}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{18}{3} \\[4pt] &= 6 \;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ Each stair must therefore be at least **6 m wide** (or equivalent multiple staircases summing to 18 m total clear width). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fire Zone Selection Decision Tree | Condition | Decision | |-----------|----------| | Predominant occupancy is Residential | Classify as **Fire Zone 1** | | Predominant occupancy is Mercantile / Office | Classify as **Fire Zone 2** | | Predominant occupancy is Industrial (non-flammable) | Classify as **Fire Zone 3** | | Storage of petroleum / explosives / LPG bulk | Classify as **Fire Zone 4** | | Fire load density $q > 1220 \;\text{MJ/m}^2$ | Upgrade to higher hazard zone | | Building within 6 m of adjacent zone boundary | Provide **fire wall of 4 h rating** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Per NBC 2016 Part 4, Cl. 3.1.3:** When a building of Fire Zone 2, 3, or 4 abuts the boundary of Fire Zone 1, it must be separated by a **fire wall** of not less than **4 hours** fire-resistance rating on the side facing the lower hazard zone. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid) ### 4.1 Fire Zone Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: City Area]) --> assessB{Hazard Assessment} assessB -->|Low Hazard
Residential, Educational, Institutional| zone1A[Fire Zone 1
Low Hazard] assessB -->|Moderate Hazard
Business, Mercantile, Assembly| zone2A[Fire Zone 2
Moderate Hazard] assessB -->|High Hazard
Industrial Non-Flammable| zone3A[Fire Zone 3
High Hazard] assessB -->|Special Hazard
Petroleum, Chemicals, LPG| zone4A[Fire Zone 4
Special Hazard] zone1A --> rule1A[Max Compartment Area: 2500 m2] zone2A --> rule2A[Max Compartment Area: 1500 m2] zone3A --> rule3A[Max Compartment Area: 1000 m2] zone4A --> rule4A[Max Compartment Area: 500 m2] rule1A --> wall1A[Fire Wall Rating: 2 h] rule2A --> wall2A[Fire Wall Rating: 2 h] rule3A --> wall3A[Fire Wall Rating: 4 h] rule4A --> wall4A[Fire Wall Rating: 4 h Plus Vapour Barrier] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fire-Compartmentalized Building ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BLDG[Fire Compartmentalized Building] direction LR comp1[Fire Compartment 1
Zone 1 Office Area
Area: 600 m2] comp2[Fire Compartment 2
Zone 1 Office Area

Zone 1 Server Room
Area: 100 m2] end FW1[Fire Wall
