# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Internal Staircase" as a means of egress. List any four design requirements as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer:**

An **internal staircase** is a vertical means of egress fully enclosed within a building, with fire-rated walls and self-closing fire doors, designed to allow safe evacuation of occupants from upper floors to the final exit at grade level during emergencies.

**Four Design Requirements (NBC 2016):**
1. **Fire Resistance Rating:** Minimum **2 hours** for walls and **1 hour** for doors in high-rise buildings.
2. **Minimum Width:** **1.5 m** for assembly/office, **1.25 m** for residential.
3. **Riser & Tread Dimensions:** Riser **100–150 mm**, Tread **250–300 mm** (for public buildings).
4. **Head Room:** Clear headroom of at least **2200 mm** measured vertically from the nosing line.
5. **No Storage:** Staircase enclosure must remain free of any storage, services, or combustible material at all times.
6. **Continuous Enclosure:** Must be a continuous fire-rated shaft from top-most floor to final discharge.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the significance of "Travel Distance" in staircase design? State the values for residential and assembly buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of Travel Distance:**
Travel distance is the actual walking distance measured along the natural path of egress from the *most remote point* on a floor to the *nearest staircase door*. It is critical because:
- It determines whether occupants can reach safety before being overcome by smoke/heat.
- It governs the **number and location** of staircases on a floor.
- It is a **mandatory NBC 2016 limit** that must be verified during design.

**Values (NBC 2016):**

| Occupancy | With Sprinklers | Without Sprinklers |
|---|---|---|
| **Residential (A)** | 45 m | 30 m |
| **Assembly (D)** | 45 m | 30 m |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of internal staircases** with neat sketches, and discuss their suitability for fire-safe evacuation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **hospital building** has 5 floors with **40 patients per floor** (including bed-bound occupants). The architect proposes **2 internal staircases** of width 1.5 m each. Calculate the **staircase capacity** and **evacuation time per floor**. State the modifications needed as per NBC 2016 for institutional occupancy. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### (a) Types of Internal Staircases

**1. Straight Run Staircase**
- Single straight flight between two floors.
- Simple, easy to construct.
- Disadvantage: Long flights (up to 16 risers) cause fatigue; not suitable for fire (panic).
- Use: Low-rise residential buildings.

**2. Quarter Turn (L-Shaped) Staircase**
- Changes direction by **90°** at a mid-landing.
- Compact, provides visual barrier to fire spread.
- Common in residential and small offices.

**3. Half Turn (U-Shaped / Dog-Legged) Staircase**
- 180° turn at mid-landing.
- Two flights separated by a mid-landing.
- Most common in multi-storey buildings.
- Fire advantage: The mid-landing acts as a buffer zone.

**4. Three-Quarter Turn Staircase**
- 270° turn, used when floor plate is restricted.
- Complex geometry, not preferred in high-rises.

**5. Spiral / Helical Staircase**
- Wedge-shaped treads around a central pole.
- Occupant load is restricted; **NOT permitted** as a main fire escape per NBC.
- Use: Fire escape staircase (secondary) for low-rise buildings only.

**6. Curved / Geometrical Staircase**
- Aesthetic, used in assembly buildings (theatres, malls).
- Tread width varies; not ideal for fast evacuation.

**7. Scissor Staircase**
- Two independent stairs in the same shaft, separated by a fire-rated wall.
- Space-saving; common in hotels and apartments.

> **Suitability for Fire Evacuation:** The **dog-legged (U-shaped)** staircase is the **most preferred** for fire safety as it provides directional change, a mid-landing refuge, and 2-hour fire-rated enclosure.

#### (b) Solution to Numerical

**Given:**
- Floors: 5
- Occupants per floor: $P = 40$ patients
- Staircases: $N = 2$
- Width per staircase: $W = 1.5$ m
- Occupancy: Institutional (C-1)

**Step 1: Staircase Capacity (Discharge) per Minute**

$$Q = \frac{W}{U_F} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3 \text{ persons/min}$$

**Step 2: Total Capacity (Both Staircases)**

$$Q_{total} = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ persons/min}$$

**Step 3: Evacuation Time per Floor**

$$T_{floor} = \frac{P}{Q_{total}} = \frac{40}{6} \approx 6.67 \text{ min per floor}$$

**Step 4: Modifications Required (NBC 2016 for Institutional C-1)**

1. **Minimum Width:** Institutional buildings require staircase width of at least **2.0 m** (not 1.5 m).
2. **Travel Distance:** Limited to **22.5 m** (without sprinklers) and **30 m** (with sprinklers).
3. **Bed-Bound Occupants:** Need **defend-in-place** refuge floors; horizontal evacuation is preferred over vertical descent.
4. **Pressurization:** Mandatory for institutional buildings ≥ 15 m height.
5. **Number of Staircases:** With 40 occupants per floor, **2 staircases are sufficient**, but they must be **at opposite ends**.
6. **Fire Doors:** 2-hour fire-rated, self-closing, with vision panel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students often forget that **institutional occupancies** have **stricter** width and travel distance requirements than commercial buildings. The 1.5 m default for "office" does **not** apply to hospitals. Marks lost: 1–2 marks per error.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 1]**
**(a)** Discuss the **fire safety requirements** for internal staircases in **high-rise buildings** as per NBC 2016. Include provisions for **pressurization, fire doors, and material specifications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **office building** has **8 floors** with **200 occupants per floor**. The minimum travel distance allowed is **30 m**. Design the **number of staircases** and **width** required. Show all calculations using the **NBC 2016 unit width factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### (a) Fire Safety Requirements for Internal Staircases in High-Rise Buildings (NBC 2016)

**1. Fire Resistance Rating**
- Walls: Minimum **2-hour** fire resistance.
- Floor slabs: **2-hour** fire resistance.
- Doors: **1-hour** fire-rated, self-closing.

**2. Pressurization System**
- All staircases in high-rise buildings (height > 15 m) must be **mechanically pressurized**.
- Maintain positive pressure of **12.5 Pa** (or 50 Pa for high-hazard occupancies) relative to the building.
- Supply air volume calculated based on leakage area: $V = 0.05 \times A_{leak}$
- Power supply backed by **emergency power (diesel generator)** with 4-hour backup.
- Independent of main HVAC system.

**3. Fire Doors**
- Self-closing fire doors of **1-hour rating**.
- Smoke seals and intumescent strips.
- Vision panel (150 mm × 150 mm minimum) for visibility.
- **No hold-open devices** unless electromagnetic release tied to fire alarm.
- **Signage:** "STAIRWAY TO EXIT" — illuminated, photo-luminescent.

**4. Material Specifications**
- Walls: Reinforced concrete (RC) or fire-rated gypsum board.
- Treads: Non-combustible, slip-resistant (e.g., granite, terrazzo, or chequered tiles).
- Railings: MS or SS, **1.0 m height** (1.2 m for hospitals).
- No combustible finishes (wood, plastic laminates) on treads/risers.

**5. Ventilation**
- Cross-ventilation at top of stairwell: **0.5 m²** opening per floor, or mechanical supply.
- Pressurization overrides natural ventilation requirement.

**6. Refuge Areas**
- For buildings **> 24 m**, refuge floors at intervals of **every 7th floor** or as per local bylaws.
- Refuge area: 25% of the floor plate area.

**7. Access for Fire Service**
- Fireman's lift (passenger lift with fireman switch) adjacent to staircase.
- Fire service inlet at ground level for booster connection.

**8. Continuous Enclosure**
- Staircase shaft must be continuous from top floor to final exit without any opening except fire doors.

#### (b) Numerical Solution — Staircase Design

**Given:**
- Floors: 8
- Occupants per floor: $P = 200$
- Travel distance limit: 30 m
- Unit width factor (NBC 2016): $U = 0.5$ m/person
- Building use: Office

**Step 1: Total Occupant Load**

$$P_{total} = 8 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 2: Staircase Width per Person (Total)**

$$\text{Total Width} = P \times U = 1600 \times 0.5 = 800 \text{ m}$$

This is theoretical — but practically, we calculate per floor.

**Step 3: Per-Floor Width**

$$W_{total/floor} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Number of Staircases Required (Per NBC 2016)**

For 200 occupants per floor (between 50 and 300): **2 staircases mandatory**.

For high-rise (> 15 m): **Minimum 2 staircases** at opposite ends.

**Step 5: Width per Staircase**

$$W_{each} = \frac{W_{total/floor}}{N} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ m}$$

This is physically impossible — therefore, the **minimum mandated width** of **1.5 m** governs.

**Step 6: Final Design**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Number of Staircases** | **2** (at opposite ends) |
| **Width of each Staircase** | **1.5 m** (NBC minimum, governs) |
| **Riser** | 150 mm |
| **Tread** | 300 mm |
| **Headroom** | 2200 mm |
| **Travel Distance Verified** | ≤ 30 m |
| **Pressurization** | **Required** (12.5 Pa) |

**Step 7: Verification of Travel Distance**

The architect must locate staircases such that the most remote point is within **30 m** of the nearest staircase door. If not, **add a 3rd staircase** centrally.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** calculate a 50 m wide staircase — recognize that **NBC minimum width** always governs for practical buildings.
> 2. **Forgetting pressurization** for high-rise = -2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting travel distance verification** = -1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning opposite-end placement** of staircases for high-rise = -1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU 2024 EXAM:**

- [x] **Internal staircase = protected means of egress**, not just a circulation element.
- [x] **Blondel's Formula:** $2R + G = 600$ mm (always check in design problems).
- [x] **Public Buildings:** Riser = 100–150 mm, Tread = 250–300 mm.
- [x] **Minimum Width:** 1.5 m (public), 1.25 m (residential), 2.0 m (institutional).
- [x] **Head Room:** ≥ 2200 mm (clear vertical).
- [x] **Number of Staircases:** 1 (< 50 occ.), 2 (50–300 occ.), 2 + fire escape (> 300 occ.).
- [x] **Travel Distance:** 30 m (without sprinklers), 45 m (with sprinklers) for most occupancies; **22.5 m** for institutional.
- [x] **Unit Width Factor (NBC 2016):** 0.5 m/person (use $W = P \times U / N$).
- [x] **Capacity:** $Q = W / 0.5$ persons per minute.
- [x] **Fire Rating:** 2-hour walls, 1-hour doors (high-rise).
- [x] **Pressurization:** 12.5 Pa positive pressure (high-rise mandatory).
- [x] **Mid-landing:** 1.25 m minimum width; max 16 risers per flight.
- [x] **Door:** Self-closing, vision panel, smoke seals.
- [x] **No Storage:** Staircase must be free of all services and storage.
- [x] **Continuous Enclosure:** From topmost floor to final exit, no break.
- [x] **Opposite-end Placement:** Mandatory for high-rise (≥ 15 m) buildings.
- [x] **Refuge Floors:** Every 7th floor for buildings > 24 m height.
- [x] **Fireman's Lift:** Adjacent to staircase, with fireman switch.
- [x] **Codes:** NBC 2016 Part 4 & Part 8, IS 1644, IS 14435.
- [x] **Dog-legged (U-shaped) staircases are preferred** for fire evacuation.
- [x] **Spiral stairs are NOT permitted as primary fire escape** in most occupancies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** The 14-mark questions from this module almost always involve (1) a numerical on staircase width/capacity and (2) a descriptive question on types and fire safety provisions. Practice **both** thoroughly. Always state the **NBC 2016 clause** you are referencing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal Exits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Horizontal Exits # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Horizontal Exit** is a passive fire protection arrangement in which an occupant travels from one building, or from one fire compartment (zone) of a building, to an adjacent building, refuge area, or fire compartment **across a fire-rated wall or through a pair of fire doors**, without changing the floor level. The crossing element is essentially a **fire barrier with self-closing fire doors** that maintains compartmentation integrity while permitting safe egress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (per NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.3):** > *"A horizontal exit is an exit from one building or fire compartment to another, through a fire-resisting wall or fire door, across a corridor, balcony, bridge, or similar passageway, at approximately the same level."* ### Key Distinguishing Features | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Level change** | Zero — no stairs, ramps are allowed only if shallow | | **Protection** | Fire-rated wall (minimum **2 hours** for most occupancies; **4 hours** for high-hazard) | | **Door** | Listed self-closing fire door set, listed hardware | | **Travel** | Provides a "safe area" or "refuge area" on the other side | | **Direction** | Lateral (horizontal) rather than vertical (downward via stairs) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Bridge Between Two Lifeboats" Analogy** > > Imagine a large cruise ship caught in a storm. Instead of forcing every passenger to jump into the sea, the crew rolls a covered, fireproof **gangway** between the burning half of the ship and the safe half. People simply walk across a flat, protected bridge from danger to safety — they do not climb up or down, they do not jump, and the fire cannot chase them across because the bridge is sealed. > > That gangway is exactly what a **horizontal exit** is in a building. The two halves of the building are the two "lifeboats," and the fire-rated wall with cross-corridor fire doors is the **protected bridge** between them. Once a person crosses, they are already in a safe fire compartment and can wait for rescue, await re-entry to the original zone, or continue evacuating by stairs from the safer side. ## 1.3 Where Horizontal Exits Are Most Useful - **Hospitals and healthcare facilities** (where bedridden patients cannot descend stairs). - **High-rise office buildings** (refuge on an adjacent tower). - **Shopping malls** (split into fire compartments with public corridors acting as horizontal exits). - **Industrial buildings** with hazardous processes. - **Detention and correctional facilities** (controlled evacuation through rated walls). - **Schools and large assembly buildings** as an alternative egress path. ## 1.4 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these values verbatim for KTU ESE — they are the most tested numerical constants.** - Minimum width of horizontal exit opening: **1.5 m** (clear, between leaf faces). - Minimum area of refuge compartment: **0.5 m² per occupant**. - Capacity factor for a horizontal exit: typically **30 to 50 persons per metre of clear width** (NBC Table 16). - Fire resistance of separating wall: **2 hours** (4 hours for high-hazard/industrial). - Maximum travel distance to a horizontal exit: as per occupancy (e.g., **22.5 m** for institutional, unsprinklered). - Maximum number of horizontal exits permitted from any single fire compartment: **at least 2**, located as far apart as practicable. - Maximum distance between two horizontal exits (or between a horizontal exit and a stair): not less than **half the maximum overall diagonal dimension** of the compartment served. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Breaking Down "How a Horizontal Exit Works" A horizontal exit is **not a single door**. It is a *system* of three integrated components that work simultaneously: 1. **The Fire Compartment (Refuge Side)** — A pre-designed, fire-rated space (room, corridor, or even an entire adjacent building) on the receiving side. This area is sized to hold the **expected number of evacuees for the design time** (typically 30 minutes to 2 hours, depending on emergency response plan). 2. **The Fire Barrier Wall** — A non-combustible, fire-rated vertical assembly that physically separates the two compartments. It must remain structurally stable and intact for the required fire resistance duration under standard fire exposure (IS 3614 / ASTM E119 / BS 476). 3. **The Cross-Corridor Fire Door Assembly** — A pair (or more) of listed, **self-closing, latching fire doors** installed in the fire wall. These doors are normally closed (or held open by a fusible-link/release device that activates on smoke/heat detection). They are never locked from the egress side. ### Step-by-Step Logic of Use During a Fire - **Step 1 — Detection:** A smoke/heat detector senses fire in Compartment A. - **Step 2 — Door Release:** The electromagnetic hold-open devices release; the cross-corridor fire doors **swing shut and latch** automatically. - **Step 3 — Compartment Isolation:** The fire barrier wall + closed doors prevent fire, smoke, and heat from migrating into Compartment B. - **Step 4 — Occupant Transfer:** Occupants of A walk **horizontally**, at the same level, through a designated doorway into the unburned Compartment B. - **Step 5 — Refuge / Continued Egress:** In B, occupants either: - (a) wait for rescue / fire brigade arrival, OR - (b) re-enter the building at a safer location, OR - (c) continue downward via a stair that originates in B (so the stair shaft is never compromised by the fire in A). > [!NOTE] > **Why this is "high-yield" for KTU:** Examiners love asking *"Why is a horizontal exit preferred for hospitals?"* The answer chain is: *No level change → no stairs → bedridden patients can be wheeled across → refuge provided on the other side → rescue time bought → patient survival maximized.* ## 2.2 Design Requirements (NBC 2016 / Part 4 / Clause 4.4.3 + 4.4.4) A horizontal exit arrangement, to be code-compliant, **must** satisfy **all** of the following simultaneously: | # | Requirement | Threshold / Limit | |---|---|---| | 1 | Fire resistance of separating wall | ≥ **2 h** (4 h for high-hazard) | | 2 | Fire resistance of refuge floor | ≥ **2 h** (or separate structure) | | 3 | Number of horizontal exits per compartment | ≥ **2**, well-separated | | 4 | Clear width of each horizontal exit opening | ≥ **1.5 m** | | 5 | Capacity of horizontal exit | per Table 16 of NBC, typically **30–50 persons/m** | | 6 | Refuge area | ≥ **0.5 m² per person** accommodated | | 7 | Travel distance from any point to nearest horizontal exit | per occupancy; e.g., 22.5 m (institutional, unsprinklered) | | 8 | Separation between two horizontal exits | ≥ **½ × diagonal of compartment** | | 9 | Doors | Self-closing, latching, fire-rated; panic hardware if occupant load > 50 | | 10 | Illumination, signage, and ventilation | As per NBC Part 8 (Electrical) and Part 4 Sec 4 | | 11 | Discharge | The receiving compartment must itself have access to a vertical exit (stair) | | 12 | Marking | Illuminated "EXIT" sign on **both** sides of the wall | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` (not the pipe character) inside any markdown table cell that requires absolute value, to prevent table syntax breakage.** | # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Required clear width of horizontal exit | $W = \dfrac{P}{C_f}$ | metres (m) | $P$ = number of persons; $C_f$ = capacity factor (persons/m) | | 2 | Required refuge area on receiving side | $A_r = 0.5 \cdot P$ | square metres (m²) | Per NBC 2016 minimum | | 3 | Capacity of horizontal exit (occupancy-based) | $P_{cap} = C_f \cdot W$ | persons | $C_f$ from NBC Table 16 | | 4 | Travel distance check (dead-end limit) | $L_{dead} \le L_{allow,occ}$ | metres (m) | $L_{allow,occ}$ depends on occupancy type | | 5 | Number of exits required | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{P_o}{P_{cap}} \right\rceil$ | integer | $P_o$ = design occupant load | | 6 | Separation between two horizontal exits | $D_{sep} \ge 0.5 \cdot d_{diag}$ | metres (m) | $d_{diag}$ = diagonal of compartment | | 7 | Fire resistance of barrier (general) | $FR \ge 2$ h (or 4 h for high-hazard) | hours | NBC Part 4 | ### Typical Capacity Factors $C_f$ (Persons per Metre of Clear Width) | Occupancy | $C_f$ (persons/m) | |---|---| | Residential | 30 | | Office | 50 | | Assembly (concentrated use, no fixed seating) | 30 | | Mercantile (street floor / upper floors) | 40 / 30 | | Institutional (hospital, detention) | 30 | | Educational | 50 | | Industrial (low / ordinary / high hazard) | 50 / 40 / 30 | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** A hospital architect designing a 200-bed ward with horizontal exit strategy will compute (i) the total occupant load including staff, patients, and visitors; (ii) the door width using $W = P/C_f$; (iii) the refuge area in the adjacent wing as $0.5 \times P$; and (iv) verify the fire wall rating. The same equations apply to a high-rise office design where evacuation of a 1000-person floor uses two horizontal exits into adjacent tower. ## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations — Examiner's View ### Advantages - **Removes dependency on stairs** for primary egress — critical for non-ambulatory occupants. - **Provides immediate refuge** — buying time for rescue. - **Reduces congestion on staircases** because the evacuation front is "split" horizontally first, then vertically. - **Compartmentation synergy** — improves overall fire safety index of the building. - **Allows phased evacuation** in high-rise towers (floor-by-floor, or block-by-block). ### Limitations - **Expensive** — requires a dedicated fire-rated wall and a refuge area that "wastes" usable space. - **Reduces leasable carpet area** — large refuge compartments reduce revenue per floor. - **Discipline required** — the doors must never be wedged open or blocked; maintenance is critical. - **Not a stand-alone solution** — must discharge ultimately to a stair that is itself in a safe compartment. > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistake:** Treating a horizontal exit as a "replacement for stairs." It is **not**. It is a **complementary** means of egress. The receiving compartment must still have a code-compliant stair path to the final discharge. Examiners routinely penalize 2–3 marks for this omission. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Width of a Horizontal Exit **Problem Setup:** A hospital ward on a single floor has a design occupant load $P$ of 180 persons (120 patients + 60 staff/visitors, with 30 patients non-ambulatory). The hospital uses a horizontal exit strategy into an adjacent wing, with the receiving side capacity factor $C_f = 30$ persons/m (institutional per NBC Table 16). Determine the **required clear width** of the horizontal exit doorway. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.** $$W = \dfrac{P}{C_f}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute known values.** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \dfrac{180 \text{ persons}}{30 \text{ persons/m}} \\[4pt] &= 6.0 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with the minimum code threshold.** The NBC requires a minimum of **1.5 m** per opening, and at least **2 openings** (i.e., 2 separate horizontal exits) so that no single failure blocks the entire egress. Required per opening $= 6.0 / 2 = 3.0$ m, which is well above the 1.5 m minimum, so the design is **code-compliant** as a single 6.0 m opening OR two 3.0 m openings. **Step 4 — Convert to standard door module.** A 3.0 m clear opening can be provided by **two 1.5 m clear doors side-by-side** (each is a standard listed double-leaf fire door set, 1500 mm clear width). **Step 5 — Final answer (with units and commentary).** $$ \boxed{W_{required} = 6.0 \text{ m total clear width, achievable as 2 doors} \times 3.0 \text{ m each}} $$ > **Conversion logic note:** The capacity factor $C_f$ is read from NBC Table 16 for the appropriate occupancy. Substituting yields a width; comparing with the minimum gives the final door count. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Refuge Area **Problem Setup:** Continuing from the hospital ward example, compute the required refuge area on the receiving (safe) side. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Governing equation.** $$A_r = 0.5 \cdot P$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $P = 180$ persons.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_r &= 0.5 \, \text{m}^2/\text{person} \times 180 \, \text{persons} \\[4pt] &= 90 \, \text{m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the result.** The adjacent wing must dedicate at least **90 m²** as a "refuge waiting area," separate from its normal circulation, sized to hold all 180 evacuees for the design refuge time. $$ \boxed{A_r = 90 \, \text{m}^2} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Number of Horizontal Exits **Problem Setup:** An office floor of an IT park has a design occupant load of 1200 persons. The capacity of a single horizontal exit (door, 1.5 m wide) is calculated as follows. Determine the **minimum number of horizontal exits** required. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute capacity of one exit.** For an office, $C_f = 50$ persons/m, and the chosen clear width is the code minimum 1.5 m. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{cap} &= C_f \cdot W \\[4pt] &= 50 \, \text{persons/m} \times 1.5 \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= 75 \, \text{persons} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the ceiling function to get the number of exits.** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \left\lceil \dfrac{P_o}{P_{cap}} \right\rceil \\[4pt] &= \left\lceil \dfrac{1200}{75} \right\rceil \\[4pt] &= \left\lceil 16 \right\rceil \\[4pt] &= 16 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply code-mandated minimum and separation rule.** NBC requires at least 2 exits per compartment and the two exits must be separated by at least $\frac{1}{2}$ of the diagonal dimension of the floor plate. Since 16 is already $\ge 2$, the design uses 16 horizontal exits distributed around the floor plate (often implemented as 8 on each of two long sides, paired with a central fire wall). $$ \boxed{N_{horizontal\ exits} = 16} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Separation Between Two Horizontal Exits **Problem Setup:** A rectangular fire compartment measures 60 m × 30 m. Find the **minimum centre-to-centre (or clear-line) separation** between any two horizontal exit openings. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the diagonal of the compartment.** $$ \begin{aligned} d_{diag} &= \sqrt{L^2 + B^2} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{3600 + 900} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{4500} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &\approx 67.08 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the half-diagonal rule.** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{sep} &\ge 0.5 \cdot d_{diag} \\[4pt] &\ge 0.5 \times 67.08 \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &\ge 33.54 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Practical round-up.** $$ \boxed{D_{sep} \ge 33.5 \, \text{m} \ (\text{typically rounded to } 34 \text{ m for design})} $$ > **Conversion logic note:** The half-diagonal rule ensures that a single localized fire cannot block both horizontal exits simultaneously. If the two exits were clustered together, a single fire plume would defeat the redundancy. --- ## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Design Parameter | Equation | Input Needed | Output (units) | KTU Question Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | Door width | $W = P/C_f$ | Occupant load $P$, capacity $C_f$ | m | "Compute clear width of a horizontal exit" | | Refuge area | $A_r = 0.5 P$ | $P$ | m² | "Compute the minimum refuge area" | | Number of exits | $N = \lceil P_o / P_{cap} \rceil$ | $P_o$, $C_f$, $W$ | integer | "How many horizontal exits are required?" | | Exit separation | $D_{sep} \ge 0.5 d_{diag}$ | $L$, $B$ of compartment | m | "Verify exit placement geometry" | | Travel distance | $L_{travel} \le L_{allow,occ}$ | $L_{allow}$ from NBC Table | m | "Check if design meets travel-distance clause" | | Fire resistance | $FR \ge 2$ h (4 h high-hazard) | Hazard class | hours | "Justify wall rating for industrial occupancy" | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip — Show all substitution steps with units.** Examiners award 1 mark per well-stated substitution; writing only the final number forfeits up to 2–3 marks per sub-question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Rendering Note:** Mermaid diagrams below are authored in strict compliance with the Alpha Node Rule (every node ID begins with a letter, no reserved keywords used as IDs) and the Clean Label Rule (no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels). ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Components of a Horizontal Exit System ```mermaid graph LR A[Fire Compartment A
Source Side
Where Fire Originates] -->|Egress Path| B[Cross Corridor
Fire Door Assembly

Self Closing Latching] B -->|Same Level Transfer| C[Fire Rated Wall

Sized at 0.5 sqm per Person] D --> E[Safe Discharge

from Floor Area and Occupancy Factor] --> stepB[Step 2: Select Capacity Factor Cf

Self Closing, Latching, Panic Hardware] stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Integrate Detection and Hold Open Release] stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Verify Travel Distance
less than or equal to Allowable per Occupancy] stepJ --> stepK[Step 11: Signage Illumination and Ventilation
per NBC Part 4 and Part 8] stepK --> stepL[Step 12: Final Compliance Sign Off
Horizontal Exit System Approved] style stepA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px style stepL fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Plan Layout Schematic — Floor Plate Showing Horizontal Exit Placement ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CompartmentA[Compartment A - Fire Origin Side] wallTop[North Wall Solid] wallBot[South Wall Solid] wallEast[East Wall with Door 1
Horizontal Exit 1.5m to 3m] wallWest[West Wall with Door 2

Beds or Workstations] occA2[Occupant Zone 2

Sized 0.5 sqm per Person

Maintained During Emergency] corridorB[Internal Corridor B] stairB[Staircase in B
Safe Vertical Egress] end wallEast ==Same Level Crossing==> refugeArea wallWest ==Same Level Crossing==> occB1 refugeArea -.Connects to.-> corridorB corridorB --> stairB occB1 -.Connects to.-> corridorB style CompartmentA fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px style CompartmentB fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px style wallEast fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:3px style wallWest fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Vertical vs Horizontal Exit (Visual) ```mermaid graph LR VE[Vertical Exit
Staircase Travel
Downward Movement] --> VEProp1[Requires Stair Shaft] VE --> VEProp2[Cannot be used by Bedridden] VE --> VEProp3[Vertical Travel Distance limited] VE --> VEProp4[Single Direction of Egress] HE[Horizontal Exit
Same Level Transfer
Lateral Movement] --> HEProp1[No Level Change] HE --> HEProp2[Usable by Bedridden and Disabled] HE --> HEProp3[Provides Immediate Refuge] HE --> HEProp4[Two Way Egress Possible] HEProp1 --> Summary[Horizontal Exit is the
Preferred Egress Mode for
Hospitals High Rises and Malls] HEProp2 --> Summary HEProp3 --> Summary style VE fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px style HE fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px style Summary fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a horizontal exit. List any four situations where it is preferred over a vertical exit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **horizontal exit** is an exit from one fire compartment to another — or from one building to an adjacent building — at the **same level**, through a **fire-rated wall** and a **self-closing fire door** (NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.3). It is preferred over a vertical (stair) exit in the following situations: 1. **Hospitals and healthcare facilities** — patients are bedridden and cannot descend stairs. 2. **High-rise office buildings** — phased, floor-by-floor horizontal evacuation reduces stair congestion. 3. **Detention/correctional buildings** — controlled movement through rated walls. 4. **Shopping malls / large assembly occupancies** — split fire compartments with public corridors act as horizontal exits. *(Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for any four situations — 0.5 each.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the NBC-mandated minimum fire resistance rating of a wall separating two fire compartments connected by a horizontal exit. What is the minimum clear width of the horizontal exit doorway?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Fire resistance rating of separating wall:** **2 hours** (for most occupancies) and **4 hours** for **high-hazard industrial** occupancies. - **Minimum clear width of horizontal exit doorway:** **1.5 metres** (clear distance between leaf faces of the open door). *(Award 1.5 marks for fire rating with correct value; 1.5 marks for width with correct value and qualifier "clear".)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Design and Justification **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Modified Model]** *(a)* Define a horizontal exit and explain, with a sketch in plan, how it functions as a passive fire protection system. Discuss the four situations in which it is the **preferred** means of egress. **(7 marks)** *(b)* A hospital ward has a **design occupant load of 240 persons** (180 patients + 60 staff/visitors). The hospital uses a horizontal exit strategy into an adjacent wing. Using NBC 2016 provisions: - (i) Calculate the **required clear width** of the horizontal exit doorway, taking the institutional capacity factor as **30 persons/m** and the minimum clear width per opening as **1.5 m**. State the number of openings required. - (ii) Calculate the **minimum refuge area** required on the receiving side. - (iii) State the **fire resistance rating** of the separating wall and list **two door hardware requirements**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] A **horizontal exit** is a means of egress from one fire compartment to another **at the same floor level**, separated by a **fire-resisting wall (≥ 2 h)** and crossed through a **self-closing fire door** assembly. The receiving side is a designated **refuge area**. **How it functions (4 marks distributed):** - The two compartments are isolated by a **fire-rated wall**, which under fire exposure remains stable and prevents fire/smoke spread for ≥ 2 h. - The **cross-corridor fire doors** are normally held open by electromagnetic hold-open devices tied to the fire detection system. On detection of smoke/heat, the holds release and the doors **swing shut and latch automatically**, sealing the opening. - Occupants of the fire-affected compartment **walk across horizontally** (no stairs) into the safe compartment. Bedridden patients can be wheeled on trolleys. - On the safe side, occupants either **await rescue** (refuge) or **continue egress** through a **staircase located in the safe compartment** — ensuring that the stair shaft itself is not compromised. **Four situations of preference (3 marks):** 1. Hospitals / healthcare — non-ambulatory patients. 2. High-rise office towers — phased floor-by-floor evacuation. 3. Shopping malls and large mercantile — multiple compartments with public-corridor horizontal exits. 4. Industrial buildings with high-hazard processes — refuge across fire walls. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **(i) Required clear width and number of openings (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \dfrac{P}{C_f} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{240 \, \text{persons}}{30 \, \text{persons/m}} \\[4pt] &= 8.0 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ Minimum code clear width per opening = **1.5 m**, so number of openings $$ N = \left\lceil \dfrac{8.0}{1.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.33 \rceil = 6 \text{ openings} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{total} = 8.0 \, \text{m}, \text{achieved as 6 openings of 1.5 m each, OR 2 openings of 4.0 m each}} $$ **(ii) Refuge area (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} A_r &= 0.5 \times 240 \\[4pt] &= 120 \, \text{m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{A_r = 120 \, \text{m}^2} $$ **(iii) Fire resistance and door hardware (2 marks):** - **Fire resistance rating of separating wall: 2 hours** (institutional, non-high-hazard). - **Door hardware:** (1) **Self-closing** mechanism (door closer); (2) **Listed latching** hardware so the door positively latches on closure; (3) (bonus) panic hardware since occupant load > 50; (4) (bonus) hold-open release tied to smoke/heat detection. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Code Compliance and Layout) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Modified Model]** *(a)* Explain the **design requirements** of a horizontal exit as per NBC 2016. State the **minimum fire resistance** of the wall, the **minimum clear width** of the doorway, the **minimum refuge area per person**, and the **separation rule** between two horizontal exits. **(7 marks)** *(b)* An office floor in a high-rise IT building is rectangular, **80 m × 40 m**, with a design occupant load of **2000 persons**. The office uses horizontal exits to a fire-rated adjacent corridor. Using NBC capacity factor of **50 persons/m** for office occupancy: - (i) Calculate the **total required clear width** of horizontal exits. - (ii) Determine the **number of exits** if each opening is **2.0 m wide**. - (iii) Calculate the **minimum separation distance** between any two horizontal exits. - (iv) Comment on the suitability of providing **only one horizontal exit** in this compartment, citing the relevant code clause. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] As per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.3 and 4.4.4**, the design requirements of a horizontal exit are: 1. **Minimum fire resistance of the separating wall — 2 hours** (4 hours for high-hazard industrial). The wall must be non-combustible and tested per IS 3614. 2. **Minimum clear width of doorway — 1.5 m**, measured between the open leaf faces of the door. 3. **Minimum refuge area — 0.5 m² per occupant** on the receiving (safe) side, sized to hold the **entire** design occupant load for the design refuge duration. 4. **Separation rule between two horizontal exits —** the centres of the two exits must be separated by **at least half the diagonal dimension** of the fire compartment they serve. This prevents a single localized fire from blocking both exits. 5. **Number of exits —** at least **2** horizontal exits per fire compartment. 6. **Doors —** self-closing, latching, fire-rated; **hold-open release** tied to detection; panic hardware if occupant load > 50. 7. **Signage and illumination —** illuminated "EXIT" sign on both sides; emergency lighting per NBC Part 8. *(Distribute 7 marks across the seven items; the first four are compulsory and carry more weight.)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **(i) Total required clear width (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \dfrac{P_o}{C_f} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{2000 \, \text{persons}}{50 \, \text{persons/m}} \\[4pt] &= 40 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{total} = 40 \, \text{m}} $$ **(ii) Number of exits (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{cap} &= C_f \cdot W_{single} \\[4pt] &= 50 \, \text{persons/m} \times 2.0 \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= 100 \, \text{persons per exit} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \left\lceil \dfrac{2000}{100} \right\rceil \\[4pt] &= 20 \text{ exits} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{N = 20 \text{ horizontal exits}} $$ **(iii) Minimum separation between two horizontal exits (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} d_{diag} &= \sqrt{80^2 + 40^2} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{6400 + 1600} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{8000} \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &\approx 89.44 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{sep} &\ge 0.5 \times 89.44 \, \text{m} \\[4pt] &\ge 44.72 \, \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{D_{sep} \ge 44.7 \, \text{m}} $$ **(iv) Single horizontal exit — suitability comment (1 mark):** **NOT permissible.** NBC 2016 (Clause 4.4.3) requires a **minimum of two horizontal exits** per fire compartment, well-separated. A single exit would be a **single point of failure** — one fire, one blocked door, and the entire 2000-person compartment is compromised. The code mandates redundancy specifically because the receiving compartment must itself be reachable from multiple points in case the primary opening is blocked by fire, smoke, or occupant congestion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read before writing your exam):** > > 1. **Forgetting the units in substitution.** Writing $W = 240/30 = 8$ without "m" forfeits **0.5–1 mark** per step. Always write $W = 240 \text{ persons} / 30 \text{ persons/m} = 8.0 \text{ m}$. > 2. **Confusing horizontal exit with fire door only.** A horizontal exit is a *system* (wall + door + refuge), not just a door. Examiners deduct marks if the wall rating and refuge area are not stated. > 3. **Treating the refuge area as the receiving compartment's "normal" area.** The 0.5 m²/person refuge area is **dedicated, additional** space for evacuees — not pre-existing usable area. > 4. **Omitting the diagonal-separation rule.** When two horizontal exits are required, simply stating "≥ 2" is incomplete — the **half-diagonal separation** is the most-tested sub-clause. > 5. **Forgetting that the receiving compartment must have its own stair access.** A horizontal exit is not a substitute for a stair. The refuge side must have a code-compliant vertical exit. > 6. **Mixing up capacity factors between occupancies.** Hospital = 30, Office = 50 — examiners specifically test this distinction. > 7. **Skipping the ceiling function in $N$ calculation.** If $N$ comes out to 5.33, writing 5.33 (instead of 6) loses 1 mark. Always round **up** to the next integer using $\lceil \cdot \rceil$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This List** - **Definition:** Horizontal exit = same-level egress through a fire-rated wall + fire doors into a refuge compartment (NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 4.4.3). - **Wall fire resistance:** ≥ **2 h** (general); **4 h** (high-hazard industrial). - **Minimum door clear width:** **1.5 m**. - **Refuge area:** ≥ **0.5 m² per person** accommodated. - **Capacity factor $C_f$:** Hospital/Institutional = **30**; Office/Educational = **50**; Assembly = **30**; Mercantile = **30–40**; Industrial = **30–50** (varies with hazard). - **Width formula:** $W = P / C_f$ (metres). - **Refuge area formula:** $A_r = 0.5 \times P$ (m²). - **Number of exits:** $N = \lceil P_o / P_{cap} \rceil$ (always round **up**). - **Minimum exits per compartment:** **2** (redundancy). - **Separation between two horizontal exits:** ≥ **½ × diagonal** of the fire compartment. - **Door hardware:** self-closing, latching, fire-rated, panic hardware if occupant load > 50, hold-open release tied to detection. - **Travel distance:** limited by occupancy; e.g., **22.5 m** for institutional (unsprinklered). - **Where used:** hospitals, high-rises, malls, industrial plants, schools, detention facilities. - **Key advantage:** allows non-ambulatory occupants to evacuate without using stairs. - **Limitation:** reduces usable carpet area; requires a stair in the refuge compartment. - **Receiving side must:** itself have access to a vertical exit (stair) for final discharge. - **Signage:** illuminated "EXIT" sign on **both** sides of the wall. - **Codes governing:** NBC 2016, IS 14435, IS 3614 (fire resistance testing), NFPA 101 (international reference). - **KTU-typical marks weightage:** 3-mark definition/short questions; 7–14 mark design/numerical questions per Part B.

topics.title: Fire Tower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Tower

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fire Tower** (also referred to as a **Fire Staircase**, **Fire Escape Tower**, or **Protected Stairway**) is a vertically oriented, fire-rated, fully enclosed circulation shaft within a building that is exclusively dedicated to the **emergency egress of occupants** during a fire or other life-threatening emergency. It is a critical component of the **Means of Egress** system, designed and constructed in accordance with the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)** and **IS 14435:1997 – Code of Practice for Fire Safety in Educational Institutions**, as well as the **Tall Building Code (Clause 8.4 – NBC 2016)** for high-rise structures.

The fire tower constitutes an **independent, continuous, unobstructed path of travel** from any occupied floor to the **exit discharge at grade level** (or to a place of safety). It is characterized by:

- **Fire-Resistant Construction:** Walls, floor slabs, and roof of the enclosure must possess a **fire resistance rating of not less than 2 hours (120 minutes)** for high-rise buildings, and **1 hour (60 minutes)** for low/medium-rise buildings.
- **Self-Closing Fire Doors:** All access doors must be **fire-rated (minimum 1–2 hours)**, **self-closing**, and **intumescent-sealed**, preventing smoke and flame ingress.
- **Pressurization:** A positive pressure differential of **12 Pa to 15 Pa** is maintained between the fire tower and the adjacent fire floor (as per NBC 2016, Table 4 – Pressurization Levels), preventing smoke infiltration.
- **Independent Ventilation:** Natural or mechanical ventilation at the top of the shaft, with a minimum opening of **0.5 m²** per floor.
- **Direct Exit Discharge:** The fire tower must terminate at an **exit discharge at ground level**, leading directly to a public way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fire Towers are non-negotiable life-safety features for all buildings exceeding **15 m in height** (Group A – Residential, Educational, Institutional) and **24 m for Group B (Office)**. They are mandatory under **Clause 4.4.2 of NBC 2016**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a multi-storey shopping mall, and a fire breaks out on the 5th floor. The regular staircase is filled with thick, toxic smoke, and the lift is automatically recalled to the ground floor (a safety feature called **Phase I & II Elevator Recall**). Where do you go?

Think of the **fire tower** as a **"lifeboat chute"** of a building — a sealed, pressurized, fireproof vertical tunnel that runs from the topmost floor all the way to the street, completely isolated from the fire-affected floors. The walls are like the **hull of a submarine**, the fire doors are like the **water-tight bulkheads** that close automatically when danger approaches, and the pressurization system is the **air pump** that pushes clean, breathable air into the shaft, keeping smoke out by positive pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** A fire tower is the building's dedicated "emergency evacuation highway" — a fireproof, smoke-proof staircase that allows people to escape safely during a fire, even when all other exits are blocked.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and standard metrics are fundamental to fire tower design:

- **Fire Resistance Rating:** **120 minutes (2 hours)** for high-rise buildings (NBC 2016, Table 1).
- **Minimum Stair Width:** **1.5 m (1500 mm)** for occupant load above 300 persons per floor.
- **Standard Riser Height:** **150 mm to 190 mm**.
- **Standard Tread Depth:** **250 mm to 300 mm**.
- **Riser-Tread Relationship:** $2R + T = 600 \text{ to } 625 \text{ mm}$ (Blondel's Formula).
- **Minimum Headroom:** **2.2 m (2200 mm)** measured from the nosing of the tread.
- **Pressurization Differential:** **12 Pa to 15 Pa** between fire tower and fire floor.
- **Maximum Travel Distance:** **22.5 m to 30 m** depending on occupancy (without sprinkler).
- **Evacuation Flow Rate:** **30 to 40 persons per minute per metre of stair width** (under emergency conditions).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept – The "Why" and the "How"

The fire tower operates on **three fundamental physical principles** that work in tandem to provide life safety:

#### Principle 1: Compartmentalization (Fire Resistance)
The fire tower shaft is constructed as a **standalone vertical compartment**, separated from the rest of the building by fire-rated walls and floors. This compartmentalization ensures that even if the adjacent floor is fully engulfed in fire, the integrity of the escape route is maintained for a minimum period of **2 hours**, which is the **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)** margin.

#### Principle 2: Smoke Exclusion (Pressurization)
The fire tower is maintained at a **higher air pressure** than the fire-affected floor. This is achieved by:
- **Mechanical pressurization fans** at the top or bottom of the shaft.
- **Leakage paths through closed fire doors** act as relief valves, allowing air to escape slowly towards the fire floor.
- The **pressure differential of 12 Pa** is sufficient to overcome the **buoyancy-driven smoke flow** through door cracks.

#### Principle 3: Continuous, Uninterrupted Path (Egress Geometry)
The fire tower provides a **continuous vertical path** without dead ends, with the following design logic:
- **Continuous handrails** at 900 mm and 700 mm heights (for adults and children).
- **Anti-slip nosings** on treads to prevent falls during panicked evacuation.
- **Landing width** ≥ stair width, with intermediate landings every **16 risers** maximum.
- **No storage, electrical panels, or combustibles** within the shaft at any floor.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic

The design of a fire tower follows this structured engineering logic:

1. **Step 1 – Occupant Load Calculation:** Determine the number of persons per floor using NBC 2016 occupant load factors (e.g., 1 person per 1.5 m² for offices, 1 person per 4 m² for assembly).
2. **Step 2 – Travel Distance Check:** Ensure the maximum travel distance from any point on a floor to the fire tower door is within the prescribed limit (22.5 m to 30 m, depending on occupancy).
3. **Step 3 – Stair Width Calculation:** Calculate the required width using the occupant load and evacuation flow rate.
4. **Step 4 – Number of Fire Towers:** Determine the number required based on floor area, occupant load, and travel distance.
5. **Step 5 – Pressurization Design:** Specify fan capacity, duct sizing, and pressure sensor locations.
6. **Step 6 – Fire Door Specification:** Select fire-rated self-closing doors with intumescent seals and vision panels.
7. **Step 7 – Discharge Design:** Ensure the exit discharge leads directly to a public way, with a minimum width equal to the stair width.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the high-yield formulas essential for KTU 2024 examinations on fire towers:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Occupant Load | $OL = \dfrac{A}{OLF}$ | persons | Where $A$ = floor area (m²), $OLF$ = Occupant Load Factor |
| 2 | Stair Width | $W = \dfrac{N}{R \times t}$ | m | Where $N$ = number of persons, $R$ = flow rate (persons/min/m), $t$ = evacuation time (min) |
| 3 | Blondel's Formula | $2R + T = 600 \text{ to } 625$ | mm | Where $R$ = riser, $T$ = tread |
| 4 | Travel Distance | $TD \leq TD_{max}$ | m | As per NBC 2016 Table 3 |
| 5 | Flow Rate | $F = W \times R_{pm}$ | persons/min | Where $R_{pm}$ = persons per minute per metre width |
| 6 | Evacuation Time | $t = \dfrac{N}{F}$ | min | Total time to evacuate |
| 7 | Number of Exits | $N_e \geq 2$ | - | Minimum 2 remote exits for any floor |
| 8 | Pressure Differential | $\Delta P \geq 12$ | Pa | For high-rise stairwell pressurization |
| 9 | Door Width | $W_d \geq 1000$ | mm | Minimum clear width of fire door |
| 10 | Landing Length | $L \geq 1.2 \times W$ | m | Where $W$ = stair width |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fire towers are not theoretical constructs — they are **life-critical infrastructure** in:

- **High-Rise Residential Buildings:** Mandatory in all buildings above 15 m height (NBC 2016).
- **Hospitals and Healthcare:** Where patients on stretchers, wheelchairs, or bedridden require protected horizontal evacuation first, then vertical descent.
- **Educational Institutions:** Schools and colleges with multi-storey blocks.
- **Commercial Complexes:** Malls, IT parks, and office towers.
- **Industrial Buildings:** Where hazardous materials are stored.

The design of fire towers integrates knowledge from **structural engineering** (fire-rated concrete), **mechanical engineering** (HVAC pressurization), **electrical engineering** (emergency lighting, fire alarm integration), and **fire protection engineering** (compartmentalization, suppression).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **World Trade Center (1973–2001)** had pressurized fire stairs that saved an estimated **25,000 lives** on 9/11. The fire towers of the twin towers are often cited as the most successful example of life-safety engineering in history.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Stair Width from Occupant Load

The minimum width of a fire tower is derived from the **fundamental egress equation**, which balances the number of persons to be evacuated against the capacity of the stair.

**Step 1:** Define the variables.

Let $N$ = total number of persons to be evacuated from the building (sum of occupant loads across all floors served by the fire tower).

Let $R$ = flow rate, expressed in **persons per minute per metre width** of the stair.

Let $t$ = total evacuation time in minutes.

Let $W$ = clear width of the stair in metres.

**Step 2:** Express the flow rate as a function of width and time.

The number of persons that can pass through a stair of width $W$ in time $t$ is:

$$
N_{capacity} = R \times W \times t
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the equilibrium condition.

For a safe evacuation, the capacity must equal or exceed the demand:

$$
R \times W \times t \geq N
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the minimum required width $W$.

$$
W \geq \frac{N}{R \times t}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute standard KTU values.

For a typical office building, $R = 30$ persons/min/m (per NBC 2016 emergency flow rate), and $t = 8$ minutes (target evacuation time for high-rise).

$$
W \geq \frac{N}{30 \times 8} = \frac{N}{240}
$$

**Step 6:** Final simplified form.

$$
W_{min} = \frac{N}{240} \text{ m}
$$

> **Logic Check:** A building with $N = 480$ persons would require $W \geq 2.0$ m, which is consistent with NBC 2016 minimum widths for high-occupancy floors.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Differential for Smoke Exclusion

The pressurization of a fire tower is derived from the **buoyancy equation** for smoke flow through door openings.

**Step 1:** Buoyancy-driven smoke pressure.

The pressure generated by a fire-driven smoke column is:

$$
\Delta P_{smoke} = K \times h \times \left(\frac{1}{T_{ambient}} - \frac{1}{T_{smoke}}\right)
$$

Where $K = 3463 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{K/m}$ (gas constant for air), $h$ = height of the smoke layer (m), $T_{ambient}$ = ambient temperature (K), $T_{smoke}$ = smoke temperature (K).

**Step 2:** Condition for smoke exclusion.

To prevent smoke ingress into the fire tower, the pressurization pressure must exceed the smoke buoyancy pressure:

$$
\Delta P_{stair} \geq \Delta P_{smoke}
$$

**Step 3:** NBC 2016 empirical value.

Based on full-scale fire tests, NBC 2016 specifies a minimum pressure differential of **12 Pa to 15 Pa** for high-rise staircases. This empirical value accounts for:

- Door leakage area (typically 0.05 m² per door)
- Cracks and gaps in construction
- Variations in fire size and heat release rate

**Step 4:** Final design specification.

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{stair} = 12 \text{ to } 15 \text{ Pa}}
$$

For the fire floor itself, a higher differential of **40 to 50 Pa** is recommended to maintain tenable conditions.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Evacuation Time for Multi-Storey Buildings

**Step 1:** Floor-by-floor evacuation time.

For a building of $n$ floors, the time for the last person to reach the discharge level is the sum of:

- **Time to descend one floor** ($t_1$)
- **Time to traverse landings** ($t_2$)
- **Time for queuing at the discharge** ($t_3$)

**Step 2:** Single-floor descent time.

$$
t_1 = \frac{h_{floor}}{v_{descent}}
$$

Where $h_{floor}$ = floor-to-floor height (typically 3.0 m), $v_{descent}$ = average descent speed (typically 0.5 m/s under emergency conditions).

**Step 3:** Substituting standard values.

$$
t_1 = \frac{3.0}{0.5} = 6 \text{ seconds per floor}
$$

**Step 4:** Total evacuation time for the topmost floor.

$$
t_{total} = n \times t_1 + t_{queue}
$$

For a 10-storey building with $n = 10$ and $t_{queue} = 30$ seconds:

$$
t_{total} = 10 \times 6 + 30 = 90 \text{ seconds} = 1.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Note:** This is the **physical movement time only**. The **Total Evacuation Time (TET)** includes detection time, alarm time, pre-movement time, and movement time, as defined in the **BS 7974 framework**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison with Regular Staircase

| Feature | Fire Tower | Regular Staircase |
|---------|------------|------------------|
| Fire Resistance | **120 minutes (2 hrs)** | 60 minutes (1 hr) or less |
| Enclosure | Fully sealed, fire-rated shaft | Open to corridor/building |
| Pressurization | **12–15 Pa positive** | None |
| Fire Doors | Self-closing, intumescent sealed | Wooden or mild steel, no fire rating |
| Ventilation | Independent, **0.5 m²/floor** | Shares building ventilation |
| Storage | **Strictly prohibited** | Often used for storage |
| Width | As per occupant load (**≥ 1.5 m**) | As per functional requirement |
| Discharge | Direct to public way at grade | May lead to lobby or corridor |
| Marking | Photoluminescent exit signs | Standard signage |
| Emergency Lighting | **2-hour battery backup** | Standard lighting only |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Tower Architecture – Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a fire tower system, mapping the interactions between its key components:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Occupant on Any Floor] --> B{Corridor Travel}
    B --> C[Fire Rated Self Closing Door]
    C --> D[Fire Tower Stairwell Shaft]
    D --> D1[Fire Rated Walls 2 hr]
    D --> D2[Fire Rated Floor Slabs 2 hr]
    D --> D3[Handrails at 700 mm and 900 mm]
    D --> D4[Anti Slip Nosings]
    D1 --> E[Pressurization Fan 12 to 15 Pa]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Pressure Sensor at Each Floor]
    F --> G[Smoke Control Panel]
    G --> H[Fire Alarm and Detection System]
    D --> I[Independent Ventilation 0.5 m sq per Floor]
    D --> J[Emergency Lighting 2 hr Backup]
    D --> K[Photoluminescent Exit Signage]
    D --> L[Intermediate Landing every 16 Risers]
    L --> M[Final Discharge at Grade Level]
    M --> N[Direct Access to Public Way]
    M --> O[Assembly Point at Safe Distance]
    H --> P[Fire Suppression Sprinkler Interface]
    P --> Q[Building Management System BMS]
```

### 4.2 Component Hierarchy – Mermaid Tree Diagram

The following tree diagram represents the **hierarchical classification** of fire tower components as per NBC 2016:

```mermaid
graph TD
    FT[Fire Tower System] --> ST[Structural Components]
    FT --> AC[Access Components]
    FT --> MS[Mechanical Systems]
    FT --> EL[Electrical Systems]
    FT --> SF[Safety Features]
    ST --> W[Fire Rated Walls 2 hr]
    ST --> FS[Fire Rated Floor Slabs]
    ST --> RT[Roof Slab 2 hr]
    ST --> CL[Column Encapsulation]
    AC --> FD[Fire Rated Doors 1 to 2 hr]
    AC --> HW[Handrails Dual Height]
    AC --> NS[Anti Slip Nosings]
    AC --> LD[Intermediate Landings]
    MS --> PR[Pressurization Fan]
    MS --> PS[Pressure Sensor]
    MS --> VN[Ventilation Opening]
    EL --> EM[Emergency Lighting]
    EL --> EX[Exit Signage]
    EL --> FA[Fire Alarm Interface]
    SF --> FH[Fire Hose Reel Cabinet]
    SF --> FE[Fire Extinguisher Mount]
    SF --> EM2[Emergency Communication]
```

### 4.3 Evacuation Flow Sequence – Mermaid Sequence Diagram

The following sequence diagram represents the **chronological evacuation process** when a fire occurs in a high-rise building:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Occupant
    participant D as Fire Detector
    participant A as Fire Alarm Panel
    participant F as Fire Tower
    participant E as Exit Discharge
    participant FS as Fire Services
    
    D->>A: Smoke or Heat Detection Signal
    A->>A: Verify Signal and Initiate Alarm
    A->>O: Audible and Visual Alarm Activation
    A->>F: Activate Pressurization Fan 12 Pa
    A->>F: Unlock All Fire Tower Doors
    A->>FS: Automatic Fire Service Notification
    O->>O: Pre Movement Time 1 to 3 min
    O->>F: Travel to Fire Tower Door
    F->>F: Self Closing Fire Door Opens
    O->>F: Descend Stair in Orderly Manner
    F->>E: Reach Exit Discharge at Grade
    E->>FS: Assembly Point Marked
    FS->>FS: Head Count and Verification
    A->>F: Maintain Pressurization Until All Clear
```

### 4.4 Pressurization Airflow Schematic – Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[External Air Intake at Roof] --> FIL[Filter Bank G4 plus F7]
    FIL --> FAN[Pressurization Fan 1500 to 3000 CFM]
    FAN --> DUCT[Galvanized Steel Duct 600 mm dia]
    DUCT --> INJ[Air Injection Point at Top of Shaft]
    INJ --> SHAFT[Fire Tower Shaft]
    SHAFT --> SHAFT2[Vertical Air Column]
    SHAFT2 --> DOOR1[Floor 10 Door Crack 0.05 m sq]
    SHAFT2 --> DOOR2[Floor 5 Door Crack 0.05 m sq]
    SHAFT2 --> DOOR3[Floor 1 Door Crack 0.05 m sq]
    SHAFT2 --> DISCH[Air Discharge at Grade]
    SHAFT --> SENS[Pressure Sensor at Floor 5]
    SENS --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    VFD --> FAN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The pressurization fan injects clean air at the top of the shaft. The air column pressurizes the entire shaft. Small leakage through door cracks prevents smoke from entering. The pressure sensor at mid-height feeds back to a VFD, which modulates fan speed to maintain a constant 12 Pa differential.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a fire tower. List any four essential design features of a fire tower as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fire tower** is a fully enclosed, fire-rated vertical circulation shaft within a building, exclusively designed for the emergency evacuation of occupants during a fire or other life-threatening emergency. It forms a critical component of the **Means of Egress** system, providing a protected path of travel from any occupied floor to a place of safety at grade level.

**Four Essential Design Features:**

1. **Fire Resistance:** Walls, floors, and roof of the fire tower must have a fire resistance rating of **not less than 2 hours (120 minutes)** for high-rise buildings.

2. **Self-Closing Fire Doors:** All access points must be fitted with **self-closing fire-rated doors** (minimum 1–2 hour rating) with intumescent seals to prevent smoke and flame ingress.

3. **Pressurization:** A positive pressure differential of **12 Pa to 15 Pa** must be maintained between the fire tower and adjacent fire-affected floors to exclude smoke.

4. **Independent Ventilation:** Natural or mechanical ventilation at the top of the shaft with a minimum opening of **0.5 m² per floor** to prevent smoke accumulation in case of pressurization failure.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four features)**

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of stairwell pressurization in fire towers. State the recommended pressure differential.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Stairwell Pressurization:**

Stairwell pressurization is a **smoke control technique** used in fire towers to maintain a **positive air pressure** within the stairwell shaft, relative to the fire-affected floor. This is achieved by a **mechanical supply fan** that injects clean, filtered outside air into the top or bottom of the shaft, creating a vertical air column that resists the **buoyancy-driven inflow of smoke** through door cracks and small openings.

The principle relies on the fact that **smoke cannot flow from a low-pressure region (fire floor) into a high-pressure region (fire tower)**. The pressurization system is automatically activated upon **fire alarm initiation** and is typically integrated with the **Building Management System (BMS)** for monitoring and control.

**Recommended Pressure Differential:**

As per **NBC 2016, Table 4 (Pressurization Levels)**, the recommended pressure differential is:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 12 \text{ Pa to } 15 \text{ Pa}}
$$

For the **fire floor itself**, a higher differential of **40 Pa to 50 Pa** is recommended to maintain tenable conditions for occupants and firefighters.

**(3 marks: 2 marks for concept, 1 mark for pressure value)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the design requirements of a fire tower as per NBC 2016. Discuss the construction, fire resistance, and pressurization aspects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-storey office building has a floor area of **800 m² per floor** and an occupant load factor of **6 m² per person**. The target evacuation time is **8 minutes**. Calculate:
1. The occupant load per floor.
2. The total occupant load for the building.
3. The minimum required width of the fire tower.
4. The number of fire towers required, given that the maximum travel distance from any point to a fire tower is **30 m**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Design Requirements of a Fire Tower as per NBC 2016:**

The design of a fire tower is governed by **Clause 4.4 and Part 4 of NBC 2016**, with the following requirements:

**1. Construction Aspects:**

- The fire tower must be a **continuous vertical shaft** from the topmost occupied floor to the **exit discharge at grade level**.
- **Walls** must be of **non-combustible material** (reinforced concrete or fire-rated masonry) with a minimum thickness of **200 mm for RCC** or **300 mm for brick**.
- **Floor slabs** must have a fire resistance rating of **2 hours**.
- The shaft must be **free of any services** (plumbing, electrical conduits) except those exclusively serving the fire tower (lighting, pressurization ducts).
- **No openings** are permitted in the shaft walls except for **fire-rated access doors** and **ventilation openings**.

**2. Fire Resistance Aspects:**

- The fire tower must achieve a **fire resistance rating of 2 hours (120 minutes)** for both **integrity** (no flame passage) and **insulation** (temperature rise on unexposed face ≤ 140°C).
- All **penetrations** (electrical conduits, pipes) must be **fire-stopped** with intumescent materials or fire-rated sealants.
- **Fire doors** must have a minimum rating of **1 hour** for low/medium-rise and **2 hours** for high-rise buildings.
- The **structural frame** of the building at the fire tower location must be **encapsulated** with fire-rated cladding to maintain 2-hour integrity.

**3. Pressurization Aspects:**

- A **dedicated pressurization system** with redundant fans (N+1 configuration) must be installed.
- The system must maintain a **pressure differential of 12 Pa to 15 Pa** between the fire tower and any adjacent fire-affected floor.
- **Pressure sensors** must be installed at every alternate floor, with feedback to a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**.
- The system must be **automatically activated** upon fire alarm signal and must have a **90-minute battery backup**.
- A **manual override** must be provided at the **Fire Command Centre**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for fire resistance, 3 marks for pressurization)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 – Occupant Load per Floor:**

Given: Floor area $A = 800 \text{ m}^2$, Occupant Load Factor $OLF = 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$.

$$
OL_{floor} = \frac{A}{OLF} = \frac{800}{6} = 133.33 \approx 134 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 2 – Total Occupant Load:**

For a 12-storey building:

$$
OL_{total} = 12 \times 134 = 1608 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 3 – Minimum Required Width of Fire Tower:**

Given: Evacuation time $t = 8$ min, Flow rate $R = 30$ persons/min/m (NBC 2016 standard).

$$
W_{min} = \frac{N}{R \times t} = \frac{1608}{30 \times 8} = \frac{1608}{240} = 6.7 \text{ m}
$$

Since the minimum width per tower is **1.5 m** (NBC 2016), the number of towers required is:

$$
N_{towers} = \frac{6.7}{1.5} = 4.47 \approx 5 \text{ fire towers}
$$

**Step 4 – Verification with Travel Distance:**

The maximum travel distance of **30 m** is within the NBC 2016 limit of **30 m** for **Group B (Office) occupancy without sprinklers**. Hence, the travel distance criterion is satisfied.

**Final Answer:**
- Occupant load per floor: **134 persons**
- Total occupant load: **1608 persons**
- Minimum stair width: **6.7 m**
- Number of fire towers required: **5**

**(7 marks: 2 marks each for steps 1–3, 1 mark for step 4)**

---

#### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between a fire tower and a regular staircase. Discuss the safety features that make a fire tower a "protected means of egress."

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital building of **8 floors** has a **total occupant load of 400 persons**, including **30 bedridden patients** on stretchers. Design the fire tower specifications:
1. Minimum width of the fire tower.
2. Type of fire doors required.
3. Pressurization differential.
4. Number of intermediate landings per floor (assuming 16 risers per landing).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Difference between Fire Tower and Regular Staircase:**

| Parameter | Fire Tower | Regular Staircase |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| Purpose | Emergency evacuation only | Daily circulation and emergency |
| Fire Resistance | **2 hours (120 min)** | 1 hour (60 min) or less |
| Enclosure | Fully sealed, fire-rated | Open to building |
| Pressurization | **12–15 Pa** | None |
| Fire Doors | Self-closing, intumescent | Standard doors |
| Width | **≥ 1.5 m** | As per functional need |
| Storage | **Prohibited** | Often used for storage |
| Discharge | **Direct to public way** | May lead to lobby |

**Safety Features that Make a Fire Tower a "Protected Means of Egress":**

1. **Fire-rated construction** (2-hour walls and floors) ensures structural integrity during a fire.
2. **Self-closing fire doors** prevent smoke and flame ingress.
3. **Pressurization** maintains positive pressure to exclude smoke.
4. **Independent ventilation** prevents smoke accumulation in case of pressurization failure.
5. **Emergency lighting** (2-hour battery backup) ensures visibility during power failure.
6. **Photoluminescent signage** guides occupants even in zero visibility.
7. **Direct exit discharge** to a public way eliminates the need to traverse fire-affected areas.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for comparison table, 4 marks for safety features)**

---

**(b) Design of Fire Tower for Hospital Building:**

**Step 1 – Minimum Width:**

For a hospital with 400 persons, the **occupant load factor is lower** (10 m²/person) due to the presence of bedridden patients and equipment. However, the **flow rate is reduced to 20 persons/min/m** (slower evacuation due to stretchers and wheelchairs).

$$
W_{min} = \frac{400}{20 \times 8} = \frac{400}{160} = 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

However, since **30 bedridden patients on stretchers** require **horizontal evacuation** first (to a refuge floor or adjacent fire compartment), the fire tower width must accommodate **stretcher passage**. The standard **stretcher width is 600 mm**, and a minimum of **2.0 m clear width** is required for stretcher manoeuvrability on landings.

**Step 2 – Fire Doors:**

- **Type:** **2-hour fire-rated, double-swing, self-closing doors** with **intumescent seals** and **vision panels (wired glass)**.
- **Width:** Minimum **1.2 m clear opening** for stretcher passage.
- **Hardware:** **Anti-panic bar** (panic bolt) for one-touch egress.

**Step 3 – Pressurization Differential:**

For a hospital (high-risk occupancy with non-ambulatory occupants):

$$
\Delta P = 15 \text{ Pa (upper limit of NBC 2016 range)}
$$

**Step 4 – Number of Intermediate Landings:**

Given: **16 risers per landing**, Riser height $R = 150$ mm.

$$
\text{Landing height} = 16 \times 150 = 2400 \text{ mm} = 2.4 \text{ m}
$$

Since the typical **floor-to-floor height is 3.0 m**, one landing is insufficient. Therefore, the configuration is:

- **1 landing at mid-height (2.4 m)** and the **floor slab (0.6 m above landing)**.
- **Number of intermediate landings per floor: 1**

**Final Answer:**
- Minimum width: **2.5 m** (but ≥ 2.0 m for stretcher passage)
- Fire doors: **2-hour, double-swing, self-closing, 1.2 m clear width**
- Pressurization: **15 Pa**
- Intermediate landings: **1 per floor**

**(7 marks: 2 marks each for steps 1–3, 1 mark for step 4)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **fire tower** with **fire escape** (external metal staircase). Remember, a fire tower is an **internal, enclosed, pressurized** staircase, not an external metal structure.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** The **pressure differential of 12 Pa** is for the **stairwell**, not the fire floor. For the fire floor, the value is **40–50 Pa**. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical problems, students forget to **convert units** (e.g., mm to m) or use the wrong **flow rate** (30 persons/min/m for offices vs. 20 persons/min/m for hospitals). Always re-read the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** The **minimum width of 1.5 m** is for **high-rise buildings**. For low-rise, it may be **1.0 m** or **1.25 m**. Always specify the building type.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Students often write **"fire lift"** instead of **"fire tower"** in definitions. These are **two different things** — a fire lift is an **elevator**, not a staircase.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a **high-density, rapid-revision checklist** for the topic "Fire Tower":

- ✅ **Definition:** A fire tower is a **fully enclosed, fire-rated, pressurized vertical circulation shaft** dedicated to **emergency egress** during a fire.
- ✅ **Fire Resistance:** **2 hours (120 minutes)** for high-rise buildings; **1 hour (60 minutes)** for low/medium-rise.
- ✅ **Minimum Width:** **1.5 m** for high-occupancy floors; **1.0 m to 1.25 m** for low-occupancy floors.
- ✅ **Pressurization:** **12 Pa to 15 Pa** for the stairwell; **40 Pa to 50 Pa** for the fire floor.
- ✅ **Fire Doors:** **Self-closing, intumescent-sealed, 1–2 hour rated**, with **panic bar** hardware.
- ✅ **Blondel's Formula:** $2R + T = 600 \text{ to } 625 \text{ mm}$ (riser-tread relationship).
- ✅ **Riser:** **150 mm to 190 mm**; **Tread:** **250 mm to 300 mm**.
- ✅ **Headroom:** **Minimum 2.2 m** measured from nosing of tread.
- ✅ **Handrails:** **Dual height — 900 mm (adults) and 700 mm (children)**.
- ✅ **Ventilation:** **0.5 m² per floor**, independent of building HVAC.
- ✅ **Emergency Lighting:** **2-hour battery backup**.
- ✅ **Travel Distance:** **22.5 m to 30 m** depending on occupancy (without sprinklers); **45 m** with sprinklers.
- ✅ **Stair Width Formula:** $W = \dfrac{N}{R \times t}$ where $R = 30$ persons/min/m for offices.
- ✅ **Evacuation Flow Rate:** **30 persons/min/m** (offices), **20 persons/min/m** (hospitals).
- ✅ **Mandatory for:** All buildings **above 15 m** (Group A) and **above 24 m** (Group B).
- ✅ **Discharge:** **Direct to public way** at grade; no obstructions.
- ✅ **Storage:** **Strictly prohibited** within the fire tower shaft.
- ✅ **NBC Reference:** **Part 4, Clause 4.4.2** of NBC 2016.
- ✅ **Historical Note:** World Trade Center fire stairs (1973–2001) saved an estimated **25,000 lives** on 9/11 — a testament to the effectiveness of well-designed fire towers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, always **memorize the numerical values** (12 Pa, 2 hours, 1.5 m, 30 persons/min/m) and **cite the NBC 2016 clause** in your answers. Examiners award bonus marks for code references.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ramps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ramps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Ramp** is defined as an inclined way of passage provided in a building, having a gradient steeper than **1 in 20 (5%)** but not steeper than **1 in 10 (10%)**, used for the movement of persons, wheelchairs, trolleys, and stretchers during normal circulation as well as during **emergency evacuation** scenarios including fire.

In the context of **Life Safety in Building Fire (PESFT416)**, ramps serve as one of the most critical **means of egress** for:
- **Occupants with disabilities (PwDs)** under the Persons with Disabilities Act, **2017**
- **Hospital patients on stretchers/wheelchairs** during fire evacuation
- **Ambulatory evacuation** when staircases are congested or smoke-filled

The governing design references in India include the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** — Part 4 *Fire and Life Safety*, Part 3 *Development Control Rules and General Building Requirements*, and the **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)** guidelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] 
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a ramp as a **"sloped bridge for emergencies"** — imagine a hospital corridor during a fire alarm. The lift is OFF (per safety code). Patients on stretchers cannot use stairs. The ramp acts like a gentle slope connecting floors, much like a playground slide turned on its side — gentle enough to roll a wheelchair down safely, yet structured to halt it firmly if needed. In fire safety engineering, the ramp is the **"universal exit highway"** that guarantees *no one is left behind* during evacuation.

## 1.3 Classification of Ramps (As per NBC 2016)

| Type | Gradient (Rise : Run) | Permissible Slope (%) | Primary Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Gentle Ramp** | 1 : 20 | **5%** | Public buildings, hospitals |
| **Moderate Ramp** | 1 : 15 | **6.7%** | Commercial complexes |
