| **Steep Ramp (Max Limit)** | 1 : 12 | **8.3%** | Short transition ramps, fire egress |
| **Absolute Maximum** | 1 : 10 | **10%** | Restricted service ramps only |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Ramps with gradient gentler than 1 in 20 (5%) are technically classified as LEVEL SURFACES** and not considered ramps under fire safety codes. The 1 in 20 threshold is therefore the **diagnostic boundary** for ramp design.

## 1.4 Fundamental Design Constants (Must Remember)

- **Maximum slope for fire egress ramp:** **1 in 12 (8.33%)**
- **Minimum clear width (single wheelchair passage):** **900 mm**
- **Minimum clear width (two-way / stretcher):** **1500 mm**
- **Maximum rise between landings:** **3.6 m (12 feet)**
- **Handrail height:** **900 mm to 1000 mm**
- **Second handrail (lower) for children:** **760 mm**
- **Anti-skid surface texture:** **Mandatory** with a coefficient of friction **≥ 0.6**
- **Landing length at top and bottom:** **At least 1200 mm** clear
- **Landing at every:** **Maximum 9.0 m of horizontal run** or **every change in direction**
- **Landing width:** **Not less than the width of the ramp**

> [!NOTE] 
> The physical constant **g = 9.81 m/s²** is used when computing the deceleration of a wheelchair on a sloped ramp, but for KTU problems, design is governed by **geometric ratios** (rise:run) rather than physics-based formulas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Ramp Parameters

Ramps in fire safety are designed around **three governing principles**, often called the **"SAFE Triangle"**:

1. **S**peed of Evacuation — Wide enough to allow rapid flow of evacuees
2. **A**ccessibility — Usable by all occupants including disabled
3. **F**riction Safety — Anti-skid surface to prevent slipping under panic loading

Each design parameter in NBC 2016 maps to one of these principles.

### 2.1.1 Slope — The "Why"

A wheelchair user self-propelling up a slope of **1 in 8** (12.5%) experiences a horizontal push force approximately equal to **climbing a 5% gradient on foot** — the human body's natural biomechanical limit. Hence **1 in 12** is selected as the **physiological limit** beyond which most users cannot independently ascend.

### 2.1.2 Width — The "How"

The **Flow Theory** in fire engineering states that evacuation time is inversely proportional to the effective width of the egress route. The **915 mm (36 inches)** rule for a single wheelchair comes from anthropometric studies: an average Indian male shoulder breadth of **~450 mm** + clearance of **~225 mm on each side** ≈ **900 mm minimum**.

### 2.1.3 Landings — The "Rest" Principle

Continuous ramps longer than **9.0 m** cause fatigue in wheelchair users and panic-induced falls in evacuating crowds. Landings provide:
- **Rest intervals** for mobility-impaired users
- **Direction change points** for L-shaped or U-shaped ramps
- **Stretcher turning zones** (1500 × 1500 mm minimum)
- **Smoke reservoir pockets** (when combined with lobby pressurization)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | KTU Code Reference |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Gradient | $G = \dfrac{\text{Rise (vertical)}}{\text{Run (horizontal)}}$ | ratio | NBC 2016, Cl. 8.4 |
| 2 | Slope (%) | $S = G \times 100$ | % | — |
| 3 | Maximum permissible slope (fire) | $S_{max} = 8.33$ | % | NBC 2016, Part 4 |
| 4 | Minimum clear width (single) | $W_{min} = 900$ | mm | Cl. 8.4.2 |
| 5 | Minimum clear width (stretcher) | $W_{st} = 1500$ | mm | Cl. 8.4.2 |
| 6 | Maximum rise between landings | $R_{max} = 3.6$ | m | Cl. 8.4.3 |
| 7 | Handrail height | $H_{hr} = 900 \text{ to } 1000$ | mm | Cl. 8.4.5 |
| 8 | Second (lower) handrail | $H_{lr} = 760$ | mm | Cl. 8.4.5 |
| 9 | Landing length (top/bottom) | $L_{ld} \geq 1200$ | mm | Cl. 8.4.3 |
| 10 | Max horizontal run before landing | $L_{max} = 9.0$ | m | Cl. 8.4.3 |
| 11 | Number of risers (ramp) | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total Rise (mm)}}{R_{max}}$ | count | derived |
| 12 | Total ramp horizontal run | $L_{total} = N \times G \times R_{max}$ | m | derived |
| 13 | Floor area served calculation | $A = N_{occ} \times f_{person}$ | m² | derived |
| 14 | Travel distance (ramp) | $T_d = \sqrt{R^2 + L^2}$ (along slope) | m | geometric |
| 15 | Anti-skid friction coefficient | $\mu \geq 0.6$ | dimensionless | IS 15644 |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Critical examination note:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol in absolute value notation MUST be written as `\vert` in KTU answer sheets if rendered in LaTeX. The above table uses only `≥`, `≤`, `→` which are safe.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional fire engineering practice, ramp design is critical for:

- **Hospital fire evacuation (NBC Part 4, Cl. 6.4)** — patient transfer ramps
- **High-rise office buildings (NBC Part 4, Cl. 9.5)** — refuge area access
- **Shopping mall emergency egress (NBC Part 4, Cl. 10.3)** — public assembly
- **Educational institutions (NBC Part 4, Cl. 11.2)** — schools and colleges
- **Metro stations and transit hubs** — disability-compliant evacuation
- **Stadium and arena evacuation (NBC Part 4, Cl. 12)** — high-occupancy venues

Ramps are also used in **fire service access** — fire tender approach ramps require a **minimum 4.5 m wide** carriageway with **1 in 15 gradient** for aerial ladder deployment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ramp Horizontal Run from Vertical Rise

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU PESFT416 Module 1. Given the **vertical rise** (floor-to-floor height) and the **maximum permissible gradient**, derive the **required horizontal run**, **number of landings**, and **total ramp length**.

### 3.1.1 Given
- Vertical rise between two floors: $V$ (in mm or m)
- Maximum permissible gradient: $G$ (rise:run ratio, dimensionless)
- Maximum rise per ramp flight: $R_{max} = 3.6$ m
- Clear ramp width: $W$ (mm)

### 3.1.2 To Find
- Number of ramp flights: $N$
- Horizontal run per flight: $L_r$
- Total horizontal run: $L_{total}$
- Total length along the slope: $L_{slope}$
- Number of intermediate landings: $N_{ld}$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Determine the total number of flights required**

Since the maximum rise per flight is fixed by code at $3.6$ m, the number of flights required to bridge a vertical rise $V$ is:

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{V}{R_{max}} \right\rceil
$$

The notation $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** — the smallest integer greater than or equal to the value. This is essential because a partial flight is not permitted in fire safety design.

**Step 2 — Compute the actual vertical rise per flight**

$$
R_{flight} = \dfrac{V}{N}
$$

This must satisfy the constraint:

$$
R_{flight} \leq R_{max} = 3.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the horizontal run per flight using the gradient**

From the definition of gradient:

$$
G = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \dfrac{R_{flight}}{L_r}
$$

Solving for the horizontal run per flight:

$$
L_r = \dfrac{R_{flight}}{G}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total horizontal run**

$$
L_{total} = N \times L_r
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ramp length measured along the slope (using the Pythagorean theorem)**

Each flight forms a right triangle with vertical leg $R_{flight}$ and horizontal leg $L_r$. The hypotenuse (the actual slope length) is:

$$
L_{slope} = \sqrt{(R_{flight})^2 + (L_r)^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the number of intermediate landings**

The total number of landings (including top and bottom) is $N + 1$. Therefore, the number of **intermediate** landings is:

$$
N_{ld} = N - 1
$$

**Step 7 — Verification of gradient compliance**

Finally, verify that the actual gradient built is within the code limit:

$$
G_{actual} = \dfrac{R_{flight}}{L_r} \leq G_{permitted}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Design a fire egress ramp for a hospital connecting **Ground Floor** to **First Floor** with a vertical floor-to-floor distance of **4.2 m**. The site constraint limits the maximum horizontal run to **30 m**. The maximum permissible gradient is **1 in 12**. Compute the number of ramp flights, horizontal run per flight, total slope length, and number of landings required.

**Step 1 — Number of flights**

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{4.2}{3.6} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.167 \rceil = 2 \text{ flights}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical rise per flight**

$$
R_{flight} = \dfrac{4.2}{2} = 2.1 \text{ m}
$$

Check: $2.1 \leq 3.6$ m ✓ (compliant)

**Step 3 — Horizontal run per flight**

$$
L_r = \dfrac{R_{flight}}{G} = \dfrac{2.1}{(1/12)} = 2.1 \times 12 = 25.2 \text{ m}
$$

Check: $25.2 \leq 30$ m ✓ (site constraint satisfied)

**Step 4 — Total horizontal run**

$$
L_{total} = 2 \times 25.2 = 50.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Slope length per flight**

$$
L_{slope} = \sqrt{(2.1)^2 + (25.2)^2} = \sqrt{4.41 + 635.04} = \sqrt{639.45}
$$

$$
L_{slope} = 25.288 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Total slope length (both flights)**

$$
L_{total,slope} = 2 \times 25.288 = 50.576 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of intermediate landings**

$$
N_{ld} = N - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1 \text{ intermediate landing}
$$

Total landings = $N + 1 = 3$ (one at ground, one at mid-height, one at first floor).

**Step 8 — Final gradient verification**

$$
G_{actual} = \dfrac{2.1}{25.2} = \dfrac{1}{12} = 0.0833 = 8.33\%
$$

Compliant with the 1:12 limit ✓

> [!NOTE] 
> **Engineering Insight:** The total slope length (50.576 m) is only **0.35% longer** than the horizontal run (50.4 m). For gentle ramps with gradients up to 1:12, the difference between slope length and horizontal run is negligible in design calculations. This is why many practicing engineers approximate $L_{slope} \approx L_{total}$ for ramp design.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Compliance Verification

The following matrix is a **unified audit tool** for verifying whether a proposed ramp design meets **all** code requirements. KTU examiners often give partial design data and expect students to identify non-compliances.

| Compliance Check | Code Limit | Designer Input | Status | Action Required |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Gradient | $\leq 1:12$ | $1:15$ | ✓ Pass | None |
| Clear width (single wheelchair) | $\geq 900$ mm | $1200$ mm | ✓ Pass | None |
| Clear width (stretcher) | $\geq 1500$ mm | $1100$ mm | ✗ Fail | Increase to 1500 mm |
| Max rise per flight | $\leq 3.6$ m | $2.8$ m | ✓ Pass | None |
| Landing length (top/bottom) | $\geq 1200$ mm | $1500$ mm | ✓ Pass | None |
| Landing width | $\geq$ ramp width | $1100$ mm | ✗ Fail | Match ramp width (1500 mm) |
| Handrail height | $900$–$1000$ mm | $850$ mm | ✗ Fail | Raise to 900 mm |
| Anti-skid surface | Required | Polished granite | ✗ Fail | Use anti-skid tiles (μ ≥ 0.6) |
| Max horizontal run | $\leq 9.0$ m | $12$ m | ✗ Fail | Insert intermediate landing |
| Landing at direction change | Required | Absent at L-turn | ✗ Fail | Add 1500×1500 mm landing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Ramp System in Fire Egress

The following Mermaid block represents the **hierarchical block-level architecture** of a fire safety ramp system, mapping how an occupant interacts with the structural and safety components during evacuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Alarm Triggered] --> B[Occupant Initiates Evacuation]
    B --> C{Is Occupant Able-Bodied?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Staircase as Primary Route]
    C -->|No| E[Activate Accessible Egress Protocol]
    E --> F[Approach Fire Ramp Entry]
    F --> G[Pass Through Fire-rated Door 120 min]
    G --> H[Traverse Ramp Flight 1 of N]
    H --> I{Is Intermediate Landing Reached?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Rest at Landing 1500 mm]
    J --> K[Hold Handrail at 900 mm]
    K --> L[Continue to Next Flight]
    L --> H
    I -->|No| M[Reach Exit Discharge Level]
    H --> M
    M --> N[Cross Assembly Point Marker]
    N --> O[Occupant Safety Confirmed]
    D --> P[Cross Staircase Landing]
    P --> M
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram of Ramp Geometry

The following block diagram decomposes a **single ramp flight** into its geometric, structural, and safety sub-components. This is the standard schematic that KTU expects in Part B 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Landing 1200 mm] --> B[Handrail Upper 900 to 1000 mm]
    B --> C[Ramp Slope 1 in 12 max]
    C --> D[Anti-skid Surface mu 0.6]
    D --> E[Ramp Clear Width 900 mm min]
    E --> F[Intermediate Landing 1500 mm]
    F --> G[Knee Rail 75 mm]
    G --> H[Handrail Lower 760 mm]
    H --> I[Continuous Handrail Extension 300 mm]
    I --> J[Bottom Landing 1200 mm]
    J --> K[Fire-rated Door 120 min]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When is a Ramp Mandatory?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Building Type Identified]) --> Q1{Is Building a Hospital or Healthcare?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Ramp Mandatory 1 in 12]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Building Assembly with Occupancy greater than 500?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Ramp Mandatory 1 in 15]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is Building Educational above G plus 2?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Ramp Mandatory 1 in 12]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is Difference in Level greater than 300 mm?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Provide Ramp 1 in 12]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[No Ramp Required - Level Surface]
    R1 --> End([Design Complete])
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
    R5 --> End
```

> [!NOTE] 
> **Why this diagram matters:** The decision tree above mirrors the **NBC 2016 Part 3 Cl. 8.4.1** which prescribes the building typologies that mandate ramp provision. In a KTU 14-mark question, presenting this decision logic along with numerical design earns full marks.

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text-Based Description)

Since Mermaid cannot render architectural cross-sections directly, the following **textual schematic** describes a typical ramp section as expected in KTU answer sheets:

```
         HANDRAIL UPPER  900 - 1000 mm
         |================================|   <- Top Landing (1200 mm)
         |                                |
         |          (Ramp Slope)          |   Gradient: 1 in 12
         |          /                     |   Rise: 3.6 m max
         |         /                      |   Run:  43.2 m max
         |        /                       |
         |       /                        |   Clear Width: 1500 mm
         |      /                         |
         |     /                          |
         |    /                           |
         |   /                            |
         |  /                             |
         | /                              |
         |/_______________________________|   <- Intermediate Landing
         |                                |      (1500 x 1500 mm)
         |================================|   <- Handrail Lower 760 mm
         |                                |
         KNEE RAIL    ANTI-SKID TILE      |
                     (μ ≥ 0.6)
```

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Anti-skid coefficient of friction $\mu \geq 0.6$** is verified using the **pendulum test** as per **IS 15644:2006**. This is a high-yield point for KTU viva-voce and is asked frequently in 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: Define a ramp as per NBC 2016 and state the maximum permissible gradient for a fire egress ramp in a hospital building.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> A **ramp** is an inclined way of passage in a building having a gradient steeper than 1 in 20 (5%) and not steeper than 1 in 10 (10%), used for the movement of persons including those on wheelchairs and stretchers. **(1 Mark)**
> 
> For **hospital buildings** classified under NBC 2016 as Group B (Institutional) occupancy, the maximum permissible gradient for a **fire egress ramp** is **1 in 12 (8.33%)**. **(1 Mark)**
> 
> This limit is prescribed to ensure that occupants on stretchers/wheelchairs can be safely evacuated without risk of run-away, and the gradient must be uniform throughout the ramp length without any cross-slope. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - December 2023]`

**Q: List any three mandatory design requirements for handrails provided along fire egress ramps.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> As per NBC 2016, Part 3, Cl. 8.4.5, handrails along fire egress ramps must comply with the following:
> 
> 1. **Height of handrail:** Must be provided at a height between **900 mm and 1000 mm** measured from the ramp surface. **(1 Mark)**
> 
> 2. **Second lower handrail:** A second handrail at a lower height of **760 mm** must be provided to assist children and short-stature occupants. **(1 Mark)**
> 
> 3. **Continuous extension:** Handrails must be continuous and must extend **horizontally by at least 300 mm** beyond the top and bottom of the ramp flight, then return to the wall or floor. **(1 Mark)**
> 
> *(Bonus acceptable point: Handrail diameter should be 40–50 mm for firm grip; gap between wall and handrail ≥ 40 mm; handrail must withstand a horizontal load of 0.75 kN/m at handrail height.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

> **Q (A): (a)** Explain in detail the various **design requirements for ramps** in a multi-storeyed hospital building as per NBC 2016, with reference to gradient, width, landings, and handrails. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A fire egress ramp is to be designed to connect the **Ground Floor** to the **Second Floor** of an office building. The floor-to-floor height is **3.6 m per floor**. The maximum permissible gradient is **1 in 12**. The clear width of the ramp is fixed at **1500 mm**. Compute the **number of ramp flights**, **horizontal run per flight**, **slope length per flight**, and the **total number of landings** required. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition and Scope (1 Mark):**
A ramp is an inclined passage with gradient steeper than 1 in 20 used for circulation and emergency evacuation. In hospital buildings (NBC Group B Institutional), ramps are **mandatory** as lifts cannot be used during fire emergencies.

**Gradient (2 Marks):**
- Maximum permissible gradient: **1 in 12 (8.33%)**
- Gradient must be **uniform** along the entire length
- **No cross-slope** is permitted as it misaligns wheelchairs
- For outdoor ramps exposed to rain, gradient is reduced to **1 in 15**

**Width (1 Mark):**
- **Single wheelchair passage:** 900 mm minimum
- **Stretcher passage:** 1500 mm minimum
- Hospitals typically use 1800 mm for two-way stretcher movement

**Landings (2 Marks):**
- Maximum rise between successive landings: **3.6 m**
- Top and bottom landings: minimum **1200 mm** long
- Intermediate landings: minimum **1500 mm × 1500 mm** for stretcher turning
- Required at **every change of direction** (90° turn)
- Maximum horizontal run before a landing: **9.0 m**

**Handrails (1 Mark):**
- Upper handrail at **900–1000 mm**
- Lower handrail at **760 mm** for children
- Continuous and extended **300 mm** beyond top and bottom

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Vertical rise per floor = 3.6 m
- Total rise (Ground to Second Floor) = 2 floors × 3.6 m = **7.2 m**
- Gradient = 1 in 12
- Clear width = 1500 mm

**Step 1 — Number of flights per floor (1 Mark):**
$$
N_{floor} = \left\lceil \dfrac{3.6}{3.6} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ flight per floor}
$$

**Step 2 — Total flights for two floors (1 Mark):**
$$
N_{total} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ flights}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical rise per flight (1 Mark):**
$$
R_{flight} = \dfrac{3.6}{1} = 3.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Horizontal run per flight (1.5 Marks):**
$$
L_r = \dfrac{R_{flight}}{G} = 3.6 \times 12 = 43.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Slope length per flight (1.5 Marks):**
$$
L_{slope} = \sqrt{(3.6)^2 + (43.2)^2} = \sqrt{12.96 + 1866.24} = \sqrt{1879.20}
$$

$$
L_{slope} = 43.350 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of landings (1 Mark):**
- Total landings = $N_{total} + 1 = 3$
- Intermediate landings = $N_{total} - 1 = 1$

**Final Answer (compilation):**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Total flights | 2 |
| Vertical rise per flight | 3.6 m |
| Horizontal run per flight | 43.2 m |
| Slope length per flight | 43.35 m |
| Total landings | 3 |
| Intermediate landings | 1 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Modified]`

> **Q (B): (a)** Discuss the **role of ramps as a means of egress in fire safety** for a high-rise office building. Compare ramps with staircases and lifts as egress routes, citing at least **three advantages and two limitations** of ramps. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A school building has a **single storey ramp** of total horizontal run **24 m** connecting the playground to the main entrance at a height of **1.8 m** above ground. The ramp is built at a gradient of **1 in 15**. Compute the **slope length of the ramp**, the **clear width required** as per NBC, the **number of intermediate landings** required, and the **length of each landing** if landings are provided at **every 9 m of horizontal run**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Role of Ramps in High-Rise Egress (3 Marks):**
1. **Universal access:** Ramps serve PwDs, elderly, injured, and stretcher-borne occupants who cannot use stairs.
2. **Refuge area connectivity:** In high-rise buildings, ramps connect floors to **refuge areas** (NBC mandates refuge at every 24 m for office occupancy).
3. **Alternative to lift:** During fire, lifts are recalled to ground floor and switched off — ramps are the only powered-free vertical egress for non-ambulatory occupants.
4. **Smoke management synergy:** When enclosed, ramps act as **pressurized smoke-free egress paths**.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Staircase | Lift | Ramp |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Disabled-friendly | ✗ | ✓ (not in fire) | ✓✓ |
| Operates during fire | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Space required | Low | Low | **Very High** |
| Speed of evacuation | Medium | Fast (normal) | Slow |
| Capital cost | Low | High | Medium |

**Three Advantages of Ramps (0.5 × 3 = 1.5 Marks):**
1. Functional during fire without power
2. No mechanical/electrical failure risk
3. Universally accessible to all categories of occupants

**Two Limitations of Ramps (0.5 × 2 = 1 Mark):**
1. Require large horizontal footprint
2. Slower evacuation speed compared to stairs
3. (Acceptable) Gradient fatigue for self-propelled wheelchair users

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Horizontal run = 24 m
- Vertical rise = 1.8 m
- Gradient = 1 in 15

**Step 1 — Verify gradient (1 Mark):**
$$
G_{actual} = \dfrac{1.8}{24} = \dfrac{1}{13.33} = 0.075 = 7.5\%
$$
This is steeper than 1:15 (6.67%), so the gradient is **non-compliant**. The required horizontal run should be $1.8 \times 15 = 27$ m, not 24 m. **(This is a key examiner trap — see warning below.)**

**Step 2 — Slope length (1.5 Marks):**
$$
L_{slope} = \sqrt{(1.8)^2 + (24)^2} = \sqrt{3.24 + 576} = \sqrt{579.24}
$$

$$
L_{slope} = 24.067 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Clear width required (1.5 Marks):**
For a school building (NBC Group B Educational), the minimum clear ramp width is **1500 mm** to allow emergency evacuation of children. *(For single wheelchair passage only, 900 mm would suffice, but schools are classified as assembly-occupancy-institutional, mandating 1500 mm.)*

**Step 4 — Number of intermediate landings (1.5 Marks):**
Horizontal run = 24 m; landings required every 9 m.

$$
N_{intermediate} = \left\lceil \dfrac{24}{9} \right\rceil - 1 = \lceil 2.667 \rceil - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ landings}
$$

(Three segments of 8 m each, separated by two intermediate landings.)

**Step 5 — Length of each landing (1.5 Marks):**
Each intermediate landing length = **1200 mm** (minimum, as per NBC 2016, Cl. 8.4.3).

**Final Summary:**

| Parameter | Computed Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Actual gradient | 1:13.33 (non-compliant) |
| Slope length | 24.067 m |
| Clear width required | 1500 mm |
| Intermediate landings | 2 |
| Landing length | 1200 mm |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] 
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks in 14-mark ramp questions:**
> 
> 1. **Ignoring the ceiling function:** Students often write $N = V / R_{max}$ as a decimal and proceed. Always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and explicitly state the ceiling operation. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 2. **Confusing rise/run with slope length:** Many students compute horizontal run but forget that the **slope length** is the hypotenuse. KTU asks for *slope length* specifically in 7 out of 10 past questions. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting landing counts:** Total landings = $N + 1$, not $N$. Many students write $N$ landings, missing the final one at the upper floor. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 4. **Not verifying gradient compliance:** In Question B(b) above, the given 24 m run at 1.8 m rise is NON-COMPLIANT. Students who compute without flagging the violation lose 1–2 marks. Always verify and **explicitly comment on compliance**.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up width codes:** 900 mm is for *single wheelchair*, 1500 mm is for *stretcher/two-way*. Hospital/assembly occupancy requires 1500 mm. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 6. **Unit errors:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. Convert everything to one unit *before* computing. **(−1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Ramps (PESFT416 Module 1):**
> 
> - [ ] **Ramp definition:** Slope steeper than 1:20 and not steeper than 1:10
> - [ ] **Maximum fire-egress gradient:** **1 in 12 (8.33%)** — the most-tested number
> - [ ] **Minimum width:** 900 mm (single) / 1500 mm (stretcher)
> - [ ] **Maximum rise per flight:** 3.6 m
> - [ ] **Maximum horizontal run between landings:** 9.0 m
> - [ ] **Landing dimensions:** 1200 mm (top/bottom) / 1500 × 1500 mm (intermediate, stretcher turn)
> - [ ] **Handrails:** Upper 900–1000 mm, lower 760 mm, extend 300 mm beyond
> - [ ] **Anti-skid surface:** Coefficient of friction $\mu \geq 0.6$
> - [ ] **Critical formula:** $L_r = R_{flight} / G$ (horizontal run)
> - [ ] **Critical formula:** $L_{slope} = \sqrt{R^2 + L_r^2}$
> - [ ] **Number of flights:** $N = \lceil V / R_{max} \rceil$
> - [ ] **Landings count:** Total = $N + 1$, Intermediate = $N - 1$
> - [ ] **Code reference:** NBC 2016, Part 3, Cl. 8.4 + Part 4, Cl. 6 (hospitals)
> - [ ] **Lifts are NOT permitted** as a means of fire egress
> - [ ] **Refuge areas** (NBC 2016) are typically connected via ramps
> - [ ] **Power backup:** Enclosed fire ramps need emergency lighting at 30 lux minimum
> - [ ] **Signage:** Photo-luminescent exit signage mandatory on every landing
> - [ ] **Door swing:** Fire-rated doors at ramp entry must open in direction of egress
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Lifts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Lift (Fireman's Lift):** A specially designed and constructed lift (elevator) intended exclusively for the use of firefighters and authorized emergency personnel during a fire emergency. It is engineered to operate reliably under severe fire conditions, providing safe vertical transportation between floors when normal lifts have been shut down for safety.

As per the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8 – Section 4)** and the **Tall Building Safety Code (IS 15330)**, a fire lift is **mandatory** in all buildings exceeding **15 meters in height** (or having more than **4 storeys**), and is a critical component of the **Life Safety System (LSS)** under NEP 2020 fire safety education outcomes.

### Key Reference Standards
| Standard Code | Scope of Application |
|---|---|
| **NBC 2016 – Part 8** | National statutory code for fire lifts in India |
| **IS 14665** | Indian Standard – Safety Rules for Lifts |
| **IS 15330** | Code of Practice for Installation of Lifts in Multi-storey Buildings |
| **EN 81-72 (Europe)** | European Standard for Firefighters Lifts |
| **ASME A17.1 (USA)** | Safety Code for Elevators and Escalators – Fire Service Access |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bunker Lift" Analogy** 🛗
>
> Imagine a regular office lift as a **regular car** — fast, smooth, but utterly useless the moment a fire alarm goes off. The car automatically parks itself and refuses to start because, electrically, the building has gone into "panic mode."
>
> Now think of a **Fire Lift** as a **dedicated, armored emergency vehicle** (like a fire brigade truck) that is **always pre-fueled, always backed up by a separate fuel tank (emergency generator)**, parked in a **blast-resistant garage (fire-rated shaft)**, and **only drives when a firefighter with a special key (Fireman's Switch) commands it**. The lift does NOT need the building's normal electrical grid — it has its own independent lifeline.

### Intuitive Takeaway
A fire lift is essentially the **"vertical fire escape highway"** — designed to be operational *when everything else fails*. It bypasses the regular lift system entirely and connects directly to the fire command post.

## 1.3 Critical Distinction from "Ordinary Lifts"

| Parameter | Ordinary Lift | Fire Lift |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Routine passenger transport | **Emergency fire-fighting transport** |
| Operation during fire | Automatically shuts down (Phase I recall only) | **Continues under manual Phase II control** |
| Power source | Building mains (with backup) | **Independent emergency supply** (mandatory) |
| Shaft fire rating | 1 hour (typical) | **Minimum 2 hours (4 hours in high-rise)** |
| Lobby | Not required | **Fire-rated lobby at every landing** |
| Controls | Standard call buttons | **Fireman's Switch + Phase I/II selector** |
| Minimum car size | 1.0 m × 1.3 m | **1.1 m × 2.1 m (stretcher-compatible)** |
| Minimum rated load | 408 kg (6 persons) | **544 kg (8 persons) – NBC clause** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** As per **NBC 2016 Clause 4.10**, every building more than **15 m tall** must have **at least ONE fire lift**, and the number must be **1 per 1000 m² of floor area per floor** in high-rise buildings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy: The "Why" Behind the Design

The fire lift is engineered around a singular principle: **Absolute Reliability Under Fire Conditions (ARUFC)**. This principle breaks down into five operational pillars:

1. **Independence of Power Supply** — The fire lift MUST receive power even when the building's main grid is dead, cut, or on fire.
2. **Independence of Control Logic** — The lift must respond to a *fireman's direct command*, not to random passenger button presses during a panic.
3. **Mechanical Resilience** — Components inside the shaft must withstand **smoke, heat, and water spray** for at least 2 hours.
4. **Spatial Integration** — The lift must be located within a **fire-rated lobby** that acts as a safe haven for evacuation staging.
5. **Strategic Siting** — The lift must be reachable from a **fire vehicle access road** and have a clear route to a **fire command center** (typically on the ground floor).

## 2.2 The Two-Phase Operation Model (NBC 2016 / EN 81-72)

### Phase I — Automatic Recall (Pre-Fireman Arrival)
Triggered the moment a fire alarm is sensed (by smoke detector or sprinkler flow switch in the lift lobby).

- All fire lifts **automatically descend to the designated floor** (usually the **ground floor** or the **fire service access floor**).
- Doors open and **remain open for 30 seconds**.
- The lift **parks with doors open** awaiting firefighter arrival.
- **Passenger call buttons are disabled.**
- This phase is **fully automatic** — no human input required.

### Phase II — Manual Fireman's Service (After Firefighter Arrival)
Activated via a **Fireman's Switch** (key-operated) located inside the car and at the designated floor lobby.

- Firefighter uses a **special key** to take exclusive control.
- The lift operates in **manual hold-to-run mode** — the car moves **ONLY while the button is held pressed**.
- Doors can be opened/closed only by firefighter command.
- Returns to designated floor automatically after a preset time (typically **2 minutes of inactivity**).
- **No automatic recall** during Phase II — firefighter has full override.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Phases?** Phase I buys time and prevents the lift from being called to the fire floor by panicked civilians. Phase II gives the firefighter **total, deterministic control** — they decide when, where, and how fast the lift moves.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Design Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values below are extracted directly from **NBC 2016 (Part 8)**, **IS 14665**, and **Tall Building Safety Code (2018)** — the three most-cited references in KTU examination papers.

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Notes / KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum car internal size (residential) | $L \geq 1.10\,\text{m},\; W \geq 2.10\,\text{m}$ | metres | Stretcher-compatible |
| Minimum car internal size (hospital) | $L \geq 1.50\,\text{m},\; W \geq 2.40\,\text{m}$ | metres | Hospital bed-compatible |
| Minimum rated load (fire lift) | $P_{\text{min}} = 544\,\text{kg}$ (8 persons) | kg | NBC mandatory |
| Shaft fire resistance | $R_{\text{shaft}} \geq 2\,\text{h}$ (high-rise: 4 h) | hours | Non-combustible enclosure |
| Lobby fire resistance | $R_{\text{lobby}} \geq 2\,\text{h}$ | hours | With self-closing fire door |
| Minimum number of fire lifts | $N = \lceil H / 15 \rceil$ (if $H>15$ m) | count | At least 1 mandatory |
| Travel time to top floor (high-rise) | $T \leq 60\,\text{s}$ | seconds | NBC 2016 clause |
| Standby power autonomy | $t_{\text{bat}} \geq 4\,\text{h}$ (battery) | hours | For controller only |
| Generator fuel autonomy | $t_{\text{gen}} \geq 8\,\text{h}$ at full load | hours | For lift motor |
| Door fire rating | $R_{\text{door}} \geq 2\,\text{h}$ | hours | With intumescent seals |
| Lobby ventilation | $A_{\text{vent}} \geq 0.5\,\text{m}^{2}$ per lobby | m² | Cross-ventilation preferred |
| Maximum building height (no fire lift) | $H \leq 15\,\text{m}$ | metres | NBC threshold |
| Stretcher dimensions (Indian standard) | $2.0\,\text{m} \times 0.55\,\text{m}$ | metres | Car must fit 45° tilted stretcher |
| Lift car speed (high-rise) | $v \geq 1.0\,\text{m/s}$ (mid), $1.5$–$3.5\,\text{m/s}$ (tall) | m/s | Per building height |
| Floor-to-floor stopping distance | $d_{\text{travel}} = (n-1) \times h_{\text{floor}}$ | metres | $n$ = number of floors served |

### Real-World Utility

- **High-rise residential towers (Kerala context)**: Mandatory in buildings above 15 m — common in Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut.
- **Hospitals (AIIMS, MCH Trivandrum, KMC Kochi)**: Critical for patient evacuation on stretchers.
- **IT parks (Technopark, Infopark)**: Required by Kerala State Fire & Rescue licensing.
- **Shopping malls and multiplexes**: Essential for firefighter staging and equipment transport.

## 2.4 Why Each Design Feature Exists (The "Engineering Why")

| Design Feature | The "Why" Behind It |
|---|---|
| **Independent emergency power** | Building mains may fail, be cut, or be destroyed by fire |
| **Phase I auto-recall** | Prevents lift from being trapped at fire floor with passengers |
| **Phase II hold-to-run control** | Eliminates false calls; firefighter's hand = car motion |
| **Fire-rated lobby** | Provides safe staging area; prevents fire spread via shaft |
| **Water-resistant components** | Fire hoses may spray into shaft; components must not short-circuit |
| **Stretcher-sized car** | Allows casualty evacuation without bending patient |
| **Fireman's key switch** | Prevents unauthorized use during emergency |
| **Direct fire command center communication** | Coordinated multi-lift deployment during rescue |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of Fire Lifts Required

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A residential building in Kochi has a height of **48 m** and a typical floor plate area of **1200 m²**. Determine the minimum number of fire lifts required per NBC 2016.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Code
The applicable clause is **NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 4, Clause 4.10** which states:
- For buildings $H > 15$ m, **at least one fire lift is mandatory**.
- For high-rise buildings, **one fire lift per 1000 m² of floor area per floor** is required.

### Step 2: Calculate the Number of Floors

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assumed floor-to-floor height} \;& h_f = 3.0\;\text{m} \\
\text{Number of floors} \;& n = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{h_f} \right\rfloor \\
n \;& = \left\lfloor \frac{48}{3.0} \right\rfloor \\
n \;& = 16\;\text{floors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** A standard residential floor in Kerala is typically 3.0 m (slab-to-slab). Dividing the total height by the floor height gives the total number of floors above ground.

### Step 3: Apply the Floor Area Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Floor area per floor} \;& A_f = 1200\;\text{m}^2 \\
\text{NBC area ratio} \;& r = \frac{1\;\text{fire lift}}{1000\;\text{m}^2\;\text{per floor}} \\
\text{NBC required lifts} \;& N_{\text{area}} = \frac{A_f}{1000} \\
N_{\text{area}} \;& = \frac{1200}{1000} \\
N_{\text{area}} \;& = 1.2 \\
\text{Round up (per NBC)} \;& N_{\text{area}} = 2\;\text{fire lifts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** NBC mandates 1 fire lift per 1000 m². Since 1200 m² exceeds 1000 m², we need 2 lifts. We **always round UP** in life safety calculations.

### Step 4: Apply the Height Rule (Cross-check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \;& = 48\;\text{m} \\
\text{Height threshold} \;& H_t = 15\;\text{m} \\
\text{Height ratio} \;& r_H = \frac{H}{H_t} = \frac{48}{15} = 3.2 \\
\text{Height-based lifts} \;& N_H = \lceil r_H \rceil = 4\;\text{fire lifts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** As a secondary rule, one fire lift is needed for every 15 m of building height as a safety margin. This gives 4 lifts. NBC generally takes the **higher of the two values** as the governing requirement.

### Step 5: Final Decision

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{fire lift, final}} \;& = \max(N_{\text{area}},\; N_H) \\
N_{\text{fire lift, final}} \;& = \max(2,\; 4) \\
\boxed{N_{\text{fire lift, final}} \;& = 4\;\text{fire lifts}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The architect must provision space for **4 fire lifts**, even though floor-area alone suggests 2. The height-based rule dominates for tall buildings.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standby Power Sizing for Fire Lift

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A fire lift in a 60 m office tower in Trivandrum has a rated load of **680 kg**, a rated speed of **1.5 m/s**, and a counterweight ratio of **45%**. Calculate the **standby generator capacity** required.

### Step 1: Identify Governing Formula

The motor power requirement for an elevator is given by:

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{(Q + Q_{\text{cw}}) \cdot v \cdot g}{1000 \cdot \eta}
$$

where $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$ and $\eta$ is the overall drivetrain efficiency.

### Step 2: Substitute Counterweight Relation

For a balanced traction lift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cw}} \;& = k \cdot Q \\
\text{where } k \;& = 0.45 \;\text{(counterweight ratio)} \\
Q_{\text{cw}} \;& = 0.45 \times 680 \\
Q_{\text{cw}} \;& = 306\;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Effective Load (Unbalanced Portion)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{eff}} \;& = Q - Q_{\text{cw}} \\
Q_{\text{eff}} \;& = 680 - 306 \\
Q_{\text{eff}} \;& = 374\;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Motor Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{Q_{\text{eff}} \cdot v \cdot g}{1000 \cdot \eta} \\
\text{Assumed efficiency} \;& \eta = 0.80\;\text{(typical geared lift)} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{374 \times 1.5 \times 9.81}{1000 \times 0.80} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{5503.71}{800} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& \approx 6.88\;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The unbalanced weight (374 kg) must be lifted by the motor. Convert to Newtons by multiplying by $g$, then divide by efficiency to account for gearbox and motor losses.

### Step 5: Apply Starting Factor and Diversity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Starting current factor} \;& S_f = 1.5 \\
\text{Standby power required} \;& P_{\text{standby}} = S_f \times P_{\text{motor}} \\
P_{\text{standby}} \;& = 1.5 \times 6.88 \\
\boxed{P_{\text{standby}} \;& \approx 10.32\;\text{kW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Note:** A practical generator sizing would round this to the next available commercial size — typically a **15 kVA diesel generator** with 8-hour fuel autonomy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stretcher Fitment Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Verify whether a fire lift car of size **1.20 m × 2.30 m** can accommodate a standard Indian stretcher of **2.0 m × 0.55 m** in the diagonal orientation.

### Step 1: Calculate Diagonal of Car

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{car}} \;& = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2} \\
D_{\text{car}} \;& = \sqrt{(1.20)^2 + (2.30)^2} \\
D_{\text{car}} \;& = \sqrt{1.44 + 5.29} \\
D_{\text{car}} \;& = \sqrt{6.73} \\
D_{\text{car}} \;& \approx 2.59\;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Diagonal of Stretcher

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{stretch}} \;& = \sqrt{(0.55)^2 + (2.0)^2} \\
D_{\text{stretch}} \;& = \sqrt{0.3025 + 4.0} \\
D_{\text{stretch}} \;& = \sqrt{4.3025} \\
D_{\text{stretch}} \;& \approx 2.07\;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compare

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check: } & D_{\text{car}} \geq D_{\text{stretch}} \\
& 2.59 \;\text{m} \;\geq\; 2.07\;\text{m} \\
\boxed{\text{Result:} \;& \text{STRETCHER FITS — design is COMPLIANT.}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** For a rectangular object to fit inside another rectangle (in any orientation), the diagonal of the inner object must be less than or equal to the diagonal of the outer container. This is the **"stretcher-fitment test"** in NBC 2016.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fire Lift vs. Other Life Safety Lifts

| Feature | Fire Lift | Ambulance Lift (Hospital) | Service Lift | Passenger Lift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary user | Firefighters | Paramedics & patients | Building staff | General public |
| Min. car size | $1.10 \times 2.10$ m | $1.50 \times 2.40$ m | $1.0 \times 1.5$ m | $1.0 \times 1.3$ m |
| Min. load | 544 kg | 1020 kg | 408 kg | 408–680 kg |
| Phase I/II control | **Mandatory** | Optional | Not required | Not required |
| Independent power | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | Optional | Not required |
| Lobby fire rating | 2 h | 2 h | 1 h | 0.5 h (typical) |
| Car finish | Steel/SS, heat-resistant | Anti-bacterial coating | Industrial finish | Decorative |
| Cost index (relative) | 1.5× | 2.0× | 1.0× | 1.0× |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Fire Lift Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalService
    NormalService --> FireSignalDetected : Smoke detector / sprinkler flow
    FireSignalDetected --> Phase1Recall : Auto-trigger
    Phase1Recall --> DesignatedFloor : Lift descends
    DesignatedFloor --> Phase1Parking : Doors open 30s, stay open
    Phase1Parking --> Phase2Service : Fireman key inserted
    Phase2Service --> ManualOperation : Hold-to-run button pressed
    ManualOperation --> FloorSelection : Car moves to target
    FloorSelection --> ManualOperation : Continues service
    FloorSelection --> Phase1Parking : 2 min idle timeout
    Phase2Service --> PowerFail : Mains lost
    PowerFail --> EmergencyPower : DG auto-start < 15s
    EmergencyPower --> Phase2Service : Resumes manual mode
    Phase1Parking --> Reset : All clear signal
    Reset --> NormalService : Auto-reset
    ManualOperation --> EmergencyStop : Manual override key off
    EmergencyStop --> Phase1Parking
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Lift Power & Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mains[Building Mains Power 415V AC] --> ATS[Automatic Transfer Switch ATS]
    DGen[Diesel Generator 15 kVA] --> ATS
    BatteryBank[Battery Bank 4hr Autonomy] --> ControllerUnit[Lift Controller Cabinet]
    ATS --> MotorDrive[Variable Voltage Variable Frequency VVVF Drive]
    MotorDrive --> HoistMotor[Hoist Motor 7.5 kW]
    HoistMotor --> Roping[Traction Roping System]
    Roping --> Car[Fire Lift Car 680kg]
    Roping --> Counterweight[Counterweight 306kg]
    FireAlarmPanel[Fire Alarm Control Panel] --> Phase1Trigger[Phase 1 Recall Signal]
    Phase1Trigger --> ControllerUnit
    FiremanKey[Fireman Key Switch at Lobby] --> Phase2Trigger[Phase 2 Enable Signal]
    Phase2Trigger --> ControllerUnit
    Car --> PositionSensor[Position Encoder and Leveling Sensor]
    PositionSensor --> ControllerUnit
    ControllerUnit --> LobbyComm[Two Way Intercom to Fire Command]
    FireCommandCenter[Fire Command Center] --> LobbyComm
    SmokeDetector[Lobby Smoke Detector] --> FireAlarmPanel
    SprinklerFlowSwitch[Sprinkler Flow Switch] --> FireAlarmPanel
    WaterResistPanel[Water Resistant Electrical Panel IP54] --> MotorDrive
    WaterResistPanel --> ControllerUnit
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Cross-Section of a Fire Lift Shaft (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BuildingCrossSection [Multi Storey Building Cross Section]
        direction TB
        Floor1[Floor 1 Ground] --> Floor2[Floor 2]
        Floor2 --> Floor3[Floor 3]
        Floor3 --> FloorN[Floor N Top]
        subgraph FireLiftShaft [Fire Rated Lift Shaft 2 to 4 hour rating]
            direction TB
            ShaftWall1[Non Combustible Shaft Wall] 
            ShaftWall2[Non Combustible Shaft Wall]
            CarPosition[Fire Lift Car travels here]
            CounterweightTrack[Counterweight travels here]
        end
        subgraph FireLobby [Fire Rated Lobby at each floor]
            direction LR
            LobbyDoor1[Fire Door 2 hour rating]
            LobbyDoor2[Fire Door 2 hour rating]
            LobbyVent[Cross Ventilation 0.5 sqm]
            LobbyFiremanSwitch[Fireman Key Switch]
        end
        FireLobby --> FireLiftShaft
    end
    DesignFloor[Designated Floor Ground] --> FireCommandPost[Fire Command Post nearby]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Fire Lift Commissioning Test Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Commissioning Begins]) --> VisualInspect[Visual Inspection of Shaft and Car]
    VisualInspect --> PowerTest[Power Supply Continuity Test]
    PowerTest --> Phase1Test[Simulate Fire Signal]
    Phase1Test --> RecallCheck{Car Recalls to Ground?}
    RecallCheck -- No --> FailReport[Issue Fail Report]
    FailReport --> Rectify[Rectify and Retest]
    Rectify --> Phase1Test
    RecallCheck -- Yes --> Phase2Test[Insert Fireman Key]
    Phase2Test --> HoldToRunTest{Hold to Run Works?}
    HoldToRunTest -- No --> FailReport
    HoldToRunTest -- Yes --> EmergencyPowerTest[Simulate Mains Failure]
    EmergencyPowerTest --> DGAutoStart{DG Starts within 15s?}
    DGAutoStart -- No --> FailReport
    DGAutoStart -- Yes --> FireRatingTest[Fire Rating Verification 2h]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define a Fire Lift
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fire lift** is a specially designed and constructed lift intended exclusively for the use of firefighters and authorized emergency personnel during a fire emergency. As per **NBC 2016, Part 8, Clause 4.10**, a fire lift must be provided in **all buildings exceeding 15 m in height** (or having more than 4 storeys). It is characterized by:
- Independent emergency power supply **(1 mark)**
- Phase I automatic recall and Phase II manual fireman's service **(1 mark)**
- Fire-rated shaft (minimum **2 hours**) and fire-rated lobby at every landing **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2: State any six design requirements of a fire lift as per NBC 2016.
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six):**

1. Minimum car size of **1.10 m × 2.10 m** to accommodate a standard stretcher. **(0.5 mark)**
2. Minimum rated load of **544 kg (8 persons)**. **(0.5 mark)**
3. Fire-rated shaft with a minimum fire resistance of **2 hours**. **(0.5 mark)**
4. Independent emergency power supply from a **diesel generator with 8-hour fuel autonomy**. **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Fire-rated lobby (2 hours)** at every landing with cross-ventilation. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Fireman's key switch** for Phase II manual operation. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Fire Lift Design and Operational Analysis
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With neat sketches, explain the **Phase I and Phase II operations** of a fire lift. Also state the conditions under which each phase is triggered.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Phase I – Automatic Recall (4 marks):**
- **Trigger condition (1 mark):** Activated automatically upon receipt of a fire signal from a lobby smoke detector, sprinkler flow switch, or fire alarm control panel.
- **Action sequence (2 marks):** 
  - The lift ignores all passenger calls and descends to the **designated floor** (usually ground floor).
  - Doors open and remain open for **30 seconds** to allow any trapped passengers to exit.
  - The lift then **parks with doors open**, awaiting firefighter arrival.
  - All landing call buttons are disabled.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** To remove the lift from the danger zone and prevent it from being called to the fire floor by panicked civilians.

**Phase II – Fireman's Manual Service (3 marks):**
- **Trigger condition (1 mark):** Activated only when a firefighter inserts a **special key** into the fireman's switch located inside the car or at the designated floor lobby.
- **Action sequence (1 mark):**
  - The lift operates in **hold-to-run mode** — the car moves only while the button is held.
  - Firefighter has exclusive control; all landing calls are bypassed.
  - Doors open/close only on explicit command.
- **Auto-exit (1 mark):** The lift automatically returns to Phase I parking if **no button is pressed for 2 minutes**, preventing the car from being stranded at a fire floor.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A commercial high-rise building in Kerala is **72 m tall** with a typical floor plate area of **2400 m²** and floor-to-floor height of **3.0 m**. Calculate the **minimum number of fire lifts required** and verify the **stretcher fitment** for a car of size **1.30 m × 2.40 m**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Number of Floors (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \;& = \frac{H}{h_f} = \frac{72}{3.0} \\
n \;& = 24\;\text{floors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Area-based fire lift count (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{area}} \;& = \left\lceil \frac{A_f}{1000} \right\rceil \\
N_{\text{area}} \;& = \left\lceil \frac{2400}{1000} \right\rceil \\
N_{\text{area}} \;& = \lceil 2.4 \rceil \\
N_{\text{area}} \;& = 3\;\text{fire lifts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Height-based fire lift count (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_H \;& = \left\lceil \frac{H}{15} \right\rceil \\
N_H \;& = \left\lceil \frac{72}{15} \right\rceil \\
N_H \;& = \lceil 4.8 \rceil \\
N_H \;& = 5\;\text{fire lifts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final count (1 mark)**

$$
N_{\text{fire lift}} = \max(3,\; 5) = \mathbf{5\;\text{fire lifts}}
$$

**Step 5: Stretcher fitment verification (1 mark)**

$$
D_{\text{car}} = \sqrt{1.30^2 + 2.40^2} = \sqrt{1.69 + 5.76} = \sqrt{7.45} = 2.73\;\text{m}
$$

$$
D_{\text{stretch}} = \sqrt{0.55^2 + 2.0^2} = \sqrt{0.3025 + 4.0} = 2.07\;\text{m}
$$

Since $2.73\;\text{m} \geq 2.07\;\text{m}$, the stretcher **fits** in diagonal orientation. **DESIGN COMPLIANT.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: Fire Lift Power and Siting Requirements
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the **power supply architecture** for a fire lift. Why is independent emergency power mandatory, and what are the standby autonomy requirements?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A fire lift receives power from **two independent sources**:
1. **Primary:** Building mains (415 V, 3-phase AC) — routed through an **Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)**.
2. **Standby:** Dedicated **diesel generator** sized to lift motor + 1.5× starting factor (typically **15–25 kVA** for a 544–680 kg lift).
3. **Tertiary (controller only):** **Battery bank** with **minimum 4-hour autonomy** to retain memory and control logic.

**Why independent power is mandatory (2 marks):**
- Building mains may fail due to fire, electrical short circuits, or be intentionally cut by fire brigade.
- Water from fire hoses may damage the main distribution board.
- Without independent power, the lift is useless at the moment it is most needed — defeating the very purpose of having a fire lift.

**Standby autonomy requirements (2 marks):**
- **Generator:** Minimum **8 hours** of full-load operation (per NBC 2016).
- **Battery:** Minimum **4 hours** for control circuitry and emergency lighting inside the car.
- **Transfer time:** ATS must switch from mains to generator within **15 seconds** of mains failure.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A fire lift is rated at **680 kg, 1.5 m/s**, with a counterweight ratio of **45%** and drivetrain efficiency of **80%**. Calculate the **motor power** and **recommended standby generator size**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Effective unbalanced load (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cw}} \;& = 0.45 \times 680 = 306\;\text{kg} \\
Q_{\text{eff}} \;& = 680 - 306 = 374\;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Motor power (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{Q_{\text{eff}} \cdot v \cdot g}{1000 \cdot \eta} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{374 \times 1.5 \times 9.81}{1000 \times 0.80} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = \frac{5503.71}{800} \\
P_{\text{motor}} \;& = 6.88\;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Generator sizing with starting factor (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{gen}} \;& = 1.5 \times 6.88 = 10.32\;\text{kW} \\
\text{Rounded to next standard size} \;& = \mathbf{15\;\text{kVA\ diesel\ generator}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** A 15 kVA DG with 8-hour fuel tank and ATS rated for ≤15 s transfer time is appropriate for this fire lift.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** — In fire safety calculations, always round UP the number of lifts, never down. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing "fire lift" with "fire-rated lift"** — A fire-rated lift can be any lift with a fire-resistant shaft, but a *fire lift* is exclusively for firefighters. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Missing the "Phase I" vs "Phase II" distinction** — Most students describe only Phase II. Phase I auto-recall is **equally important** and frequently asked. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting units in calculations** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit. Always write **kg, m, s, kW, hours**.
> 5. **Stating only the formula but not the conclusion** — Always end with a boxed answer and a 1-line engineering interpretation. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Not mentioning NBC 2016 clause numbers** — Examiners expect a code reference. Use **"NBC 2016, Part 8, Clause 4.10"**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fire Lifts (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Specially designed lift for **firefighters only**, operational under fire conditions. **(Must know verbatim)**
- **Code Reference:** **NBC 2016, Part 8, Clause 4.10** (most-cited in KTU).
- **Height Trigger:** **H > 15 m** ⇒ Fire lift mandatory.
- **Minimum Car Size:** **1.10 m × 2.10 m** (stretcher-compatible).
- **Minimum Load:** **544 kg (8 persons)**.
- **Fire Resistance:** Shaft **≥ 2 h**, lobby **≥ 2 h**, door **≥ 2 h** (intumescent seals).
- **Two-Phase Operation:** **Phase I (auto-recall)** + **Phase II (manual hold-to-run)**.
- **Power Supply:** **Independent DG + 4h battery**; ATS transfer **≤ 15 s**; DG autonomy **8 h**.
- **Siting Rules:** Near **fire vehicle access road**, near **fire command post**, in a **fire-rated lobby**.
- **Sizing Formula:** $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{(Q - Q_{\text{cw}}) \cdot v \cdot g}{1000 \cdot \eta}$
- **Generator Sizing:** $P_{\text{gen}} = 1.5 \times P_{\text{motor}}$ (starting factor).
- **Stretcher Test:** Diagonal of car **≥ 2.07 m** (for $0.55 \times 2.0$ m stretcher).
- **Area Rule:** **1 fire lift per 1000 m² per floor** (round UP).
- **Height Rule:** **1 fire lift per 15 m of building height** (round UP).
- **Final Rule:** **Max of area-based and height-based counts** governs.
- **Kerala Context:** Mandatory for high-rises in **Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut, Infopark, Technopark, Smart City**.
- **Equipment Standard:** **IP54 water-resistant** electrical enclosures inside shaft.
- **Communication:** **Two-way intercom** to fire command center mandatory.
- **Auto-Return:** Idle timeout of **2 minutes** in Phase II returns lift to Phase I parking.
- **Test Frequency:** **Monthly Phase I test** + **quarterly full-load Phase II drill** (per IS 14665).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** External Fire Escape Ladders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 External Fire Escape Ladders — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **External Fire Escape Ladder** is a fixed, permanently installed vertical or inclined climbing apparatus affixed to the **exterior face** of a building, intended to provide an **alternative means of egress** for occupants during fire emergencies, smoke conditions, or any situation where the primary internal staircase becomes untenable (impassable, smoke-filled, or structurally compromised).

These ladders are classified under **Means of Egress – Type E (External Escape Routes)** as per **NBC 2016 (Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)** and **IS 1646:1961**. They form a critical component of the **defence-in-depth** life safety strategy in low-rise residential, mercantile, and industrial occupancies where providing a second internal staircase is architecturally infeasible or economically unjustified.

### Taxonomy of External Escape Ladders

| Class | Description | Typical Use |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Type A – Vertical Ladder (Ship's Ladder / Cat Ladder)** | Near-vertical rungs, with or without cage; climb angle 75°–90° | Low-rise, single-storey, industrial sheds |
| **Type B – Inclined Ladder (Sloped Ladder)** | Climb angle 25°–60°, full treads with risers | Mid-rise buildings (up to 4 storeys) |
| **Type C – Step Ladder (Balcony Ladder)** | Short flights connecting landing balconies at each storey | Buildings with intermediate landings |
| **Type D – Retractable / Drop-Down Ladder** | Folded under window sill, deployable in emergency | Hostels, dormitories, hospital wards |

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping**
> This topic directly addresses **CO1 (Module 1):** *"Identify and analyse the various means of escape in buildings during fire emergencies."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-storey building** as a vertical tunnel. When fire breaks out inside, the main staircase — the "highway out" — becomes choked with smoke, heat, and panicked occupants. A regular lift (elevator) is strictly **forbidden** during a fire (it can act as a chimney, drawing flames upwards).

Now picture **external fire escape ladders** as the **emergency fire-truck ladder** that firefighters lean against the building — but here it is **permanently fixed** to the wall, like a **giant metallic climbing frame** that children use in playgrounds, only much sturdier and engineered to bear the load of evacuating adults.

Think of it this way:
- The **building wall** = the cliff face
- The **rungs/treads** = the footholds
- The **landing balconies** = rest platforms, like the steps a mountain climber pauses on
- The **cage/hoop guard** = a safety net, like the rope around a trapeze artist
- The **access window/door** = the door through which you step **OUT of the burning room** and **ONTO** the ladder

> [!TIP] **Mnemonic for KTU Exam**
> **"D-W-M-L-H"** — *Design – Width – Material – Load – Height/Handrail*. These are the five parameters examiners love to test.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following design constants are **universally codified** in Indian Standards and must be memorised for the KTU ESE:

- **Minimum clear width of ladder** = **450 mm** (NBC 2016)
- **Minimum tread depth (rung)** = **125 mm** for inclined; **200 mm** for ship-type
- **Riser height** = **190 mm maximum**
- **Handrail height** = **900 mm to 1000 mm**
- **Cage hoop spacing** = **750 mm maximum** vertical centres (for caged vertical ladders > 3 m height, per IS 3696)
- **Design live load** = **3.0 kN/m²** (residential) to **5.0 kN/m²** (assembly) — IS 875 Part 2
- **Minimum number of parallel stringers** = **2**
- **Landing platform minimum size** = **900 mm × 1200 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind External Ladders

The fundamental engineering rationale for installing an external fire escape ladder rests on **three pillars of life safety design**:

1. **Redundancy** → If the primary staircase fails, the external ladder is the **secondary egress route**.
2. **Compartmentation Adjunct** → External ladders bypass the **internal fire/smoke compartments**, allowing evacuation without entering a fire-loaded zone.
3. **Time Compliance** → The **ASET (Available Safe Egress Time)** must exceed the **RSET (Required Safe Egress Time)**. External ladders, by providing a parallel path, reduce congestion and lower RSET.

> [!NOTE] **The Tenability Triangle for Ladder Use**
> External ladders are usable as long as: (a) wall surface temperature < **100 °C** (otherwise skin burns), (b) ambient CO < **200 ppm**, (c) visibility > **5 m**, and (d) wind speed < **40 km/h** (evacuation panic threshold).

---

## 2.2 Design Logic Flow — Step-by-Step Engineering

### **Step 1: Occupancy Classification & Egress Load Calculation**
Determine the **occupant load (OL)** using:

$$OL = \frac{\text{Floor Area } (m^2)}{\text{Occupant Load Factor } (m^2/\text{person})}$$

| Occupancy (NBC 2016) | OLF (m²/person) |
|---|---|
| Residential (A-2) | 12.5 |
| Educational (B) | 1.5 |
| Institutional (C) | 7.5 |
| Assembly (D) | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Mercantile (F) | 2.0 – 5.0 |
| Industrial (G) | 10.0 |
| Storage (H) | 30.0 |

### **Step 2: Ladder Width Determination**
The aggregate width of all escape routes (including the external ladder) must satisfy:

$$\sum W_{exit} \geq \frac{OL \times Unit \text{ } Width \text{ } Factor}{50}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **NBC 2016 Unit Width**
> A "Unit of Exit Width" = **500 mm**. An external ladder with 450 mm clear width ≈ **1 unit of exit width**, capable of serving up to **50 persons per storey** (or 100 persons for stairs, reduced for ladders).

### **Step 3: Ladder Inclination & Geometry Selection**

The **climb angle (θ)** determines the ladder type:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{\text{Horizontal Run}}\right)$$

- θ ≈ **90°** → Vertical (ship) ladder
- θ ≈ **45°–60°** → Standard inclined ladder
- θ ≈ **25°–45°** → Low-slope ladder (preferred for public occupancies)

### **Step 4: Structural Material Selection**
Common materials and their allowable stresses per IS 800:2007:

$$\sigma_{allowable} = \frac{f_y}{1.1} \quad \text{(for steel members)}$$

| Material | Yield Stress $f_y$ | Allowable $\sigma_{allow}$ | Application |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Mild Steel (IS 2062 Grade A) | 250 MPa | 227 MPa | General external ladders |
| Stainless Steel (SS 304) | 215 MPa | 195 MPa | Coastal / corrosive zones |
| Galvanised Steel | 250 MPa | 227 MPa | Humid climates |
| Aluminium Alloy (6061-T6) | 240 MPa | 218 MPa | Lightweight, mobile ladders |

### **Step 5: Loading & Load Combinations**
Per **IS 875 (Part 2:1987) – Live Loads**, escape ladders must be designed for:

$$w = D.L. + L.L. \quad \text{where} \quad D.L. = \text{Self-weight}, \quad L.L. = 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (residential)}$$

For **concentrated load check** (single occupant with rescue equipment):

$$P = 1.5 \text{ kN at any rung tip} \quad \text{(NBC Part 4, Cl. 4.6.2)}$$

Load combinations per IS 800:2007:

$$1.5 (D.L. + L.L.) \quad \text{or} \quad 0.9 D.L. + 1.5 L.L. \quad \text{(whichever governs)}$$

### **Step 6: Rung/Step Design — Bending & Shear**
Each rung is treated as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the stringer.

Maximum bending moment at the stringer junction:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{2} \quad \text{(for uniformly distributed load on a cantilever)}$$

$$M_{max} = P \cdot L \quad \text{(for point load at tip)}$$

Where $L$ = half the rung length (cantilever) or full span (simply supported twin stringer).

Required section modulus:

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

### **Step 7: Stringer Design (Bending + Axial)**
For inclined stringers, the **axial compression component** is:

$$C = W \cdot \sin\theta$$

And the **bending component** is:

$$M_{stringer} = \frac{w \cdot L_{stringer}^2}{8}$$

Combined stress check (IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.3):

$$\frac{C}{A \cdot \sigma_{ac}} + \frac{C_{y}}{A \cdot \sigma_{bc}} + \frac{M_{y}}{Z_{y} \cdot \sigma_{bc}} \leq 1.0$$

### **Step 8: Fixity, Anchorage & Bolt Design**
Anchor bolts transmit lateral + pull-out loads. Pull-out capacity of an expansion bolt:

$$P_{pull-out} = 0.5 \cdot \tau_{conc} \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot h_{embed}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{conc}$ = bond stress ≈ **1.4 MPa** for M20 grade concrete
- $d$ = bolt diameter
- $h_{embed}$ = embedment depth (typically **100–150 mm**)

### **Step 9: Corrosion Protection**
External ladders are exposed to weather → mandatory protection:

- **Hot-dip galvanisation** → Min coating **85 μm** (IS 2629)
- **Paint system** → 2 coats epoxy primer + polyurethane topcoat (total DFT ≥ **120 μm**)
- **Stainless steel (SS 304/316)** → Preferred for coastal zones

### **Step 10: Cage/Guard Provision (Vertical Ladders > 3 m)**
Per **IS 3696 (Part 1):1991** and **OSHA 1910.27**, vertical ladders exceeding **3 m height** require a **safety cage** with:

- Hoops of **50 × 6 mm MS flat** at **750 mm c/c**
- Vertical straps of **50 × 6 mm flat**, 5 numbers minimum
- Clearance to rungs: **700 mm**
- Bottom hoop flared; offset from wall: **150 mm minimum**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

External fire escape ladders are not theoretical — they are mandated in:

- **Hostels and dormitories** (Kerala state fire safety circular)
- **Old heritage buildings** where internal retrofits are impossible
- **Industrial godowns** (single-storey, large footprint)
- **Hospital isolation wards** (NBC 2016, Group C occupancy)
- **Educational institutions** above 2 storeys in semi-urban regions

The **Kerala State Fire and Rescue Services** explicitly cites **Kerala Municipality Building Rules (KMBR 2019), Rule 67** for escape ladder provisions in buildings up to 4 storeys without a second staircase.

---

|---|---------|---------|------|

| 4 | $w = D.L. + L.L.$ | Total design load | kN/m² |

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

### **Problem Statement**

A 4-storey residential building in Kochi has external fire escape ladders of mild steel (IS 2062 Grade A) connecting the four floor landings. Design data:

- Floor-to-floor height = **3.2 m**
- Ladder clear width = **500 mm**
- Climb angle θ = **60°**
- Rung spacing (rise) = **250 mm**
- Rung length (each side, cantilever) = **250 mm**
- Each rung supports 1.5 kN point load at tip
- Ladder is supported on two stringers of **ISA 50 × 50 × 6 mm** angle sections

**Required:** Check the adequacy of (a) rungs, (b) stringers, and (c) design the anchor bolts.

---

### **Step 1: Material Properties (Mild Steel, IS 2062 Grade A)**

$$f_y = 250 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{yield strength})$$

$$\sigma_{allowable} = \frac{f_y}{1.1} = \frac{250}{1.1} = 227.27 \text{ MPa} \approx 227 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Logic:** IS 800:2007 mandates a factor of safety of 1.1 on yield stress for working stress design of mild steel members in building applications.

---

### **Step 2: Rung Design — Bending Check**

Each rung is a cantilever of length **L = 250 mm = 0.25 m** from the stringer.

Point load at the free tip: $P = 1.5$ kN

Maximum bending moment at the fixed end (junction with stringer):

$$M_{max} = P \times L = 1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375 \text{ kN·m} = 0.375 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Required section modulus:

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{0.375 \times 10^6}{227} = 1651.98 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 1652 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Section provided:** Solid round bar, diameter **d = 22 mm**

Section modulus of a solid circular section:

$$Z_{provided} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 22^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 10648}{32} = \frac{33450.6}{32} = 1045.33 \text{ mm}^3$$

> [!WARNING] **Inadequacy detected!** Section provided is **less than required**. The 22 mm round bar is unsafe.

**Revised Section:** Try **d = 25 mm**

$$Z_{provided} = \frac{\pi \times 25^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 15625}{32} = \frac{49087.4}{32} = 1533.98 \text{ mm}^3$$

Still inadequate. Try **d = 30 mm**:

$$Z_{provided} = \frac{\pi \times 30^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 27000}{32} = \frac{84823}{32} = 2650.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\text{Utilisation Ratio} = \frac{1652}{2650.7} = 0.623 < 1.0 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

> **Logic:** The 30 mm solid round rung has 38% reserve capacity, accounting for dynamic impact during panicked evacuation. Use **30 mm Ø solid round bar** for rungs.

---

### **Step 3: Shear Check on Rung**

Maximum shear at fixed end = applied point load = 1.5 kN = 1500 N.

Shear stress in 30 mm round bar:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3A} = \frac{4 \times 1500}{3 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 30^2} = \frac{6000}{3 \times 706.86} = \frac{6000}{2120.6} = 2.83 \text{ MPa}$$

Allowable shear stress per IS 800:2007:

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.4 \times f_y = 0.4 \times 250 = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{max} (2.83) \ll \tau_{allow} (100) \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE (shear is not critical)}$$

---

### **Step 4: Stringer Design — Axial + Bending**

Stringer length per floor (along slope):

$$L_{stringer} = \frac{\text{Rise per flight}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{3.2 \text{ m}}{\sin 60°} = \frac{3.2}{0.866} = 3.695 \text{ m}$$

**Total load on stringer per metre (along horizontal projection):**

Live load on ladder surface:

$$L.L. = 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Width of ladder: 0.5 m → Load per metre run (horizontal):

$$w_{horiz} = 3.0 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}$$

Load along slope (component perpendicular to stringer):

$$w_{slope} = w_{horiz} \times \cos\theta = 1.5 \times \cos 60° = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 \text{ kN/m}$$

(Alternatively, simply supported on landings at both ends; design span = **3.2 m** effective vertical span)

**Maximum bending moment in stringer (simply supported, UDL on inclined span):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_{horiz} \times L_{horiz}^2}{8} = \frac{1.5 \times 3.2^2}{8} = \frac{1.5 \times 10.24}{8} = \frac{15.36}{8} = 1.92 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 1.92 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Axial Compression Component (along stringer axis):**

$$C = W_{total} \times \sin\theta$$

Total weight supported by stringer = $w_{horiz} \times L_{horiz} = 1.5 \times 3.2 = 4.8$ kN

$$C = 4.8 \times \sin 60° = 4.8 \times 0.866 = 4.157 \text{ kN}$$

**Section Properties of ISA 50 × 50 × 6 mm:**

- Area: $A = 5.68 \text{ cm}^2 = 568 \text{ mm}^2$
- $Z_{xx} = Z_{yy} = 4.62 \text{ cm}^3 = 4620 \text{ mm}^3$
- $r_{xx} = r_{yy} = 1.53 \text{ cm} = 15.3 \text{ mm}$

**Permissible Axial Stress** (assuming effective length $L_e = 0.85 L = 0.85 \times 3695 = 3141$ mm):

Slenderness ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{3141}{15.3} = 205.3$$

For $\lambda > 180$, the column is treated as a long column. Permissible stress per IS 800:2007 (Table 9):

$$\sigma_{ac} = 0.6 \times \frac{f_y}{\lambda^2} \times E \cdot \text{coefficient...}$$

> [!NOTE] **For simplicity in KTU exams, use IS 800 Table 9(c) interpolation:**

For $\lambda = 205$ and $f_y = 250$ MPa:

$$\sigma_{ac} \approx 0.0576 \times f_y \times \text{(reduction factor)}$$

Acceptable simplification used in KTU valuation:

$$\sigma_{ac} \approx \frac{0.6 \times f_y}{\lambda^2} = \frac{0.6 \times 250}{205.3^2} = \frac{150}{42148} = 0.00356 \text{ MPa}$$

This is too low — section may buckle. Hence **lateral bracing** is mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Decision:** The stringer is laterally restrained by rungs at 250 mm centres. Effective length between rungs = 250 mm.

$$\lambda_{effective} = \frac{250}{15.3} = 16.3 \quad \text{(very low, treated as short column)}$$

$$\sigma_{ac} = 0.6 \times f_y = 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(short column, axial load + bending)}$$

**Combined Stress Check (IS 800 Cl. 9.3.2):**

$$\frac{C}{A \cdot \sigma_{ac}} + \frac{M}{Z \cdot \sigma_{bc}} \leq 1.0$$

$$\frac{4157}{568 \times 150} + \frac{1.92 \times 10^6}{4620 \times 227}$$

$$= \frac{4157}{85200} + \frac{1920000}{1048740}$$

$$= 0.0488 + 1.8310 = 1.880$$

> [!WARNING] **Section is OVERSTRESSED! (Ratio = 1.88 > 1.0)**

**Revised Section: Use ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm**

- Area: $A = 11.46 \text{ cm}^2 = 1146 \text{ mm}^2$
- $Z_{xx} = Z_{yy} = 12.10 \text{ cm}^3 = 12100 \text{ mm}^3$
- $r_{min} = 2.30 \text{ cm} = 23.0 \text{ mm}$

$$\lambda_{eff} = \frac{250}{23.0} = 10.87 \quad \text{(short column)}$$

$$\sigma_{ac} = 150 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{bc} = 0.66 \times f_y = 165 \text{ MPa}$$

**Check:**

$$\frac{4157}{1146 \times 150} + \frac{1.92 \times 10^6}{12100 \times 165}$$

$$= \frac{4157}{171900} + \frac{1920000}{1996500}$$

$$= 0.0242 + 0.9617 = 0.9859 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

> **Logic:** The stringer ratio of 0.99 means the section is just adequate. Recommended: use **ISA 80 × 80 × 8 mm** for 5% reserve, or use channel section [ISMC 75] for higher stiffness.

---

### **Step 5: Anchor Bolt Design**

Each stringer is anchored at top and bottom landings. Lateral load per bolt = shear from stringer = $V = w \times L/2 = 1.5 \times 3.2/2 = 2.4$ kN. Pull-out load = half the total weight = $4.157/2 = 2.08$ kN.

Total tension per anchor = 2.4 + 2.08 = **4.48 kN** (worst case).

**Anchor bolt specification:** M16 expansion type (IS 2062 bolt), $d = 16$ mm.

Embedment depth: $h_e = 100$ mm in M20 concrete.

Bond stress (pull-out): $\tau_{conc} = 1.4$ MPa.

Pull-out capacity:

$$P_{pull-out} = 0.5 \times \tau_{conc} \times \pi \times d \times h_e = 0.5 \times 1.4 \times \pi \times 16 \times 100$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.4 \times 3.1416 \times 16 \times 100 = 0.5 \times 7037.16 = 3518.6 \text{ N} = 3.52 \text{ kN}$$

Insufficient. Try **$h_e = 130$ mm:**

$$P_{pull-out} = 0.5 \times 1.4 \times \pi \times 16 \times 130 = 4574.9 \text{ N} = 4.57 \text{ kN}$$

$$4.57 \text{ kN} > 4.48 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

> **Final specification:** M16 anchors, 130 mm embedment, in M20 concrete.

---
