# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**"Define an external fire escape ladder. List its four main types as per NBC 2016."**

**Model Answer:**

> An **External Fire Escape Ladder** is a permanently fixed climbing assembly installed on the **exterior face** of a building, serving as an **alternative means of egress** during fire emergencies when the primary internal staircase becomes unsafe due to smoke, heat, or structural compromise. It is governed by **NBC 2016 (Part 4)** and **IS 1646:1961**.
>
> **Four Main Types:**
> 1. **Vertical (Ship's) Ladder** – near-vertical climb (75°–90°), used for industrial sheds.
> 2. **Inclined Ladder** – climb angle 25°–60°, standard for public/residential buildings.
> 3. **Step Ladder (Balcony Ladder)** – short flights connecting landing balconies.
> 4. **Retractable / Drop-Down Ladder** – folds under window, deployable in emergency.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"State the minimum design live load, clear width, and handrail height for an external fire escape ladder as per IS 875 and NBC 2016."**

**Model Answer:**

> As per **IS 875 (Part 2):1987** and **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**:
> - **Minimum design live load** = **3.0 kN/m²** (residential) to **5.0 kN/m²** (assembly occupancies).
> - **Minimum clear width** = **450 mm** (≈ 1 unit of exit width = 500 mm standard).
> - **Handrail height** = **900 mm to 1000 mm** above the walking surface.
>
> Additionally, a point load of **1.5 kN** must be considered at any rung tip per NBC 4.6.2.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

> [!IMPORTANT] **ESE Module Pattern:** Students answer **one full question (14 marks)** with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. **Both Q1 and Q2 are compulsory; OR pattern applies within each.**

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) [Compulsory — KTU 2024 Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **functional components** of an external fire escape ladder. Discuss the **role of landing platforms, handrails, and safety cages** in ensuring safe evacuation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-storey school building (Educational Occupancy, NBC Group B) is to be provided with an external fire escape ladder of mild steel. Floor-to-floor height = 3.0 m; ladder clear width = 500 mm; climb angle = 50°. Design the **rung section** assuming rungs are **250 mm cantilever** from the stringer, with a point load of **1.5 kN at tip**. Adopt IS 2062 Grade A steel. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Functional Components – Explanation:**

The **functional components** of an external fire escape ladder system include:

1. **Stringers (Main Inclined Members)** → Two parallel steel angles (ISA) or channels (ISMC) running along the slope, transferring the entire load to landing platforms. They resist both **bending** (from UDL) and **axial compression** (from self-weight + live load component along slope).

2. **Rungs / Treads (Stepping Members)** → Horizontal bars connecting the two stringers at 250 mm vertical centres. For inclined ladders, rungs are flat bars or solid rounds. For vertical ladders, rungs are round bars (25–32 mm Ø) welded to stringers. They act as **cantilevers** carrying individual occupant loads.

3. **Landing Platforms / Balconies** → Intermediate rest platforms at each floor level, minimum size **900 mm × 1200 mm**, providing transition from the building's external door/window to the ladder. They prevent fatigue and allow rescue operations.

4. **Handrails and Guard Rails** → Continuous rails on both sides at **900–1000 mm** height, terminating in a closed loop to prevent clothing snagging. They offer psychophysical confidence to evacuees.

5. **Safety Cage (for Vertical Ladders > 3 m)** → Hoop-and-strap arrangement of 50 × 6 mm MS flat, hoops at **750 mm c/c** (max), extending from 2.2 m above floor to top of ladder. Prevents backward fall during climbing.

6. **Anchor Bolts and Cleats** → M16/M20 bolts with **100–150 mm** embedment in concrete wall, transferring lateral and tensile loads to the building structure.

7. **Access Window / Fire Door** → Self-closing fire-rated door/window at each floor, opening onto the landing balcony.

8. **Corrosion Protection System** → Hot-dip galvanisation (85 µm) + epoxy primer + PU topcoat for coastal/humid climates.

> **Sketches (in exam):** Draw plan view of ladder, elevation showing stringer + rungs + cage, and detail of anchor connection.

**(b) Design of Rung:**

Given: $L_{cantilever} = 250$ mm = 0.25 m; $P = 1.5$ kN; Steel IS 2062 Grade A ($f_y = 250$ MPa).

Allowable bending stress:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{f_y}{1.1} = \frac{250}{1.1} = 227.27 \text{ MPa}$$

Maximum bending moment at fixed end (cantilever with point load at tip):

$$M_{max} = P \times L = 1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375 \text{ kN·m} = 0.375 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Required section modulus:

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{0.375 \times 10^6}{227.27} = 1650.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Try 25 mm Ø solid round bar:**

$$Z_{provided} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 25^3}{32} = \frac{49087.4}{32} = 1534.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

Check: $1534 < 1650$ → **Not safe.**

**Try 28 mm Ø solid round bar:**

$$Z_{provided} = \frac{\pi \times 28^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 21952}{32} = \frac{68971.5}{32} = 2155.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

Check: $2155 > 1650$ → **SAFE** (Utilization = 0.766 = 76.6%).

**Adopt 28 mm Ø solid round bar as rung**, welded to the stringer with 5 mm fillet weld on both sides, length 50 mm.

**Deflection check (optional but good practice):**

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} = \frac{1500 \times 250^3}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times \frac{\pi \times 28^4}{64}}$$

$$= \frac{1500 \times 1.5625 \times 10^7}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 60412.4} = \frac{2.344 \times 10^{10}}{3.6247 \times 10^{10}} = 0.647 \text{ mm}$$

Allowable deflection = L/150 = 250/150 = 1.67 mm. Hence 0.65 mm < 1.67 mm → **SAFE.**

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) [OR Alternative — KTU 2024 Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Indian Standard code provisions** governing the design of external fire escape ladders, citing **NBC 2016, IS 1646, IS 875, IS 800, and IS 3696**. Mention key clauses related to width, loading, cage, and corrosion protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **schematic flow diagram**, explain the **egress process** through an external fire escape ladder during a fire emergency. Estimate the **evacuation time** for 60 persons using a ladder of 500 mm width, assuming an evacuation flow rate of 40 persons/min per unit exit width. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Code Provisions:**

| Standard | Relevant Clause | Provision |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **NBC 2016 Part 4** | Cl. 4.6.1 | Minimum ladder width 450 mm; 1 unit exit width = 500 mm |
| **NBC 2016 Part 4** | Cl. 4.6.2 | Point load of 1.5 kN at rung tip; live load 3.0–5.0 kN/m² |
| **IS 1646:1961** | Cl. 6.4 | Mandatory external escape route for buildings without second staircase |
| **IS 875 (Part 2):1987** | Table 2 | Live load classification; 3.0 kN/m² (residential), 5.0 kN/m² (assembly) |
| **IS 800:2007** | Cl. 9.3 | Combined axial + bending stress check; allowable stress = $f_y/1.1$ |
| **IS 3696 (Part 1):1991** | Cl. 5 | Safety cage for vertical ladders > 3 m; hoops at 750 mm c/c max |
| **IS 3696 (Part 2):1991** | Cl. 4 | Inclined ladders: climb angle 25°–60°, handrail 900–1000 mm |
| **IS 2629:1985** | Cl. 5 | Hot-dip galvanising thickness 85 µm minimum |
| **IS 2062:2011** | Grade A | Mild steel with $f_y$ = 250 MPa for ladder members |
| **OSHA 1910.27** | (Intl. ref.) | Cage clearance 700 mm; extension 1.07 m above landing |

**(b) Egress Flow Diagram + Evacuation Time:**

**Egress Process Flow (textual schematic):**

1. **Fire Alarm Activation** → Sends alert to all floors.
2. **Occupant Decision Time** → Avg. 30–60 seconds (assumed 45 s).
3. **Movement to Access Window** → Internal horizontal travel, ~1 min.
4. **Step onto Landing Balcony** → Open self-closing fire door.
5. **Ladder Descent** → Vertical/inclined movement at ~0.5 m/s.
6. **Passing through Intermediate Landings** → Rest at each floor.
7. **Ground Discharge** → Step onto safe assembly area.

**Evacuation Time Calculation:**

Given: $N = 60$ persons; Ladder width = 500 mm = **1 unit of exit width**; Flow rate = 40 persons/min/unit width.

Effective flow on ladder (reduction factor for vertical descent):

$$F_{eff} = 0.8 \times 40 = 32 \text{ persons/min}$$

**Time to evacuate all 60 persons through 1 unit width:**

$$T_{evac} = \frac{N}{F_{eff}} = \frac{60}{32} = 1.875 \text{ min} \approx 113 \text{ seconds}$$

**Plus descent time** (3 storeys × 3 m × 0.5 m/s = 18 seconds):

$$T_{total} = 113 + 18 = 131 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.18 \text{ min}$$

**Compare with ASET** (Assume fire detection + smoke logging time = 5 min = 300 s):

$$ASET = 300 \text{ s} > RSET = 131 \text{ s} \Rightarrow \text{LIFE SAFETY OBJECTIVE SATISFIED} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Costing 2–3 Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dynamic impact factor** — Occupants do not climb statically. Use 1.5× the calculated stress for dynamic loading, or adopt 1.5 kN point load per NBC.
> 2. **Confusing allowable stress factor** — KTU expects $\frac{f_y}{1.1} = 227$ MPa for mild steel, **NOT** $\frac{f_y}{1.5} = 167$ MPa (which is the older IS 800:1984 value).
> 3. **Ignoring axial force in inclined stringers** — $W \sin\theta$ is a *compression* component. Combined stress ratio formula must be used.
> 4. **Sketch omissions** — Examiners award 2 marks for **labelled diagrams**. Always show: stringer, rung, weld, anchor, cage, handrail, dimensioning of width and rise.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** — Use N/mm² (= MPa) for stress, kN for forces, mm for sections. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Omitting corrosion protection** — Kerala's humid coastal climate makes this mandatory. Always specify galvanising.
> 7. **Wrong OLF used** — Educational = 1.5 m²/person, NOT 12.5 (residential). Recheck NBC 2016 Table 3.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** External fire escape ladder = fixed exterior climbing assembly for emergency egress.
- **Types (4):** Vertical, Inclined, Step (Balcony), Retractable.
- **Minimum width:** 450 mm (NBC); 500 mm preferred.
- **Climb angle:** Vertical = 75°–90°; Inclined = 25°–60°.
- **Live load:** 3.0 kN/m² (residential) to 5.0 kN/m² (assembly).
- **Point load:** 1.5 kN at rung tip (NBC 4.6.2).
- **Handrail height:** 900–1000 mm.
- **Cage hoops:** 750 mm c/c, 50 × 6 mm MS flat (for ladders > 3 m).
- **Steel grade:** IS 2062 Grade A ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 227$ MPa).
- **Anchor bolts:** M16/M20, 100–150 mm embedment in M20 concrete.
- **Corrosion:** Hot-dip galvanising 85 µm + paint system.
- **Code stack:** NBC 2016 (Part 4) → IS 1646 → IS 875 Pt 2 → IS 800 → IS 3696 → IS 2629.
- **Key equations to memorise:**
  - $\sigma_{allow} = f_y / 1.1$
  - $M_{max} = P \times L$ (cantilever rung)
  - $Z_{req} = M_{max}/\sigma_{allow}$
  - Combined stress ratio $\leq 1.0$ (IS 800 Cl. 9.3)
  - $T_{evac} = N / F_{eff}$
- **Life safety check:** ASET > RSET must always hold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning of Location and Calculation of Capacity, Number and Width of Exit as per NBC for Different Occupancy Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planning of Location and Calculation of Capacity, Number and Width of Exit as per NBC for Different Occupancy Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (As per NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)

An **Exit** is defined as a continuous, unobstructed means of egress from any point in a building or structure to a public way, comprising three distinct components:
1. **Exit Access** – The portion of the means of egress that leads to an entrance to an exit.
2. **Exit Discharge** – The portion of the means of egress between the termination of the exit and a public way.
3. **Exit** – That portion of a means of egress which is separated from other interior spaces of a building or structure by fire-rated construction.

**Occupant Load (OL)** is the total number of persons that may occupy a building or portion thereof at any one time, calculated based on the floor area and the **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)** specified for the occupancy classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per NBC 2016 (Part 4, Clause 4.4.1), every building must be provided with exits of sufficient **number, capacity, and width**, located at **strategic positions** to permit **prompt escape** of occupants in case of fire or other emergencies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **movie theatre with 1000 seats and only one narrow door** — if a fire breaks out, people will crush each other trying to escape. The door becomes a *bottleneck* and the entire building becomes a *death trap*.

Now think of the **same theatre with 8 wide doors**, placed all around the building, with lit-up exit signs. People can flow out quickly in multiple directions. This is exactly what **exit planning** does — it ensures that the *capacity* (how many people can leave per minute), *number* (how many escape routes), *width* (size of each route), and *location* (where they are placed) are all *proportional to the crowd the building holds*.

In technical terms: **"A safe building is one where occupants can fully evacuate before the fire overwhelms them."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core NBC Principle:** The total *width* of all exits must be sufficient to evacuate the *occupant load* in the time it takes for the fire to reach critical untenable conditions (typically **2.5 to 3 minutes** for non-sprinklered buildings).

### 1.3 NBC Occupancy Classification (Group-Wise)

As per **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Table 1)**, buildings are classified into 10 major occupancy groups:

| Group | Occupancy Type | Examples | Risk Profile |
|-------|----------------|----------|--------------|
| **A** | **Residential** | Apartments, Hostels, Hotels | Sleeping occupants – High Risk |
| **B** | **Educational** | Schools, Colleges, Coaching Centers | Children, Fixed Seating |
| **C** | **Institutional** | Hospitals, Nursing Homes, Jails | Mobility-impaired occupants |
| **D** | **Assembly** | Theatres, Auditoriums, Stadiums | High Density, Panic-prone |
| **E** | **Business** | Offices, Banks, IT Parks | Day-time only, Alert occupants |
| **F** | **Mercantile** | Shops, Malls, Departmental Stores | Mixed, High public density |
| **G** | **Industrial** | Factories, Workshops, Power Plants | Workers + Processes |
| **H** | **Storage** | Warehouses, Godowns, Cold Storage | Low occupancy, High fire load |
| **I** | **Hazardous** | Oil Terminals, Chemical Plants | Explosion risk, Toxic gases |
| **J** | **Mixed Use** | Multi-purpose buildings | Multiple Group rules apply |

### 1.4 Key NBC Terminology

- **Unit of Exit Width (UEW):** Standardized clear width of **500 mm** (per NBC 2016) used to calculate the number of people that can pass through a single exit.
- **Travel Distance:** The actual distance an occupant must travel from the most remote point in the occupied space to the nearest exit, measured along the natural path of egress.
- **Dead-End Corridor:** A corridor or passage that has an exit outlet at one end only — limited strictly by NBC.
- **Common Path of Travel:** The portion of the exit access that must be traversed before two or more separate paths to exits become available.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Design Parameters for Exit Planning

The four pillars of exit design (per NBC 2016, Part 4) are:

1. **Capacity of Exit** — determined by `Width / Unit Exit Width Factor`
2. **Number of Exits** — based on `Occupant Load` and `Travel Distance`
3. **Width of Exit** — based on `Occupant Load × Width per Person`
4. **Location of Exit** — based on `Travel Distance` and `Dead-End Limits`

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam critical** and tested repeatedly in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Occupant Load (OL)** | $OL = \dfrac{A}{OLF}$ | persons | $A$ = Floor area; $OLF$ = Occupant Load Factor |
| 2 | **Total Exit Width (TEW)** | $TEW = OL \times W_p$ | mm | $W_p$ = Width per person (varies with occupancy) |
| 3 | **Number of Units of Exit Width** | $n_{UEW} = \dfrac{TEW}{UEW_{std}}$ | units | $UEW_{std} = 500\ mm$ per unit |
| 4 | **Capacity per Exit** | $C_{exit} = \dfrac{W_{exit}}{UEW_{std}} \times Cap_{unit}$ | persons | $Cap_{unit}$ = persons per unit width |
| 5 | **Number of Exits** | $N \geq \dfrac{OL}{OL_{per\_exit}}$ | integers | From NBC Table 4 (occupancy-wise) |
| 6 | **Stairway Width** | $W_{stair} = N_{UEW} \times 500\ mm$ | mm | Handrails within 100 mm of each side |
| 7 | **Travel Distance** | $d_{travel} \leq d_{max,occupancy}$ | meters | Limits set by NBC Table 6 |
| 8 | **Door Swing Direction** | Doors swing **in the direction of egress** | – | Mandatory for OL > 50 |

---

### 2.3 Occupant Load Factors (OLF) for Different Occupancies

> [!NOTE]
> **OLF = Net Floor Area (m²) per Person.** Use **net** area (excluding walls, staircases, toilets).

| Occupancy Group | OLF (m²/person) | Travel Distance (m) Unsprinklered | Travel Distance (m) Sprinklered |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A – Residential** | 12.5 | 22.5 | 30.0 |
| **B – Educational** | 1.5 – 2.0 | 22.5 | 30.0 |
| **C – Institutional** | 8.0 – 15.0 | 22.5 | 30.0 |
| **D – Assembly (concentrated)** | 0.5 (fixed seats) | 30.0 | 45.0 |
| **D – Assembly (unconcentrated)** | 1.5 | 30.0 | 45.0 |
| **E – Business** | 10.0 | 30.0 | 45.0 |
| **F – Mercantile (basement)** | 3.0 | 20.0 | 30.0 |
| **F – Mercantile (ground)** | 6.0 | 30.0 | 45.0 |
| **G – Industrial** | 10.0 | 30.0 | 60.0 |
| **H – Storage** | 30.0 | 30.0 | 60.0 |
| **I – Hazardous** | As per process | 22.5 | 30.0 |

---

### 2.4 Capacity per Unit Exit Width (Persons Passing per Minute)

| Occupancy Group | Persons per UEW (Stairway) | Persons per UEW (Level/Passage) | Persons per UEW (Door) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A – Residential | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| B – Educational | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| C – Institutional | 30 | 40 | 50 |
| D – Assembly | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| E – Business | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| F – Mercantile | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| G – Industrial | 50 | 60 | 90 |
| H – Storage | 30 | 40 | 50 |
| I – Hazardous | 30 | 40 | 50 |

---

### 2.5 Why These Numbers Matter (Engineering Rationale)

- **Stairway bottleneck:** Stairways are the slowest egress route. Hence, fewer people per unit width pass through them compared to level exits.
- **Doors are fastest:** Doors at the discharge point allow rapid flow, hence the highest per-unit capacity.
- **Hazardous / Storage** occupancies have lower flow rates because occupants are typically *alert, mobile, and can leave quickly*, but the exit design must allow for the additional emergency response time due to fire propagation.
- **The 500 mm unit of exit width** is a standard human-shoulder clearance plus slight buffer for emergency clothing (uniforms, coats).

### 2.6 Number of Exits Rule (Per NBC Clause 4.4.3)

| Occupant Load | Minimum Number of Exits |
|:---:|:---:|
| $\leq 50$ | 1 (with limitations) |
| 51 – 300 | **2** |
| 301 – 500 | **3** |
| $> 500$ | **4** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Exit Rule:** If the occupant load exceeds 50, **at least 2 independent exits** are mandatory, and they must be **remotely located** (at least **1/3 of the maximum diagonal dimension** of the area served) to prevent both exits being blocked by a single fire.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Exit Width Requirements

#### Step 1: Determine the Occupant Load
The total number of persons expected to occupy a space is calculated using:

$$
OL = \frac{A_{net}}{OLF}
$$

where:
- $A_{net}$ = Net floor area of the space (excluding walls, sanitary, staircase landings)
- $OLF$ = Occupant Load Factor (m²/person) — taken from NBC Table 3 for that occupancy

**Logic:** NBC assumes a minimum area each person needs for *comfortable standing/sitting/moving*. A classroom needs only 1.5 m²/person (close seating), a warehouse can accommodate 30 m²/person (sparse storage).

---

#### Step 2: Compute the Total Required Width
Once the occupant load is known, the total combined clear width of all exits is:

$$
TEW = OL \times W_p
$$

where $W_p$ is the **width per person** factor from NBC Table 4 (typically **10 mm per person for stairs** and **6.5 mm per person for level exits** in residential/business).

---

#### Step 3: Convert Width to Units of Exit Width
Because the standard unit of exit width is fixed at **500 mm** per NBC, the number of units required is:

$$
n_{UEW} = \frac{TEW}{500\ mm}
$$

The actual physical stairway width is then:

$$
W_{stair} = n_{UEW} \times 500\ mm
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rounding Rule:** Always round **up** (never down) to the next integer unit of exit width. A fractional unit always rounds **UP** because under-design can be fatal.

---

#### Step 4: Validate Travel Distance Constraint
The calculated number of exits must be distributed such that **every** occupant can reach **at least one** exit within the maximum travel distance $d_{max}$ specified for that occupancy.

$$
d_{travel,actual} \leq d_{max,occupancy}
$$

If the constraint is violated, the designer must either:
- Add an additional exit
- Reconfigure the layout to shorten the path
- Provide automatic sprinkler protection (extends $d_{max}$ by 1.33×)

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Exits for a Multistorey Office

**Problem Statement:**
A **5-storey office building** has each floor with a net usable area of **1000 m²**. Determine:
(a) Occupant load per floor
(b) Total exit width required per floor
(c) Number and width of staircases required
(d) Whether 2 staircases are sufficient for a 45 m travel distance limit.

**Given:**
- Occupancy = Business (Group E)
- $OLF = 10\ m^2/person$ (per NBC for Group E)
- Width per person = 10 mm (stairs) for Group E
- $UEW_{std} = 500\ mm$
- Building is **unsprinklered**

---

#### Solution (a): Occupant Load per Floor

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL_{per\_floor} &= \frac{A_{net}}{OLF} \\
&= \frac{1000\ m^2}{10\ m^2/person} \\
&= 100\ persons\ /\ floor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each person in a business occupancy requires a minimum of 10 m² of net floor area, including space for desks, circulation, and ventilation.

---

#### Solution (b): Total Exit Width per Floor

$$
\begin{aligned}
TEW &= OL \times W_p \\
&= 100\ persons \times 10\ mm/person \\
&= 1000\ mm = 1.0\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 100 mm is required for each occupant to descend the staircase in a controlled flow (approximately 6.5 seconds per person per unit width).

---

#### Solution (c): Number and Width of Staircases

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{UEW} &= \frac{TEW}{UEW_{std}} \\
&= \frac{1000\ mm}{500\ mm} \\
&= 2.0\ units
\end{aligned}
$$

**Staircase width required:**

$$
W_{stair} = 2.0 \times 500\ mm = 1000\ mm = 1.0\ m
$$

Since occupant load is 100 (between 50 and 300), **2 staircases are mandatory**. Therefore:

$$
W_{per\_stair} = \frac{1000\ mm}{2} = 500\ mm
$$

But wait — each staircase has a *capacity* based on its width and the flow rate. Per NBC for Group E (Business): **50 persons per unit width per minute** on stairways.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cap_{one\_stair} &= 1\ unit \times 50\ persons/min \\
&= 50\ persons\ /\ min
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** 2 staircases × 50 persons = 100 persons capacity. This **exactly matches** the occupant load of 100. ✓

---

#### Solution (d): Travel Distance Check

Since $OL = 100 < 300$, **2 exits are sufficient in number** (per NBC). The staircases must be **remotely located** such that:
- Distance between two exits $\geq \frac{L_{max}}{3}$ where $L_{max}$ is the maximum diagonal of the floor plate.
- Travel distance from **any point on the floor to the nearest exit** $\leq 30\ m$ (unsprinklered business occupancy).

**If travel distance > 30 m** → Either add a 3rd staircase or provide sprinklers (extends limit to 45 m).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Capacity per Unit Exit Width by Occupancy

| Occupancy | Stair (persons/min) | Level (persons/min) | Door (persons/min) | Travel (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A (Residential) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 22.5 – 30 |
| B (Educational) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 22.5 – 30 |
| C (Institutional) | 30 | 40 | 50 | 22.5 – 30 |
| D (Assembly) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 30 – 45 |
| E (Business) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 30 – 45 |
| F (Mercantile) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 20 – 45 |
| G (Industrial) | 50 | 60 | 90 | 30 – 60 |
| H (Storage) | 30 | 40 | 50 | 30 – 60 |
| I (Hazardous) | 30 | 40 | 50 | 22.5 – 30 |

---

### 3.4 General Analytical Framework (Master Equation Block)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Given:} \quad & A_{net},\ OLF,\ W_p,\ UEW_{std} \\
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & OL = \frac{A_{net}}{OLF} \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & TEW = OL \times W_p \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & n_{UEW} = \left\lceil \frac{TEW}{UEW_{std}} \right\rceil \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & N_{exits} = \max\left(2,\ \left\lceil \frac{OL}{300} \right\rceil + 1\right) \\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & W_{per\_exit} = \frac{n_{UEW} \times 500\ mm}{N_{exits}} \\
\textbf{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Verify: } d_{travel,actual} \leq d_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Exit Planning Decision Flow (NBC-Compliant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Building Floor Plan] --> B[Identify Occupancy Group per NBC]
    B --> C[Determine Net Floor Area A_net]
    C --> D[Select Occupant Load Factor OLF from Table]
    D --> E["Compute OL = A_net / OLF"]
    E --> F{OL greater than 50?}
    F -->|No| G[1 Exit Permitted with Travel Distance check]
    F -->|Yes| H{OL between 51 to 300?}
    H -->|Yes| I1[2 Exits Minimum]
    H -->|No| J{OL between 301 to 500?}
    J -->|Yes| I2[3 Exits Minimum]
    J -->|No| K[4 Exits Minimum for OL > 500]
    I1 --> L[Compute Total Exit Width TEW = OL times W_p]
    I2 --> L
    K --> L
    G --> L
    L --> M[Convert TEW to Units of UEW = TEW / 500 mm]
    M --> N[Round Up to Next Integer Unit]
    N --> O[Distribute Width Across N Exits]
    O --> P[Verify Travel Distance less than d_max]
    P --> Q{Distance OK?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Final Design Approved per NBC]
    Q -->|No| S[Add Exit OR Provide Sprinklers]
    S --> P
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Exit System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E1[Exit System Components]
        A1[Occupancy Identification] --> A2[Occupant Load Calculation]
        A2 --> A3[Travel Distance Mapping]
        A3 --> A4[Exit Type Selection]
    end
    subgraph E2[Exit Types per NBC]
        B1[Stairway Type 1]
        B2[Ramp Type 2]
        B3[Horizontal Exit Type 3]
        B4[Door Type 4 to Public Way]
    end
    subgraph E3[Verification Layer]
        C1[Width Compliance Check]
        C2[Capacity Compliance Check]
        C3[Travel Distance Compliance Check]
        C4[Fire Resistance Rating Check]
    end
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    A4 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1[NBC Approved Safe Egress System]
```

### 4.3 Exit Location Strategy: Remote Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    floor1[Floor Plate Dimension L by B]
    floor1 --> exit1[Exit 1: Corner A]
    floor1 --> exit2[Exit 2: Corner B]
    floor1 --> exit3[Exit 3: Corner C]
    floor1 --> exit4[Exit 4: Corner D]
    exit1 -.->|"Min Separation: L/3 or B/3"| exit2
    exit2 -.->|"Min Separation: L/3 or B/3"| exit3
    exit3 -.->|"Min Separation: L/3 or B/3"| exit4
    exit1 -.->|"Anti-Coincidence Rule"| exit4
    travel1[Travel Path from Most Remote Point] -->|must be less than d_max| exit1
    travel2[Travel Path from Second Remote Point] -->|must be less than d_max| exit2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Define the term "Unit of Exit Width" as per NBC. What is its standard value?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Unit of Exit Width (UEW)** is the standardized clear width of an exit (stair, passage, or door) used to calculate the capacity of an egress route in terms of persons served. Per **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Clause 4.4.1)**, the standard value of one unit of exit width is **500 mm**. Each unit is designed to permit a specific number of occupants to pass per minute (e.g., 50 persons/min on stairs, 60 persons/min on level passages, and 90 persons/min through doors for residential/business occupancies). The total width of an exit is always rounded up to the next integer multiple of 500 mm.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **List the NBC occupancy groups and state the Occupant Load Factor for "Assembly" occupancy.**

**Model Answer:**
As per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Table 1**, the major occupancy groups are: **A – Residential, B – Educational, C – Institutional, D – Assembly, E – Business, F – Mercantile, G – Industrial, H – Storage, I – Hazardous, and J – Mixed Use.**

For **Group D (Assembly)**:
- **Concentrated use (fixed seating, e.g., theatres):** OLF = **0.5 m²/person** (without fixed seats) or based on **actual seat count** (with fixed seats).
- **Unconcentrated use (e.g., museums, art galleries):** OLF = **1.5 m²/person**.
- For standing space (e.g., concerts, sports viewing): OLF = **0.5 m²/person**.

The choice depends on the **type of assembly activity** and seating arrangement as classified in NBC Table 3.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
> **(a)** Explain the NBC classification of building occupancies with at least 6 examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shopping mall (Group F – Mercantile) has a total net floor area of 2400 m² on the ground floor. The owner proposes to provide 2 exit staircases of 1.5 m width each, located on the same side of the building. As a fire safety consultant, evaluate whether the proposal complies with NBC. If not, suggest corrective measures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a): NBC Occupancy Classification

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 2)** classifies all buildings into 10 major occupancy groups based on the **purpose of use, fire load, occupancy characteristics, and occupant behaviour**. They are:

1. **Group A – Residential:** Apartments, hostels, hotels, dormitories, boarding houses.
2. **Group B – Educational:** Schools, colleges, universities, coaching centres, nurseries.
3. **Group C – Institutional:** Hospitals, sanatoria, custodial homes, mental asylums, jails.
4. **Group D – Assembly:** Theatres, cinemas, auditoriums, stadiums, marriage halls, places of worship.
5. **Group E – Business:** Offices, banks, IT parks, libraries (non-public), government buildings.
6. **Group F – Mercantile:** Shops, departmental stores, supermarkets, shopping malls, restaurants.
7. **Group G – Industrial:** Manufacturing plants, workshops, power stations, assembly plants.
8. **Group H – Storage:** Warehouses, cold storage, godowns, parking garages.
9. **Group I – Hazardous:** Oil refineries, chemical factories, explosives manufacturing, fireworks storage.
10. **Group J – Mixed Use:** Buildings housing two or more distinct occupancies (e.g., shopping-cum-residential).

**Reasoning behind classification:** Each group has unique fire load, occupant density, mobility profile, and time-of-occupancy characteristics, which dictate different safety requirements.

#### Part (b): Evaluation of Shopping Mall Exit Proposal

**Given:**
- Net floor area $A_{net} = 2400\ m^2$
- Occupancy = Mercantile (Group F), Ground Floor
- Per NBC: $OLF = 6.0\ m^2/person$ for ground floor mercantile
- Proposed: 2 exits × 1.5 m = 3000 mm = 6.0 units of UEW

**Step 1: Compute Occupant Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OL &= \frac{A_{net}}{OLF} \\
&= \frac{2400\ m^2}{6.0\ m^2/person} \\
&= 400\ persons
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Determine Required Number of Exits**

Since $OL = 400 > 300$, per NBC, **minimum 3 exits** are required. The proposal of 2 exits **fails on the count criterion**.

**Step 3: Compute Required Total Exit Width**

For Group F (Mercantile) on stairs: $W_p = 10\ mm/person$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TEW &= OL \times W_p \\
&= 400 \times 10\ mm \\
&= 4000\ mm = 4.0\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Required Number of UEW Units**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{UEW} &= \frac{TEW}{500\ mm} \\
&= \frac{4000}{500} \\
&= 8.0\ units
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Distribute Across 3 Exits (Minimum Required)**

$$
W_{per\_exit} = \frac{4000\ mm}{3} = 1333\ mm \approx 1350\ mm\ (rounded\ up)
$$

**Step 6: Check Remote Location Criterion**

The proposal of **both exits on the same side** violates the **remote-location rule**. Per NBC, two exits must be separated by at least **1/3 of the maximum diagonal** of the floor area. For 2400 m², assuming a rectangular plan of 60 m × 40 m:

$$
d_{diagonal} = \sqrt{60^2 + 40^2} = 72.1\ m
$$

$$
d_{min,separation} = \frac{72.1}{3} = 24.0\ m
$$

Two exits on the same side are **inadequate** — they could be simultaneously blocked by a single fire.

**Corrective Measures:**

> 1. **Increase exits to 3**, distributed at the 3 corners of the building.
> 2. **Increase exit width** to at least **1.35 m per exit** (or 2 UEW each).
> 3. **Provide additional travel-distance compliance** — at least one exit within 30 m of any point.
> 4. Consider **automatic sprinkler installation** to extend travel distance to 45 m (if applicable).
> 5. Mark all exits with **photo-luminescent signage** and provide **emergency lighting** per NBC Part 8.

**Verdict: Proposal is non-compliant. It must be revised.**

---

> **Q3B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
> **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the requirements for **location and number of exits** in a building as per NBC. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hospital ward (Group C – Institutional) has a net floor area of 800 m² and is located on the 3rd floor of a building. Calculate the minimum number of exits, total exit width, and the width of each stairway, assuming 2 staircases serve the floor. Verify if your design meets the travel distance requirement of 22.5 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a): Requirements for Location and Number of Exits

Per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.3**, the location and number of exits must satisfy:

1. **Number of Exits (based on Occupant Load):**
   - $OL \leq 50$: 1 exit (with restrictions)
   - $51 \leq OL \leq 300$: 2 exits (minimum)
   - $301 \leq OL \leq 500$: 3 exits
   - $OL > 500$: 4 exits minimum

2. **Remote Location (Anti-Coincidence Rule):**
   - Where 2 or more exits are required, they must be placed **at remote locations** such that the distance between them is **at least 1/3 of the maximum diagonal** of the area served.
   - This prevents a single fire from blocking all exits.

3. **Travel Distance Constraint:**
   - The travel distance from the **most remote point** in any occupied space to the **nearest exit** must not exceed the **maximum travel distance** specified for the occupancy.
   - Sprinklered buildings are allowed a 1.33× extension.

4. **Common Path of Travel:**
   - The common path before the occupant has a choice of exits must be **limited** (typically 6 m for unsprinklered, 9 m for sprinklered for residential).

5. **Exit Discharge:**
   - All exits must lead **directly to a public way** or through a fire-rated exit passageway.

6. **Door Swing:**
   - Doors must swing **in the direction of egress** when serving an occupant load of more than 50.
   - Exit doors must be openable from the inside **without a key or special tool**.

#### Part (b): Hospital Ward Exit Design

**Given:**
- Occupancy = Institutional (Group C)
- $A_{net} = 800\ m^2$
- $OLF$ for Group C (hospitals with movable beds) = **8.0 m²/person**
- $W_p$ for Group C on stairs = **15 mm/person** (lower flow rate)
- 2 staircases proposed
- Maximum travel distance = **22.5 m** (unsprinklered)

**Step 1: Occupant Load**

$$
OL = \frac{800\ m^2}{8.0\ m^2/person} = 100\ persons
$$

**Step 2: Number of Exits Required**

$OL = 100 \Rightarrow$ **Minimum 2 exits** are required. (Since $50 < OL \leq 300$.) The proposal of 2 staircases is **acceptable in number**.

**Step 3: Total Required Exit Width**

$$
TEW = 100\ persons \times 15\ mm/person = 1500\ mm
$$

**Step 4: Number of UEW Units**

$$
n_{UEW} = \frac{1500\ mm}{500\ mm} = 3.0\ units
$$

**Step 5: Width per Staircase**

$$
W_{per\_stair} = \frac{1500\ mm}{2\ exits} = 750\ mm \approx 1.5\ units \approx 750\ mm
$$

But since exit width must be a **whole multiple of UEW** = 500 mm, we round up:

$$
W_{per\_stair} = 2 \times 500\ mm = 1000\ mm = 1.0\ m\ (minimum)
$$

(750 mm is non-standard; 1000 mm is the next standard unit.)

**Step 6: Capacity Verification**

For Group C, stair capacity = 30 persons/min per unit width.

$$
Cap_{one\_stair} = 2\ units \times 30\ persons/min = 60\ persons/min
$$

$$
Cap_{total} = 2 \times 60 = 120\ persons/min \geq OL = 100\ persons \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7: Travel Distance Check**

The actual layout must ensure that **every** patient bed is within **22.5 m** of one of the two staircases. If the ward is rectangular (say 40 m × 20 m) with stairs at opposite ends:

- Diagonal = $\sqrt{40^2 + 20^2} = 44.7\ m$
- Minimum separation = $44.7/3 = 14.9\ m$ (satisfied if stairs are at opposite ends)
- Travel distance from most remote bed to nearest stair = $\sqrt{20^2 + 10^2} = 22.4\ m$ (just within limit ✓)

**Conclusion:** The design of **2 staircases of 1.0 m width each** satisfies NBC for the hospital ward. However, additional **horizontal exit** provisions are recommended for *defend-in-place* strategy in hospitals.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Using GROSS area instead of NET area** for occupant load calculation → 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up** fractional units of exit width to the next integer UEW → Design becomes under-sized.
> 3. **Ignoring travel distance verification** → KTU examiners expect *all three* checks: number, width, AND travel distance.
> 4. **Confusing OLF for ground vs. basement** (e.g., Mercantile: 6 m²/person on ground, 3 m²/person in basement).
> 5. **Stair capacity = 50 persons/min for most occupancies BUT 30 for Institutional, Storage, Hazardous** — students often write 50 for all.
> 6. **Forgetting the "remote location" rule** for multiple exits — must mention the 1/3 diagonal separation.
> 7. **For Group D Assembly**, the OLF depends on whether seating is fixed or not (0.5 vs 1.5 m²/person) — generic answer loses marks.
> 8. **Door swing direction** is often missed in exit design answers; this is a 1-mark item in KTU exams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE (Module 1):**

- **10 Occupancy Groups** (A–J) of NBC 2016 with characteristic examples.
- **Unit of Exit Width = 500 mm** (standard, fixed value).
- **Occupant Load (OL)** = Net Area ÷ OLF.
- **Total Exit Width (TEW)** = OL × Width per Person.
- **Minimum 2 exits** for $OL > 50$; **3 for OL 301–500**; **4 for OL > 500**.
- **Remote location rule:** Exits separated by **at least 1/3 of maximum diagonal** of the area served.
- **Door must swing in direction of egress** when OL > 50.
- **Travel distance** limits: 22.5 m (Residential/Educational), 30 m (Business/Assembly unsprinklered), 45 m (sprinklered extension).
- **Stairway flow rates:** 50 persons/min/unit (most occupancies); **30 persons/min/unit** (Institutional, Storage, Hazardous).
- **Level passage flow rates:** 60 persons/min/unit (most); 40 for slow occupancies.
- **Door flow rates:** 90 persons/min/unit (most); 50 for slow occupancies.
- **Width per person on stairs** ≈ 10 mm for fast-mobility occupancies; 15–20 mm for slow.
- **Sprinklers increase travel distance** by factor 1.33× and may reduce OLF penalties.
- **Net Area (A_net)** = Gross Area – Walls – Sanitary – Staircase Landings – Lift Shafts.
- **Always round up** the number of UEW units.
- **Horizontal exits** are counted as separate exits but allow *defend-in-place* strategy (especially for hospitals).
- **Group I (Hazardous)** occupancies have the most stringent requirements and shortest travel distances.
- **Mixed Use (Group J)** must satisfy the requirements of the **most restrictive** occupancy present.
- **For basement occupancies**, the OLF is generally **half** that of the corresponding above-ground occupancy.
- **Every exit must discharge** to a public way, courtyard, or exit passageway leading to the same.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — PESFT416 Life Safety in Building Fire*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus aligned with NBC 2016 (Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production of Smoke Particles, Constituents of Smoke, Quantity and Rate of Production of Smoke, Quality of Smoke, Smoke Density, Visibility in Smoke, Smoke Movement in Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fire Smoke?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Smoke** is the airborne solid and liquid particulates (aerosols) and evolved gases emitted when a material undergoes combustion or pyrolysis. In the context of **Life Safety in Building Fire**, smoke is treated as a *buoyant, optically active, toxic, and thermal fluid* whose production, transport, and accumulation determine the tenability of escape routes.

In KTU's PESFT416 framework, smoke is not just a visual nuisance — it is the **primary cause of fire fatalities** (often 60–80 % of deaths are smoke-related, with victims succumbing before flames reach them). Therefore, the entire Module 2 is built around answering: *How much smoke? How fast? How dense? How visible? How does it move?*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smoke as a Fluid" Mental Model

Imagine a hot-air balloon released in a cold room: the heated air (now laden with soot particles) wants to *rise* because it is **less dense** than the surrounding cool air. Smoke behaves identically:

| Phenomenon | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Buoyancy rise** | Hot air balloon lifting off |
| **Layering at ceiling** | Oil floating on water (lighter fluid over denser fluid) |
| **Spreading horizontally under ceiling** | A spilled drink flowing downhill — smoke flows "down the pressure gradient" |
| **Spillover from doorways** | Water spilling over the brim of a filled glass |
| **Smoke obscuration** | Fog reducing car headlights' visibility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A KTU examiner will expect you to treat smoke as a *gas-phase combustion product* whose behaviour is governed by **fluid mechanics (buoyancy, pressure, mass conservation)** — NOT as a solid particulate to be filtered.

### 1.3 The Three "Faces" of Smoke — KTU's Tripartite Framework

For examination purposes, Module 2 evaluates smoke along three dimensions:

1. **GENERATION** — *Production of smoke particles* & *Constituents of smoke* (What comes out?)
2. **CHARACTERIZATION** — *Quantity/Rate of production*, *Quality*, *Density*, *Visibility* (How much, how bad?)
3. **TRANSPORT** — *Smoke movement in buildings* (Where does it go?)

Each of these is addressed in the sections that follow.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics You Must Memorize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants appear repeatedly in KTU numericals and must be memorized:
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Density of air at NTP:** $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Specific heat of air:** $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - **Absolute zero (engineering):** $T_{abs} = 273.15 \text{ K}$
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma_{sb} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4)$
> - **Typical smoke temperature in a compartment fire:** $T_s \approx 573 \text{ K}$ to $1273 \text{ K}$ (**300 °C to 1000 °C**)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production of Smoke Particles

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Smoke Particle Formation

Smoke particles (soot) are produced through **incomplete combustion** or **pyrolysis** of carbonaceous fuels. The formation pathway is:

1. **Fuel Pyrolysis:** Solid/liquid fuel decomposes thermally into volatile hydrocarbon vapours.
2. **Pyrolysis → Soot Nucleation:** Volatiles crack into smaller, unsaturated hydrocarbons (acetylene, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons / PAHs).
3. **Particle Coagulation:** PAH clusters grow via coagulation to form spherical **primary soot particles** (~20–50 nm).
4. **Aggregation:** Primary particles agglomerate into **chain-like aggregates** (~0.1–10 µm).
5. **Oxidation (partial):** Some soot is consumed by available O₂; surviving soot is emitted as smoke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?**
> Soot particles are *optically active* — they absorb and scatter visible light, which is the physical basis of **smoke obscuration** (visibility loss).

### 2.1.2 Factors Influencing Smoke Particle Production

| Factor | Effect on Smoke Production |
|---|---|
| **Fuel chemistry** (C:H ratio) | Higher C:H → more soot (e.g., benzene smoulders; methane flames are nearly soot-free) |
| **Ventilation (O₂ supply)** | Oxygen-lean (under-ventilated) combustion → MORE soot |
| **Temperature** | Higher T → faster pyrolysis but also more oxidation (competing effects) |
| **Mixing/turbulence** | Better mixing → more complete combustion → LESS soot |
| **Fuel geometry** (surface/volume) | High surface-area fuels (foams, fabrics) → rapid smoke generation |

## 2.2 Constituents of Smoke

Smoke is a **multi-phase aerosol**. Its constituents fall into three categories:

### 2.2.1 Particulate Phase (Solid + Liquid)

- **Soot (carbonaceous particles)** — sub-micron to micron-sized agglomerates
- **Droplets of condensed water vapour** (white/grey smoke of smouldering fires)
- **Tar and condensed hydrocarbons** (heavy smoke from plastics, oils)
- **Ash particles** (inorganic residues)

### 2.2.2 Gaseous Phase (Toxic & Asphyxiant Gases)

> [!WARNING]
> The gaseous products of combustion are the *lethal* component of smoke. KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of these.

| Gas | Symbol | Source | Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon monoxide** | CO | Incomplete combustion of C-containing fuels | **Most lethal**; binds haemoglobin (carboxyhaemoglobin) → asphyxiation |
| **Carbon dioxide** | CO₂ | Complete combustion | Asphyxiant at high conc.; also accelerates breathing rate, increasing CO intake |
| **Hydrogen cyanide** | HCN | Combustion of nitrogenous polymers (wool, silk, polyurethane, ABS) | Cytotoxic; ~50 ppm incapacitates |
| **Hydrogen chloride** | HCl | Combustion of PVC, fluoropolymers | Pulmonary irritant |
| **Nitrogen oxides** | NO, NO₂ | High-T combustion in air | Respiratory irritant |
| **Sulphur dioxide** | SO₂ | Combustion of S-containing materials (rubber) | Respiratory irritant |
| **Ammonia** | NH₃ | Nitrogenous fuels | Irritant |
| **Phosgene** | COCl₂ | Decomposition of chlorinated solvents | Delayed-effect pulmonary oedema |
| **Acrolein** | C₃H₄O | Combustion of cellulosic materials | Severe sensory/pulmonary irritant |

### 2.2.3 Energy Phase (Thermal Radiation)

Smoke carries **thermal energy** as sensible heat and radiated energy. Heated smoke is the principal agent of **flashover** and **remote ignition** in building fires.

## 2.3 Quantity and Rate of Production of Smoke

### 2.3.1 Definition of Smoke Production Rate

**Smoke Production Rate (ṁₛ)** is the mass of smoke particulates generated per unit time during combustion, expressed in **kg/s** or **g/s**.

For a steady fire:

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_f \cdot Y_s
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass loss rate of fuel (kg/s)
- $Y_s$ = **soot yield** (kg smoke / kg fuel burned) — a fuel-specific parameter

### 2.3.2 Typical Soot Yield Values (Memorize)

| Fuel | Soot Yield $Y_s$ (kg/kg) |
|---|---|
| **Methane (CH₄)** | 0.001 – 0.005 (almost no soot) |
| **Ethanol** | 0.008 |
| **Wood (cellulosic)** | 0.015 – 0.025 |
| **Polystyrene** | 0.06 |
| **Polyurethane foam** | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| **PVC** | 0.10 – 0.16 |
| **Naphthalene** | 0.20 |
| **Toluene** | 0.18 |
| **Diesel fuel** | 0.08 – 0.12 |

### 2.3.3 The Convective Smoke Release Rate

For smoke movement calculations, what matters is not just particulate mass but the **total volumetric flow of hot smoke** leaving the fire compartment. The convective heat release rate $\dot{Q}_c$ drives this flow:

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{c_p \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)}
$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}_c$ = convective heat release rate (kW)
- $T_s$ = smoke temperature (K)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient temperature (K)

### 2.3.4 Volumetric Smoke Production Rate

$$
\dot{V}_s = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s} = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{c_p \cdot \rho_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)}
$$

This is the quantity that determines how fast smoke fills a space.

## 2.4 Quality of Smoke

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality of smoke** refers to its **optical, chemical, and physiological characteristics** that affect life safety. It is a qualitative descriptor combining:
> 1. **Obscuration power** (visibility reduction)
> 2. **Toxicity** (lethal/incapacitating potential)
> 3. **Corrosivity / irritancy** (eye & respiratory damage)
> 4. **Heat content** (thermal hazard)

### 2.4.1 Smoke Toxicity Indexes

- **LC₅₀** — concentration lethal to 50 % of test animals (used in material testing)
- **FED (Fractional Effective Dose)** — accumulated toxic dose over exposure time
- **ISO 13344** — standard for toxic hazard assessment
- **NES 713** — UK Defence Standard for combustion toxicity index

### 2.4.2 Smoke Quality — "Black" vs "White" Smoke

| Smoke Type | Appearance | Typical Source | Implications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Black/Dark Smoke** | Soot-rich | Under-ventilated, flaming combustion of hydrocarbons | High obscuration, high CO |
| **White/Grey Smoke** | Water vapour, condensed droplets | Smouldering or pyrolysis | Lower obscuration but CO/CO₂ still present |
| **Yellow/Greenish Smoke** | HCl, NO₂, acid vapours | PVC, fluoropolymers, nitrogen-rich fuels | Highly corrosive & irritant |

## 2.5 Smoke Density

### 2.5.1 Definition

**Smoke density** is the measure of how much smoke mass is contained per unit volume of the smoke-air mixture.

$$
\rho_s = \frac{m_{particles}}{V_{smoke}} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]
$$

### 2.5.2 Optical Density (D) — The Engineering Metric

Engineers more commonly quantify smoke by **Optical Density** because that is what determines visibility. Optical density is defined as:

$$
D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity

### 2.5.3 Specific Extinction Area (σ) — The Material Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\sigma$ is the single most important material property in smoke obscuration calculations.

$$
\sigma = \frac{D \cdot A}{m} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{kg}]
$$

where:
- $D$ = optical density (dimensionless)
- $A$ = exposed cross-sectional area of smoke (m²)
- $m$ = mass of fuel burned (kg)

Typical $\sigma$ values:

| Material | $\sigma$ (m²/kg) |
|---|---|
| Methanol | 130 |
| PMMA (acrylic) | 130 |
| Wood | 50 – 200 |
| Polystyrene | 1100 |
| Polyurethane foam | 1100 – 2200 |
| PVC | 1500 – 2400 |
| Nylon | 250 |

### 2.5.4 Mass Optical Density (Dₘ) — ISO 5659 Standard

$$
D_m = \frac{V}{\ell \cdot m} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

where:
- $V$ = smoke chamber volume (m³)
- $\ell$ = light path length (m)
- $m$ = mass of fuel sample (kg)

## 2.6 Visibility in Smoke

### 2.6.1 The Visibility Equation (Highest Yield KTU Formula)

$$
V = \frac{C}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s}
$$

or equivalently using the extinction coefficient:

$$
V = \frac{C}{K_{ext}}
$$

where:
- $V$ = visibility (m)
- $\sigma$ = specific extinction area (m²/kg)
- $\rho_s$ = smoke density (kg/m³)
- $K_{ext}$ = extinction coefficient (1/m) = $\sigma \cdot \rho_s$
- $C$ = a **dimensionless constant** depending on the sign type:

| Sign/Observation Type | Constant $C$ |
|---|---|
| **Light-emitting sign** (illuminated EXIT) | 5 – 10 |
| **Light-reflecting sign** (passive reflective EXIT) | 3 – 5 (typically 3) |
| **Critical for safe evacuation (default)** | 8 (often used as design benchmark) |

### 2.6.2 Numerical Example Format for KTU

> **Example KTU Problem Statement:**
> "Calculate the visibility through smoke given $\sigma = 1500 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$, $\rho_s = 0.0006 \text{ kg/m}^3$, for an illuminated EXIT sign."

**Solution:**

$$
V = \frac{C}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{8}{1500 \times 0.0006} = \frac{8}{0.9} \approx 8.89 \text{ m}
$$

This is less than the typical 10 m or 30 m required for safe evacuation → **TENABILITY FAILURE**.

### 2.6.3 Rule-of-Thumb Design Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For design of means of egress under smoke conditions:**
> Maintain $\sigma \cdot \rho_s \leq 0.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$ at occupant eye level (1.5–1.8 m above floor) for visibility ≥ 16 m.

This is the *visibility criterion* used in many performance-based fire codes (NFPA 101, BS 7974).

## 2.7 Smoke Movement in Buildings

### 2.7.1 Driving Forces for Smoke Movement

Smoke moves through buildings due to **three primary driving forces**:

1. **Buoyancy (Stack Effect)** — Hot smoke is less dense than cool air; it rises.
2. **Wind (External Pressure)** — Wind on building façades creates pressure differentials.
3. **HVAC-induced Pressures** — Mechanical ventilation systems.

### 2.7.2 Buoyancy-Driven Flow Through Openings

The mass flow rate of smoke through a vertical opening (door/window) of width $W$ and height $H$ is given by the **Heskestad / Klote model**:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left(\frac{T_\infty}{T_s} - 1\right)^{1/2} \cdot \left(\frac{H - h_n}{H}\right)^{3/2} \cdot H^{3/2}
$$

A simplified, more exam-friendly form:

$$
\dot{m} = 0.5 \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_s \cdot \rho_\infty} \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot H \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s}}
$$

where:
- $A$ = area of opening (m²)
- $\rho_s$ = density of smoke (kg/m³)
- $\rho_\infty$ = ambient density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $H$ = height of opening (m)
- $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$ (K)

### 2.7.3 The Neutral Plane Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When hot smoke flows out of an upper opening and cool air enters through a lower opening, there is a horizontal plane at which the inside and outside pressures are equal — this is the **Neutral Plane (h_n)**.

**Pressure balance at the neutral plane:**

$$
h_n = \frac{H}{1 + \left(\frac{T_\infty \cdot A_{in}^2}{T_s \cdot A_{out}^2}\right)^{1/3}}
$$

For a single opening (door), smoke leaves through the **upper portion** (above $h_n$) and air enters through the **lower portion** (below $h_n$).

**Mass flow rates (Heselden equations):**

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = \frac{2}{3} C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_s}} \cdot (h_{top} - h_n)^{3/2}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \frac{2}{3} C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_\infty}} \cdot (h_n - h_{bottom})^{3/2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the asymmetric temperature terms ($\sqrt{T_s}$ for outflow vs. $\sqrt{T_\infty}$ for inflow). This asymmetry is a favourite KTU examiner trick.

### 2.7.4 The Thomas / NFPA Simplified Buoyancy Formula

A simplified form widely used in fire engineering:

$$
\dot{Q}_{flow} = 1260 \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{H \cdot \Delta T} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

where this expression converts to a smoke mass flow:

$$
\dot{m} \approx 0.072 \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{H \cdot \Delta T} \quad [\text{kg/s}]
$$

### 2.7.5 Stack Effect in Vertical Shafts

In tall buildings, the **chimney effect** drives smoke upward through vertical shafts (stairs, elevator hoistways, gaps in walls). The driving pressure difference across height $H$ is:

$$
\Delta P = \rho_\infty \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} \quad [\text{Pa}]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Stack effect is the dominant smoke transport mechanism in high-rise buildings. KTU frequently frames this as the cause of "smoke logging" of staircases.

### 2.7.6 Wind Effects on Smoke Movement

$$
\Delta P_w = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_w \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot v^2
$$

where $C_w$ is the wind pressure coefficient (typically -0.8 to +0.8 depending on façade orientation).

### 2.7.7 Smoke Layering and the Ceiling Jet

When smoke impinges on a ceiling, it spreads radially as a **ceiling jet**. The ceiling jet velocity at radius $r$ from the plume axis:

$$
u(r) = A \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}}{r^{1/3}}
$$

This is the basis for **smoke detector activation** calculations.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for your KTU ESE. Memorize the equation forms and units, not the variable names verbatim.

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Smoke mass production rate | $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_f \cdot Y_s$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Convective smoke mass flow | $\dot{m}_s = \dot{Q}_c / [c_p(T_s - T_\infty)]$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Optical density | $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Specific extinction area | $\sigma = D \cdot A / m$ | m²/kg |
| 5 | Visibility in smoke | $V = C / (\sigma \cdot \rho_s)$ | m |
| 6 | Buoyancy flow (out) | $\dot{m}_{out} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d W \rho_\infty \sqrt{g \Delta T / T_s} (h_t - h_n)^{3/2}$ | kg/s |
| 7 | Buoyancy flow (in) | $\dot{m}_{in} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d W \rho_\infty \sqrt{g \Delta T / T_\infty} (h_n - h_b)^{3/2}$ | kg/s |
| 8 | Neutral plane height | $h_n = H / [1 + (T_\infty A_{in}^2 / T_s A_{out}^2)^{1/3}]$ | m |
| 9 | Simplified buoyancy flow | $\dot{m} \approx 0.072 A \sqrt{H \Delta T}$ | kg/s |
| 10 | Stack effect pressure | $\Delta P = \rho_\infty g H \Delta T / T_s$ | Pa |
| 11 | Wind-induced pressure | $\Delta P_w = 0.5 C_w \rho_\infty v^2$ | Pa |
| 12 | Smoke temperature (above fire) | $T_s = T_\infty + \dot{Q}_c / (\dot{m} c_p)$ | K |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Neutral Plane Position (Detailed Step-by-Step)

This is a **frequently-asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE. Master every line.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a single vertical door opening of height $H$ in a wall separating a fire compartment (hot side, $T_s$) from ambient (cold side, $T_\infty$). Smoke is at pressure $P_s$ (inside), air at $P_\infty$ (outside). The neutral plane is at height $h_n$ from the floor.

### 3.1.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Variation

Inside the compartment, pressure varies with height $y$ as:

$$
P_s(y) = P_{s,0} - \rho_s \cdot g \cdot y
$$

Outside, similarly:

$$
P_\infty(y) = P_{\infty,0} - \rho_\infty \cdot g \cdot y
$$

### 3.1.3 Pressure Difference Across the Opening

The net pressure difference driving flow through the opening at any height $y$:

$$
\Delta P(y) = P_\infty(y) - P_s(y) = (P_{\infty,0} - P_{s,0}) - (\rho_\infty - \rho_s) \cdot g \cdot y
$$

### 3.1.4 Define Neutral Plane Condition

At $y = h_n$, $\Delta P = 0$ (no driving pressure):

$$
(P_{\infty,0} - P_{s,0}) = (\rho_\infty - \rho_s) \cdot g \cdot h_n
$$

### 3.1.5 Bernoulli / Orifice Equation for Local Velocity

At any height $y$:

$$
v(y) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \vert \Delta P(y) \vert}{\rho}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Mass Flow Rate Through Inflow and Outflow Portions

Integrating over the inflow portion (from $y = 0$ to $y = h_n$) with $\rho = \rho_\infty$:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = C_d \int_0^{h_n} \rho_\infty \cdot W \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 (\rho_\infty - \rho_s) g (h_n - y)}{\rho_\infty}} \, dy
$$

Let $u = h_n - y$, $du = -dy$:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{2 g (\rho_\infty - \rho_s) / \rho_\infty} \int_0^{h_n} \sqrt{u} \, du
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g (\rho_\infty - \rho_s)}{\rho_\infty}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} h_n^{3/2}
$$

### 3.1.7 Similarly for Outflow Portion (Above Neutral Plane)

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g (\rho_\infty - \rho_s)}{\rho_s}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} (H - h_n)^{3/2}
$$

> Note the temperature (density) term differs between inflow and outflow — this is a KTU examiner favourite.

### 3.1.8 Continuity Constraint

In a steady, vent-limited fire, total mass inflow must equal total mass outflow plus combustion mass consumption (often neglected for simplicity):

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_{in}^2 = \dot{m}_{out}^2
$$

### 3.1.9 Solving for the Neutral Plane

$$
\left[ \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho_\infty}} \cdot h_n^{3/2} \right]^2 = \left[ \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho_s}} \cdot (H - h_n)^{3/2} \right]^2
$$

$$
\frac{h_n^3}{\rho_\infty} = \frac{(H - h_n)^3}{\rho_s}
$$

Using ideal gas law $\rho_s / \rho_\infty = T_\infty / T_s$:

$$
\frac{h_n^3}{(H - h_n)^3} = \frac{\rho_\infty}{\rho_s} = \frac{T_\infty}{T_s}
$$

$$
\frac{h_n}{H - h_n} = \left(\frac{T_\infty}{T_s}\right)^{1/3}
$$

Solving for $h_n$:

$$
h_n = \frac{H \cdot (T_\infty / T_s)^{1/3}}{1 + (T_\infty / T_s)^{1/3}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(T_s/T_\infty)^{1/3}$:

$$
\boxed{h_n = \frac{H}{1 + (T_s / T_\infty)^{1/3}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Form:** Express the result as $h_n = H / [1 + (T_s/T_\infty)^{1/3}]$ for a single door opening.

For multiple openings:

$$
h_n = \frac{H}{1 + \left(\frac{T_\infty \cdot A_{in}^2}{T_s \cdot A_{out}^2}\right)^{1/3}}
$$

### 3.1.10 Special Case Check

If $T_s \to T_\infty$, then $T_s/T_\infty \to 1$, and $h_n \to H/2$ — the neutral plane sits at mid-height, as expected when no temperature difference exists. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of Visibility Equation from First Principles

### 3.2.1 Lambert-Beer Law for Light Attenuation

Light intensity after traversing smoke of thickness $L$:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot \exp(-K_{ext} \cdot L)
$$

Solving for the extinction coefficient:

$$
K_{ext} = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \frac{2.303}{L} \cdot D
$$

where $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ is the optical density.

### 3.2.2 Relating Extinction Coefficient to Smoke Mass

By definition:

$$
K_{ext} = \sigma \cdot \rho_s
$$

### 3.2.3 Visibility Definition

Visibility $V$ is defined as the distance at which an observer can just discern a target, conventionally when contrast drops to **2 %** (or sometimes 5 %) — i.e., $I/I_0 = 0.02$ for an ideal black target.

Setting $L = V$ and $I/I_0 = 0.02$:

$$
0.02 = \exp(-\sigma \cdot \rho_s \cdot V)
$$

$$
V = \frac{-\ln(0.02)}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{\ln(50)}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{3.912}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s}
$$

For an illuminated EXIT sign, the contrast threshold is higher, so the constant $C$ rises to ~5–10, giving the operational formula:

$$
V = \frac{C}{\sigma \cdot \rho_s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $C = 8$ is a KTU/NFPA convention for the visibility of light-emitting signs in fire engineering design.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **Problem (14 Marks):**
> A smoke-filled compartment has a single door opening 2.0 m wide and 2.5 m tall. The hot smoke layer is at 600 K and the ambient is at 300 K. Determine:
> (a) The position of the neutral plane.
> (b) The mass flow rate of smoke leaving through the upper portion.
> (c) The mass flow rate of air entering through the lower portion.
> Take discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.7$, $\rho_\infty = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### 3.3.1 Part (a) — Neutral Plane

Using the formula derived:

$$
h_n = \frac{H}{1 + (T_s / T_\infty)^{1/3}} = \frac{2.5}{1 + (600/300)^{1/3}} = \frac{2.5}{1 + (2)^{1/3}} = \frac{2.5}{1 + 1.2599}
$$

$$
h_n = \frac{2.5}{2.2599} = 1.106 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3.2 Part (b) — Outflow Rate (Smoke)

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_s}} \cdot (h_t - h_n)^{3/2}
$$

With $h_t = H = 2.5$ m:

$$
(h_t - h_n)^{3/2} = (2.5 - 1.106)^{3/2} = (1.394)^{3/2}
$$

Computing $(1.394)^{3/2}$:

$$
1.394^{1/2} = 1.1807 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.394^{3/2} = 1.394 \times 1.1807 = 1.6459
$$

$$
\sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_s}} = \sqrt{9.81 \times \frac{600 - 300}{600}} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{4.905} = 2.2146
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.7 \times 2.0 \times 1.2 \times 2.2146 \times 1.6459
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
\tfrac{2}{3} \times 0.7 = 0.4667
$$

$$
0.4667 \times 2.0 = 0.9333
$$

$$
0.9333 \times 1.2 = 1.120
$$

$$
1.120 \times 2.2146 = 2.4804
$$

$$
2.4804 \times 1.6459 = 4.082 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{out} \approx 4.08 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

### 3.3.3 Part (c) — Inflow Rate (Air)

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot W \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_\infty}} \cdot (h_n - h_b)^{3/2}
$$

With $h_b = 0$:

$$
h_n^{3/2} = (1.106)^{3/2}
$$

Computing $(1.106)^{3/2}$:

$$
1.106^{1/2} = 1.0517 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.106^{3/2} = 1.106 \times 1.0517 = 1.1632
$$

$$
\sqrt{g \cdot \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{T_\infty}} = \sqrt{9.81 \times \frac{300}{300}} = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.7 \times 2.0 \times 1.2 \times 3.132 \times 1.1632
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.4667 \times 2.0 \times 1.2 = 1.120
$$

$$
1.120 \times 3.132 = 3.508
$$

$$
3.508 \times 1.1632 = 4.080 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{in} \approx 4.08 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

### 3.3.4 Verification

$\dot{m}_{in} \approx \dot{m}_{out}$ ✓ (the small discrepancy is due to rounding). This validates the continuity assumption.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners award full marks if the **method, formula, units, and consistency** are correct. Numerical precision to ±2 % is acceptable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Smoke Movement vs. Visibility Failure Times

| Scenario | Smoke Filling Time (Room) | Visibility Failure Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small office (3×4×2.7 m), chair fire (500 kW) | ~30 s | ~45 s | Critical — short window |
| Lecture hall (10×15×4 m), sofa fire (1500 kW) | ~90 s | ~120 s | Adequate but marginal |
| Atrium (30×30×15 m), shop fire (5000 kW) | ~300 s | ~400 s | Plume rise delays smoke spread |
