# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy Levels** (L1–L5), aligned with PESFT416 Course Outcomes CO2 (Analyze compartment fire dynamics using zone modeling) and CO3 (Apply NBC smoke control provisions).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define zone modeling of compartment fires. Differentiate between one-zone and two-zone models with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Zone modeling is a lumped-parameter fire modeling approach in which a fire compartment is divided into a small number of idealized, internally uniform zones (control volumes) within which temperature, density, and species concentrations are assumed spatially uniform. Conservation of mass, momentum, and energy is applied zone-wise.

**Two-Zone Model (1 mark):** Used for **pre-flashover fires**. The compartment is divided into:
- **Upper hot (smoke) layer** — hot combustion products at ceiling with temperature $T_u$.
- **Lower cool layer** — ambient breathable air at temperature $T_\infty$.
- The **interface height z** is the key design parameter.

**One-Zone (Single-Zone) Model (1 mark):** Used for **post-flashover** fires where the entire compartment is at near-uniform high temperature $T_g$. Mass and energy are conserved for the compartment as a single control volume.

**Sketch:** Draw a rectangular compartment with horizontal interface line at height z — upper half shaded (smoke), lower half clear (air).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the National Building Code (NBC) 2016 guidelines for general smoke control in buildings. List any four key design requirements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **NBC 2016, Part 4 (Fire and Life Safety), Section 4.6 (Smoke Control and Management)**, the general design requirements are:

1. **Minimum clear smoke-free layer height of 2.5 m above floor level** along all evacuation routes (1 mark).
2. **Stairwell pressurization with a minimum pressure differential of 12.5 Pa** relative to adjacent fire-affected floors (1 mark).
3. **Mechanical smoke exhaust rate of 6 air changes per hour (6 ACH)** for large compartments, atria, and basements (1 mark).
4. **Maximum optical smoke density AOD < 0.2 OD/m** and **visibility ≥ 10 m** along escape routes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **two-zone model of pre-flashover compartment fires**. Derive the **mass conservation equation** for the upper hot layer and discuss its physical significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m office compartment experiences a **medium t² fire** with growth coefficient α = 0.01172 kW/s². Using the **Heskestad plume correlation**, estimate the **plume mass entrainment rate** and **upper layer temperature rise** at **t = 200 s**. Take convective fraction χ_c = 0.7, h_k = 30 W/m²K, and assume no leakage. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a rectangular compartment showing the fire source at floor, the buoyant plume rising, the upper hot smoke layer at ceiling, the lower cool layer at floor, and the interface height z labeled.

**Conceptual explanation (2 marks):**
- Pre-flashover fire → localized burning, well-defined layer stratification.
- Upper layer: hot, smoke-laden, accumulates from plume.
- Lower layer: ambient conditions.
- Two conservation laws govern: mass and energy.

**Derivation of mass conservation (3 marks):**

Take the upper hot layer as the control volume of mass $m_u$.

$$\frac{dm_u}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

- **Inflow** = plume entrainment: $\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3} \left[1 + 0.026 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} z^{-5/3}\right]$
- **Outflow** = through vents/leakage: $\dot{m}_{out}$

$$\boxed{\frac{dm_u}{dt} = 0.071 \, \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} \, z^{5/3} \left[1 + 0.026 \, \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} z^{-5/3}\right] - \dot{m}_{out}}$$

**Physical significance (built into the explanation):** This equation tells us the rate at which the smoke mass in the upper layer changes. It is the foundation for computing the smoke layer interface descent rate, which is critical for ASET calculations.

### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute HRR at t = 200 s (1 mark):**
$$\dot{Q}(200) = 0.01172 \times 200^2 = 0.01172 \times 40000 = 468.8 \text{ kW}$$
$$\dot{Q}_c = 0.7 \times 468.8 = 328.16 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Assume initial interface height z = 2.0 m (first iteration) (1 mark):**

- $328.16^{1/3} = 6.896$
- $2.0^{5/3} = 3.175$
- $328.16^{2/3} = 47.55$
- $2.0^{5/3} = 3.175$
- Inner bracket: $1 + 0.026 \times 47.55/3.175 = 1 + 0.389 = 1.389$

$$\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \times 6.896 \times 3.175 \times 1.389 = 2.158 \text{ kg/s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute upper layer temperature rise (3 marks):**

$$A_T = 2 \times (10 \times 3.5) + 2 \times (8 \times 3.5) + (10 \times 8) = 70 + 56 + 80 = 206 \text{ m}^2$$

$$h_k A_T = 30 \times 206 = 6180 \text{ W/K} = 6.18 \text{ kW/K}$$

$$\Delta T_u = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{\dot{m}_p c_p + h_k A_T} = \frac{328.16}{2.158 \times 1.005 + 6.18} = \frac{328.16}{2.169 + 6.18} = \frac{328.16}{8.349} = 39.31 \text{ K}$$

$$T_u = 293 + 39.31 = 332.3 \text{ K} = 59.3 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Iterative refinement (1 mark):** The interface z at t = 200 s can be refined using the interface descent equation. The student should note the **iterative nature** of the calculation and present a refined estimate of z ≈ 1.85 m, recomputing $\dot{m}_p \approx 2.05$ kg/s and $T_u \approx 325$ K. (Full marks awarded for demonstrating the iteration approach, even if the final value is approximate.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Differentiate between **pre-flashover and post-flashover** compartment fires. Explain the **Hasemi / Kawagoe** empirical relation for post-flashover HRR and list its limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **National Building Code (NBC) 2016 guidelines** for **active and passive smoke control** in buildings. With a labeled sketch, explain the design of a **mechanical smoke exhaust system** for a basement car park. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Tabular comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Flashover | Post-Flashover |
|---|---|---|
| Fire location | Localized at seat | Entire compartment |
| Temperature | Upper layer 100–600 °C | Uniform 800–1200 °C |
| Zone model | Two-zone | Single-zone |
| Oxygen regime | Fuel-controlled (early) | Ventilation-controlled |
| HRR | Q < Q_fo | Q ≈ Q_fo |

**Kawagoe–Sekine relation (3 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_{fo} = 1260 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

Where $A_v$ = area of ventilation opening (m²), $H_v$ = height of opening (m). This is derived from combustion stoichiometry assuming complete combustion in a ventilation-limited regime.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Valid only for ventilation-controlled post-flashover fires.
- Assumes single rectangular opening.
- Ignores radiative fraction variations.
- Empirical — based on wood crib experiments by Kawagoe (1958).

### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Active vs. Passive Smoke Control (3 marks):**

- **Passive (NBC 4.6.1):** Smoke barriers, fire-rated compartments, smoke curtains, natural venting through openings, smoke shafts.
- **Active (NBC 4.6.2):** Mechanical smoke exhaust, stairwell pressurization (ΔP ≥ 12.5 Pa), dedicated smoke management for atria, automatic detection-actuation systems.

**Mechanical Smoke Exhaust for Basement Car Park (4 marks):**

Design parameters per NBC 2016:
- Exhaust rate = **6 ACH** for basement car parks (some codes require 10 ACH).
- For V = 10 m × 20 m × 3 m = 600 m³ → exhaust rate = **3600 m³/h**.

**Sketch requirements:** Show basement with car park, smoke detectors, exhaust fans at ceiling, supply air inlets at low level, fire-rated segregation from upper floors, smoke reservoir maintained at 2.5 m clear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the two-zone and one-zone model applicability** — Students frequently apply the two-zone model to post-flashover fires. This is conceptually wrong and will cost 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting units in plume correlation** — The Heskestad correlation has implicit units. $\dot{Q}_c$ must be in kW, z in meters, and $\dot{m}_p$ emerges in kg/s. Mixing units yields numerical nonsense and full marks loss.
> 3. **Using the t² fire growth law without specifying the growth coefficient** — Simply writing $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ without defining α is incomplete. Always cite the fire growth class (Slow, Medium, Fast, Ultra-Fast) and the value of α.
> 4. **Omitting the convective fraction χ_c = 0.7** — The plume correlation uses the **convective** HRR, not the total. Students using Q directly overestimate m_p by ~43% and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing NBC 2016 with NFPA or IS codes** — NBC 2016 Part 4, Section 4.6 is the relevant Indian codal reference. Mixing up numbers from NFPA 92 (US) or BS 7974 (UK) is a common error. The 12.5 Pa stairwell pressure and 6 ACH are **NBC 2016 specific**.
> 6. **Skipping the iterative nature of zone modeling** — Zone model solutions are inherently iterative (z depends on m_p which depends on z). A single-shot answer is marked down by at least 1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing the smoke layer interface height with the smoke detector mounting height** — These are two different quantities; students often state "z = 2.5 m for detector placement," which is incorrect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — High-Density Recap (read this the night before the exam):**

- [x] **Zone Model** = lumped-parameter model; compartment divided into 2 (pre-flashover) or 1 (post-flashover) uniform zones.
- [x] **Two-Zone Model** → upper hot layer + lower cool layer; interface height z is the design-critical parameter.
- [x] **Single-Zone Model** → uniform temperature after flashover; Kawagoe HRR $\dot{Q}_{fo} = 1260 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ applies.
- [x] **Pre-Flashover:** localized fire, layer stratification exists, two-zone model valid, HRR follows t² law.
- [x] **Post-Flashover:** full compartment involvement, uniform high temperature, ventilation-controlled, single-zone model.
- [x] **Flashover** = transition when upper layer reaches fuel ignition temperature and entire compartment ignites simultaneously.
- [x] **Heskestad Plume:** $\dot{m}_p = 0.071 \dot{Q}_c^{1/3} z^{5/3} [1 + 0.026 \dot{Q}_c^{2/3} z^{-5/3}]$ — uses convective HRR.
- [x] **Zukoski Plume:** $\dot{m}_p = 0.076 \dot{Q}_c z^{5/3} (g / c_p T_\infty \rho_\infty)^{1/3}$ — simpler, weak plume regime.
- [x] **t² Fire Growth Law:** $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$; α = 0.00293 (slow), 0.01172 (medium), 0.0469 (fast), 0.1876 (ultra-fast) kW/s².
- [x] **NBC 2016 Part 4 Section 4.6** is the codal reference for smoke control in India.
- [x] **Passive Smoke Control** = architectural features (barriers, curtains, shafts, natural venting).
- [x] **Active Smoke Control** = mechanical systems (exhaust 6 ACH, stairwell pressurization ΔP ≥ 12.5 Pa).
- [x] **Tenability criterion** = clear smoke-free layer ≥ 2.5 m above floor along egress routes.
- [x] **Stairwell pressurization** = minimum 12.5 Pa pressure differential; max door-opening force 133 N.
- [x] **Optical smoke density AOD** < 0.2 OD/m, **visibility** ≥ 10 m on egress routes.
- [x] **Hot layer temperature rise (quasi-steady):** $\Delta T_u = \dot{Q}_c / (\dot{m}_p c_p + h_k A_T)$.
- [x] **Mass conservation (upper layer):** $dm_u/dt = \dot{m}_p - \dot{m}_{out}$.
- [x] **Energy conservation (upper layer):** $dE_u/dt = \dot{Q}_c - h_k A_T (T_u - T_\infty) - \dot{m}_p c_p (T_u - T_\infty)$.
- [x] **Interface descent rate:** $dz/dt = (\dot{m}_p - \dot{m}_{out}) / (\rho_\infty A T_\infty / T_u)$.
- [x] **ASET > RSET** is the fundamental performance-based design criterion.
- [x] **CFAST, OZone, ASET-B, FDS (with single compartment)** are practical zone-modeling tools.
- [x] **Post-flashover HRR (Kawagoe):** $1260 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ — ventilation-controlled estimate.
- [x] **Convective fraction χ_c ≈ 0.7** for typical fuels; radiative fraction ≈ 0.3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke, Fire and Explosion Venting Requirements in Industrial Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Smoke, Fire and Explosion Venting Requirements in Industrial Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke, Fire and Explosion Venting** refers to the engineered provision of deliberately designed openings (vents) in the walls or roof of an industrial building to **release heat, smoke, and combustion gases during a fire event**, and to **relieve overpressure generated during a deflagration (explosion)**. The system is governed by standards such as **NFPA 68** (Explosion Venting), **NFPA 204** (Smoke and Heat Venting), **IS 3594**, and **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**.

In the specific context of KTU Module 2, three distinct — but interrelated — venting strategies are covered:

| Sub-System | Purpose | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoke Venting** | Maintain a smoke-free layer near the floor for safe egress & firefighting | Heat from fire |
| **Heat / Fire Venting** | Release hot gases to limit ceiling temperature & prevent structural collapse | High heat flux |
| **Explosion Venting** | Relieve rapid pressure rise from a deflagration, protecting the enclosure | Pressure rise |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pressure Cooker Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an industrial building as a giant pressure cooker on a stovetop.** A pressure cooker has a small weighted vent on its lid. As the contents heat up, steam pushes the weight and escapes — preventing the cooker from exploding. A building's venting system does exactly the same job:
> - **Smoke vents** are like opening a window when the kitchen gets smoky while cooking — you let the bad air out so you can still see and breathe.
> - **Fire (heat) vents** are like lifting the lid briefly when the food is about to burn — you let out the heat before the pot itself gets damaged.
> - **Explosion vents** are the **safety valve** of the pressure cooker — they burst open automatically when pressure rises dangerously fast, saving the entire vessel from rupturing.

This simple analogy captures the three key ideas: **(a) controlled release, (b) automatic activation, and (c) preservation of the structure.**

### 1.3 Why Industrial Buildings Specifically?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industrial occupancies (Group G & H per NBC 2016) carry **higher fire loads** than residential or office buildings because of:
> - Combustible raw materials, finished goods, solvents, dust
> - High ceilings (often **8 m to 20 m**)
> - Large uninterrupted floor plates
> - Presence of suspended dust clouds (grain, flour, metal, plastic)
> - Possible flammable vapors
>
> Therefore, **vent ratios** (vent area ÷ floor area) for industrial buildings range from **1:50 (high hazard) to 1:200 (low hazard)** — far more generous than the **1:200 to 1:300** typical of offices.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Vent Activation Temperature** for fusible links: **68 °C to 74 °C** (NFPA 204)
- **Reduced Explosion Pressure ($P_{red}$)**: typically limited to **0.05 bar to 0.2 bar** for occupied buildings
- **Static Activation Pressure ($P_{stat}$)**: typically **0.1 bar to 0.35 bar** for rupture panels
- **Vent Flow Coefficient ($K_{stat}$)**: **0.5** for frangible panels, **0.65** for hinged doors
- **Standard vent ratio unit**: dimensionless ($m^2$ / $m^2$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Venting Strategy

#### A. Why **Smoke Venting**?
In a fire, the hot combustion products rise and form a hot, smoky upper layer. Without venting, this layer descends, causing:
1. Loss of visibility (egress blocked)
2. Toxic gas exposure
3. Heat radiation back to the floor — eventually triggering **flashover** at ~ **600 °C** ceiling temperature.

**Smoke vents** are placed in the **upper third** of the wall/roof. They maintain a smoke-clear lower layer by **buoyancy-driven flow** (stack effect). The vent openings must be **automatically** triggered (fusible link) or **manually** operated from the fire floor.

#### B. Why **Heat / Fire Venting**?
The structural steel members of an industrial building begin to lose strength dramatically above **550 °C** — at **600 °C**, structural steel retains only ~ **50 %** of its yield strength. Heat vents:
- Reduce ceiling temperature
- Delay or prevent flashover
- Protect roof structures, sprinklers, and the building's load-bearing elements.

#### C. Why **Explosion Venting**?
A dust cloud (grain, flour, sugar, plastic) or vapor-air mixture can produce a **deflagration** with a maximum explosion pressure $P_{max}$ of **7 to 10 bar** and a rate of pressure rise $(dP/dt)$ of **100 to 500 bar/s**. Without venting, the enclosure fails catastrophically. Explosion vents **open at a small $P_{stat}$ (e.g., 0.1 bar)**, expelling flame + gas and limiting the **reduced pressure $P_{red}$** seen by the structure to **< 0.2 bar**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**For Smoke / Heat Venting:**
1. Fire ignites → ceiling jet spreads horizontally under the roof.
2. Hot layer accumulates, descending at rate determined by **HRR (Heat Release Rate)** and vent flow.
3. When the **fusible link reaches 68–74 °C**, the vent opens automatically.
4. Hot gases exit through the vent, drawing in cooler air below.
5. Lower smoke layer remains tenable for **10–20 minutes** for evacuation.

**For Explosion Venting:**
1. Ignition source triggers combustion of dust/vapor cloud.
2. Pressure rises rapidly inside enclosure.
3. When pressure exceeds $P_{stat}$, the vent panel **ruptures** (frangible) or **swings open** (hinged).
4. Combustion products vent outward through the vent opening.
5. Internal pressure is held to a **reduced value $P_{red}$** (often < 0.1 bar) — well within structural tolerance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most heavily tested set of equations in this module. Memorize them along with their boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Standard / Source | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{A_f}{A_v}$ | Vent ratio = Floor area ÷ Vent area | NBC 2016 | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_v = \dfrac{A_f}{R}$ | Required vent area | NBC 2016 | m² |
| 3 | $A_v = \dfrac{A_s \cdot (1 + 1.75 \cdot P_{red})}{K_{stat} \cdot \sqrt{P_{red}}}$ | NFPA 68 vent area (low pressure, $P_{red} \lt 0.5$ bar) | NFPA 68 | m² |
| 4 | $A_v \propto V^{2/3}$ | Cube-root (cubical) law for geometrically similar enclosures | NFPA 68 | — |
| 5 | $\dfrac{A_{v1}}{A_{v2}} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{2/3}$ | Scaling of vent area between two similar enclosures | NFPA 68 | — |
| 6 | $P_{red} = P_{max} - \Delta P_{vent}$ | Reduced pressure = max pressure − vent relief | NFPA 68 | bar |
| 7 | $K_{stat}$ vent flow coefficient | Panel type: **0.5** (rupture), **0.65** (hinged), **0.4** (small domes) | NFPA 68 | — |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{smoke} = A_v \cdot v$ | Volumetric smoke flow rate | NFPA 204 | m³/s |
| 9 | $v = k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot H}{T_{ambient}}}$ | Buoyant velocity through vent | Buoyancy theory | m/s |
| 10 | $A_f \le 1.5 \cdot A_{v,panel}$ | For wind exposure: vent area compensation | NFPA 204 | m² |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Design**: Used to size explosion panels on dust collectors, silos, cyclones, and bag filters in pharmaceutical, food, and polymer plants.
- **Warehouse Fire Safety**: NBC-mandated roof vents in **Group H (Storage)** buildings — e.g., e-commerce fulfillment centers, paper godowns.
- **Insurance Compliance**: Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC) of India mandates venting under **IS 3594** for fire insurance.
- **Tunnel & Underground Structures**: Heat venting is now standard in metro stations and underground car parks.
- **Loss Prevention**: Factories Mutual (FM Global) and LPCB certifications require venting per NFPA 68 for dust-handling equipment.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vent Area from Vent Ratio (NBC 2016)

The vent ratio $R$ is defined as the **ratio of floor area to vent area**. This is an empirical figure set by NBC based on hazard category.

$$R = \frac{A_f}{A_v}$$

**Rearranging for vent area:**

$$A_v = \frac{A_f}{R}$$

**Step-by-step substitution example:**

> Given: Industrial building of floor area $A_f = 1500 \;\text{m}^2$, high-hazard storage, $R = 1:50$

$$A_v = \frac{1500 \;\text{m}^2}{50}$$

$$A_v = 30 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** At least **30 m²** of effective vent opening is required for the 1500 m² floor. If individual roof vent panels are **1.5 m × 2 m = 3 m²**, then we need **10 panels**, distributed evenly over the roof.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: NFPA 68 Low-Pressure Explosion Vent Area

The NFPA 68 equation arises from **thermodynamic energy balance** during a deflagration. The energy released by combustion is partially vented, and the maximum allowable reduced pressure is governed by the structural limit.

**Starting form (empirical NFPA 68, low P_red):**

$$A_v = A \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 \cdot P_{red})}{K_{stat} \cdot \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

**Where:**
- $A_v$ = required vent area, in m²
- $A$ = internal surface area of enclosure, in m²
- $P_{red}$ = reduced (maximum allowed) explosion pressure, in bar
- $K_{stat}$ = dimensionless vent flow coefficient

**Working Example (Step-by-Step):**

> A dust filter housing is a cube of side $L = 4\;\text{m}$.
> Material: **Wheat flour dust**, $P_{max} = 9.2\;\text{bar}$, $K_{stat} = 0.5$, target $P_{red} = 0.1\;\text{bar}$.

**Step 1 — Compute internal surface area $A$:**

$$A = 6 L^2 = 6 \times (4\;\text{m})^2$$

$$A = 6 \times 16\;\text{m}^2 = 96\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into NFPA 68 equation:**

$$A_v = 96 \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 \times 0.1)}{0.5 \times \sqrt{0.1}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$1 + 1.75 \times 0.1 = 1 + 0.175 = 1.175$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$0.5 \times \sqrt{0.1} = 0.5 \times 0.3162 = 0.1581$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{1.175}{0.1581} = 7.4325$$

**Step 6 — Final vent area:**

$$A_v = 96 \times 7.4325 = 713.5\;\text{m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> The enormous vent area (**~ 713 m²**) for a **4 m cube** explains why explosion venting alone is impractical for large enclosures — this is why industrial practice uses **suppression** (chemical isolation) for vessels above **~ 50 m³**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Cubical Law (Cube-Root Law)

**Premise:** For **geometrically similar enclosures** (same shape, different size), the vent area scales with the **2/3 power of volume**.

**Start with a cube of side $L$:**
- Volume: $V = L^3 \;\Rightarrow\; L = V^{1/3}$
- Surface area: $A = 6 L^2 = 6 \cdot (V^{1/3})^2 = 6 \cdot V^{2/3}$

**NFPA 68 substitution for vent area:**

$$A_v = 6 \cdot V^{2/3} \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 \cdot P_{red})}{K_{stat} \cdot \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

Since $P_{red}$ and $K_{stat}$ are constants for a given scenario:

$$A_v = C \cdot V^{2/3} \quad \text{where} \quad C = \frac{6 \cdot (1 + 1.75 \cdot P_{red})}{K_{stat} \cdot \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

**Scaling form for two similar enclosures:**

$$\frac{A_{v1}}{A_{v2}} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Numerical demonstration:**

> Enclosure A: $V_1 = 8\;\text{m}^3$ → $A_{v1} = 1.2\;\text{m}^2$
> Find $A_{v2}$ when $V_2 = 64\;\text{m}^3$

$$\frac{A_{v2}}{1.2} = \left(\frac{64}{8}\right)^{2/3} = 8^{2/3}$$

$$8^{2/3} = (2^3)^{2/3} = 2^2 = 4$$

$$A_{v2} = 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8\;\text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> When volume **increased 8×**, the vent area increased only **4×**. This is the essence of the **cube-root law** — an 8× volume increase requires only 4× vent area for the same reduced pressure.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Smoke Vent Buoyant Velocity

The buoyant flow velocity through a vent is governed by **stack-effect** pressure difference.

**Hydrostatic pressure difference across vent height $H$:**

$$\Delta P = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}\right)$$

**Equating with dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 = \rho \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_{ambient}}$$

**Solving for $v$:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \Delta T}{T_{ambient}}}$$

$$v = k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \Delta T}{T_{ambient}}}$$

The discharge coefficient $k$ (typically **0.4 to 0.7**) accounts for friction and vent geometry.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Vent Ratio by Hazard Category (NBC 2016 / IS 3594)

| Hazard Category | Occupancy Examples | Vent Ratio R | Effective Vent Area per 1000 m² Floor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Hazard** | Non-combustible storage, metal parts | 1 : 200 | 5.0 m² |
| **Moderate Hazard** | Furniture, textiles, paper | 1 : 100 | 10.0 m² |
| **High Hazard** | Flammable liquids, plastics, chemicals | 1 : 50 | 20.0 m² |
| **Extra High Hazard** | Solvent processing, grain elevators | 1 : 30 | 33.3 m² |
| **Sprinklered + Low Hazard** | Sprinklered warehouses | 1 : 200 (may be reduced) | 5.0 m² |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between smoke venting and explosion venting in industrial buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Smoke Venting | Explosion Venting |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Heat (fusible link at 68–74 °C) | Rapid pressure rise ($P_{stat}$ ~ 0.1 bar) |
| **Purpose** | Maintain tenable egress layer, delay flashover | Prevent structural failure from deflagration |
| **Time Scale** | Minutes (sustained) | Milliseconds (transient) |
| **Standard** | NFPA 204 / NBC 2016 | NFPA 68 / IS 3594 |
| **Vent Area** | 1:200 to 1:50 (per hazard) | Sized from $P_{max}$, $P_{red}$, $K_{stat}$ |
| **Activation** | Slow (thermal) | Very fast (pressure-driven) |

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: What is the significance of the cubical law (cube-root law) in explosion vent design?**

**Model Answer:**
The cubical law states that for **geometrically similar enclosures**, the required vent area is proportional to the **2/3 power of the volume** ($A_v \propto V^{2/3}$). It allows engineers to **scale vent area from a known test result** on a small enclosure to a much larger one without re-running the full NFPA 68 calculation. The law reflects the underlying physics: as the enclosure volume grows faster than its surface area, less vent area per unit volume is needed to achieve the same reduced pressure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **design criteria for smoke and heat venting** in industrial buildings as per **NFPA 204** and **NBC 2016**. Discuss vent ratio, vent placement, fusible link temperature, and interaction with sprinklers.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vent Ratio (R)**
The vent ratio $R = A_f / A_v$ is selected based on hazard classification:

- Low hazard: 1:200
- Moderate hazard: 1:100
- High hazard: 1:50
- Extra high hazard: 1:30

**Step 2 — Effective Vent Area**
Effective vent area is the **geometric open area** of the panel after activation. Panels must remain unobstructed and not be covered by snow, leaves, or guards.

**Step 3 — Vent Placement**
Vents are placed in the **upper third of the wall** or **roof**, with at least **25 %** of total vent area in the top third. Maximum spacing between vents should not exceed **30 m** in any direction to prevent smoke pooling.

**Step 4 — Fusible Link Temperature**
Fusible links rated at **68 °C to 74 °C** (per NFPA 204) are used. For sprinklered buildings, the fusible link rating may be raised to **93 °C** to **140 °C** so that vents open **only when sprinklers fail or are absent**.

**Step 5 — Sprinkler-Vent Interaction**
NFPA 204 and FM Global Loss Prevention Data Sheet 1-12 recommend:
- Heat vents and sprinklers are **complementary**, not substitutes.
- Vents should open **at or above** sprinkler activation temperature.
- For high-rack storage (> 12 m), roof vents may be omitted if ESFR sprinklers are present and approved by AHJ.

---

**(b)** An **industrial storage building** has dimensions **60 m × 30 m × 9 m (height)**. It stores **plastics and rubber** (high hazard). Calculate the **required vent area** as per **NBC 2016** if the building is **non-sprinklered**. If each vent panel is **2 m × 1.5 m**, how many panels are required?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Floor dimensions: 60 m × 30 m
- Floor area: $A_f = 60 \times 30 = 1800 \;\text{m}^2$
- Hazard: High (plastics and rubber)
- Vent ratio: $R = 1:50$ (from NBC 2016 / IS 3594)

**Step 1 — Calculate required vent area:**

$$A_v = \frac{A_f}{R} = \frac{1800}{50} = 36 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate area of one panel:**

$$A_{panel} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Number of panels required:**

$$N = \frac{A_v}{A_{panel}} = \frac{36}{3} = 12 \;\text{panels}$$

**Step 4 — Distribution:**
Place 12 panels in **two rows of 6 each** along the ridge, with spacing:

$$\text{Longitudinal spacing} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \;\text{m} \;\;(\text{within NFPA 204 limit of 30 m})$$

$$\text{Transverse offset} = 15 \;\text{m from each eave}$$

**Step 5 — Verification — Upper third requirement:**
With 9 m height, upper third is from 6 m to 9 m. All 12 panels at the ridge (9 m) satisfy this requirement.

**Final Answer: 12 vent panels of 2 m × 1.5 m each, distributed evenly along the ridge.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive the **cubical law (cube-root law)** of explosion venting from first principles. State the key assumptions, and explain its **limitations** in practical industrial applications.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**
Consider a cubic enclosure of side $L$. Volume and surface area are:

$$V = L^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L = V^{1/3}$$

$$A = 6 L^2 = 6 \cdot V^{2/3}$$

**Step 2 — NFPA 68 Vent Area Equation:**

$$A_v = A \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 P_{red})}{K_{stat} \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution of Surface Area:**

$$A_v = 6 \cdot V^{2/3} \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 P_{red})}{K_{stat} \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

**Step 4 — Grouping Constants:**
Define a constant $C$:

$$C = \frac{6 \cdot (1 + 1.75 P_{red})}{K_{stat} \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

Then:

$$A_v = C \cdot V^{2/3}$$

This is the **cubical law**: vent area scales as $V^{2/3}$.

**Step 5 — Scaling Between Two Similar Enclosures:**

$$\frac{A_{v1}}{A_{v2}} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 6 — Key Assumptions:**
1. Both enclosures are **geometrically similar** (same shape, scaled size).
2. Same **combustible material** (same $P_{max}$ and $K_{st}$).
3. Same **target $P_{red}$** and same $K_{stat}$.
4. Enclosure is **closed** except for the vent.

**Step 7 — Limitations:**
- Fails for **elongated vessels** (e.g., long ductwork, silos) where $L/D \gt 3$ — the $L/D$ correction is needed.
- Assumes **uniform turbulence** — invalid for obstructed vessels.
- Does not account for **vent ducting** — adds back-pressure, reduces effective area.
- Inapplicable when **internal volume changes** during the deflagration (e.g., partial filling).

---

**(b)** A **pharmaceutical dust collector** has a **cylindrical volume of 250 m³** and an internal surface area of **180 m²**. Using the **NFPA 68 simplified method** with $P_{red} = 0.2 \;\text{bar}$ and $K_{stat} = 0.5$, calculate the required vent area. If the enclosure is square in plan (assume for verification), check whether the cubical law agrees with the direct calculation.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Direct NFPA 68 Calculation:**

$$A_v = A \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 P_{red})}{K_{stat} \sqrt{P_{red}}}$$

$$A_v = 180 \cdot \frac{(1 + 1.75 \times 0.2)}{0.5 \times \sqrt{0.2}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$1 + 0.35 = 1.35$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$0.5 \times \sqrt{0.2} = 0.5 \times 0.4472 = 0.2236$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$A_v = 180 \times \frac{1.35}{0.2236} = 180 \times 6.037 = 1086.6 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Cubical Law Verification:**
For a cube of volume 250 m³: $L = V^{1/3} = 250^{1/3} = 6.30 \;\text{m}$
Surface area of a cube: $A_{cube} = 6 L^2 = 6 \times 39.7 = 238.2 \;\text{m}^2$

Vent area by cubical law:

$$A_v = 238.2 \times \frac{1.35}{0.2236} = 1438 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**
- Direct (using actual $A = 180 \;\text{m}^2$): **1086.6 m²**
- Cubical law (using $A = 238.2 \;\text{m}^2$): **1438 m²**

The discrepancy arises because the **actual cylinder is more compact** (lower surface-to-volume ratio) than a cube of the same volume. The cubical law gives a **conservative (higher) vent area**, which is acceptable on the safe side.

**Step 7 — Practical Note:**
A vent area of **~ 1087 m²** on a 250 m³ vessel is enormous. In practice, **explosion suppression** (using chemical isolation or suppression systems per NFPA 69) is preferred for vessels above ~ 50 m³, supplemented by isolation devices (flame arresters, valves) to prevent pressure-piling and flame propagation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing units** — $P_{red}$ must be in **bar** for the NFPA 68 equation; converting incorrectly from kPa (1 bar = 100 kPa) is a frequent error.
> 2. **Forgetting $K_{stat}$ dependence** — Students often hard-code $K_{stat} = 0.5$ even for hinged doors (should be 0.65). Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for this.
> 3. **Inverting the vent ratio** — Some students compute $A_v = A_f \times R$ instead of $A_v = A_f / R$. This gives a vent area **50× or 200× too small** — a major error.
> 4. **Ignoring upper-third placement rule** — Always state that **at least 25 % of vent area must be in the upper third** of the wall.
> 5. **Failing to verify cubical law assumptions** — If you invoke the cubical law, you **must** state the assumption of geometric similarity, or marks are lost.
> 6. **Confusing $P_{max}$ with $P_{red}$** — $P_{max}$ is the *unvented* peak pressure; $P_{red}$ is the *target* reduced pressure *after* venting. Use $P_{red}$ in the vent area formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ☐ **Three venting types**: smoke (heat-driven, slow), heat/fire (heat-driven, sustained), explosion (pressure-driven, millisecond).
- ☐ **Vent ratio R** = $A_f / A_v$ (NOT the reverse). Common values: **1:200 (low), 1:100 (moderate), 1:50 (high), 1:30 (extra high)**.
- ☐ **NBC 2016 / IS 3594** is the Indian reference; **NFPA 68** for explosion venting; **NFPA 204** for smoke and heat venting.
- ☐ **Fusible link rating**: **68–74 °C** (standard) or **93–140 °C** (sprinklered buildings).
- ☐ **NFPA 68 vent area equation**: $A_v = A \cdot (1 + 1.75 P_{red}) / (K_{stat} \sqrt{P_{red}})$.
- ☐ **Cubical law**: $A_v \propto V^{2/3}$ — valid for **geometrically similar** enclosures only.
- ☐ **K_stat values**: **0.5 (rupture panel)**, **0.65 (hinged door)**, **0.4 (domes)**.
- ☐ **P_red target**: typically **0.1 to 0.2 bar** for occupied buildings; **≤ 0.05 bar** for high-value equipment.
- ☐ **Upper-third rule**: at least **25 %** of vent area in the upper third of the wall.
- ☐ **Max spacing** between vents: **30 m** (NFPA 204).
- ☐ **Sprinkler-vent synergy**: vents and sprinklers are **complementary**, not substitutes.
- ☐ **For volumes above ~ 50 m³**, prefer **explosion suppression (NFPA 69)** over venting alone.
- ☐ **Flame arrester / isolation valve** must be added to **prevent pressure-piling** and flame propagation in ductwork.
- ☐ **Snow, wind, and corrosion** can reduce effective vent area — provide **1.5× safety factor** in exposed locations.
- ☐ **Cubical law limitation**: fails for **elongated vessels** ($L/D > 3$) — use NFPA 68 $L/D$ correction chart.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Prevention Measures in Buildings as per NBC: Classification of Buildings Based on Occupancy and Type of Construction, Fire Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Prevention Measures in Buildings as per NBC: Classification of Buildings Based on Occupancy and Type of Construction, Fire Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (As per National Building Code of India, NBC 2016)

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**, published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, serves as the national instrument providing guidelines for regulating building construction activities across the country. Part 4 of NBC 2016 titled *"Fire and Life Safety"* lays down the comprehensive fire prevention, fire protection, and life safety requirements for all buildings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 2.1):**
> *"Every building shall be classified, based on the purpose for which it is used, into one or more of the occupancy groups (Group A to Group J) and, based on the fire resistance of its structural elements, into one of the four types of construction (Type 1 to Type 4). Further, every area in a town planning scheme shall be designated as Fire Zone 1, 2, or 3 depending upon the dominant occupancy and hazard potential."*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of NBC Fire Classification

| # | Classification Axis | Purpose | Reference |
|---|---------------------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Occupancy Classification** | Identifies *what the building is used for* and the associated life-safety risk profile | NBC 2016, Part 4, Sec 3 |
| 2 | **Type of Construction** | Identifies *how the building is built* and the fire resistance of its load-bearing elements | NBC 2016, Part 4, Sec 4 |
| 3 | **Fire Zones** | Identifies *where the building is located* in a city/town based on the surrounding hazard potential | NBC 2016, Part 4, Sec 5 |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Triage" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Think of NBC classification as a Doctor's Triage System.**
>
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Before treating patients, doctors perform a **Triage** — they classify patients into categories (critical, serious, stable) and assign treatment bays (ICU, General Ward, Outpatient). They also know which bay has which equipment (Type of Construction) and which hospital wing can handle which patient profile (Fire Zone).
>
> - **Occupancy Group** = *Patient's disease* (what is the building used for? → Residential, Hospital, School, Factory, etc.)
> - **Type of Construction** = *Treatment Bay Strength* (Is the structure concrete/steel, or wood? How long can it withstand a "crisis" — i.e., fire?)
> - **Fire Zone** = *Hospital Wing* (Can this wing accept a patient on oxygen cylinders? Or only stable patients?)
>
> Without this triage, you cannot prescribe the right **fire safety prescription** (egress width, sprinkler, fire rating, fire door, etc.).

### 1.4 Why Classification is the *Foundation* of Fire Prevention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (Fire Engineering Triad):**
> Every fire safety design parameter in a building is a *function* of its three classifications:
>
> $$\boxed{\text{Fire Safety Design} = f(\text{Occupancy},\ \text{Construction Type},\ \text{Fire Zone})}$$
>
> For example:
> - **Exit width** depends on Occupant Load, which depends on Occupancy Group.
> - **Fire resistance rating (in hours)** of structural members depends on Type of Construction.
> - **Permissible floor area, height, and setback** depend on Fire Zone.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CLASSIFICATION OF BUILDINGS BASED ON OCCUPANCY (NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 3)

NBC 2016 divides **all buildings into 9 major Occupancy Groups (A to I)**, with Group J reserved for buildings of *mixed occupancy*. Each group is further divided into sub-divisions based on the specific use and associated hazard.

---

#### **GROUP A — RESIDENTIAL**
*Any building or part thereof used as a dwelling for permanent or temporary residence.*

| Sub-Group | Description | Examples |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| A-1 | Private dwelling (single family) | Bungalows, Villas, Row Houses |
| A-2 | Lodging or rooming houses | Hotels (≤ 15 rooming units), Dormitories, Hostels |
| A-3 | Apartment houses (multi-family) | Flats, Apartments, Condominiums |
| A-4 | Hotels (tall) | Star Hotels, Service Apartments |
| A-5 | Dormitories, hostels with common cooking | KTU Hostels, Working Women's Hostels |
| A-6 | Apartment hotels | Furnished apartments for short stay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Residential the *lowest hazard* group?**
> Occupants are familiar with the building layout (sleeping occupants excepted), load is predictable, and fuel load is moderate (furniture, clothes). However, the *sleeping risk* in A-2/A-4/A-5 elevates detection requirements (smoke detectors mandatory in corridors).

---

#### **GROUP B — EDUCATIONAL**
*Any building used for purposes of education, training, or care of children/students.*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| B-1 | Schools up to Senior Secondary level |
| B-2 | All other training/educational institutions (colleges, ITIs, coaching centres) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Requirement (NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 4.4.3):** Every room with occupancy > **45 students** must have **at least two exit doors** remote from each other, and corridors must be a minimum of **2.4 m wide** for assembly type classrooms.

---

#### **GROUP C — INSTITUTIONAL**
*Any building used for purposes of medical, physical, or other care/treatment, or detention.*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| C-1 | Hospitals, Sanatoria, Nursing Homes (where occupants are non-ambulatory or partially ambulatory) |
| C-2 | Custodial institutions (mental asylums, old age homes) |
| C-3 | Penal institutions (prisons, reformatories) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical NBC Rule:** In **C-1 (Hospitals)**, the "defend-in-place" strategy is adopted. The horizontal evacuation distance must be **≤ 30 m** between smoke compartments, and the travel distance to an exit must not exceed **22.5 m** in non-sprinklered areas.

---

#### **GROUP D — ASSEMBLY**
*Any building or part thereof where groups of people congregate for recreation, amusement, social, religious, or civic purposes.*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| D-1 | Buildings with **fixed seats** (theatres, auditoriums, cinemas) |
| D-2 | Buildings with **loose seats** (worship places, museums, art galleries) |
| D-3 | **Educational/Instructional** assembly (≤ 300 persons) |
| D-4 | **Passenger assembly** (airports, bus terminals, railway stations) |
| D-5 | **Amusement/recreation** (stadiums, gymnasiums, dance halls) |
| D-6 | **Exhibition halls** (trade fairs, expo centres) |
| D-7 | **Places of worship** with congregation > 300 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Assembly = Highest Occupant Density Group.** The occupant load factor for D-1 (theatres with fixed seats) is as low as **0.5 m²/person**. A small error in classification here can lead to **catastrophic under-design of exits** (stampede risk).

---

#### **GROUP E — BUSINESS / OFFICE**
*Any building used for transaction of business, keeping of accounts, professional services, etc.*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| E-1 | Offices, banks, professional establishments |
| E-2 | Data centres, IT offices, call centres (with high-density electronic load) |
| E-3 | Laboratories (research, testing) |

---

#### **GROUP F — MERCANTILE**
*Any building used for display and sale of merchandise (shops, stores, markets, malls).*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| F-1 | Small shops, marts (≤ 500 m² floor area per floor) |
| F-2 | Large departmental stores, supermarkets, shopping malls |
| F-3 | Wholesale establishments, godams with public access |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key NBC Rule:** In F-2, the entire mall must be protected by **automatic sprinkler systems** if the total floor area exceeds **500 m²** on any floor.

---

#### **GROUP G — INDUSTRIAL**
*Any building in which products or materials of all kinds and properties are fabricated, assembled, manufactured, processed, repaired, or stored in a factory environment.*

| Sub-Group | Hazard Level | Examples |
|-----------|--------------|----------|
| G-1 | Low hazard | Assembly of electronics, instruments, non-combustible goods |
| G-2 | Moderate hazard | Manufacturing of wood products, leather, textiles, food processing |
| G-3 | High hazard | Chemical plants, paint shops, paper mills, plastic factories |

---

#### **GROUP H — STORAGE**
*Any building used primarily for storage of goods, merchandise, vehicles (warehouses, godowns, parking garages).*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| H-1 | Low hazard storage (non-combustible goods) |
| H-2 | Moderate hazard storage (combustible goods in cartons) |
| H-3 | High hazard storage (flammable liquids, gases, rubber, hay) |

---

#### **GROUP I — HAZARDOUS**
*Any building used for storage, handling, manufacture, or processing of highly combustible, explosive, or toxic materials.*

| Sub-Group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| I-1 | Storage of explosives, fireworks, ammunition |
| I-2 | Storage of flammable liquids (petrol, kerosene) in bulk |
| I-3 | Storage of flammable gases (LPG, hydrogen) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Group I has the *strictest* setback requirements.** As per NBC 2016, the minimum distance from a Group I building to any other building must be **≥ 15 m** (or as specified by Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation, PESO).

---

#### **GROUP J — MIXED / MULTIPLE OCCUPANCY**
*Any building used for more than one occupancy purpose (e.g., a high-rise with retail on lower floors and apartments above).*

> [!NOTE]
> **NBC Rule (Part 4, Cl. 3.2):** In mixed-occupancy buildings, the **most restrictive requirements** of all applicable occupancy groups shall apply to the entire building *unless* fire separation walls (with a fire resistance of **≥ 2 hours**) divide the building into isolated fire compartments.

---

### 2.2 CLASSIFICATION OF BUILDINGS BASED ON TYPE OF CONSTRUCTION (NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 4)

The "Type of Construction" classifies buildings by the **fire resistance rating (FRR) of their structural and non-structural elements** (columns, beams, floors, walls, roofs). NBC defines **4 main Types** (Type 1 to Type 4), with Type 1 further sub-divided into 1A, 1B, and 1C.

#### **Type 1 — Non-Combustible Construction (Highest Fire Resistance)**

| Sub-Type | Structural Material | Fire Resistance |
|----------|--------------------|----------------- |
| **Type 1A** | Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC), fully protected steel | **4 hours** |
| **Type 1B** | RCC, partially protected steel | **3 hours** |
| **Type 1C** | Unprotected steel (with calculated fire engineering) | **2 hours** |

> Use: High-rise buildings, hospitals, airports, IT parks.

#### **Type 2 — Non-Combustible Construction (Moderate Fire Resistance)**
- All structural members are non-combustible (RCC, masonry, steel with fire cladding) but with **lower fire resistance** (typically **2 hours**).
- Permitted in low to mid-rise buildings (≤ 15 m height in most cases).

> Use: Office buildings, schools, hotels up to mid-rise.

#### **Type 3 — Externally Non-Combustible, Internally Combustible (Limited Fire Resistance)**
- **External walls** are masonry/RCC (non-combustible) with FRR of **≥ 2 hours**.
- **Internal structural members** (floors, roof) may be of **combustible material** (e.g., timber) with FRR of **1 hour**.
- Common in traditional Kerala *Nalukettu* houses and older urban buildings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Type 3 buildings are at HIGH risk of rapid fire spread internally** because the timber floors and roof act as a "chimney" for vertical fire propagation. NBC requires Type 3 to be limited to **2 storeys and ≤ 1000 m²** total floor area.

#### **Type 4 — Combustible Construction (Lowest Fire Resistance)**
- All structural elements (walls, floors, roof) are made of **combustible materials** (timber, bamboo, thatch).
- **No minimum fire resistance** requirement.
- Common in **rural and temporary structures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 4 buildings are PROHIBITED in Fire Zone 1 and Fire Zone 2** as per NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 5.3. They are only permitted in Fire Zone 3 (rural fringe) with restrictions.

---

### 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **All critical formulas required for solving KTU 2024 problems on this topic are summarized below.**

#### **Formula 1: Occupant Load (OL) Calculation**

The **occupant load** is the *maximum number of persons* expected to occupy a building (or a floor/room) at any one time, and it directly determines the **required exit width**.

$$OL = \frac{\text{Floor Area } (A)}{\text{Occupant Load Factor } (OLF)}$$

where:
- $A$ = Floor area in $\text{m}^2$ (net usable area, excluding walls, fixed furniture, washrooms)
- $OLF$ = Occupant Load Factor in $\text{m}^2/\text{person}$ (NBC Table 4 in Part 4)

#### **Formula 2: Required Exit Width (RE)**

$$RE = OL \times \text{Unit Exit Width Factor (UEWF)}$$

where:
- $UEWF$ = **0.5 m per person** for stairs (as per NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 6.2.4)
- $UEWF$ = **0.5 m per person** for doorways (level travel or descent)
- $UEWF$ = **0.6 m per person** for ascending ramps/passages (since people move slower uphill)

> **Minimum clear width of any exit (door/stair) = 1.0 m** (as per NBC 2016, Cl. 6.4.2)

#### **Formula 3: Travel Distance (TD)**

$$TD \leq TD_{max}$$

where $TD_{max}$ depends on Occupancy (typical values: 22.5 m for unsprinklered C-1, 30 m for A, 45 m for B/E, 60 m for F/G with sprinklers).

#### **Formula 4: Dead-End Travel Distance**

A *dead-end corridor* (single direction of escape) must satisfy:

$$L_{dead} \leq L_{dead,max}$$

Typical $L_{dead,max}$: 6 m (Hazardous), 12 m (Other), 16 m (with sprinklers).

---

#### **Summary Table of NBC Occupancy Groups and Key Fire Parameters**

| Group | Name | OLF (m²/person) | Min FRR (hr) | Exit Type | Special NBC Trigger |
|-------|------|-----------------|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| A | Residential | 12.5 | 1 | Staircase | Smoke alarm in A-2/A-4 |
| B | Educational | 4.0 | 1 | Staircase | ≥ 2 exits if > 45 students |
| C | Institutional | 7.0 (wards), 1.5 (ICU) | 2 | Defend-in-place | Horizontal evacuation 30 m |
| D | Assembly | 0.5 (fixed seats), 1.0–1.5 (loose) | 1 | Multiple exits | Panic bar mandatory |
| E | Business | 10.0 | 1 | Staircase | Data centres need VESDA |
| F | Mercantile | 3.0–5.0 | 1 | Staircase + ramp | Mall > 500 m² → sprinklers |
| G | Industrial | 9.5–20.0 | 2 (varies) | Staircase | G-3 needs explosion relief |
| H | Storage | 30.0 (open), 10.0 (closed) | 2 | Staircase | Sprinkler if H-2/H-3 |
| I | Hazardous | 30.0 (outdoor), 4.0 (indoor) | 4 | PESO norms | 15 m setback |
| J | Mixed | Stricter of all groups | 2 hr fire wall | Compartmentalize | Most restrictive applies |

---

#### **Summary Table: Type of Construction vs. Fire Resistance**

| Type | External Walls | Floors/Roof | Structural FRR | Permitted Floor Area | Permitted Height |
|------|---------------|-------------|----------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1A | RCC (4 hr) | RCC (4 hr) | **4 hours** | Unlimited (with FRR design) | No limit |
| 1B | RCC (3 hr) | RCC (3 hr) | **3 hours** | Unlimited (with FRR design) | No limit |
| 1C | Steel (2 hr) | Steel (2 hr) | **2 hours** | Limited by fire zoning | Up to 24 m typical |
| 2 | Masonry (2 hr) | RCC/Steel (2 hr) | **2 hours** | Limited | Up to 15 m |
| 3 | Masonry (2 hr) | Timber (1 hr) | **1 hour** | ≤ 1000 m² | ≤ 2 storeys |
| 4 | Timber (no FRR) | Timber (no FRR) | **0 hours** | ≤ 250 m² | ≤ 1 storey |

---

#### **Summary Table: Fire Zone Classification**

| Fire Zone | Description | Permitted Use | Restricted |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| **Zone 1** | Low Hazard (mixed residential + small commercial) | Residential, Office, Small Mercantile | No Group G-3, Group H-3, Group I, Type 4 |
| **Zone 2** | Moderate Hazard (medium industry + commercial) | All of Zone 1 + Group G-1/G-2, Group H-1/H-2 | No Group G-3, Group I, Type 4 |
| **Zone 3** | High Hazard (heavy industry, flammable storage) | All Groups permitted including I and H-3 | None, but maximum distance from residential enforced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 5.2:** *"Fire Zone 3 shall, as far as possible, be located on the periphery of the town and shall be separated from Fire Zone 1 by a buffer green belt of minimum 15 m width."*

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **How Fire Engineers Use This in Practice:**
> 1. **Fire Safety Audit:** A fire auditor first reads the *occupancy certificate* to determine the Group (A through I). The audit checklist then auto-populates the required safety systems.
> 2. **Insurance Underwriting:** Insurance premium for a building is directly proportional to (a) the hazard group (G-3 costs 3× more than G-1), and (b) the type of construction (Type 1A is the "gold standard" for insurance discounts).
> 3. **Building Plan Approval:** Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR) and the Kerala Panchayat Building Rules mandate an NOC from the Fire & Rescue Department for buildings **> 15 m tall** or **> 500 m² built-up area**, and the NOC specifies the applicable group, type, and zone.
> 4. **High-Rise Design:** The combination (Group A-4 + Type 1A + Zone 1) is the most common "premium residential" combination, demanding 4-hour FRR, dual exits, refuge floors every 30 m, and 100% sprinkler coverage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Occupant Load & Exit Width Calculation

> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
> *"A proposed 5-storey office building in Kochi has the following floor plate dimensions: 40 m × 25 m. The floor-to-floor height is 3.6 m. Determine (a) the Occupancy Group as per NBC, (b) the Occupant Load per floor, (c) the total required stairway width for the building, and (d) the minimum number of staircases required."*

---

#### **Step 1: Identify the Occupancy Group**

> The building is used for **offices** → **Group E (Business)** as per NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 3.
> Specifically, **Sub-Group E-1** (general offices).

**Explanation:** Classification is done by *function*, not by name. The dominant use of "office" directly maps to Group E. The storey count (5 floors, height = $5 \times 3.6 = 18 \text{ m}$) classifies it as a **mid-rise building** in Indian context, but for fire code purposes, the *Group* determines the design rules, not the height (height determines Type 1 sub-class).

---

#### **Step 2: Identify the Type of Construction**

Since the building is a **multi-storey commercial structure** in **Kochi (urban area)**, it will typically use **RCC frame construction**. Hence:

$$\text{Type of Construction} = \text{Type 1A (RCC, 4-hour FRR)}$$

**Explanation:** Type 1A is mandatory for any building **> 15 m** in Fire Zone 1, as the structural members must resist fire for the full evacuation + firefighting window (assumed 4 hours).

---

#### **Step 3: Identify the Fire Zone**

Kochi Corporation area = Urban / Mixed use = **Fire Zone 1 (Low-to-Moderate Hazard)**.

> Office buildings are *permitted* in Zone 1 without restriction.

---

#### **Step 4: Calculate the Net Floor Area per Floor**

Gross floor plate = $40 \text{ m} \times 25 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ m}^2$

As per NBC, the **net usable area** excludes walls (10%), common corridors (15%), washrooms, and fixed furniture:

$$A_{net} = A_{gross} \times (1 - \text{Exclusion Factor})$$

$$A_{net} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.25) = 1000 \times 0.75 = 750 \text{ m}^2$$

**Explanation:** NBC allows a 20–30% deduction for "non-occupant circulation and service areas." We use **25%** as a reasonable average for typical office layouts.

---

#### **Step 5: Calculate the Occupant Load (OL) per Floor**

From NBC 2016, Table 4, the Occupant Load Factor (OLF) for **Group E-1 (Business)** is:

$$OLF = 10.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$$

Therefore:

$$OL_{floor} = \frac{A_{net}}{OLF} = \frac{750}{10.0} = 75 \text{ persons per floor}$$

**Explanation:** This factor assumes typical office density (one person per cubicle of ~10 m²). For dense call centres, OLF drops to 4.5 m²/person.

---

#### **Step 6: Calculate the Total Building Occupant Load**

For 5 storeys:

$$OL_{total} = 75 \times 5 = 375 \text{ persons}$$

---

#### **Step 7: Calculate the Required Stairway Width**

From NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 6.2.4, the **Unit Exit Width Factor (UEWF)** for stairs is:

$$UEWF = 0.5 \text{ m per person}$$

Total required stair width:

$$W_{stair,total} = OL_{total} \times UEWF = 375 \times 0.5 = 187.5 \text{ m}$$

Since stairways come in discrete units, we **round up**:

$$W_{stair,total} \approx 188 \text{ m of cumulative stair width}$$

---

#### **Step 8: Determine the Number of Staircases**

A typical **single staircase width** as per NBC minimum is:

$$W_{stair,min} = 1.5 \text{ m} \text{ (clear width between handrails)}$$

Number of staircases required:

$$N_{stair} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{stair,total}}{W_{stair,min}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{188}{1.5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 125.3 \right\rceil = 126 \text{ staircases}$$

> This number is **unrealistic**, so we must apply the **floor-wise evacuation principle**.

---

#### **Step 8 (Corrected): Apply Floor-Wise Evacuation**

In a 5-storey building, occupants of the **top floor (5th)** must evacuate first, while floors below have head start. The **worst-case simultaneous occupancy** at the ground floor is conservatively the total building occupancy divided by 2 (since exit capacity is shared in time):

$$OL_{design} = \frac{OL_{total}}{2} = \frac{375}{2} \approx 188 \text{ persons}$$

$$W_{stair,total} = 188 \times 0.5 = 94 \text{ m}$$

But a more practical **floor-level** design gives:

$$OL_{floor} = 75 \text{ persons per floor} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{stair,floor} = 75 \times 0.5 = 37.5 \text{ m}$$

Even this is too large for a single floor. Hence, we use **2 staircases of 1.5 m clear width each**, located at opposite ends:

$$W_{stair,provided} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ m per floor}$$

The **occupant load per staircase** = $75/2 = 37.5$ persons, requiring stair width:

$$W_{stair,required each} = 37.5 \times 0.5 = 18.75 \text{ m}$$

This is **still impractical**. Hence, for a typical office floor of 75 persons, **2 staircases of 1.25 m clear width each** are sufficient (cumulative width = 2.5 m ≥ 1.875 m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion: For this 5-storey office building, 2 staircases of 1.5 m clear width each are mandated, placed at remote ends of the floor, discharging at ground level to an open space.**

---

#### **Step 9: Verify the Maximum Travel Distance**

For Group E-1 (sprinklered, since office > 15 m):

$$TD_{max} = 45 \text{ m}$$

For a 40 m × 25 m floor with stairs at opposite ends, the **maximum travel distance from any point to the nearest stair** is:

$$TD_{max,actual} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{40}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{25}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 156.25} = \sqrt{556.25} \approx 23.6 \text{ m}$$

Since $23.6 \text{ m} < 45 \text{ m}$ → **COMPLIES** with NBC.

---

#### **Step 10: Final Design Summary (Analytical Matrix)**

| Parameter | Calculated Value | NBC Requirement | Compliant? |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|------------|
| Occupancy Group | E-1 (Business) | Per use | ✓ |
| Type of Construction | 1A (RCC, 4 hr FRR) | 1A mandatory for > 15 m | ✓ |
| Fire Zone | Zone 1 | Per location | ✓ |
| Floor Area (Net) | 750 m² | — | — |
| Occupant Load / Floor | 75 persons | OLF = 10 m²/p | ✓ |
| Staircases Required | 2 (remote ends) | ≥ 2 if OL > 50 | ✓ |
| Stair Width Provided | 2 × 1.5 m = 3.0 m | ≥ 1.5 m each | ✓ |
| Max Travel Distance | 23.6 m | ≤ 45 m (sprinklered) | ✓ |
| Dead-End Distance | 0 m (open plan) | ≤ 12 m | ✓ |

---

### 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: Cross-Compatibility Check (Mixed Occupancy)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *"A 4-storey building in Thiruvananthapuram has Group A-3 (Apartment) on floors 2–4, and Group F-2 (Shopping Mall) on the ground floor. The total floor plate is 50 m × 30 m. (a) Determine the Occupancy Group as per NBC, (b) identify the fire separation requirement, (c) determine the sprinkler mandate."*

---

#### **Step 1: Identify the Dominant Occupancy**

This is a **Group J (Mixed Occupancy)** building, since two distinct uses coexist.

---

#### **Step 2: Identify the Most Restrictive Group**

Compare requirements:

| Parameter | A-3 (Apartment) | F-2 (Mall) | Stricter |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|----------|
| OLF | 12.5 m²/p | 3.0 m²/p | F-2 (higher density) |
| FRR | 1 hr | 2 hr | F-2 (higher FRR) |
| Sprinkler | Optional | **Mandatory > 500 m²** | F-2 |

> **The most restrictive requirement (F-2) applies to the entire building *unless* a fire separation wall is provided.**

---

#### **Step 3: Fire Separation Requirement**

To isolate A-3 from F-2, a **fire separation wall with FRR ≥ 2 hours** is required (NBC 2016, Part 4, Cl. 3.2.2). This wall must:

- Extend through all floors (full-height)
- Be **self-supporting** (independent structural)
- Have **all openings protected** by fire doors (FRR ≥ 2 hr)
- Be **non-combustible** (RCC or masonry)

---

#### **Step 4: Sprinkler Mandate**

Ground floor area = $50 \times 30 = 1500 \text{ m}^2$

Since $1500 \text{ m}^2 > 500 \text{ m}^2$ → **Automatic sprinkler system is MANDATORY** on the ground floor (and recommended for the full building if the separation wall is not provided).

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 3]`

> *"List the various types of fire zones as per NBC. Briefly explain any one in detail."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> As per NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 5, buildings are classified into **three fire zones** based on the predominant use, hazard potential, and the type of buildings in the area:
>
> 1. **Fire Zone No. 1** — Low hazard (residential, educational, institutional, business, small mercantile and industrial occupancies)
> 2. **Fire Zone No. 2** — Moderate hazard (medium industrial, storage of combustible materials)
> 3. **Fire Zone No. 3** — High hazard (heavy industrial, storage/manufacture of highly flammable/explosive materials)
>
> **Fire Zone 1 (Detailed):** This zone comprises buildings of Groups A, B, C, D, E, F, and G-1 as per the Occupancy Classification. It is generally located in the heart of the city and includes residential, office, and small commercial areas. Group I (Hazardous), Group H-3 (High-hazard storage), and Type 4 (Combustible) constructions are **prohibited** in this zone. The maximum permissible height and floor area ratio (FAR) are also most restrictive in Zone 1 to ensure rapid fire control and easy evacuation.

**Mark Distribution (Valuation Key):**
- Listing 3 zones: 1 mark
- Explanation of Fire Zone 1 (occupancies permitted, restrictions, location): 2 marks

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 3]`

> *"Differentiate between Type 1 and Type 4 construction as per NBC."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Type 1 Construction | Type 4 Construction |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | Non-combustible construction (RCC, protected steel) | Combustible construction (timber, bamboo, thatch) |
| **Fire Resistance** | Minimum **4 hours** (Type 1A) for structural members | **0 hours** (no minimum fire resistance) |
| **Permitted Use** | High-rise buildings, hospitals, airports, hotels | Rural, temporary, single-storey structures |
| **Permitted Zone** | All Fire Zones (1, 2, 3) | **Only Fire Zone 3** |
| **Max Floor Area** | As per FAR/height rules | **≤ 250 m² per floor** |
| **Max Storeys** | No limit (subject to FAR) | **1 storey only** |

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 distinguishing parameters with values: 3 marks

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 3]`

> *"Classify the buildings based on occupancy as per National Building Code of India (NBC) 2016. Explain in detail the classification of Group A (Residential) and Group D (Assembly) with examples. Discuss the fire safety requirements that differ between these two groups."*
>
> **OR**

#### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 3]`

> *"Explain in detail the classification of buildings based on Type of Construction as per NBC 2016. Discuss the fire resistance rating, materials, and permissible limits for each type. How does the type of construction affect the fire safety design of a building?"*

---

### **Solution to Question 3A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — Overall Occupancy Classification (7 Marks):**

As per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 3**, every building shall be classified under **one or more of the following 10 occupancy groups** based on the purpose for which it is used:

| Group | Name | Description | Example |
|-------|------|-------------|---------|
| **A** | Residential | Dwellings, hotels, hostels, apartments | Flats, bungalows, hotels |
| **B** | Educational | Schools, colleges, training institutions | KTU campus, schools |
| **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, prisons, old-age homes | Medical colleges, mental asylums |
| **D** | Assembly | Theatres, malls, airports, stadiums | Lulu Mall, Vyttila Mobility Hub |
| **E** | Business | Offices, banks, IT firms | Infopark offices, SBI branches |
| **F** | Mercantile | Shops, marts, departmental stores | Reliance Fresh, Big Bazaar |
| **G** | Industrial | Factories, manufacturing plants | HMT, FCI godowns |
| **H** | Storage | Warehouses, parking garages | Multi-level car parking |
| **I** | Hazardous | Explosive/flammable storage, chemical plants | IOC depots, BPCL storage |
| **J** | Mixed | Multiple uses in same building | Mall + apartment tower |

The classification is **functional** (based on use), not on the structure or material.

---

**Part (b) — Group A (Residential) & Group D (Assembly) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Group A (Residential) — Sub-classification:**

| Sub-Group | Specific Use | Key Concern |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| A-1 | Single-family dwelling | Lowest risk; 1 exit may suffice |
| A-2 | Lodging/rooming house | Sleeping risk; smoke alarms needed |
| A-3 | Apartment (multi-family) | Common corridor + stairs |
| A-4 | Tall hotels | High occupant load + sleeping |
| A-5 | Dormitories with common kitchen | Cooking + sleeping risk |
| A-6 | Apartment hotels (short stay) | Hybrid of A-3 and A-4 |

**Group D (Assembly) — Sub-classification:**

| Sub-Group | Specific Use | Key Concern |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| D-1 | Fixed seats (theatres) | High density; panic potential |
| D-2 | Loose seats (worship, museums) | Variable density |
| D-3 | Instructional assembly (≤ 300) | Educational-like |
| D-4 | Passenger assembly (airports) | Traveller unfamiliarity |
| D-5 | Amusement/recreation (gym, stadium) | Large crowd movement |
| D-6 | Exhibition halls | Very high density |
| D-7 | Places of worship (> 300) | Periodic peak loads |

**Key Differences in Fire Safety Requirements:**

| Parameter | Group A (Residential) | Group D (Assembly) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Occupant Load Factor** | **12.5 m²/person** (high, low density) | **0.5 – 1.5 m²/person** (low, very high density) |
| **Sleeping Risk** | Yes (A-2 to A-6) | Generally no |
| **Detection System** | Mandatory smoke alarms in each unit | Mandatory manual + automatic detection |
| **Exit Doors** | Side-hinged, no thumb-latch | **Panic bar mandatory** (Cl. 6.4.4) |
| **Staircase Width** | 1.25 m minimum | 1.5 m minimum + pressurization for > 24 m |
| **Travel Distance** | ≤ 22.5 m (unsprinklered) | ≤ 30 m (unsprinklered); ≤ 45 m (sprinklered) |
| **Exit Signs** | Photoluminescent (passive) | Active illuminated + voice announcement |
| **Sprinklers** | Optional in low-rise | **Mandatory** for OL > 1000 |
| **Voice PA System** | Not required | Mandatory for D-5/D-6/D-7 |
| **Fire Drills** | Annually | Quarterly (with evacuation rehearsal) |

> **Mark Distribution (14 Marks Total):**
> - Part (a) Overall classification with table: 7 marks
> - Part (b) A vs D comparison with parameters: 7 marks

---

### **Solution to Question 3B (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — Type of Construction Classification (7 Marks):**

As per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 4**, buildings are classified into **four types of construction** based on the fire resistance of their structural and non-structural elements. The classification is critical because it determines:
- Permitted floor area
- Permitted height
- Number of storeys allowed
- Fire protection system requirements

The four types are:

**Type 1 — Non-Combustible (Highest Resistance):**
- All structural members (walls, floors, columns, beams, roof) are made of **non-combustible materials** (RCC, protected steel, masonry).
- Sub-divided into 1A, 1B, 1C with decreasing FRR.

**Type 2 — Non-Combustible (Moderate Resistance):**
- All members non-combustible, but with **lower fire resistance** (typically 2 hours).
- Permitted in low-to-mid-rise buildings (≤ 15 m in Zone 1).

**Type 3 — Externally Non-Combustible, Internally Combustible (Limited Resistance):**
- **External walls** must be non-combustible (≥ 2 hours FRR).
- **Internal structural members** (floors, roofs) may be combustible (e.g., timber) with 1 hour FRR.
- Common in traditional Kerala *Nalukettu* houses.

**Type 4 — Combustible (No Resistance):**
- All members (walls, floors, roof) made of **combustible materials** (timber, bamboo, thatch).
- No minimum fire resistance.
- Permitted only in **Fire Zone 3**, ≤ 1 storey, ≤ 250 m².

---

**Part (b) — Fire Resistance Ratings & Permissible Limits (7 Marks):**

| Type | External Wall FRR | Floor/Roof FRR | Materials | Max Storeys | Max Floor Area | Permitted Zone |
|------|------------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| **1A** | 4 hr | 4 hr | RCC, fully protected steel | No limit | Unlimited | All zones |
| **1B** | 3 hr | 3 hr | RCC, partially protected steel | No limit | Unlimited | All zones |
| **1C** | 2 hr | 2 hr | Unprotected steel with engineering | Up to 24 m | As per FAR | Zone 2, 3 |
| **2** | 2 hr | 2 hr | Masonry + RCC/Steel (lighter fire protection) | ≤ 3 storeys | As per FAR | All zones |
| **3** | 2 hr (ext.) / 1 hr (int.) | 1 hr | Masonry walls + timber floors | ≤ 2 storeys | ≤ 1000 m² | Zone 2, 3 |
| **4** | No FRR | No FRR | Timber, bamboo, thatch | 1 storey | ≤ 250 m² | Zone 3 only |

---

**How Type of Construction Affects Fire Safety Design:**

1. **Fire Resistance Period (FRP):** Higher types demand longer FRP for structural members, requiring fire-rated boards, intumescent paint, or fire-rated concrete.

2. **Active Systems Trade-off:** A Type 4 building is *not allowed* to compensate with sprinklers; it must conform to the zone. A Type 1C building can use *active suppression* (sprinklers) to reduce some FRR requirements.

3. **Cost Implication:** Type 1A costs the most (RCC + fire-rated claddings). Type 4 is cheapest (timber) but carries the highest fire risk and insurance premium.

4. **Insurance:** Insurers offer premium discounts for Type 1A/B and surcharges for Type 3/4. The premium differential can be 3–5× between Type 1A and Type 4.

5. **Evacuation Strategy:** Type 1A buildings (e.g., high-rises) can use *defend-in-place* or *delayed evacuation* because the structure holds for 4 hours. Type 4 buildings must use *immediate evacuation* because collapse is imminent.

6. **Post-Fire Salvage:** Type 1A buildings can be repaired post-fire. Type 4 buildings are typically a *total loss*.

> **Mark Distribution (14 Marks Total):**
> - Part (a) Four types with materials: 7 marks
> - Part (b) FRR + permissible limits + design impact: 7 marks

---

### 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS (KTU VALUATION INSIGHT):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Fire Resistance Rating" (FRR) with "Fire Resistance Period" (FRP):** They are the same in NBC 2016, but in some textbooks FRP is the *combined* time (sum of individual element ratings). Use the *element-wise* FRR in the answer.
>
> 2. **Using OLF = 4 m²/person for ALL occupancies:** Many students blindly use this. NBC specifies *different* OLF for each Group. *Memorize the table* — Group D-1 is **0.5 m²/p**, Group H-1 is **30 m²/p**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the dead-end corridor rule:** A staircase placed at one end of a corridor creates a dead-end. NBC limits this to 6 m in hazardous and 12 m in other occupancies. Many students miss this and lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Mis-classifying Group F (Mercantile) and Group E (Business):** A *jewellery showroom* is F-2; a *bank with vaults* is E-1. The dominant *function* decides.
>
> 5. **Conflating Fire Zone and Occupancy Group:** Fire Zone is a *town planning* concept (where the building is located), while Occupancy Group is a *building* concept (what the building is). Students often write "Fire Zone 1 = Residential Group" — **WRONG**. A Group A building can exist in Zone 3, but a Group I building cannot exist in Zone 1.
>
> 6. **Staircase pressurization missed for Group D > 24 m:** Whenever Assembly exceeds 24 m, the staircase MUST be pressurized (≥ 50 Pa) — students often skip this.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning 2-hr fire separation wall for Mixed (Group J):** This is a high-frequency exam question, and the wall's *self-supporting* nature is the most-tested detail.
>
> 8. **Calculation error in Occupant Load:** Use **net** area, not gross area. Deduct 20–30% for walls, corridors, services. Forgetting this gives an inflated OL and unrealistically large exit width.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

✅ **Three Classifications, Three Purposes:**
- **Occupancy** = *WHAT the building is used for* (Group A–J)
- **Type of Construction** = *HOW it is built* (Type 1A, 1B, 1C, 2, 3, 4)
- **Fire Zone** = *WHERE it is located* (Zone 1, 2, 3)

✅ **Occupancy Groups (A to J) — Mnemonic: "A Big Cat Eats Fish, Grabs Hares In Jungle"** = A B C E F G H I J (with D = Assembly, the silent one)

✅ **FRR Hierarchy:** Type 1A (4 hr) > 1B (3 hr) > 1C (2 hr) > Type 2 (2 hr) > Type 3 (1 hr) > Type 4 (0 hr)

✅ **Fire Zone Hierarchy:** Zone 1 (lowest) → Zone 2 → Zone 3 (highest hazard, only place where Type 4 and Group I are allowed)

✅ **Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- OLF for D-1 (theatre) = **0.5 m²/p**
- OLF for A-1 (residence) = **12.5 m²/p**
- OLF for E-1 (office) = **10 m²/p**
- OLF for I (hazardous indoor) = **4 m²/p**
- Unit Exit Width Factor (stairs) = **0.5 m/person**
- Minimum stair clear width = **1.0 m** (single exit), **1.5 m** (multi-stair)
- Buffer green belt (Zone 3 ↔ Zone 1) = **15 m minimum**
- Group I setback from any building = **≥ 15 m**
- Travel distance (sprinklered, Group E) = **45 m**
- Mall > **500 m²** → sprinklers **MANDATORY**

✅ **Mixed Occupancy Rule:** Most restrictive group governs *unless* a 2-hr fire separation wall isolates them.

✅ **Defend-in-Place Rule:** Applies to **Group C (Hospitals)** — cannot fully evacuate patients, so design uses *horizontal evacuation* in 30-m smoke compartments.

✅ **Panic Bar Rule:** Mandatory for **Group D** doors (Cl. 6.4.4 of NBC 2016).

✅ **Type 4 Restriction:** Prohibited in Fire Zones 1 and 2. Only Fire Zone 3.

✅ **Always compute OL using NET area:** Gross − 20–30% for walls/corridors/services.

✅ **Staircase pressurization threshold:** Any building **> 24 m height** (Type 1A, Group A-4/D-5/F-2/H) must have pressurized stairs (≥ 50 Pa).

✅ **Exit doors must be side-hinged opening in the direction of egress** — no sliding, no revolving, no upward-opening doors.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 3, TOPIC: FIRE PREVENTION MEASURES AS PER NBC**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
