# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (NBC 2016, Part 4 & Part 8)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXIT (NBC 2016 Definition):** An exit is a continuous, unobstructed means of egress from any point in a building or structure to a public way, consisting of three integrated components: **Exit Access**, **Exit**, and **Exit Discharge**.

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** is the apex reference document governing fire and life safety in Indian buildings. **Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety** and **Part 8 – Building Services** lay down mandatory exit requirements applicable to **ALL individual occupancies** (Residential, Educational, Institutional, Assembly, Business, Mercantile, Industrial, Storage, and Hazardous).

> [!NOTE]
> **General Exit Requirements (NBC 4.4) – Core Philosophy:** *Every building must be designed so that **every occupant** can reach a **safe place** in the **shortest possible time** through a **protected escape route**, without depending on any single component (single means of egress) for safety.*

---

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Occupant Load (OL)** | Maximum number of persons permitted to occupy a building or portion thereof at any one time. |
| **Exit Access** | The portion of a means of egress that leads from any occupied point to an exit (e.g., corridor, aisle, passageway). |
| **Exit** | The portion of a means of egress that is separated from other interior spaces of a building by fire-resistive construction (e.g., enclosed staircase, fire-rated corridor, external exit). |
| **Exit Discharge** | The portion of a means of egress between the termination of an exit and a public way (e.g., ground-level open area, street). |
| **Travel Distance (TD)** | The distance measured from the most remote point in the occupied area, along the natural path of egress, to the nearest exit. |
| **Unit of Exit Width (UEW)** | Standardized measure of exit width used to calculate required exit capacity. In NBC: **500 mm = 1 Unit**. |
| **Dead-End Corridor** | A corridor or passage having an exit (or exit access) at one end only, terminating at a blank wall. |
| **Refuge Area** | Designated safe waiting area in staircases for persons unable to evacuate quickly (especially in high-rise buildings). |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Building as a Theatre" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Theatre Evacuation:**
> Imagine you are sitting in a packed cinema hall (occupant load = 600). When fire breaks out, you need to get out *fast*. The corridor you walk in is the **exit access**, the staircase at the end is the **exit**, and the open street outside is the **exit discharge**. Now ask:
> 1. Are the corridor and staircase wide enough? (Capacity)
> 2. How far is the staircase from your seat? (Travel distance)
> 3. Are there enough staircases so you are NOT stuck? (Number of exits)
> 4. Can the fire reach the staircase before you? (Fire-rated enclosure)
> 5. Can you see the exit in thick smoke? (Exit signage & emergency lighting)
>
> **NBC exit requirements exist so that the answer to ALL of these questions is YES — every time, for every occupant.**

---

## 1.4 General Fire Safety Requirements Applicable to All Occupancies (NBC 4.4 Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Applicability:** The following requirements apply **irrespective of occupancy type** (Group A through J):

1. **Minimum Two Exits** — Every building, room, or space with occupant load > **50 persons** shall have at least **two exits**, located as far apart as possible.
2. **No Dead-End Distance** exceeding **6.0 m** for educational/institutional; **specific to occupancy** otherwise.
3. **Illumination of Exits** — All exits shall be illuminated at all times when the building is occupied.
4. **Exit Signs** — Every exit, exit access, and direction of travel shall be marked with visible, photoluminescent/illuminated signs, visible from a distance of at least **30 m** (or 100 ft equivalent).
5. **Doors Shall Swing in the Direction of Egress** — Where occupant load > **50**, all exit doors shall open outward (in the direction of exit travel).
6. **No Locking on Exits** — No lock, bolt, or fastening device shall impede egress; only panic hardware / single-motion latch permitted.
7. **Capacity of Exits** — Calculated using **Unit of Exit Width** concept.
8. **Travel Distance** — Shall not exceed the prescribed limit (typically 22.5 m to 45 m depending on occupancy).
9. **Staircase Enclosure** — Enclosed by **2-hour fire-rated walls** for high-rise buildings; **1-hour** for low-rise.
10. **Smoke Control & Venting** — Required in basements, atria, and high-rise staircases.
11. **Refuge Areas** — Mandatory in buildings with **floor height > 24 m** (High-rise definition per NBC).

---

## 1.5 Classification of Exits (Per NBC)

NBC recognizes the following **types of exits**:

1. **Horizontal Exit** — Exit through a fire wall to an adjacent fire compartment (minimum **2-hour fire resistance**).
2. **Vertical Exit** — Staircase serving multiple floors.
3. **External Exit (Fire Escape)** — Open external staircase on the building façade.
4. **Ramp Exit** — Sloped path with gradient ≤ **1:12** (per NBC).
5. **Doorways** — Final exit opening to a public way.

---

## 1.6 Standard Metrics (to be remembered verbatim)

> [!NOTE]
> - **1 Unit of Exit Width (UEW)** = **500 mm (or 0.5 m)** — NBC standard.
> - **Minimum Staircase Width** = **1250 mm** (1.25 m) for buildings with height ≤ 15 m; **1500 mm** for higher.
> - **Maximum Travel Distance** = **22.5 m (single exit)** to **45 m (with sprinklers)** depending on occupancy.
> - **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)** — Area per person, prescribed per occupancy (e.g., **1.5 m²/person** for assembly, **4.5 m²/person** for office).
> - **Fire Rating of Exit Enclosure** = **2 hours** (high-rise); **1 hour** (low-rise).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Philosophy of Exit Design – The "5-Pillar Egress Model"

KTU examiners frequently test the *conceptual logic* of NBC's exit philosophy. Every safe exit design rests on **five pillars**:

1. **Capacity** — Exit must be wide enough for the occupant load.
2. **Number** — At least **two** independent exits (redundancy principle).
3. **Distance** — Travel distance must be within limits.
4. **Protection** — Exit must be enclosed, fire-rated, smoke-free.
5. **Visibility** — Exit must be illuminated, signed, and reachable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Exit Exception (NBC 4.4.2.2):** A single exit is permitted ONLY when:
> - Occupant load ≤ **50**, **AND**
> - Travel distance ≤ **22.5 m**, **AND**
> - Building height ≤ **15 m** (3 storeys).
>
> Failure to satisfy ANY of these → **minimum 2 exits mandatory**.

---

## 2.2 Occupant Load (OL) Calculation

The **Occupant Load** is the **foundation** of every exit design calculation. The procedure (per NBC 4.4.1) is:

> **Step 1:** Identify the occupancy classification of the building.
> **Step 2:** Determine the **net usable floor area** (gross area minus permanent walls, columns, fixed furniture).
> **Step 3:** Divide by the **Occupant Load Factor (OLF)** prescribed for that occupancy.
> **Step 4:** Round **UP** to the next whole number (occupant load is always a whole person, never fractional).

### Occupant Load Factors (OLF) per NBC – KTU High-Yield Table

| Occupancy | Sub-type | OLF (m²/person) |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **A – Residential** | Single-family dwelling | **12.5** |
| | Apartment, hotel | **18.0** |
| **B – Educational** | Classroom | **1.5** |
| | Workshop, laboratory | **4.5** |
| **C – Institutional** | In-patient ward | **8.0** |
| | Day-care | **3.0** |
| **D – Assembly** | Standing space | **0.5** |
| | Fixed seating | **0.6** (per seat) |
| | Dining (with tables) | **1.5** |
| **E – Business** | Office | **4.5** |
| **F – Mercantile** | Street-floor, basement | **3.0** |
| | Upper floors, storage | **6.0** |
| **G – Industrial** | General | **9.5** |
| **H – Storage** | Warehouse | **28.0** |
| **I – Hazardous** | — | **Per special NBC clause** |

---

## 2.3 Exit Capacity – Unit of Exit Width Concept

NBC standardizes exit capacity using the **"Unit of Exit Width (UEW)"** concept:

> **1 Unit of Exit Width = 500 mm of clear width**

The number of occupants that can pass through **1 UEW per minute** is called the **Capacity Factor (CF)**, which depends on the **type of exit**:

| Type of Exit | Capacity Factor (Persons per UEW per minute) |
|--------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Staircase (going down)** | **25** |
| **Staircase (going up)** | **17** |
| **Level passage / corridor / ramp / door** | **60** |
| **Door at final discharge** | **90** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Staircase direction matters:** People move **slower UP** a staircase (CF = 17) compared to **DOWN** (CF = 25). During fire, evacuation is always **downward**, so **CF = 25** is used for normal evacuation calculations. The **UP capacity (17)** is used for **fire-fighter access** (reverse direction).

---

## 2.4 Number of Exits (N) Calculation

The **minimum number of exits** is determined by the total occupant load and the capacity of each exit:

$$
N_{\text{required}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Occupant Load (OL)}}{\text{Capacity per Exit}} \right\rceil
$$

Where the **Capacity per Exit** is:

$$
\text{Capacity per Exit} = \text{Width of Exit (m)} \times \text{UEW per m} \times \text{CF} \times t_{\text{evac}}
$$

> **Simplified KTU Exam Form (most commonly used):**

$$
\text{Capacity of 1 UEW} = \text{CF} \times 1 = \text{CF} \text{ persons/minute}
$$

$$
N_{\text{exit units}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{OL}}{\text{CF} \times 1} \right\rceil \text{ (for 1 minute evacuation)}
$$

For multi-minute evacuation:

$$
N_{\text{exit units}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{OL}}{\text{CF} \times t_{\text{evac}}} \right\rceil
$$

---

## 2.5 Travel Distance (TD) Requirements

Travel distance is the **horizontal** distance measured along the **natural path of egress** (corridors, aisles, etc.) from the **most remote point** in the occupied area to the **nearest exit**.

| Occupancy | Travel Distance (Non-Sprinklered) | Travel Distance (Sprinklered) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **A – Residential** | 30 m | 45 m |
| **B – Educational** | 22.5 m | 30 m |
| **C – Institutional** | 22.5 m | 30 m |
| **D – Assembly** | 30 m | 45 m |
| **E – Business** | 30 m | 45 m |
| **F – Mercantile** | 30 m | 45 m |
| **G – Industrial** | 30 m | 45 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dead-End Limit:** A dead-end corridor shall not exceed **6.0 m** for any occupancy (NBC 4.4.2.3).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — it is the single most important reference for KTU Part B (14-mark) problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------|
| 1 | Occupant Load (OL) | $\text{OL} = \dfrac{\text{Net Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}{\text{Occupant Load Factor (m}^2\text{/person)}}$ | persons |
| 2 | Number of Exit Units (UEW) | $N_{\text{UEW}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{OL}}{\text{CF} \times t_{\text{evac}}} \right\rceil$ | units |
| 3 | Total Exit Width | $W_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{UEW}} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$ | m |
| 4 | Width per Exit | $W_{\text{exit}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{exits}}}$ | m |
| 5 | Minimum Width per Exit | $W_{\text{exit}} \geq 1.25 \text{ m}$ (low-rise); $\geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ (high-rise) | m |
| 6 | Travel Distance Limit | $TD \leq 22.5$–$45 \text{ m}$ (per occupancy & sprinkler status) | m |
| 7 | Staircase Riser | $R \leq 150 \text{ mm}$ (residential); $\leq 190 \text{ mm}$ (other) | mm |
| 8 | Staircase Tread | $T \geq 250 \text{ mm}$ (residential); $\geq 250 \text{ mm}$ (other) | mm |
| 9 | 2R + T Rule | $2R + T \in [500 \text{ mm}, 700 \text{ mm}]$ | mm |
| 10 | Head Room | $\geq 2.2 \text{ m}$ (clear) | m |
| 11 | Landing Width | $\geq \text{Staircase Width}$ | m |
| 12 | Number of Exits Required | $\geq 2$ (if OL > 50 or TD > limit) | — |

---

## 2.7 Staircase Geometry – The "2R + T" Rule

For every staircase serving as a means of egress:

$$
2R + T \in [500 \text{ mm},\ 700 \text{ mm}]
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Riser height (mm)
- $T$ = Tread width (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is derived from the **ergonomic "average human stride"** principle. $2R + T$ represents **one full step cycle** (vertical up + horizontal forward). If less than 500 mm → steps are too small (tripping hazard). If more than 700 mm → steps are too large (unclimbable in emergency).

---

## 2.8 Engineering Real-World Application

In practice, the **Exit Width Calculation** drives:
1. **Structural design** of staircase enclosures (slab thickness, fire-rated walls).
2. **HVAC & pressurization** design (positive pressure of **12.5 Pa** in stairwell).
3. **Signage and lighting layout** (one exit sign per 30 m of corridor).
4. **Fire door selection** (2-hour fire rating, panic bar, self-closing).
5. **Refuge area sizing** = **0.5 m² per wheelchair-bound person** plus 1 wheelchair space per **200 occupants** per floor.
6. **Evacuation time estimation** (used in performance-based design under NBC 2016 Part 4, Annex A).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Performance-based design (e.g., **RSET vs ASET** — Required vs Available Safe Egress Time) is now permitted under NBC 2016. RSET is calculated using **occupant load × travel time + detection time + premovement time**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Occupant Load & Exit Width for a Classroom Building

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> An educational building has **4 floors**, each with **8 classrooms of 60 m²** each, and a **corridor of 200 m²** per floor. Determine the **occupant load per floor** and the **total exit width required** for a staircase, assuming evacuation time = **2.5 minutes**.

### Step 1: Identify the Occupancy and OLF

The building is **Educational (Group B)**. Per NBC Table 3 (OLF):
- **Classroom OLF** = **1.5 m²/person**
- **Corridor OLF** = **4.5 m²/person** (treated as ancillary)

### Step 2: Calculate Net Floor Area

$$
\text{Classroom area per floor} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Corridor area per floor} = 200 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total net floor area per floor} = 480 + 200 = 680 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Occupant Load per Floor

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{classroom}} = \frac{480}{1.5} = 320 \text{ persons}
$$

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{corridor}} = \frac{200}{4.5} = 44.44 \approx 45 \text{ persons}
$$

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{per floor}} = 320 + 45 = 365 \text{ persons}
$$

> Per NBC, round **up** to **365 persons** (already whole).

### Step 4: Total Occupant Load for 4 Floors

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 365 = 1460 \text{ persons}
$$

### Step 5: Determine Capacity Factor

For **downward staircase evacuation** (normal fire scenario):
$$
\text{CF} = 25 \text{ persons per UEW per minute}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate Required Number of Exit Units (UEW)

$$
N_{\text{UEW}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{OL}_{\text{total}}}{\text{CF} \times t_{\text{evac}}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{UEW}} = \left\lceil \frac{1460}{25 \times 2.5} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{UEW}} = \left\lceil \frac{1460}{62.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 23.36 \rceil = 24 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 7: Calculate Total Exit Width

$$
W_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{UEW}} \times 0.5 \text{ m} = 24 \times 0.5 = 12.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 8: Distribute Among 4 Staircases (assuming N = 4)

$$
W_{\text{per exit}} = \frac{12.0}{4} = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

> **Check:** $W_{\text{per exit}} = 3.0 \text{ m} \geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ (high-rise minimum) ✅

### Final Answer

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| OL per floor | **365 persons** |
| OL total (4 floors) | **1460 persons** |
| Required UEW | **24 units** |
| Total exit width | **12.0 m** |
| Width per staircase (4 staircases) | **3.0 m** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Travel Distance & Number of Exits Verification

**Problem Statement:**
> An office building floor has dimensions **50 m × 20 m** with a **central corridor** of **2 m width**. The occupant load is **220 persons**. Verify the number of exits and travel distance.

### Step 1: Occupancy and OLF

Office (Group E): **OLF = 4.5 m²/person**

### Step 2: Net Area Calculation

$$
\text{Net Area} = 50 \times 20 = 1000 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Occupant Load (verify)

$$
\text{OL} = \frac{1000}{4.5} = 222.22 \approx 223 \text{ persons}
$$

> Given OL = 220, we proceed with 220 (slightly below calculated, acceptable).

### Step 4: Number of Exits (NBC Rule)

Since OL = 220 > 50:
$$
N_{\text{required}} = \text{at least 2 exits}
$$

For better safety and load distribution, **3 exits** are typically provided.

### Step 5: Travel Distance Verification

The most remote point is the corner of the floor, **diagonally opposite** to the nearest exit.

Path along corridor (assuming exit at one end):
- Distance from far corner to corridor = 10 m (half width)
- Distance along corridor = 50 m
- **Total = 60 m** (exceeds 30 m limit ❌)

With **2 exits at opposite ends** of corridor:
- Maximum distance = 10 m + 25 m = 35 m ❌ (still exceeds 30 m)
- **Solution:** Add a **mid-corridor exit** to reduce TD to ~25 m ✅

### Step 6: Dead-End Check

If the corridor has a **single T-junction exit**, dead-end length = 10 m > 6 m ❌ → **non-compliant** → redesign required.

### Final Verification Matrix

| Parameter | Calculated | NBC Limit | Status |
|-----------|------------|-----------|--------|
| OL | 223 persons | — | — |
| Min. Exits | 2 (3 preferred) | ≥ 2 | ✅ |
| Travel Distance | 25 m (with mid-exit) | 30 m | ✅ |
| Dead-End | 0 m (redesigned) | 6 m | ✅ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Staircase Geometry using 2R + T Rule

**Problem Statement:**
> Design the riser (R) and tread (T) of a staircase for a high-rise residential building, given the **floor-to-floor height = 3.2 m**, and **15 risers per flight**.

### Step 1: Calculate Riser Height

$$
R = \frac{\text{Floor height}}{\text{Number of risers}} = \frac{3.2 \text{ m}}{15} = 0.2133 \text{ m} = 213.33 \text{ mm}
$$

> NBC limit for residential: $R \leq 150$ mm. ❌ **Non-compliant**.

### Step 2: Recalculate Number of Risers

For R = 150 mm:

$$
N_{\text{risers}} = \frac{3200}{150} = 21.33 \rightarrow 22 \text{ risers}
$$

### Step 3: Recalculate Actual Riser

$$
R_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3200}{22} = 145.45 \text{ mm} \quad ✅
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Tread using 2R + T Rule

$$
2R + T = 600 \text{ mm (ideal mid-value)}
$$

$$
T = 600 - 2(145.45) = 600 - 290.9 = 309.1 \text{ mm}
$$

> NBC: $T \geq 250$ mm ✅

### Final Design

| Parameter | Value | NBC Limit | Status |
|-----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| Riser (R) | **145.45 mm** | $\leq 150$ mm | ✅ |
| Tread (T) | **309.1 mm** | $\geq 250$ mm | ✅ |
| 2R + T | **600 mm** | 500–700 mm | ✅ |
| No. of Risers | **22** (12 + 1 landing + 9 or 11+1+10) | — | — |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Exit Requirements at a Glance

| Occupancy | Min. Exits | Travel Distance (m) | Dead End (m) | Min. Staircase Width (mm) | OLF (m²/p) |
|-----------|------------|--------------------|--------------|--------------------------|------------|
| A – Residential | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1250 | 12.5–18 |
| B – Educational | 2 | 22.5 | 6 | 1500 | 1.5 |
| C – Institutional | 2 | 22.5 | 6 | 1500 | 8 |
| D – Assembly | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1500–2000 | 0.5–1.5 |
| E – Business | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1500 | 4.5 |
| F – Mercantile | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1500 | 3–6 |
| G – Industrial | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1500 | 9.5 |
| H – Storage | 2 | 30 | 6 | 1500 | 28 |
| I – Hazardous | 2 | 22.5 | 0 | 1500 | Special |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Most occupancies share **TD = 30 m, OLF ≈ 4.5**, except **Educational/Institutional (TD = 22.5, OLF = 1.5)** and **Hazardous (strictest)**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]: Define the terms "Exit Access", "Exit", and "Exit Discharge" as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Exit Access** (1 mark): The portion of a means of egress that leads from any occupied point in a building to an **exit**. It includes corridors, aisles, passageways, and doors that are **not** part of a fire-rated enclosure.
- **Exit** (1 mark): The portion of a means of egress that is **separated from other interior spaces of the building by fire-resistive construction** (fire-rated walls, doors, floors) to provide a protected path of travel. Examples: enclosed staircase, fire-rated lobby, horizontal exit through a fire wall.
- **Exit Discharge** (1 mark): The portion of a means of egress between the **termination of an exit** and a **public way** (street, road, open ground). It is the final link connecting the protected exit to a safe open space.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]: What is "Unit of Exit Width"? State its value as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition** (2 marks): The **Unit of Exit Width (UEW)** is a standardized measure of the width of an exit, used to calculate the **capacity** of that exit in terms of the number of persons it can serve. The width is measured as the **clear, unobstructed width** between the faces of the walls, handrails, or other obstructions.
- **Standard Value** (1 mark): As per NBC 2016, **1 Unit of Exit Width = 500 mm (0.5 m)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Choice: Answer ONE full question (14 marks). All sub-parts are compulsory.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Question:**
A **5-storey office building** has a floor plate of **40 m × 25 m** per floor, with a **central core of 10 m × 10 m** occupied by lifts, services, and electrical rooms. The **occupant load factor (OLF)** for office use is **4.5 m²/person**. Each floor has a **2 m wide central corridor** running the full length.

**(a)** Calculate the **total occupant load** for the entire building. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the **number of staircases, total exit width, and width per staircase**, assuming a staircase evacuation time of **3 minutes** and the capacity factor for downward evacuation. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a): Total Occupant Load (7 marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Net Usable Floor Area per Floor** (2 marks)

$$
\text{Gross Floor Area} = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Core Area} = 10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Net Usable Area per Floor} = 1000 - 100 = 900 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply Occupant Load Factor** (2 marks)

$$
\text{OL per Floor} = \frac{900}{4.5} = 200 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 3: Total Occupant Load for 5 Floors** (2 marks)

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 4: Verification of OLF Compliance** (1 mark)

Since OLF for office is 4.5 m²/person, and the calculated load is 200 persons/floor (within design limits), the calculation is valid.

**Answer:** $\boxed{\text{Total Occupant Load} = 1000 \text{ persons}}$

---

##### Part (b): Number of Staircases and Exit Width (7 marks)

**Step 1: Determine Capacity Factor (CF)** (1 mark)

For **downward staircase evacuation** (normal fire scenario):
$$
\text{CF} = 25 \text{ persons per UEW per minute}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Required UEW** (2 marks)

$$
N_{\text{UEW}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{OL}_{\text{total}}}{\text{CF} \times t_{\text{evac}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{25 \times 3} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 13.33 \rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{UEW}} = 14 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Exit Width** (1 mark)

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 14 \times 0.5 \text{ m} = 7.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Determine Number of Staircases** (1 mark)

Since OL = 1000 > 50, minimum 2 staircases required. For redundancy and balanced evacuation, use **4 staircases** (one at each corner of the central core).

**Step 5: Width per Staircase** (1 mark)

$$
W_{\text{per staircase}} = \frac{7.0}{4} = 1.75 \text{ m}
$$

> **Check:** $1.75 \text{ m} \geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ (NBC high-rise minimum) ✅

**Step 6: Travel Distance Check** (1 mark)

Most remote point: opposite corner of floor = diagonal distance + corridor walk.

$$
\text{TD}_{\max} = \sqrt{(15)^2 + (12.5)^2} + 2.5 = \sqrt{225 + 156.25} + 2.5 = 19.53 + 2.5 = 22.03 \text{ m}
$$

> **NBC Limit for Office:** 30 m. $\text{TD} = 22.03 \text{ m} \leq 30 \text{ m}$ ✅

**Final Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Total Occupant Load | **1000 persons** |
| Required UEW | **14 units** |
| Total Exit Width | **7.0 m** |
| Number of Staircases | **4** |
| Width per Staircase | **1.75 m** |
| Max Travel Distance | **22.03 m** (within 30 m limit) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] – Alternative Choice**

**Question:**
A **4-storey residential apartment building** has floor dimensions of **30 m × 20 m**, with a **staircase core of 6 m × 4 m** in one corner. The OLF for apartments is **12.5 m²/person**. Each staircase has a clear width of **1.5 m**.

**(a)** Calculate the **occupant load per floor** and the **total occupant load** for the building. (7 marks)

**(b)** Verify whether the **single staircase of 1.5 m width is sufficient**, or if additional exits are required. Use **travel distance criteria** and **dead-end limits** as per NBC 2016. Assume evacuation time = **2.5 minutes**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a): Occupant Load Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Gross Floor Area** (1 mark)

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Staircase Core Area** (1 mark)

$$
A_{\text{core}} = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Net Usable Floor Area** (2 marks)

$$
A_{\text{net}} = 600 - 24 = 576 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Occupant Load per Floor** (1 mark)

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{per floor}} = \frac{576}{12.5} = 46.08 \approx 47 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 5: Total Occupant Load for 4 Floors** (2 marks)

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 47 = 188 \text{ persons}
$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\text{OL per floor} = 47 \text{ persons}; \text{OL total} = 188 \text{ persons}}$

---

##### Part (b): Staircase Adequacy Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Single Exit vs. Multiple Exit Rule** (2 marks)

- OL per floor = 47 persons (less than 50, but only marginally).
- OL total = 188 persons (far exceeds 50).
- Per NBC 4.4.2: **A single exit is permitted only if OL ≤ 50 AND building height ≤ 15 m AND travel distance ≤ 22.5 m.**

**Step 2: Travel Distance Check** (2 marks)

Most remote point: opposite corner of floor from the staircase.
- Diagonal distance = $\sqrt{(24)^2 + (20)^2} = \sqrt{576 + 400} = \sqrt{976} = 31.24$ m
- NBC limit for residential = 30 m.
- $31.24 \text{ m} > 30 \text{ m}$ ❌ **Exceeds limit — additional exit required.**

**Step 3: Dead-End Check** (1 mark)

Since the staircase is in **one corner**, all apartments on the adjacent side form a dead-end.
- Dead-end length = 24 m > 6 m ❌ **Severely non-compliant.**

**Step 4: Capacity Check of 1.5 m Staircase** (1 mark)

For 2.5 minutes evacuation:
$$
\text{Capacity} = 1.5 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{0.5} \text{ UEW/m} \times 25 \text{ p/UEW/min} \times 2.5 \text{ min} = 187.5 \text{ persons}
$$

$$
\text{OL}_{\text{total}} = 188 \text{ persons}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity} = 187.5 < 188 \rightarrow \text{INADEQUATE by } 0.5 \text{ person} ❌
$$

**Step 5: Recommended Design Modification** (1 mark)

| Recommendation | Specification |
|----------------|---------------|
| **Add second staircase** | At diagonally opposite corner |
| **Staircase width each** | 1.5 m (redundancy factor satisfied) |
| **Reduces TD** | To $\leq 22.5$ m ✅ |
| **Eliminates dead-end** | If central corridor connects both staircases ✅ |

**Final Verdict:** The single 1.5 m staircase is **NOT sufficient**. A **second staircase** at the diagonally opposite corner is **mandatory** to satisfy travel distance, dead-end, and capacity requirements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract core/service areas** from gross floor area when calculating OL. Always use **NET USABLE AREA**.
> 2. **Using CF = 60 (corridor value) for staircases**. Use CF = 25 for **downward** staircase evacuation.
> 3. **Rounding OL DOWN** instead of UP. NBC mandates **round UP to next whole person**.
> 4. **Forgetting 2R + T rule**. Staircase design questions ALWAYS test this. Common answer: $R = 150$ mm, $T = 250$ mm → 2R+T = 550 mm ✅
> 5. **Confusing "Exit Width" with "Door Width"**. Exit width = clear width between handrails/walls, NOT door leaf width.
> 6. **Single-exit exception misuse**: Students often claim "1 exit is enough" for OL = 49, ignoring the **22.5 m TD limit** or the **15 m building height** clause. ALL three conditions must be satisfied.
> 7. **Missing Dead-End compliance**: Even if exit count is correct, a 10 m dead-end corridor is a direct **deduction of 1-2 marks**.
> 8. **Ignoring direction of door swing**: Doors for OL > 50 must open **in the direction of egress**. Marking them as "swing inward" loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **1 UEW = 500 mm = 0.5 m** (NBC standard).
- ✅ **CF for staircase DOWN = 25**, **UP = 17**, **level = 60**, **door = 90** (persons/UEW/min).
- ✅ **OL = Net Floor Area / OLF** (always round UP).
- ✅ **2R + T must lie in [500, 700] mm** for every egress staircase.
- ✅ **R ≤ 150 mm (residential), ≤ 190 mm (other)**; **T ≥ 250 mm**.
- ✅ **Staircase width ≥ 1.25 m (low-rise, ≤15 m), ≥ 1.5 m (high-rise, >15 m)**.
- ✅ **Minimum 2 exits** if **OL > 50** OR **building height > 15 m** OR **TD > 22.5 m**.
- ✅ **Dead-end corridor ≤ 6.0 m** for all occupancies.
- ✅ **Exit doors swing outward** in direction of egress when **OL > 50**.
- ✅ **Travel Distance limits**: 22.5 m (Educational, Institutional), 30 m (others), 45 m (sprinklered).
- ✅ **Fire rating of exit enclosure**: 1 hour (low-rise), **2 hours (high-rise)**.
- ✅ **Headroom in staircase ≥ 2.2 m**; **landing width ≥ staircase width**.
- ✅ **Headroom in staircase ≥ 2.2 m**; **landing width ≥ staircase width**.
- ✅ **Refuge area**: mandatory when **building height > 24 m**; size = 0.5 m²/wheelchair user.
- ✅ **Exit signs** must be visible from **30 m distance**; photoluminescent + illuminated.
- ✅ **5-Pillar Egress Model**: Capacity + Number + Distance + Protection + Visibility.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For any 14-mark Part B question on this topic, structure your answer in **5 logical steps**: (1) Identify occupancy + OLF, (2) Calculate OL, (3) Determine CF, (4) Calculate UEW and width, (5) Verify TD + dead-end + staircase geometry. This structure alone guarantees **80%+ marks** even with minor calculation errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements for Different Types of Exit Access and Exits: Doorway, Corridors and Passageways, Horizontal Exits, Internal Staircases, Exit Passageways, External Staircases, Ramps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Requirements for Different Types of Exit Access and Exits: Doorway, Corridors and Passageways, Horizontal Exits, Internal Staircases, Exit Passageways, External Staircases, Ramps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Exit Access – Exit Continuum: A Foundational Concept

In the context of life safety engineering, the term **"Means of Egress"** is the legally codified continuous path of travel that allows occupants to move from **any point within a building** to a **place of ultimate safety (public way)**. Under the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)** and **NFPA 101 – Life Safety Code**, the means of egress is mathematically and physically divided into three distinct sub-components:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Egress Equation (K TU Exam High-Yield):**
> $$\text{Exit Access} \;+\; \text{Exit} \;+\; \text{Exit Discharge} \;=\; \text{Complete Means of Egress}$$

1. **Exit Access** – That portion of the means of egress which leads from any occupied point in a building to an **exit** (e.g., rooms, doorways, corridors, passageways, ramps).
2. **Exit** – A portion of the means of egress that is **separated from other building spaces** by fire-resistant construction, providing a protected path of travel (e.g., enclosed staircases, horizontal exits, exit passageways, external staircases).
3. **Exit Discharge** – The final portion leading from the termination of an exit to a **public way** (street, open space, refuge area).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for B.Tech Students):**
Imagine a multi-storey shopping mall during a fire alarm. A mother with two children is on the **4th floor inside a clothing store**. The "exit access" is the **doorway of the shop, the mall corridor, and the ramp connecting wings**. The "exit" is the **enclosed fire-rated staircase with pressurization**. The "exit discharge" is the **final door opening into the public street**. If **any single component** fails (e.g., a locked corridor door or a stairway filled with smoke), the entire evacuation chain breaks and lives are lost. This is why every component has **codified, non-negotiable design requirements**.

### 1.2 Component-Wise Working Definitions

| Sl. No. | Component | Formal Definition (NBC 2016 / NFPA 101) |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Doorway** | An opening in a wall, partition, or barrier that permits the passage of occupants and is fitted with a door, shutter, or similar closure. |
| 2 | **Corridor / Passageway** | A narrow enclosed passage providing direct communication between rooms, apartments, halls, or staircases, with **fire-resistant walls, ceiling, and floor**. |
| 3 | **Horizontal Exit** | An exit consisting of one or more openings through or around a **fire wall**, or a bridge or balcony connecting two buildings/floors, used as a means of egress. |
| 4 | **Internal Staircase** | A staircase **completely enclosed by fire-resistant walls and self-closing fire doors**, providing a vertical protected path of egress. |
| 5 | **Exit Passageway** | A horizontal extension of an exit stairway that provides a protected path of travel from the stairway to the exit discharge. |
| 6 | **External Staircase** | A staircase **open to the external atmosphere**, used as a secondary or alternative means of egress. |
| 7 | **Ramp** | An inclined walking surface with a gradient not exceeding **1 in 10 (10 %)** for occupant evacuation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Litmus Test:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently ask students to *differentiate* between Exit Access and Exit components. The presence of **fire-rated enclosure** is the **single most important parameter** that separates an Exit from an Exit Access element.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Design Philosophy – The "Three Pillars" of Egress Design

Every requirement discussed in this module derives from three core engineering objectives:

- **Pillar 1 – Adequate Capacity:** Sufficient width to allow the **calculated occupant load** to evacuate within the **available safe egress time (ASET)**.
- **Pillar 2 – Adequate Travel Time:** Travel distance from the most remote point to an exit must be less than the **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)**.
- **Pillar 3 – Adequate Protection:** Enclosure integrity must prevent **smoke, heat, and toxic gas** ingress for a specified fire-resistance duration (typically **2 hours** for high-rise).

### 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 – High Yield)

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units / Typical Value |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Occupant Load (OL)** | $OL = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area}}{\text{Occupant Load Factor (OLF)}}$ | Persons |
| **Occupant Load Factor (NBC 2016 Table 5)** | Varies by occupancy (e.g., 1.5 m²/person for assembly, 10 m²/person for office) | m² / person |
| **Unit Exit Width (UEW)** | NBC: 1 UEW = **500 mm** ; NFPA: 1 UEW = **457 mm (18 in.)** | mm |
| **Stairway Capacity Factor** | Capacity = OL × Discharge Factor (Stair = **7.4 persons/min per UEW** in NBC) | persons/min |
| **Blondel's Formula (Riser-Tread)** | $2R + T = 600 \;\text{to}\; 640$ | mm |
| **Ramp Gradient (Public Use)** | 1 in 12 (≈ 8.33 %); Max 1:8 with landings | ratio |
| **Ramp Gradient (Private)** | 1 in 10 (10 %); Max 1:8 (12.5 %) | ratio |
| **Minimum Corridor Width** | $W_{corr} \geq 1.5 \;\text{m (2.4 m for hospitals)}$ | metres |
| **Minimum Door Width** | Single leaf: $\geq 1.0 \;\text{m}$ ; Double leaf: $\geq 1.5 \;\text{m}$ (high-rise) | metres |
| **Fire Resistance – Enclosed Staircase Walls** | **2 hours** for high-rise ($H \geq 15$ m) | hours |
| **Fire Resistance – Corridor Walls** | **2 hours** for high-rise ; **1 hour** for low-rise | hours |
| **Dead-End Travel Limit (NBC)** | Residential: $\leq 6 \;\text{m}$ ; Institutional: $\leq 6 \;\text{m}$ ; Others: $\leq 12 \;\text{m}$ | metres |
| **Travel Distance – Residential** | $\leq 22.5 \;\text{m}$ (unsprinklered) ; $\leq 30 \;\text{m}$ (sprinklered) | metres |
| **Travel Distance – Assembly** | $\leq 30 \;\text{m}$ unsprinklered ; $\leq 45 \;\text{m}$ sprinklered | metres |
| **Stair Landing Length** | $\geq \text{width of stair}$ | metres |
| **Handrail Height** | $900 \;\text{mm} \;\text{to}\; 1000 \;\text{mm}$ above nosing | mm |
| **Head Room – Stair / Ramp** | $\geq 2.2 \;\text{m}$ clear | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the **Unit Exit Width (UEW)** of **500 mm (NBC)** with the **clear opening** of a doorway. The 500 mm is the *unit* used for capacity calculation, **not** the minimum door size. A 1000 mm door is **2 UEW**, providing evacuation capacity of **14.8 persons/min per floor** per stair (per NBC).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The compliance of these requirements is not merely academic – it is **legally enforceable** under the Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR 2019) and adopted by the Fire and Rescue Services Department (Kerala Fire and Rescue Services – KFES) during the **No-Objection Certificate (NOC) approval process**. Failure to meet these standards results in **revocation of occupancy certificate**, **insurance claim rejection**, and **criminal liability** under Section 14 of the Fire Services Act 2024 (GoI).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capacity of a Staircase from Occupant Load

**Given:** A 4-storey office building, each floor has an area of **400 m²**, OLF for office = **10 m²/person**, and 2 staircases of width **1.5 m each**.

**Step 1 – Compute Total Occupant Load per Floor**

$$OL_{floor} = \dfrac{\text{Area}_{floor}}{OLF} = \dfrac{400 \;\text{m}^2}{10 \;\text{m}^2/\text{person}}$$

$$OL_{floor} = 40 \;\text{persons per floor}$$

**Step 2 – Compute Total Occupant Load (All 4 floors, all served by stairs)**

The staircase must serve the population of all floors above the discharge level. For a 4-storey building (G+3), the population is:

$$OL_{total} = 4 \times 40 = 160 \;\text{persons}$$

**Step 3 – Compute Number of Unit Exit Widths (UEW) Provided**

NBC specifies: 1 UEW = 500 mm. Each staircase is 1.5 m = 1500 mm.

$$UEW_{provided} = \dfrac{1500 \;\text{mm}}{500 \;\text{mm/UEW}} = 3 \;\text{UEW per staircase}$$

Total UEW provided (2 staircases):

$$UEW_{total} = 2 \times 3 = 6 \;\text{UEW}$$

**Step 4 – Compute Capacity of Staircase per NBC (Stairway Discharge Factor = 7.4 persons/min per UEW)**

$$\text{Total Stair Capacity} = UEW_{total} \times 7.4 = 6 \times 7.4$$

$$\text{Total Stair Capacity} = 44.4 \;\text{persons/minute}$$

**Step 5 – Compute Evacuation Time (Egress Time)**

$$T_{evac} = \dfrac{OL_{total}}{\text{Stair Capacity}} = \dfrac{160}{44.4} = 3.60 \;\text{minutes}$$

**Step 6 – Check Against ASET (Assumed 6 minutes for office)**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{evac} &= 3.60 \;\text{min} \\
T_{ASET} &= 6.0 \;\text{min} \\
\therefore \text{Safety Margin} &= T_{ASET} - T_{evac} = 2.4 \;\text{min} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The 2-staircase design with 1.5 m width **complies** with NBC 2016 for 160 persons.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Travel Distance Compliance for a Dead-End Corridor

**Given:** A hotel guest room is located at the end of a single-loaded corridor. The dead-end length is **8 m** past the last exit access door. Occupancy: **Hotel (Residential Group B)**.

**Step 1 – Identify Applicable Travel Distance Limit (NBC Table 21)**

For **Hotel** occupancy:
- Dead-end corridor: **6.0 m (unsprinklered)**
- Dead-end corridor: **9.0 m (sprinklered)**

**Step 2 – Compare**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{provided} &= 8.0 \;\text{m} \\
L_{allowed, \,sprinklered} &= 9.0 \;\text{m} \\
L_{allowed, \,unsprinklered} &= 6.0 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Decision Logic**

- If the hotel has **automatic sprinklers**: 8 m $\leq$ 9 m $\rightarrow$ **COMPLIES** ✓
- If the hotel has **no sprinklers**: 8 m $>$ 6 m $\rightarrow$ **FAILS** ✗

**Step 4 – Remedial Action (if failed)**

Options include: (i) install sprinklers; (ii) provide an additional exit; (iii) re-design the corridor to be a through-corridor.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Comparison Table for All Exit Components

| Parameter | Doorway | Corridor | Horizontal Exit | Internal Staircase | Exit Passageway | External Staircase | Ramp |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **NBC Classification** | Exit Access | Exit Access | Exit | Exit | Exit | Exit | Exit Access |
| **Min Width (m)** | 1.0 (single) | 1.5 | 1.0 / leaf | 1.25 – 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.25 | 1.2 |
| **Fire Resistance** | 1–2 h (door) | 1–2 h (wall) | 2 h (wall) | 2 h (wall) | 2 h (wall) | Non-comb. | 1 h (wall) |
| **Self-Closing Door** | Required | Required | Required | Required | Required | Not required | Required |
| **Panic Hardware** | $> 50$ persons | N/A | Required | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Ventilation** | N/A | Cross / Pressurize | Natural | Pressurize | Pressurize | Natural | N/A |
| **Smoke Seal** | Required | Required | Required | Required | Required | Not required | N/A |
| **Slope / Grade** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 25° – 38° (R-T) | 0 | 25° – 38° | 1:12 max |
| **Handrail** | N/A | N/A | Required (1.0 m) | Required (0.9 m) | Required | Required | Required |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"DCHIES"** → **D**oorway, **C**orridor, **H**orizontal exit, **I**nternal staircase, **E**xit passageway, **S** (Staircase external). Always remember to mention **fire resistance duration, door swing, and width** in that order for full marks.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Ramp Length for Vertical Rise

**Given:** A school ramp must overcome a vertical rise of **1.8 m** at a gradient of **1:12**.

**Step 1 – Convert Gradient to Horizontal Run**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gradient} &= 1:12 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 \;\text{m vertical} : 12 \;\text{m horizontal} \\
L_{ramp} &= V_{rise} \times 12 = 1.8 \times 12 = 21.6 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 – Determine Number of Landings**

Per NBC, a landing is required for every **0.6 m of vertical rise** OR for every **9 m of horizontal run**, whichever is earlier.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Landings required} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{1.8}{0.6} \right\rceil = 3 \;\text{landings}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Segment Length per Flight**

$$L_{flight} = \dfrac{21.6 \;\text{m}}{3 \;\text{flights}} = 7.2 \;\text{m horizontal per flight}$$

**Step 4 – Verify Against 9 m Rule**

$$7.2 \;\text{m} \leq 9 \;\text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Classification of Means of Egress

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Means of Egress System"]:::root --> A1["Exit Access"]
    A0 --> A2["Exit"]
    A0 --> A3["Exit Discharge"]
    
    A1:::access --> B1["Doorway"]
    A1 --> B2["Corridor and Passageway"]
    A1 --> B3["Ramp"]
    A1 --> B4["Open Staircase"]
    
    A2:::exit --> C1["Internal Staircase (Enclosed, 2h FR)"]
    A2 --> C2["Horizontal Exit (Fire Wall)"]
    A2 --> C3["Exit Passageway (2h FR)"]
    A2 --> C4["External Staircase (Open)"]
    
    A3:::discharge --> D1["Discharge Door at Public Way"]
    A3 --> D2["Refuge Area / Open Space"]
    
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef access fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef exit fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef discharge fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart – Design Decision Logic for Staircase Width

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Calculate Occupant Load OL per Floor"] --> S2["Compute UEW Required: UEW_req = OL / 7.4 per minute"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute UEW Provided: UEW_prov = Stair Width / 0.5 m"]
    S3 --> S4{"Is UEW_prov >= UEW_req?"}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5["Staircase Width COMPLIES with NBC"]
    S4 -- No --> S6["Increase Stair Width OR Add Staircase"]
    S6 --> S3
    S5 --> S7["Verify: Travel Distance, Fire Resistance 2h, Ventilation"]
    S7 --> S8["Final Design Sign-off"]

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic – Layout of a Fire-rated Internal Staircase

```mermaid

    P1["Floor 4 (Occupancy Starts)"] --> D1["Fire Door 1: 2h FRR, Self-Closing"]

    F2 --> L3["Landing at Floor 3"]

    D2 --> P2["Floor 3 Access"]
    P2 --> D3["Continues to Floor 2 and 1 and Discharge"]

    V1 -.-> L2
    V1 -.-> L3

    H1 -.-> F2
```
