<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between "Exit Access" and "Exit" as per NBC 2016, Part 4. List any two components that fall under each category.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety**, the **Means of Egress** is divided into three segments:

- **Exit Access** is the portion of the means of egress that leads from any occupied space within a building to an **exit**. It is **not required to be separated** from the building by fire-resistant construction. *Examples:* (i) Doorway, (ii) Corridor, (iii) Open staircase in a low-rise occupancy, (iv) Ramp.
- **Exit** is that portion of the means of egress which is **separated from other building spaces by fire-rated walls, floors, and self-closing fire doors**, providing a protected path of travel to the exit discharge. *Examples:* (i) Enclosed internal staircase, (ii) Horizontal exit, (iii) External staircase, (iv) Exit passageway.

The key differentiator is the **presence of fire-rated enclosure (typically 2 hours)** and the **function** of the space.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
**State the NBC 2016 requirement for the minimum width and gradient of a ramp used for occupant evacuation in public buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **NBC 2016 (Part 3 – Development Control Rules and General Building Requirements)**:

- **Minimum clear width of ramp:** **1.20 m** (1.2 m) for general public use, and **1.80 m** for hospital / institutional occupancy.
- **Maximum gradient:** **1 in 12** (≈ 8.33 %) for ramps serving the public. For short, private ramps, a maximum of **1 in 8** (12.5 %) is permitted only with intermediate landings.
- **Handrails:** Required on **both sides** at a height of **900 mm to 1000 mm**.
- **Landing:** A level landing (slope 1:50 or flatter) of length **at least 1.2 m** is required at every **9 m of horizontal run** or **0.6 m of vertical rise**, whichever is lesser.
- **Surface:** Must be **non-slip / anti-skid**, with contrasting nosing strips for visually impaired users.

---

### PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** – *Doorway, Corridors and Horizontal Exits*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the NBC 2016 requirements for the **design, construction, and fire rating of doorways and corridors** used as part of the means of egress in a high-rise residential building ($H = 45$ m). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept, applications, and design requirements of a horizontal exit** in a multi-storey building. Illustrate with a suitable plan diagram (describe in text). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) – Doorways and Corridors (7 Marks):**

For a **high-rise building of height 45 m (Group A – Residential)**:

**Doorway Requirements:**
- **Minimum clear width:** **1000 mm** for single-leaf doors and **1500 mm** for double-leaf doors.
- **Door swing:** Doors must swing **in the direction of egress** when serving more than 50 persons or in high-hazard occupancies.
- **Fire Rating:** Fire doors leading to enclosed staircases or exit passageways must have a **2-hour fire-resistance rating (FRR)**. Other corridor doors require **1-hour FRR**.
- **Hardware:** **Panic bar / panic latch** is required for occupancies with more than 50 occupants.
- **Vision Panel:** A **wired-glass or fire-rated glass** vision panel of size 0.2 m² (minimum) is recommended.
- **Signage:** Photoluminescent "EXIT" sign of **minimum 150 mm height**, visible from 30 m, must be installed.
- **Revolving Doors:** Not permitted as the only means of egress; a side-hung door of 1.0 m width must be provided adjacent.

**Corridor Requirements:**
- **Minimum width:** **1.5 m** (residential) and **2.4 m** (institutional / hospital).
- **Fire Resistance:** Walls of the corridor must have a **2-hour fire-resistance rating** in high-rise buildings.
- **Ceiling and Floor:** Floor assembly must have a **2-hour FRR** and must be continuous to the stairwell.
- **Ventilation:** Either **cross-ventilation** (windows at both ends, each min 0.5 m²) or **mechanical pressurization** of **12 to 15 Pa** above ambient.
- **Dead-End Limit:** Dead-end corridor length must not exceed **6.0 m** (unsprinklered) or **9.0 m** (sprinklered).
- **Obstructions:** No storage, electrical panels, or protrusions within the clear width.

**(b) – Horizontal Exits (7 Marks):**

A **horizontal exit** is a means of egress consisting of one or more openings through or around a **fire wall** (or a bridge / balcony connecting two buildings or two floors of the same building) that allows occupants to move from a fire-affected area to a **refuge area** on the other side of the wall.

**Design Requirements (NBC 2016):**
1. **Fire Wall:** The separating wall must have a **minimum 2-hour fire-resistance rating** and must extend from the floor assembly below to the roof / floor above.
2. **Self-Closing Fire Doors:** Every opening must be fitted with **listed, self-closing, fire-rated doors** of **2-hour FRR** on both sides.
3. **Area Balance:** The **refuge area** (area on the non-fire side) must have sufficient capacity to accommodate not only its own occupants but also the evacuees from the fire side. Standard practice requires the refuge area to be **equal to or greater than the area of the largest compartment** served.
4. **Width:** The aggregate width of horizontal exits must be calculated based on the **occupant load** at **1 UEW = 500 mm** and capacity factor of **15 persons/min per UEW** (NBC, horizontal passage).
5. **Maximum Occupancy:** No single horizontal exit shall serve more than **50 occupants per leaf** of the door.
6. **No Re-entry Confusion:** The refuge area must have **independent access to a vertical exit** (staircase).
7. **Application:** Commonly used in **hospitals (Group I), hotels, large assembly buildings, and shopping malls** where horizontal evacuation is more practical for mobility-impaired occupants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **horizontal exits with fire walls**. Remember: the **fire wall** is a *structural* element; the **horizontal exit** is the *opening within the fire wall* that allows occupant movement. Drawing the cross-section without showing the **2-hour FRR fire door** results in **2–3 marks deduction**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** – *Internal Staircases, Exit Passageways, External Staircases, and Ramps*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled sketch (describe in text), explain the **NBC 2016 requirements for internal enclosed staircases** in a high-rise office building ($H = 30$ m). Include provisions for **width, riser/tread, fire resistance, ventilation, and handrails**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **design requirements of external staircases and ramps** as means of egress. List the **advantages and limitations** of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) – Internal Enclosed Staircase Requirements (7 Marks):**

For a **high-rise office building ($H = 30$ m, Group B – Business / Office)**:

| Parameter | NBC 2016 Requirement |
|:---|:---|
| **Minimum Width (clear)** | **1.50 m** (for OL $\leq$ 200 per floor) ; **2.00 m** (for larger OL) |
| **Riser Height (R)** | Max **150 mm to 190 mm** |
| **Tread Width (T)** | Min **250 mm to 300 mm** |
| **R-T Relationship** | Blondel's: $2R + T = 600 \text{ to } 640$ mm |
| **Head Room** | $\geq$ **2.2 m** clear |
| **No. of Risers per Flight** | Max **16** ; Min **3** |
| **Landing Width** | $\geq$ width of stair |
| **Landing Length** | $\geq$ **1.0 m** at intermediate landings |
| **Fire Resistance of Walls** | **2 hours** for height $H \geq 15$ m |
| **Fire Resistance of Doors** | **2 hours** ; **self-closing** ; **panic bar** |
| **Ventilation** | **Pressurization** to **25-30 Pa** (preferred); alternatively, **cross-ventilated window** of min **0.5 m²** at every landing |
| **Handrail** | **Both sides**, height **900 mm – 1000 mm** above nosing, continuous |
| **Railing Balusters** | Spacing $\leq$ **150 mm** to prevent child entrapment |
| **Anti-Skid Nosing** | Required with **contrasting colour** band |
| **Number of Staircases** | Min **2** for buildings $H \geq$ 15 m, located **as far apart as possible** (ideally at opposite ends) |
| **Access to Staircase** | Through **fire-rated lobby** of width $\geq$ 1.2 m, with **cross-talk prevention** |

**Sketch Description (Schematic Layout):**
A typical flight begins at a **landing protected by a 2-hour FRR fire door**, descends through 12 risers of 150 mm height with 300 mm treads, lands on an **intermediate landing** (1.2 m length, 1.5 m width) pressurized by a **dedicated stairwell pressurization fan**, then continues to the next floor and finally to the **discharge level** at grade.

**(b) – Comparison of External Staircases and Ramps (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | External Staircase | Ramp |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **NBC Classification** | Exit | Exit Access (primarily) |
| **Geometry** | Stepped inclined surface | Continuous inclined surface |
| **Slope / Gradient** | 25° to 38° (per riser/tread) | Max **1:12 (public)** ; 1:8 (private) |
| **Min Width** | **1.25 m** | **1.20 m** |
| **Use for Disabled** | **Not suitable** (step hazards) | **Suitable** (level transitions) |
| **Fire Resistance** | Non-combustible material (steel / concrete) | Non-combustible surface |
| **Enclosure** | Open to external atmosphere | Can be open or enclosed |
| **Handrails** | Required both sides | Required both sides + **kerb rail** of 100 mm |
| **Landings** | At every floor level | Every **0.6 m rise / 9 m run** |
| **Construction Cost** | Moderate | Higher (longer footprint) |
| **Space Required** | Vertical compact | Large horizontal footprint |
| **Advantage** | **Smoke venting natural**; no pressurization needed; serves as secondary exit | **Wheelchair / stretcher accessible**; suitable for **hospital** and **mobility-impaired** users |
| **Limitation** | Not for physically disabled; **exposed to weather**; not primary exit for high-rise | Consumes large floor area; **slip hazard** in wet conditions; limited by gradient |

**Conclusion:** External staircases serve as **secondary / emergency exits** for able-bodied occupants, while ramps are primarily designed to **facilitate accessibility** and must be combined with stairs for full evacuation compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Tip):** When asked about "**staircase width**", students often write **1.0 m** – this is the **door width**, NOT the staircase width. **Staircase minimum width is 1.25 m to 1.5 m**. Mixing up these two values costs **1–2 marks** in Part B. Also, **forget the Blondel's formula ($2R+T$)** and you lose 1 mark explicitly reserved for design coordination.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10-Point KTU Module 3 Quick Reference:**

1. **Means of Egress = Exit Access + Exit + Exit Discharge.** Every component in this chain must be **simultaneously** compliant.

2. **Unit Exit Width (UEW) = 500 mm** (NBC). Stair capacity = **7.4 persons/min per UEW**. This is the **single most-tested** numerical relationship in this module.

3. **Doorways must swing in the direction of egress** when serving more than 50 occupants. **Panic hardware is mandatory** for such occupancies.

4. **Corridors in high-rise must be 1.5 m wide** with **2-hour fire-resistant walls** and either **cross-ventilation or pressurization (12-15 Pa)**.

5. **Dead-end corridor limit** is **6.0 m (residential, unsprinklered)** – a very commonly asked numerical question in KTU exams.

6. **Horizontal Exit** = a doorway through a **2-hour fire wall** leading to a **refuge area of equal or greater size**, used commonly in hospitals and shopping malls.

7. **Internal staircase width** = **1.25 m (low occupancy) to 1.5 m (high-rise)**. **Riser 150-190 mm**, **Tread 250-300 mm**, and the fundamental relationship is **$2R + T = 600$ to $640$ mm** (Blondel's formula).

8. **Staircase fire resistance = 2 hours** for buildings $H \geq 15$ m. **Pressurization = 25-30 Pa** above ambient.

9. **External staircase** is open to atmosphere, non-combustible, and acts as a **secondary exit**. It is **not suitable for disabled occupants**.

10. **Ramps** have **maximum gradient 1:12 (public)** with **landings every 0.6 m of vertical rise**, **min width 1.2 m**, and **handrails on both sides at 900-1000 mm height** with a **100 mm kerb rail**.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this Module:** *Width, Fire Resistance, Door Swing, Ventilation, and Travel Distance* – if your answer covers these five parameters for any given component, you have a **90 %+ chance of full marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning of Location and Calculation of Capacity, Number and Width of Exit as per NBC for Different Occupancy Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planning of Location and Calculation of Capacity, Number and Width of Exit as per NBC for Different Occupancy Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (NBC 2016, Part 4: Fire and Life Safety)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXIT** (As per NBC 2016, Clause 4.4.1):
> An *exit* is a continuous and unobstructed way of travel from any point in a building or structure to a public way, comprising of three distinct parts: (a) **Exit Access**, (b) **Exit Discharge**, and (c) **Exit Staircase/Passage**. It may include doorways, corridors, stairways, ramps, lobbies, and the exterior exit discharge.

An **Exit System** is a planned architectural life-safety arrangement designed to permit the **prompt evacuation** of occupants from any point within a building to an open exterior space (public way) during emergencies such as fire, smoke, structural failure, or panic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bottle Neck" Principle

Imagine a large water tank connected to the outside through a narrow pipe. No matter how much water (occupants) is in the tank, the **discharge rate is governed by the smallest bottleneck** (the door/stair width). If the bottleneck is too narrow, water (people) will back up, overflow, and cause a **stampede**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** The human evacuation rate (≈ **40 persons/minute per 500 mm unit of exit width**) is a physical constant. Designing a building with insufficient exits is a direct violation of NBC and a criminal offense under Section 13 of the Kerala Fire Force Act.

## 1.3 Three Components of an Exit (Per NBC)

| Component | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Exit Access** | The portion of a means of egress that leads to an exit entrance. | Room door → Corridor |
| **Exit** | The portion between the exit access and the exit discharge. | Staircase, exit passageway, ramp |
| **Exit Discharge** | The portion between the termination of the exit and the public way. | Final door to street/road |

## 1.4 Occupancy Classification (NBC 2016, Table 3, Part 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Occupancy Classification Matters:**
> A cinema hall and a chemical factory cannot have the same exit design. Each occupancy has unique occupant density, fire load, and behavior patterns. NBC classifies buildings into **10 groups (A to J)** based on purpose, fire risk, and occupant characteristics.

| Group | Occupancy Type | Examples | Fire Risk Profile |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Residential | Apartments, hostels, hotels | Moderate (sleeping occupants) |
| **B** | Educational | Schools, colleges, coaching centers | High (young, unfamiliar) |
| **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, prisons, mental asylums | Critical (immobile occupants) |
| **D** | Assembly | Theatres, malls, airports, temples | Very High (panic-prone, dense) |
| **E** | Business | Offices, banks, IT parks | Moderate (alert, mobile) |
| **F** | Mercantile | Shops, supermarkets, showrooms | Moderate (dense retail) |
| **G** | Industrial | Manufacturing, workshops | Variable (process-dependent) |
| **H** | Storage | Warehouses, godowns, cold storage | High (combustible stock) |
| **I** | Hazardous | Petroleum, explosives, LPG plants | Extreme |
| **J** | Special | Power plants, telecom towers, data centers | Specialized |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Exit Design

Exit design is governed by **three interlinked design parameters**:

1. **CAPACITY** → Can the exit handle the number of occupants?
2. **NUMBER** → Are there enough alternate escape routes?
3. **WIDTH** → Is each route wide enough for the flow rate?

## 2.2 Occupant Load — The Foundation Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Occupant Load (OL)** is the maximum number of persons a floor or building is designed to accommodate at any one time. It is calculated based on the **floor area per occupant (OAF)** prescribed by NBC for each occupancy.

### 2.2.1 The Governing Equation

$$
\text{Occupant Load} = \frac{\text{Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}{\text{Occupant Area Factor (m}^2\text{/person)}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Occupant Load Factors (OLF) — NBC Table 5, Part 4

| Occupancy Group | Use | OLF (m²/person) | Reasoning |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **A-1** | Single family dwelling | 30 | Low density |
| **A-2** | Apartments, hostels | 12 | Sleeping |
| **A-3** | Hotels, lodging | 18 | Furnished rooms |
| **B** | Classrooms | **1.5** | High density, seated |
| **B** | Library reading rooms | 2.0 | Seated study |
| **C** | Hospital ward | 15 | Beds + circulation |
| **D** | Concentrated assembly (theatre) | **0.6** (net) | Fixed seats |
| **D** | Standing space (malls) | **0.46** (net) | Free movement |
| **D** | Unconcentrated assembly (museums) | 1.5 | Browsing |
| **E** | Office rooms | **8.0** | Workstations |
| **E** | Conference rooms | 1.5 | Seated meetings |
| **F-1** | Street floor shops | **3.0** | Browsing + display |
| **F-2** | Upper floor shops | 6.0 | Less dense |
| **G-1** | Low hazard industrial | 9.3 | Spaced machinery |
| **G-2** | Moderate hazard industrial | 4.5 | Dense operations |
| **G-3** | High hazard industrial | 3.7 | Tight process |
| **H** | Storage, godowns | 30 | Sparse occupancy |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU exams!**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Reference |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Occupant Load | $OL = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{OLF}$ | persons | NBC 4.4.3 |
| 2 | Width of Stair | $W = \dfrac{OL}{50 \text{ persons/unit}}$ × **0.5 m** | metres | NBC 4.4.3.1.1 |
| 3 | Total Exit Width | $W_{total} = \dfrac{OL}{C_u}$ | metres | NBC 4.4.3.1 |
| 4 | Number of Exits (min.) | $N_{min} = \begin{cases} 1, & OL \le 50 \\ 2, & 50 < OL \le 300 \\ 3, & 300 < OL \le 500 \\ 4, & OL > 500 \end{cases}$ | count | NBC 4.4.5.1 |
| 5 | Travel Distance | $L_{travel} \le L_{limit}$ (see Table) | metres | NBC 4.4.6 |
| 6 | Dead-end Corridor | $L_{dead} \le L_{limit}$ (see Table) | metres | NBC 4.4.6.1 |
| 7 | Capacity per unit width (Stair) | $C_u = 50$ persons per **500 mm** | persons | NBC 4.4.3.1.1 |
| 8 | Capacity per unit width (Level/Passage) | $C_u = 60$ persons per **500 mm** | persons | NBC 4.4.3.1.2 |
| 9 | Capacity per unit width (Door) | $C_u = 60$ persons per **500 mm** | persons | NBC 4.4.3.1.3 |
| 10 | Common Path Limit | $L_{common} \le 6$ m (typical, sprinklered: 12 m) | metres | NBC 4.4.6.2 |

> **Conversion note:** 1 unit of exit width = **500 mm = 0.5 m** (this is the *unit width factor*).

## 2.4 Travel Distance Limits (NBC 4.4.6, Table 8)

| Occupancy | Unsprinklered (m) | Sprinklered (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A (Residential) | 30 | 45 |
| B (Educational) | 22.5 | 30 |
| C (Institutional) | 22.5 | 30 |
| D (Assembly) | 30 | 45 |
| E (Business) | 30 | 45 |
| F (Mercantile) | 30 | 45 |
| G (Industrial) | 22.5 | 30 |
| H (Storage) | 30 | 45 |
| I (Hazardous) | 22.5 | 30 |

## 2.5 Staircase Design Rules (NBC 4.4.7)

- **Minimum width of staircase** for occupant load > 50: **1500 mm (1.5 m)**
- **Maximum riser height**: **150 mm** (residential: 190 mm)
- **Minimum tread depth**: **250 mm to 300 mm**
- **Head room**: Minimum **2200 mm**
- **Handrail height**: **900 mm to 1000 mm**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The exit design framework is applied in:
- **Fire Safety Audits** for high-rises (mandatory in Kerala for buildings > 15 m)
- **Building Plan Approvals** by local municipalities
- **Forensic Fire Investigations** to determine code violations
- **Performance-Based Design (PBD)** using evacuation simulation tools like **Pathfinder, FDS+EVAC, STEPS**
- **Insurance Risk Assessment** of commercial properties
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked-Out Numerical Examples

## 3.1 The Master Design Algorithm

The exit design problem is solved using a **7-step logical sequence**:

```
Step 1 → Classify the building occupancy (Group A–J)
Step 2 → Compute Floor Area (carpet + circulation, but NOT including walls)
Step 3 → Compute Occupant Load (OL) using OLF table
Step 4 → Determine Minimum Number of Exits from OL
Step 5 → Calculate Total Required Width of Exits
Step 6 → Allocate Width to each Exit (must be equal ± 50 mm)
Step 7 → Verify Travel Distance & Dead-End Limits
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Office Building (Group E)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
> An IT office building has 3 floors. Each floor measures **40 m × 20 m = 800 m²** (carpet + circulation). Classify occupancy, calculate the occupant load, minimum number of exits, and required staircase width per floor as per NBC.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Occupancy Classification**
This is an **Information Technology office** → falls under **Group E (Business)** occupancy.

**Step 2 — Floor Area**
$$
A_{floor} = 40 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ m} = 800 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Occupant Load**
For Group E (Office rooms), from NBC Table 5:
$$
OLF = 8.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}
$$
$$
OL = \frac{A_{floor}}{OLF} = \frac{800 \text{ m}^2}{8.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}} = 100 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Number of Exits**
Since $50 < OL = 100 \le 300$:
$$
N_{min} = 2 \text{ exits (staircases)}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Required Width of Staircase**
Staircase capacity per unit width:
$$
C_u = 50 \text{ persons per 500 mm (unit width)}
$$
Total number of units of width required:
$$
n = \frac{OL}{C_u} = \frac{100 \text{ persons}}{50 \text{ persons/unit}} = 2 \text{ units}
$$
Total width:
$$
W_{total} = 2 \text{ units} \times 0.5 \text{ m/unit} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Allocation per Staircase**
Distributing equally between 2 staircases:
$$
W_{each} = \frac{1.0 \text{ m}}{2} = 0.5 \text{ m} \;\; \text{(NOT acceptable — minimum is 1.5 m)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC Minimum Width Rule:** Even if calculation gives 0.5 m, the **absolute minimum** is **1.5 m** for any staircase serving occupant load > 50. So we take $W_{each} = 1.5 \text{ m}$ minimum.

**Step 7 — Travel Distance Verification**
Assuming the farthest point is **28 m** from the stair on each floor. Unsprinklered office limit = **30 m**. ✅ **Compliant**.

### **Final Design:**
- **Occupant Load per floor:** 100 persons
- **Number of Staircases:** 2
- **Width of each Staircase:** 1500 mm (1.5 m) — governed by minimum, not calculation
- **Tread/Riser:** 300 mm / 150 mm
- **Total Population for evacuation:** $100 \times 3 = 300$ persons

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cinema Hall (Group D)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
> A cinema hall has a seating area of **600 m²** with a balcony of **400 m²**. Determine occupant load, exits required, and total exit width per NBC.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Classification**
Cinema hall → **Group D (Assembly, D-1 Concentrated)**

**Step 2 — Occupant Load**
For concentrated assembly (fixed seats): $OLF = 0.6 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$ (net)
$$
OL = \frac{600 + 400}{0.6} = \frac{1000}{0.6} = 1667 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Exits**
Since $OL > 500$:
$$
N_{min} \ge 4 \text{ exits}
$$

**Step 4 — Staircase Width (Door, Level Exit)**
Doors/level exits:
$$
n_{door} = \frac{1667}{60} = 27.78 \approx 28 \text{ units}
$$
$$
W_{door, total} = 28 \times 0.5 = 14.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Per Exit Width (4 exits)**
$$
W_{each} = \frac{14.0}{4} = 3.5 \text{ m per exit}
$$

### **Final Design:**
- **Occupant Load:** 1667 persons
- **Minimum Exits:** 4
- **Total Exit Width (Doors):** 14.0 m
- **Width per Exit Door:** 3.5 m
- **Staircase Width:** Calculated separately per floor based on that floor's population

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Hostel Building (Group A-2)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
> A hostel has 4 floors with 20 rooms per floor. Each room is 5 m × 4 m. Design the exit system as per NBC.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Classification**
Hostel → **Group A-2 (Residential, multi-family)**

**Step 2 — Floor Area per Floor**
$$
A = 20 \text{ rooms} \times (5 \times 4) = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Occupant Load (per floor)**
For A-2: $OLF = 12 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$
$$
OL_{floor} = \frac{400}{12} = 33.33 \approx 34 \text{ persons/floor}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Population**
$$
OL_{total} = 34 \times 4 = 136 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 5 — Exits Required**
Since $50 < 136 \le 300$:
$$
N_{min} = 2 \text{ exits (staircases)}
$$

**Step 6 — Staircase Width per Floor**
Using OL per floor = 34:
$$
n = \frac{34}{50} = 0.68 \text{ units}
$$
$$
W_{calc} = 0.68 \times 0.5 = 0.34 \text{ m}
$$

But ground floor and **aggregate load** at lower stair landings must be considered.

**Cumulative Load on Ground Floor Stair Landing:**
$$
OL_{cumulative} = 34 \times 4 = 136
$$
$$
n_{ground} = \frac{136}{50} = 2.72 \text{ units} \Rightarrow W_{ground} = 1.36 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Minimum**
Final width = **1500 mm (1.5 m) minimum**, served by 2 staircases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Stair width is governed by the **maximum cumulative occupant load** at the **ground floor discharge level**, not by the per-floor load. Many students make this mistake in KTU exams!

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Exit Requirements by Occupancy

| Occupancy | OLF (m²/p) | Min. Stair Width (mm) | Min. # Exits @ OL > 50 | Max. Travel Dist. (m) | Dead End (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A-1 (House) | 30 | 1000 (internal) | 1 (any) | 30 | 6 |
| A-2 (Apartment) | 12 | 1250 | 2 | 30 | 6 |
| B (School) | 1.5 | 1500 | 2 | 22.5 | 6 |
| C (Hospital) | 15 | 2000 | 2 | 22.5 | 6 |
| D-1 (Cinema) | 0.6 | 1500 | 4 (for OL > 500) | 30 | 6 |
| D-2 (Mall) | 0.46 | 1500 | 4 (for OL > 500) | 30 | 6 |
| E (Office) | 8.0 | 1500 | 2 | 30 | 6 |
| F (Shop) | 3.0 | 1500 | 2 | 30 | 6 |
| G-2 (Industry) | 4.5 | 1500 | 2 | 22.5 | 6 |
| H (Storage) | 30 | 1500 | 2 | 30 | 6 |
| I (Hazardous) | 9.3 | 2000 | 3 | 22.5 | 0 (no dead ends) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Components of a Means of Egress (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ROOM_OR_OCCUPANCY_SPACE] -->|EXIT_ACCESS| B[CORRIDOR_HALLWAY]
    B -->|DOOR_THRESHHOLD| C[EXIT_STAIRCASE_OR_RAMP]
    C -->|VIA_STAIRWELL| D[GROUND_FLOOR_LOBBY]
    D -->|EXIT_DISCHARGE| E[PUBLIC_WAY_STREET]
    
    A:::occupiedStyle
    B:::accessStyle
    C:::exitStyle
    D:::dischargeStyle
    E:::safeStyle
    
    classDef occupiedStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef accessStyle fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef exitStyle fill:#FF4500,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef dischargeStyle fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safeStyle fill:#00CED1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** An occupant starts in the **Occupancy Space** (yellow), travels through the **Exit Access** (orange) which includes corridors and doors, enters the **Exit** (red) which is the protected stair/ramp, descends to the **Exit Discharge** (green) at ground level, and finally reaches the **Public Way** (cyan) which is the public street. Each arrow represents a critical transition that must be **unobstructed, signed, and illuminated**.

---

## 4.2 The Exit Design Decision Flowchart (NBC-Compliant Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START: New Building Project]) --> Classify[STEP 1: Classify Occupancy Group A to J]
    Classify --> Area[STEP 2: Measure Floor Area per Floor in m squared]
    Area --> OLF[STEP 3: Select Occupant Load Factor OLF from NBC Table 5]
    OLF --> CalcOL[STEP 4: Calculate Occupant Load OL equals Area divided by OLF]
    CalcOL --> ExitsCheck{STEP 5: OL Threshold}
    ExitsCheck -->|OL less than or equal to 50| OneExit[1 Exit Required]
    ExitsCheck -->|50 less than OL less than or equal to 300| TwoExits[2 Exits Required]
    ExitsCheck -->|300 less than OL less than or equal to 500| ThreeExits[3 Exits Required]
    ExitsCheck -->|OL greater than 500| FourPlusExits[4 or More Exits Required]
    
    OneExit --> WidthCalc[STEP 6: Calculate Exit Width]
    TwoExits --> WidthCalc
    ThreeExits --> WidthCalc
    FourPlusExits --> WidthCalc
    
    WidthCalc --> Formula[W_total equals OL divided by 50 multiplied by 0.5 m]
    Formula --> Distribute{Distribute Equally}
    Distribute --> CheckMin{Width less than 1.5 m?}
    CheckMin -->|Yes| ApplyMin[Apply 1.5 m Absolute Minimum]
    CheckMin -->|No| TravelCheck
    ApplyMin --> TravelCheck[STEP 7: Verify Travel Distance less than Limit]
    
    TravelCheck --> DistOK{Travel less than NBC Limit?}
    DistOK -->|Yes| Signage[Add Signage Emergency Lights Fire Extinguishers]
    DistOK -->|No| Redesign[Redesign: Add More Exits or Compartmentalize]
    
    Signage --> FinalDesign[FINAL EXIT DESIGN APPROVED]
    Redesign --> Area
    
    classDef startStyle fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px
    classDef stepStyle fill:#2196F3,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFC107,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warningStyle fill:#F44336,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef successStyle fill:#00C853,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    
    class Start startStyle
    class Classify,Area,OLF,CalcOL,WidthCalc,Formula,TravelCheck,Signage stepStyle
    class ExitsCheck,Distribute,CheckMin,DistOK decisionStyle
    class OneExit,TwoExits,ThreeExits,FourPlusExits,ApplyMin,Redesign warningStyle
    class FinalDesign successStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Block Functional Architecture — Exit Staircase Enclosure (Fire-rated Compartment)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SE[STAIRWELL_ENCLOSURE_2HR_FIRE_RATED]
        direction TB
        S1[FLOOR_3_LANDING] --> S2[FLIGHT_DOWN]
        S2 --> S3[FLOOR_2_INTERMEDIATE_LANDING]
        S3 --> S4[FLIGHT_DOWN]
        S4 --> S5[FLOOR_1_GROUND_LEVEL]
    end
    
    S5 --> DOOR[FIRE_DOOR_SELF_CLOSING_120_MIN]
    DOOR --> LOBBY[GROUND_LOBBY_DETECTION_SYSTEM]
    LOBBY --> EXIT_FINAL[FINAL_EXIT_TO_PUBLIC_WAY]
    
    SE:::enclosureStyle
    DOOR:::doorStyle
    LOBBY:::lobbyStyle
    EXIT_FINAL:::finalStyle
    
    classDef enclosureStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef doorStyle fill:#DC143C,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef lobbyStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef finalStyle fill:#228B22,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Stairwell Integrity:** The staircase must be a **separate fire-rated compartment** (typically **2 hours** for buildings > 15 m height) with **self-closing fire doors** at every floor entry. This prevents smoke ingress during evacuation — a critical NBC requirement often violated in Kerala buildings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Pattern]**

### **Q1. Define the term "Unit of Exit Width" as per NBC and state its value.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Unit of Exit Width** is the standard width increment used in NBC for sizing exits (staircases, doors, ramps, corridors) based on occupant load.
$$
1 \text{ Unit of Exit Width} = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}
$$
This unit is the basis for computing the total required width of any exit component, using the capacity factors: **50 persons/unit width** for stairs, **60 persons/unit width** for level components like doors, corridors, and passages.

---

### **Q2. List the three main components of a means of egress and give one example for each.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
As per NBC Clause 4.4.1, the three components are:
1. **Exit Access** — e.g., a corridor leading from a room to a staircase.
2. **Exit** — e.g., a fire-rated enclosed staircase.
3. **Exit Discharge** — e.g., a final exit door opening to a public street or road.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Never use the obsolete** term "fire escape" — NBC mandates the term **"Exit"** or **"Means of Egress"**.
> - **Always specify** that you are using **NBC 2016, Part 4** (not older revisions).
> - **Calculation errors** in occupant load factor (OLF) selection are the #1 reason for losing marks. Match the **sub-group** (e.g., A-2, D-1, E) precisely.
> - **Show units** in every step — "m²/person", "persons", "mm". Marks are deducted for missing units.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, ESE Module 3]**

> **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the various **occupancy classifications** as per National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 4. List the major groups (A to J) with one example and a brief comment on the **fire risk profile** of each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An **office building** has 5 floors. Each floor has a carpet area of **600 m²** plus circulation area of **200 m²**. Determine as per NBC: (i) Occupancy classification, (ii) Occupant load per floor, (iii) Minimum number of exits, (iv) Required staircase width. Use $OLF = 8.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$ for office space.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Occupancy Classification (7 marks)**

> **[Detailed Model Answer — 7 marks]**

The National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 4 (Fire and Life Safety) classifies all buildings into **ten major occupancy groups** based on their **purpose, occupant characteristics, and fire risk profile**. The classification is essential because the exit design, fire protection systems, and construction requirements are all occupancy-dependent.

| Group | Occupancy | Example | Fire Risk & Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Residential | Apartments, hostels, hotels, dormitories | **Moderate** — Sleeping occupants may be unaware of fire; smoke spread through corridors is rapid. |
| **B** | Educational | Schools, colleges, universities, libraries, training centres | **High** — Young/inexperienced occupants; panic-prone; need quick evacuation. |
| **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, nursing homes, mental asylums, prisons | **Critical** — Immobile or restrained occupants; horizontal evacuation preferred; defend-in-place strategy. |
| **D** | Assembly | Cinemas, theatres, malls, airports, temples, stadiums | **Very High** — High density, panic-prone, unfamiliar occupants, often with limited egress time. |
| **E** | Business | Offices, banks, IT parks, government buildings | **Moderate** — Alert, mobile occupants; orderly evacuation; moderate density. |
| **F** | Mercantile | Shops, supermarkets, showrooms, restaurants | **Moderate** — High display fire load; consumer behaviour delays exit. |
| **G** | Industrial | Factories, workshops, manufacturing plants | **Variable** — Depends on process hazard (G-1 low, G-3 high fire load). |
| **H** | Storage | Warehouses, godowns, cold storage, parking garages | **High** — Combustible stock; low human occupancy but high fire growth rate. |
| **I** | Hazardous | Petroleum refineries, explosives, LPG, chemical plants | **Extreme** — Explosions, toxic releases; requires blast-resistant design. |
| **J** | Special | Power stations, telecom towers, data centres, heritage structures | **Specialized** — Unique risks; engineered case-by-case. |

> **Conclusion:** Each occupancy demands a tailored exit design, fire suppression system, and structural fire protection as per NBC 2016.

---

#### **Part (b) — Office Building Exit Design (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Floors: 5
- Carpet Area: 600 m²/floor
- Circulation Area: 200 m²/floor
- OLF (Office): 8.0 m²/person

**Step 1 — Occupancy Classification**
Office → **Group E (Business)**

**Step 2 — Total Floor Area per Floor**
$$
A_{floor} = A_{carpet} + A_{circulation} = 600 + 200 = 800 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Occupant Load per Floor**
$$
OL_{floor} = \frac{A_{floor}}{OLF} = \frac{800}{8.0} = 100 \text{ persons/floor}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative Occupant Load at Ground Floor Discharge**
$$
OL_{cum} = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Number of Exits**
For $OL = 500$: minimum **4 exits** required (since $OL > 300$).

**Step 6 — Required Staircase Width**
Using capacity $C_u = 50$ persons/unit width (stair):
$$
n = \frac{OL_{cum}}{50} = \frac{500}{50} = 10 \text{ units}
$$
$$
W_{total} = 10 \times 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$
Per staircase (4 staircases):
$$
W_{each} = \frac{5.0}{4} = 1.25 \text{ m}
$$
But this is below the **minimum 1.5 m** for occupant load > 50.

**Step 7 — Apply Minimum Width**
$$
\boxed{W_{each} = 1.5 \text{ m (minimum per NBC)}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Design Summary**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Occupancy | Group E (Business) |
| OL per floor | 100 persons |
| Cumulative OL | 500 persons |
| Min. Exits | 4 staircases |
| Staircase Width | 1500 mm each |
| Riser / Tread | 150 mm / 300 mm |
| Travel distance check | ≤ 30 m ✅ |

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, ESE Module 3]**

> **(a)** *(7 marks)* Define the terms **occupant load**, **occupant load factor**, **unit of exit width**, and **travel distance** as per NBC. How are these parameters interrelated in exit design?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A **shopping mall** (Group F-1) on a single floor has a total floor area of **3000 m²**. Design the exit system: (i) Determine occupant load, (ii) Number of exits, (iii) Total door width required, (iv) Travel distance check. Use $OLF = 3.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$ and $C_u = 60$ for doors.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks)**

1. **Occupant Load (OL):** The total number of persons a building or floor is designed to accommodate at one time, calculated as the floor area divided by the occupant load factor. Units: persons.

2. **Occupant Load Factor (OLF):** The net floor area (in m²) assigned to each person, prescribed by NBC for each occupancy type. Units: m²/person. Lower OLF = Higher density (e.g., cinema: 0.6 m²/p).

3. **Unit of Exit Width:** The standard width increment (500 mm or 0.5 m) used for sizing all exit components. Capacity factors: 50 persons/unit (stairs), 60 persons/unit (doors, passages).

4. **Travel Distance:** The actual path length measured from the **most remote point** in an occupancy space to the **nearest exit**, following the natural path of travel. NBC specifies maximum limits for each occupancy.

**Interrelation:** OL determines required exit width through $W = OL/C_u$. Travel distance limits ensure that no occupant is too far from an exit. The OLF converts floor area to people, which then drives width and number of exits.

---

#### **Part (b) — Shopping Mall Exit Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Occupant Load**
$$
OL = \frac{3000}{3.0} = 1000 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Exits**
Since $OL = 1000 > 500$: minimum **4 exits**.

**Step 3 — Door Width**
For doors, $C_u = 60$ persons/unit:
$$
n = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.67 \approx 17 \text{ units}
$$
$$
W_{total,doors} = 17 \times 0.5 = 8.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Per Exit Width (4 exits)**
$$
W_{each,door} = \frac{8.5}{4} = 2.125 \text{ m per exit}
$$

**Step 5 — Travel Distance**
For Group F (Mercantile), unsprinklered limit = **30 m**. If the farthest shop is 25 m from the nearest exit, ✅ **Compliant**.

**Final Design:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Occupant Load | 1000 persons |
| Min. Exits | 4 |
| Total Door Width | 8.5 m |
| Per Exit Door | 2125 mm |
| Travel Distance | 25 m ≤ 30 m ✅ |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS CHECKLIST BEFORE THE KTU EXAM!**

- ✅ **1 Unit of Exit Width = 500 mm = 0.5 m** (Universal constant)
- ✅ **Staircase Capacity** = **50 persons/unit width** | **Door/Passage** = **60 persons/unit width**
- ✅ **Minimum staircase width** = **1500 mm** (1.5 m) for any staircase serving OL > 50
- ✅ **Number of exits:** 1 (OL ≤ 50), 2 (51–300), 3 (301–500), 4 (OL > 500)
- ✅ **Occupant Load Formula:** $OL = A_{floor} / OLF$
- ✅ **Stair width is governed by CUMULATIVE OL at GROUND FLOOR discharge**, not per-floor OL
- ✅ **Travel Distance limits:** 22.5 m (B, C, G, I) and 30 m (A, D, E, F, H) — unsprinklered
- ✅ **Dead-end corridor** must not exceed **6 m** (typical)
- ✅ **Hazardous occupancies (Group I)** have **zero dead-end tolerance**
- ✅ **Group D (Assembly)** has the highest OLF density (0.46 m²/p for standing)
- ✅ **Hospitals (Group C)** require **horizontal evacuation** before vertical
- ✅ **Always cite NBC 2016, Part 4** in your KTU answers (specific clause numbers earn bonus marks)
- ✅ **NBC classifies** buildings into **10 groups: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J**
- ✅ **Staircase must be fire-rated** (2 hours for high-rises > 15 m) with self-closing fire doors
- ✅ **Common path limit** = 6 m (unsprinklered) / 12 m (sprinklered)
- ✅ **Headroom** in staircase = minimum 2.2 m; **Riser** ≤ 150 mm; **Tread** ≥ 250 mm
- ✅ **Sprinklered buildings get longer travel distances** — remember to verify sprinkler status
- ✅ **Floor area for OL calculation** = Carpet + Circulation, but **NOT** walls and structural columns
- ✅ **Number of staircases must be separated by minimum 1/3 the diagonal distance** of the floor
- ✅ **Group I (Hazardous)** needs **2000 mm minimum staircase width**
- ✅ **Group C (Institutional)** needs **2000 mm minimum staircase width**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements for Compartmentation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Requirements for Compartmentation

# 🧱 Requirements for Compartmentation — KTU 2024 Premium Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (As per NBC 2016 & KTU Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compartmentation** is the subdividing of a large building or floor area into smaller, fire-tight sections (called **Fire Compartments**) using fire-resistant walls, floors, and ceilings, so that the spread of fire, smoke, and hot gases is contained within the compartment of origin for a specified duration.

A **Fire Compartment** is defined as:

> A space within a building that is completely enclosed by **fire-resisting walls, floors, and ceilings**, including all openings (doors, ducts, services penetrations) that are protected by **fire-rated assemblies** of an equivalent or higher Fire Resistance Rating (FRL).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚢

Imagine a **submarine divided into water-tight bulkheads**. If one section is breached by water, the entire submarine does NOT sink — only that one compartment fills. The crew can isolate the problem, evacuate, and survive.

**Compartmentation works the same way with FIRE and SMOKE:**
- The building = the submarine
- Fire-resistant walls & floors = bulkheads
- The fire = the leak
- The people = the crew that must be protected

If fire breaks out in a hotel room, the **compartment walls prevent** it from jumping to the corridor, then to other rooms, then to the staircase — buying **TIME** for evacuation and **firefighter intervention**.

### 1.3 Core Objectives of Compartmentation

| # | Objective | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Life Safety** | Allow occupants safe egress via protected escape routes |
| 2 | **Property Protection** | Confine fire damage to a limited zone |
| 3 | **Fire Spread Control** | Prevent fire jumping across floors or wings |
| 4 | **Business Continuity** | Critical in data centres, hospitals, industrial plants |
| 5 | **Smoke Control** | Limit smoke migration to non-fire floors |

> [!NOTE]
> The **single most important metric** in compartmentation is the **Fire Resistance Rating (FRL)** — expressed in minutes or hours (e.g., 120 minutes / 2 hours). This is the duration a compartment boundary must withstand a standard fire test (IS 3809 / BS 476 Part 20–22 / ISO 834).

### 1.4 Fire Severity vs. Resistance — Conceptual Foundation

Two competing quantities govern compartmentation design:

$$
\boxed{\text{Fire Resistance of Barrier} \;\geq\; \text{Fire Severity of the Fire}}
$$

- **Fire Severity** = the destructive intensity of the actual fire (depends on fuel load, ventilation, compartment geometry).
- **Fire Resistance** = the time a structural element survives a standard test furnace exposure.

### 1.5 Key Standards Referenced in KTU Module 3

- **NBC 2016 – Part 4** (Fire and Life Safety)
- **IS 1641** – Code of practice for fire safety of buildings
- **IS 1642** – Code of practice for safety of buildings
- **IS 1643** – Code of practice for fire safety of industrial buildings
- **IS 3809** – Fire resistance test for structures
- **BS 476 Parts 20–22** – Fire resistance tests
- **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** guidelines for insurance
- **NFPA 221** – Standard for High Challenge Fire Walls, Fire Walls, and Fire Barrier Walls

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Requirements of Compartmentation (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4)

A compartmentation design is valid **only when ALL of the following are simultaneously satisfied**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Mandatory Requirements of Fire Compartmentation:**
> 1. Compartment must be **fully enclosed** (walls, floor, ceiling all fire-rated).
> 2. All **openings** (doors, windows) must have **equal or higher FRL** fire-rated assemblies.
> 3. All **service penetrations** (pipes, cables, ducts) must be sealed with **fire-stop systems**.
> 4. **Cavity barriers** must interrupt concealed voids (suspended ceilings, raised floors).
> 5. The compartment area must **not exceed** the maximum permissible limit for the occupancy.
> 6. The compartment boundary must be **continuous from floor slab to floor slab** (or to a fire-rated ceiling).

### 2.2 Maximum Compartment Area (NBC 2016 – Table 4.4.1)

| Occupancy Group | Max Compartment Area (m²) | Max Floor Area (m²) | Min FRL (hours) |
|-----------------|---------------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Group A – Residential** | 1000 | 1500 | 2 h |
| **Group B – Educational** | 750 | 1200 | 2 h |
| **Group C – Institutional** | 500 | 750 | 2 h |
| **Group D – Assembly** | 600 | 1000 | 2 h |
| **Group E – Business** | 1500 | 2500 | 2 h |
| **Group F – Mercantile** | 750 | 1000 | 2 h |
| **Group G – Industrial** | 500 | 1000 | 2 h (up to 4 h for high fire load) |
| **Group H – Storage** | 250 | 500 | 2 h (up to 4 h) |
| **Group J – Hazardous** | 100 | 150 | 4 h |

> [!NOTE]
> For **high-rise buildings** (height > **15 m** as per NBC 2016), compartmentation FRL is **upgraded by 1 hour** over the base requirement.

### 2.3 Fire Resistance Rating (FRL) — Three Components

A wall or floor is rated for three failure criteria:

$$
\boxed{\text{FRL} = \text{Stability} \;/\; \text{Integrity} \;/\; \text{Insulation}}
$$

| Component | Symbol | What it means | Failure criterion |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Stability (R)** | Load-bearing | Wall/floor must not collapse | Collapse |
| **Integrity (E)** | Flame/Smoke | No through-cracks, gaps, flaming | Sustained flaming on unexposed face |
| **Insulation (I)** | Heat transmission | Average temp rise < 140 K; max < 180 K | Temperature limit exceeded |

**Example notation:** A wall rated **"FRL 120/120/120"** = 2 hours on all three criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-load-bearing compartment walls** (most fire walls) require only **Integrity + Insulation (E/I)**, not Stability (R), because they carry no structural load.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834) | $T_g = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | $T_g$ in °C, $t$ in minutes |
| 2 | Equivalent Fire Severity (tₑ) | $t_e = \dfrac{\sum (c_v \, W_i \, H_i)}{\text{Reference rate}}$ | Minutes; Pettersson's method |
| 3 | Fire Load Density (q) | $q = \dfrac{\sum (M_i \, H_i)}{A_{floor}}$ | MJ/m² |
| 4 | Compartment Limit (Area) | $A_{comp} \le A_{NBC,\max}$ | m² (per Table 4.4.1) |
| 5 | Ventilation Factor (D) | $D = \dfrac{A_{vent}\sqrt{H_{vent}}}{A_{floor}}$ | m¹ᐟ² — Lie's method |
| 6 | Fire Duration (τ) | $\tau = \dfrac{q \cdot A_{floor}}{F}$ | hours |
| 7 | Time-Equivalent Formula (CIB W14) | $t_e = e_q \cdot w \cdot k$ | minutes, with coefficients |
| 8 | Smoke Layer Interface | $z = 1.0 + 0.1\,H - 0.8\,H_{clear}$ | m (approximate) |

> [!WARNING]
> In any markdown table, **never** use the vertical bar `|` inside a cell (e.g., for absolute value $\vert x \vert$). Use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table integrity.

### 2.5 Penetration Protection Requirements (Cl. 4.4.2, NBC 2016)

Every penetration through a compartment wall/floor **must** be sealed using:

| Service Type | Required Fire Stop System | Test Standard |
|--------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| **Electrical Cables** | Intumescent fire pillows / sealants | IS 12458 / BS 476 Pt 20 |
| **Pipes (non-combustible)** | Fire-rated collars + cementitious mortar | IS 3809 |
| **Pipes (combustible, e.g., PVC)** | Intumescent fire collars (swell on heating) | UL 1479 / EN 1366-3 |
| **Ducts (HVAC)** | Fire dampers (fusible link at 68 °C) | IS 1646 / BS 476 Pt 20 |
| **Cable Trays** | Fire-rated foam / pillows | IS 12458 |
| **Expansion Joints** | Mineral wool with fire-rated sealant | BS 476 |

### 2.6 Cavity Barriers — Critical in Modern Construction

**Cavity barriers** are required in:

- **Suspended ceilings** (hidden plenum spaces)
- **Raised access floors**
- **External wall cavities** (rainscreen systems)
- **Roof voids**
- **Drywall partition cavities**

**Rule (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.5):**
- A cavity barrier is required every **20 m** maximum along the length of the cavity.
- At every **floor slab level** (continuous interruption).
- At every **compartment wall intersection**.
- Minimum FRL: **equal to the compartment wall** it intersects.

### 2.7 Fire Doors — The Most Vulnerable Element

Fire doors are typically the **weakest link** in a compartment wall. Requirements:

| Feature | Specification |
|---------|---------------|
| **FRL** | Equal to compartment wall rating |
| **Self-closing** | Mandatory (no wedges, no chocks) |
| **Smoke seal** | Required for all fire doors in escape routes |
| **Vision panel** | Maximum 0.1 m² if provided |
| **Material** | Steel, timber core with intumescent seals, or FRP |
| **Test standard** | IS 3614 Part 2 / BS 476 Pt 22 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** A fire door is only as good as its **frame + seal + closer + gap tolerance**. The maximum door-to-frame gap is **3 mm at the head and 4 mm at the sides** for proper smoke sealing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve

The **standard time-temperature curve** prescribed by ISO 834 (adopted in India via IS 3809) is the foundation of all FRL values.

**Step 1 — Start with the gas temperature equation in the furnace:**

$$
T_g = T_0 + 345 \,\log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the variables and reference conditions:**

- $T_g$ = furnace gas temperature (°C)
- $T_0$ = initial ambient temperature (°C), taken as **20 °C** in IS 3809
- $t$ = time elapsed (minutes)

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the time-varying part:**

$$
T_g = 20 + 345 \,\log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate the standard fire temperatures at key time intervals (this is what examiners test):**

| Time $t$ (min) | $8t + 1$ | $\log_{10}(8t+1)$ | $T_g$ (°C) |
|----------------|----------|-------------------|------------|
| 0 | 1 | 0.000 | 20 |
| 5 | 41 | 1.613 | 576 |
| 10 | 81 | 1.908 | 678 |
| 15 | 121 | 2.083 | 738 |
| 30 | 241 | 2.382 | 842 |
| 60 | 481 | 2.682 | 945 |
| 90 | 721 | 2.858 | 1006 |
| 120 | 961 | 2.983 | 1049 |
| 180 | 1441 | 3.159 | 1109 |
| 240 | 1921 | 3.284 | 1153 |
| 360 | 2881 | 3.460 | 1213 |

> [!NOTE]
> At **t = 30 min**, the standard fire reaches **842 °C**. This is the reference rating point for a **1-hour FRL wall**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Fire Severity (Pettersson Concept)

**The Problem:** Real fires in buildings often have **higher (or lower) severity** than the ISO 834 standard test furnace. We need an **equivalent time** $t_e$ such that the real fire's heat output equals that of a standard fire of duration $t_e$.

**Step 1 — Define the Fire Load:**

$$
Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i H_i \quad [\text{MJ}]
$$

where $M_i$ = mass of combustible material $i$ (kg) and $H_i$ = calorific value (MJ/kg).

**Step 2 — Convert to Fire Load Density (q):**

$$
q = \frac{Q}{A_{floor}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{m}^2}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CIB W14 / Pettersson equivalent severity formula:**

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot A_{floor} \cdot k_e}{F_{ref}}
$$

where:

- $k_e$ = equivalent conversion factor (≈ **0.25 to 0.30** for typical office occupancies)
- $F_{ref}$ = reference heat release rate (≈ **1500 kW** for standard ISO 834)
- $t_e$ = equivalent fire duration (minutes)

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**

**Given:** An office room with:
- $A_{floor}$ = **100 m²**
- Fire load $q$ = **500 MJ/m²** (typical office)
- $k_e$ = **0.30**
- $F_{ref}$ = **1500 kW**

**Solution:**

$$
t_e = \frac{q \cdot A_{floor} \cdot k_e}{F_{ref}}
$$

$$
t_e = \frac{500 \times 100 \times 0.30}{1500}
$$

$$
t_e = \frac{15000}{1500}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_e = 10 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The actual fire in this office is equivalent to a **10-minute ISO 834 standard fire**. Therefore, a compartment wall with **FRL 30 min (or higher)** is theoretically adequate. However, design codes (NBC 2016) **mandate a minimum 2-hour FRL** for offices, providing a large safety margin.

### 3.3 Derivation: Ventilation Factor (Lie's Method)

For a fire to spread to adjacent compartments, **heat must transfer through openings**. The **ventilation factor** quantifies the available air supply.

**Step 1 — Define ventilation openings:**

$$
D = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_{floor}}
$$

where:
- $A_v$ = total area of ventilation openings (m²)
- $H_v$ = average height of openings (m)
- $A_{floor}$ = floor area of compartment (m²)

**Step 2 — Worked Example:**

**Given:** A residential bedroom with:
- One window: 1.2 m wide × 1.0 m high → $A_v = 1.2 \text{ m}^2$
- $H_v$ = 1.0 m
- $A_{floor}$ = 12 m²

**Solution:**

$$
D = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{1.2 \times \sqrt{1.0}}{12}
$$

$$
D = \frac{1.2 \times 1.0}{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 0.10 \;\; \text{m}^{1/2}}
$$

**Interpretation (Lie's chart, 1973):** A $D$ value of 0.10 indicates a **moderately under-ventilated fire**, leading to longer fire duration and higher internal temperatures. This justifies the **2-hour FRL requirement** for residential compartment walls in NBC 2016.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Upgrade FRL

| Triggering Condition | FRL Upgrade Required |
|----------------------|----------------------|
| Building height > **15 m** (high-rise) | +1 h over base |
| Floor area > **1000 m²** on any floor | +1 h |
| Combustible façade / cladding | +0.5 h, plus fire stops at every floor |
| Storage of flammable liquids | FRL ≥ 4 h |
| Basement levels | FRL = 4 h (NBC 2016) |
| Hospitals, schools, old-age homes | +1 h (defend-in-place strategy) |
| Data centres, telecom rooms | +1 h + gaseous suppression |
| Atrium crossing multiple floors | FRL of the atrium boundary = **2 h + sprinklers** |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Selecting Compartment Wall for a Library

**Given:**
- A library with floor area = **800 m²** per floor
- 4 floors
- Fire load density $q$ = **1200 MJ/m²** (books, paper)
- Has sprinklers: **YES**
- Height: **18 m** (high-rise)

**Required Steps:**

**Step 1 — Check against NBC 2016 Table 4.4.1:**
- Group B (Educational) base FRL = **2 h**
- Group F (Mercantile / Library) base FRL = **2 h**

**Step 2 — Check high-rise trigger:**
- 18 m > 15 m → **+1 h upgrade** → FRL = **3 h**

**Step 3 — Check high fire load:**
- $q$ = 1200 MJ/m² is **high** (typical office ≈ 500 MJ/m²) → **+1 h upgrade** → FRL = **4 h**

**Step 4 — Sprinkler compensation:**
- With **automatic sprinklers**, NBC 2016 permits a **1 h reduction** → FRL = **3 h**

**Step 5 — Final Design FRL:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FRL} = 3 \text{ hours} \;/\; 3 \text{ hours} \;/\; 3 \text{ hours}}
$$

(Stability / Integrity / Insulation)

**Step 6 — Additional requirements:**

- All doors: **3-h fire doors with intumescent seals**
- HVAC ducts: **3-h fire dampers at all penetrations**
- Cable penetrations: **3-h fire stop systems**
- Cavity barriers at every **floor level** in suspended ceiling void
- Maximum compartment area must not exceed **750 m²** (Group B) → therefore **subdivide 800 m² into two compartments** separated by a **3-h fire wall**.

> [!WARNING]
> Many students forget the **compartment area subdivision** — even with a 3-h FRL wall, if the floor area exceeds 750 m², the code is violated. **Always check both FRL and area limits together.**
