## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a "Fire Compartment" as per NBC 2016. List the THREE performance criteria of Fire Resistance Rating (FRL).*

**Model Answer:**

A **Fire Compartment** is a space within a building fully enclosed by fire-resisting walls, floors, and ceilings, with all openings protected by fire-rated assemblies of equivalent or higher FRL.

The three performance criteria of FRL are:

$$
\boxed{\text{FRL} = R \;/ \; E \;/ \; I}
$$

- **R — Stability (Load-bearing capacity)** : The element must not collapse.
- **E — Integrity** : No through-cracks, gaps, or sustained flaming on the unexposed face.
- **I — Insulation** : Temperature rise on the unexposed face must not exceed 140 K (average) and 180 K (maximum).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What are cavity barriers? Why are they required in compartmentation?*

**Model Answer:**

**Cavity barriers** are fire-resistant materials (mineral wool, intumescent strips) installed within concealed voids of a building (suspended ceilings, raised floors, external wall cavities, roof voids) to **prevent the unseen spread of fire and smoke** through these hidden spaces.

They are required because:
1. Fire and smoke can travel through concealed voids **undetected** for long distances.
2. Without cavity barriers, a fire originating in a lower floor can bypass the compartment wall by traveling through the ceiling plenum.
3. NBC 2016 mandates cavity barriers at every **floor level**, every **compartment wall intersection**, and at intervals not exceeding **20 m** along the cavity length.
4. The FRL of the cavity barrier must be **equal to that of the compartment wall** it intersects.

---

### 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Traditional Design & Code Application*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for Module 3]**

A multi-storey office building has the following features:

- **Number of floors:** 6
- **Floor area per floor:** 1200 m²
- **Occupancy:** Group E (Business)
- **Height:** 22 m
- **Fire load density:** 800 MJ/m²
- **Automatic sprinklers:** Installed
- **HVAC system present:** YES
- **Suspended ceiling:** YES

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the required **Fire Resistance Rating (FRL)** of the compartment walls, citing the relevant clauses of NBC 2016. Show the step-by-step calculation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List the requirements for:
(i) Fire doors in the compartment wall, (ii) HVAC duct penetrations, (iii) Cavity barriers in the suspended ceiling, and (iv) Cable and pipe penetrations.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the base FRL (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.1, Table 4.4.1):**
- Group E (Business) base FRL = **2 hours**

**Step 2 — Check high-rise trigger (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.3):**

$$
H = 22 \text{ m} > 15 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Add 1 h}
$$

$$
\text{FRL after Step 2} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3 — Check fire load density (NBC 2016 Annex A):**
- $q$ = 800 MJ/m² — this is **moderate to high** (not exceeding 1000 MJ/m² threshold). **No further upgrade required** at this stage.

**Step 4 — Sprinkler compensation (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.4):**

$$
\text{Sprinklers installed} \Rightarrow \text{Reduce by 1 h}
$$

$$
\text{FRL after Step 4} = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum permissible FRL check:**
- NBC 2016 mandates a **minimum 2 h FRL** even with sprinklers.

**Step 6 — Compartment area check (NBC 2016 Table 4.4.1):**
- Group E max compartment area = **1500 m²**
- Floor area = 1200 m² < 1500 m² → **No subdivision required**

**Final Answer (a):**

$$
\boxed{\text{FRL of Compartment Wall} = 2 \text{ hours } / 2 \text{ hours } / 2 \text{ hours}}
$$

The wall is a **non-load-bearing partition** so only **E/I** criteria apply; if load-bearing, all three (R/E/I) apply.

---

##### **Model Solution — Q A (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Fire Door Requirements [2 Marks]:**

- FRL = **2 h** (equal to compartment wall)
- **Self-closing** mechanism (IS 3614 Pt 2)
- **Intumescent seals** on all three edges (head, jambs)
- Maximum gap tolerance: **3 mm at head, 4 mm at jambs**
- **Smoke seals** mandatory (escape route doors)
- Vision panel: ≤ **0.1 m²** (toughened or wired glass)
- **No wedges, no chocks** — must be self-closing at all times

**(ii) HVAC Duct Penetrations [2 Marks]:**

- **Fire dampers** must be installed at **every compartment wall penetration** (IS 1646)
- Damper **fusible link** rated at **68 °C** (or 74 °C for some codes)
- Damper FRL = **2 h** (equal to wall)
- Duct material: **galvanized steel, minimum 1.0 mm thick**
- Ducts in plenum: **external insulation** of 2 h FRL if they pass through the compartment

**(iii) Cavity Barriers in Suspended Ceiling [1.5 Marks]:**

- Installed at **every floor slab level** (continuous, full depth of cavity)
- At **every 20 m** maximum along the cavity length
- At **every intersection with a compartment wall**
- FRL = **equal to the compartment wall** = 2 h
- Material: **mineral wool, intumescent strips, or fire-rated board**

**(iv) Cable and Pipe Penetrations [1.5 Marks]:**

- **Cables:** Fire-rated foam seals, fire pillows, or intumescent putty (IS 12458)
- **Metal pipes:** Cementitious mortar + mineral wool packing
- **Combustible pipes (PVC):** **Intumescent fire collars** (swell on heating to seal the melted pipe opening)
- All penetrations must be tested to **BS 476 Pt 20 / EN 1366-3**
- FRL of penetration seal = **2 h** (equal to compartment wall)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Analytical / Design-Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **six mandatory requirements of a fire compartment**. How does failure of any one of these compromise the entire compartmentation strategy?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A storage godown (Group H) has the following details:
- Floor area = 600 m²
- Height = 8 m (single-storey)
- Fire load density = 2500 MJ/m² (high — rubber goods stored)
- No sprinklers

Determine:
(i) Required FRL of compartment wall,
(ii) Maximum permissible compartment area,
(iii) Whether the godown complies with NBC 2016.
(iv) If non-compliant, what corrective measures do you recommend?

---

##### **Model Solution — Q B (a) — 7 Marks**

The **six mandatory requirements** of a fire compartment are:

**1. Fully Enclosed Boundary (Walls, Floor, Ceiling) — [1.2 Marks]**
Every compartment must be enclosed on **all six sides** with fire-resisting construction. Failure = fire can bypass the wall through the ceiling or floor plenum.

**2. Fire-Rated Openings (Doors, Windows) — [1.2 Marks]**
Every opening must be protected by **fire-rated assemblies of equal or higher FRL**. A 2-h wall with a wooden door destroys the entire 2-h rating.

**3. Sealed Service Penetrations — [1.2 Marks]**
Pipes, cables, ducts, and trays must be **fire-stopped** at every crossing. A 2-h wall with an unsealed 100 mm pipe penetration is reduced to ~**15 minutes** in practice.

**4. Cavity Barriers — [1.2 Marks]**
Hidden voids (suspended ceilings, raised floors) must be **interrupted by cavity barriers**. Otherwise, smoke can travel 50+ m above a compartment wall undetected.

**5. Maximum Compartment Area — [1.1 Marks]**
The compartment floor area must not exceed the **NBC limit for that occupancy**. A larger area allows flashover and full-room involvement before suppression.

**6. Continuity from Slab to Slab — [1.1 Marks]**
The compartment wall must extend **continuously from floor slab to floor slab (or to a fire-rated ceiling)** with no termination mid-height.

> **Conclusion (1.0 Mark):** A fire compartment is **only as strong as its weakest element**. Even one failure in any of these six requirements reduces the entire FRL to the **weakest component's rating** — the *weakest link principle* (WLP).

---

##### **Model Solution — Q B (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Required FRL [2 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Group H (Storage) base FRL (NBC Table 4.4.1) = **2 h**

**Step 2:** Fire load density = 2500 MJ/m² — this is **high fire load** (≥ 1500 MJ/m² threshold as per NBC Annex).

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Upgrade to } 4 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 3:** No sprinklers → no reduction.

**Step 4:** Height = 8 m < 15 m → no high-rise upgrade.

**Final FRL:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FRL} = 4 \text{ hours} \;/\; 4 \text{ hours} \;/\; 4 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

**(ii) Maximum Permissible Compartment Area [2 Marks]:**

From NBC 2016 Table 4.4.1, Group H (Storage):

$$
A_{max} = 250 \text{ m}^2
$$

(For high fire load, NBC further restricts to **150 m²** in some editions.)

---

**(iii) Compliance Check [1.5 Marks]:**

$$
A_{provided} = 600 \text{ m}^2 \quad > \quad A_{max} = 250 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \boxed{\text{NON-COMPLIANT}}
$$

The godown **exceeds the maximum compartment area by 140%**. This is a serious code violation.

---

**(iv) Corrective Measures [1.5 Marks]:**

1. **Subdivide** the godown into **3 equal compartments** of 200 m² each (each ≤ 250 m²), separated by **4-h fire walls**.
2. **Install automatic sprinklers** (ESFR or high-output type) to reduce FRL to 2 h and increase area to 500 m² — but **subdivision is still preferred**.
3. **Separate the high-fire-load storage** (rubber goods) from the main storage with a 4-h fire wall.
4. **Provide fire doors** of 4-h rating at all openings.
5. **Install fire dampers** in any HVAC ducts.
6. **Add smoke venting** (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.6) to remove hot gases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Forgetting to **check both FRL and compartment area** (marks lost for incomplete design).
> - ❌ Writing FRL as "2 hours" without specifying **R / E / I** components.
> - ❌ Confusing **fire wall** (separates buildings) with **fire compartment wall** (separates rooms/zones).
> - ❌ Ignoring the **+1 h upgrade** for buildings above 15 m height.
> - ❌ Forgetting **cavity barriers** — examiner's favourite 2-mark question.
> - ❌ Treating **fire doors and fire walls as separate**; the door FRL must **equal** the wall FRL.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Requirements for Compartmentation**

- [x] **Definition:** Compartmentation = dividing a building into fire-tight sections to **confine fire and smoke**.
- [x] **Fire Compartment:** Fully enclosed by fire-rated walls, floors, ceilings with protected openings.
- [x] **FRL Format:** **R (Stability) / E (Integrity) / I (Insulation)** — tested in hours.
- [x] **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834):** $T_g = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$.
- [x] **6 Mandatory Requirements:** Enclosure, Rated openings, Penetration seals, Cavity barriers, Max area, Continuity.
- [x] **NBC 2016 Reference:** Part 4, Cl. 4.4 & Table 4.4.1.
- [x] **High-rise trigger:** Height > **15 m** → add **+1 h** to FRL.
- [x] **Sprinkler compensation:** Sprinklers installed → FRL can be **reduced by 1 h**.
- [x] **High fire load trigger:** $q \geq 1000$ MJ/m² → **+1 h** to FRL.
- [x] **Fire door gap tolerance:** 3 mm head, 4 mm jambs.
- [x] **Fire damper fusible link:** **68 °C** (IS 1646).
- [x] **Cavity barrier spacing:** every **20 m** + every **floor level** + every wall intersection.
- [x] **PVC pipe penetrations:** use **intumescent collars** (swell on heating).
- [x] **Equivalent severity formula:** $t_e = \dfrac{q A_{floor} k_e}{F_{ref}}$.
- [x] **Ventilation factor (Lie):** $D = \dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_{floor}}$.
- [x] **Always cross-check:** FRL *and* Area limits — both must comply.
- [x] **Hazardous occupancies (Group J):** Min FRL = **4 h**.
- [x] **Basements:** FRL = **4 h** (NBC 2016).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements of Fire Detection and Alarm System in Different Occupancies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Requirements of Fire Detection and Alarm System in Different Occupancies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Fire Detection and Alarm System (FDAS)** is an integrated network of automatic sensors, manual call points, control panels, notification appliances, and power supplies designed to identify the incipient stage of a fire through the detection of one or more physical phenomena (smoke, heat, flame, gas) and to alert building occupants and emergency responders through auditory, visual, and signaling mechanisms. The **occupancy-specific requirements** of an FDAS refer to the customized design, selection, placement, and performance criteria of detection and alarm components that are mandated based on the **functional use, fire load, occupant characteristics, and risk profile** of a building, as classified under **NBC 2016 (National Building Code of India), IS 2189:2008, NFPA 72, and IS 3218** standards. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Occupancy classification is the *primary design driver* for FDAS. The same heat detector that works in a warehouse may fail catastrophically in a hospital ICU or a clean room. KTU examinations frequently test this *occupancy-detector matching logic*. ## 1.2 Occupancy Classification (NBC 2016 Framework) The National Building Code of India classifies buildings into the following major occupancy groups, each demanding distinct FDAS architectures: | Group | Occupancy Type | Examples | |:---|:---|:---| | **A** | Residential | Apartments, hostels, dormitories, hotels | | **B** | Educational | Schools, colleges, training institutes | | **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, nursing homes, prisons | | **D** | Assembly | Theatres, cinemas, auditoriums, malls | | **E** | Business | Offices, banks, IT parks | | **F** | Mercantile | Shops, department stores, supermarkets | | **G** | Industrial | Factories, workshops, manufacturing units | | **H** | Storage | Warehouses, godowns, cold storage | | **J** | Hazardous | Petrol pumps, chemical plants, LPG storage | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sensory Nervous System" of a Building Imagine your body as a building: - **Skin sensors (touch, temperature, pain)** = **Smoke, heat, and flame detectors** mounted on ceilings. - **Pain signal to the brain** = **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** that processes sensor data. - **Brain issuing alarm signals to muscles/organs** = **Notification appliances** (sounders, strobes, speakers). - **Specialized pain receptors in sensitive areas** = **Aspirating smoke detectors (ASD)** in server rooms or **flame detectors** in turbine halls. Just like your fingertips have a much higher density of nerve endings than your back (because precision matters more there), a **server room requires far more sensitive detection than a parking garage**. This is *occupancy-based detector sensitivity tuning*. ## 1.4 Why Occupancy-Specific Design is Mandatory > [!NOTE] > **Core Engineering Rationale:** A "one-size-fits-all" FDAS design violates two fundamental engineering principles: > 1. **False Alarm Immunity** — A smoke detector in a kitchen (Group A) will trigger false alarms from cooking aerosols; thus *heat detectors* are preferred. > 2. **Life Safety Priority** — Sleeping occupants (hotels, hospitals) cannot self-evacuate quickly; thus *smoke detection in occupied rooms + L1/L2 coverage* is mandated. The **life safety risk hierarchy** (from highest to lowest detection sensitivity requirement) is: $$ \text{Sleeping occupants} > \text{Captive occupants} > \text{Mobile aware occupants} > \text{Unoccupied spaces} $$ This hierarchy directly dictates whether **smoke (very early detection)** or **heat (delayed but robust)** detection is mandated for each space. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Core Design Philosophy The design of an FDAS for any occupancy follows a **four-pillar framework**: 1. **Detection Layer** — Senses the fire phenomenon. 2. **Decision Layer** — FACP processes sensor signals using algorithms. 3. **Notification Layer** — Alerts occupants and emergency services. 4. **Power & Supervision Layer** — Ensures 24/7 reliability with backup. Each pillar must be tailored based on the **occupancy group, fire load, evacuation strategy, and occupant vulnerability**. ## 2.2 Detector Selection Logic by Occupancy ### 2.2.1 Group A — Residential Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - High occupant load, often sleeping. - Furnishings generate **smouldering fires** (slow smoke production). - Kitchens produce **cooking aerosols** (false alarm source). **Detector Selection:** - **Apartments/Hostels:** Photoelectric smoke detectors in bedrooms and living areas; heat detectors in kitchens. - **Hotel guest rooms:** Smoke detectors + sounder base (combined detector + notification in one unit). - **Corridors:** Smoke detectors at maximum 7.5 m spacing (per IS 2189). ### 2.2.2 Group B — Educational Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - High occupant density, mostly awake. - Paper, chemicals, and labs introduce varied fire loads. - Children may have slower evacuation speeds. **Detector Selection:** - **Classrooms:** Smoke detectors (ionization or photoelectric). - **Laboratories:** Heat detectors (to avoid false alarms from fumes) + manual call points. - **Auditoriums:** Aspirating smoke detection (ASD) for high-ceiling, stratified smoke scenarios. ### 2.2.3 Group C — Institutional (Healthcare) **Fire Risk Profile:** - **Defend-in-place** strategy — patients cannot self-evacuate. - Oxygen-enriched environments accelerate fire growth. - Sterile areas require aspirating systems (no ceiling-mounted detectors). **Detector Selection:** - **Patient rooms:** Addressable smoke detectors with sounder bases. - **ICU/Operation theatres:** Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) with very early warning. - **Medical gas storage:** Explosion-proof heat detectors (intrinsically safe). ### 2.2.4 Group D — Assembly Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - Very high occupant load (hundreds to thousands). - Crowd panic potential. - Large open spaces with high ceilings → **smoke stratification**. **Detector Selection:** - **Atriums/Malls:** Beam smoke detectors across the ceiling void. - **Stadia:** Linear heat detection + CCTV-based flame detection. - **Notification:** Voice evacuation systems (VES) with pre-recorded multilingual messages. ### 2.2.5 Group E — Business Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - IT equipment, paper, cable insulation fires (Class A & Class C). - High daytime occupancy, low night-time occupancy. **Detector Selection:** - **Offices:** Photoelectric smoke detectors. - **Server rooms:** ASD + gaseous suppression integration. - **Cable trays:** Linear heat detection cable (LHDC). ### 2.2.6 Group F — Mercantile Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - High fire load from merchandise (textiles, plastics). - Open floor plans allow rapid fire spread. **Detector Selection:** - **Shopping floors:** Smoke detectors with VES. - **Storage backrooms:** Heat detectors (resistant to dust). ### 2.2.7 Group G — Industrial Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - High fire loads (fuels, solvents, metals). - Harsh environments (dust, humidity, vibration, temperature). - Explosive atmospheres. **Detector Selection:** - **General industry:** Heat detectors (rate-of-rise or fixed temperature). - **Oil & gas / paint shops:** Flame detectors (IR/UV). - **Explosive zones:** Intrinsically safe (IS) or explosion-proof detectors certified to **IS/IEC 60079**. ### 2.2.8 Group H — Storage Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - Deep-seated smouldering fires in stored goods. - High ceilings, often unmanned. **Detector Selection:** - **Warehouses:** Aspirating detection (multi-level) or beam detectors. - **Cold storage:** Specialized low-temperature smoke detectors. ### 2.2.9 Group J — Hazardous Occupancies **Fire Risk Profile:** - Immediate life threat from explosion/toxic release. - Fires may not follow normal smoke-heat-flame progression. **Detector Selection:** - **Gas detectors** (LPG, methane, CO). - **UV/IR flame detectors** for hydrocarbon fires. - **Linear heat detection** for cable trenches and tank farms. ## 2.3 Alarm System Requirements by Occupancy ### 2.3.1 Audibility Levels The alarm must produce a sound pressure level of at least **65 dB(A)** at the bedhead in sleeping areas and **75 dB(A)** at 3 m from any notification appliance in non-sleeping areas, per **IS 2189:2008**. $$ L_{p,\text{req}} \geq 65 \text{ dB(A)} \quad \text{(sleeping)} $$ $$ L_{p,\text{req}} \geq 75 \text{ dB(A)} \quad \text{(non-sleeping, ambient noise up to 60 dB)} $$ For ambient noise exceeding **75 dB(A)**, visual alarm devices (VADs / strobes) are mandatory. ### 2.3.2 Voice Evacuation Systems (VES) Mandatory for: - Buildings with **occupant load > 1000**. - High-rise buildings (> 15 m for residential, > 24 m for others). - Hospitals, airports, metro stations, shopping malls. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |:--|:---|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | dB | Alarm audibility | | 2 | Detector Spacing (smooth ceiling) | $S \leq 4.5 \sqrt{H}$ for heat; $\leq 7.5$ m for smoke | m | IS 2189 spacing | | 3 | Coverage area per smoke detector | $A_c \leq 50$ to $80$ m² (smooth ceiling) | m² | Per IS 2189 | | 4 | Coverage area per heat detector | $A_c \leq 30$ to $50$ m² | m² | Per IS 2189 | | 5 | Response Time Index (RTI) | $RTI = \tau \sqrt{u}$ | m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ² | Heat detector rating | | 6 | Beam detector max range | $L \leq 100$ m (typical) | m | Atriums, warehouses | | 7 | ASD pipe length max | $L_{\text{pipe}} \leq 200$ m per zone | m | Server rooms | | 8 | Sounder count formula | $n \geq \dfrac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_{\text{coverage,sounder}}}$ | — | Notification design | | 9 | Evacuation time (simple) | $t_{\text{evac}} = \dfrac{N}{f \cdot W}$ | s | Crowd egress | | 10 | Battery backup duration | $t_{\text{bat}} \geq 24$ h standby + 15 min alarm | h | IS 2189 Clause 4.4 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The spacing formula $S \leq 4.5\sqrt{H}$ is derived from the **ceiling jet velocity decay** and is a favourite numerical in KTU exams. Always show units and substitute values clearly. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Occupancy-based FDAS design is applied by **Fire Safety Engineers, MEP Consultants, and Code Compliance Auditors** in: - **High-rise residential towers** (Group A) — phased evacuation with smoke detection in every dwelling unit. - **IT parks and data centres** (Group E) — ASD + gaseous suppression integration. - **Refineries and LNG terminals** (Group J) — flame and gas detection with SIL-rated loops. - **Hospitals (Group C)** — defend-in-place with voice evacuation in patient care areas. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Detector Spacing on a Smooth Ceiling The maximum spacing between two heat detectors on a smooth, flat ceiling is governed by the **ceiling jet velocity profile** as a function of radial distance from the fire plume axis. ### Step 1 — Ceiling Jet Velocity Model For an axisymmetric fire plume impinging on an unconfined ceiling at height $H$ below the ceiling, the maximum horizontal velocity of the ceiling jet at radial distance $r$ is given empirically as: $$ u_{\max}(r) = \dfrac{A \, \dot{Q}^{1/3}}{H^{1/3} \, (r/H)^{1/2}} \quad \text{for} \quad r/H \geq 0.4 $$ where: - $\dot{Q}$ = convective heat release rate (kW) - $H$ = ceiling height above fire (m) - $r$ = radial distance from plume axis (m) - $A$ = empirical constant $\approx 0.95$ m/(s·kW¹ᐟ³) ### Step 2 — Activation Condition for Rate-of-Rise Heat Detector A rate-of-rise (ROR) heat detector activates when the ceiling jet velocity at its location exceeds a critical threshold $u_{\text{crit}}$ that corresponds to a temperature rise of **8.3 °C/min** (per IS 2189 classification). $$ u_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{RTI}{t} \quad \text{with} \quad RTI = \tau \cdot u^{1/2} $$ ### Step 3 — Solving for Maximum Spacing Setting $r = S/2$ (spacing between two detectors with the fire plume at the centre of one square), and requiring $u_{\max} \geq u_{\text{crit}}$, we obtain: $$ S \leq 2 \cdot r_{\max} = 2 H \left( \dfrac{A \dot{Q}^{1/3}}{H^{1/3} \, u_{\text{crit}}} \right)^2 $$ For a **standard test fire** with $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW and typical RTI values, IS 2189 simplifies this to the engineering formula: $$ S \leq 4.5 \sqrt{H} \quad \text{(m)} $$ ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A warehouse has a smooth ceiling at height $H = 9$ m. Determine the maximum spacing of heat detectors as per IS 2189. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\max} &= 4.5 \sqrt{H} \\ &= 4.5 \sqrt{9} \\ &= 4.5 \times 3 \\ S_{\max} &= 13.5 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ However, IS 2189 caps the practical maximum at **9 m** to account for non-ideal ceiling conditions, obstructions, and air movement. Hence the **governing spacing is 9 m**, and detectors are placed on a square grid not exceeding $9 \text{ m} \times 9 \text{ m} = 81$ m². > [!NOTE] > The **81 m²** maximum coverage area for heat detectors is a direct application of the simplified spacing rule. For smoke detectors, the limit is typically **50 m²**, reflecting higher sensitivity requirements. ## 3.2 Derivation: Required Number of Notification Appliances ### Step 1 — Coverage Area of One Sounder A single sounder with **75 dB(A) at 3 m** in free-field conditions has a coverage area approximated as a circle: $$ A_{\text{sounder}} = \pi r_{\text{eff}}^2 $$ where $r_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective radius at which the sound level drops to **65 dB(A)** (sleeping area threshold). From the inverse square law for sound: $$ L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right) $$ Setting $L_p(r_{\text{eff}}) = 65$ dB and $L_p(3 \text{ m}) = 75$ dB: $$ \begin{aligned} 65 &= 75 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_{\text{eff}}}{3}\right) \\ 10 &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_{\text{eff}}}{3}\right) \\ 0.5 &= \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_{\text{eff}}}{3}\right) \\ \dfrac{r_{\text{eff}}}{3} &= 10^{0.5} \approx 3.162 \\ r_{\text{eff}} &\approx 9.49 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Coverage Area per Sounder $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{sounder}} &= \pi (9.49)^2 \\ &\approx \pi \times 90.06 \\ A_{\text{sounder}} &\approx 283 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Sounder Count For a hotel floor of area $A_{\text{floor}} = 1500$ m²: $$ \begin{aligned} n &= \dfrac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_{\text{sounder}}} = \dfrac{1500}{283} \\ &\approx 5.30 \\ n_{\text{req}} &= 6 \text{ sounders (rounded up)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Detector-Occupancy Suitability | Occupancy | Primary Detector | Secondary Detector | Alarm Type | Code Reference | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | A (Residential) | Photoelectric smoke | Heat (kitchen) | Sounder + Visual | IS 2189 Table 1 | | B (Educational) | Smoke (photoelectric) | Manual call points | VES + Bell | NBC 2016 Pt 4 | | C (Hospital) | ASD + Smoke (patient rooms) | Heat (service areas) | VES + Visual | NFPA 72 Ch. 18 | | D (Assembly) | Beam smoke (atriums) | Manual call points | VES (multilingual) | NBC 2016 Pt 4 | | E (Office) | Smoke (photoelectric) | ASD (server rooms) | Sounder + VES | IS 2189 | | F (Mercantile) | Smoke | Heat (storage) | Sounder + VES | IS 2189 | | G (Industrial) | Heat (ROR) / Flame | Manual call points | Siren + Visual | IS 3218 | | H (Storage) | ASD / Beam | LHDC | Siren | NFPA 72 Ch. 29 | | J (Hazardous) | Flame (IR/UV) + Gas | LHDC | Siren + Visual | IS/IEC 60079 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fire Detection and Alarm System — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A1[Smoke Detector
Photoelectric] --> FACP A2[Ionization Smoke Detector] --> FACP A3[Heat Detector
Rate of Rise] --> FACP A4[Fixed Temp Heat Detector] --> FACP A5[Beam Smoke Detector] --> FACP A6[Aspirating Smoke Detector
ASD] --> FACP A7[Flame Detector IR and UV] --> FACP A8[Manual Call Point MCP] --> FACP A9[Gas Detector
Catalytic or IR] --> FACP FACP[Fire Alarm Control Panel
FACP] --> B1[Addressable Loop Controller] B1 --> C1[Sounder and Bell] B1 --> C2[Strobe and Visual Alarm] B1 --> C3[Voice Evacuation System
VES] B1 --> C4[Fire Brigade Interface] B1 --> C5[BMS Integration] B1 --> C6[Suppression System
Gas Sprinkler Interface] FACP --> D1[Primary Power
230V AC Mains] FACP --> D2[Battery Backup
24V DC 24h plus 15min] FACP --> E1[Repeater Panel] FACP --> E2[Remote Annunciator] FACP --> E3[Graphic Workstation] ``` ## 4.2 Occupancy-Based Detector Selection Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD Start[Start Occupancy Identification] --> Q1{Group A
Residential Sleeping} Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Photoelectric Smoke plus
Sounder Base in Bedrooms] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Group C
Hospital ICU or OT} Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Aspirating Smoke Detection
Very Early Warning plus VES] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Group D
Assembly High Ceiling} Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Beam Smoke Detector
plus Voice Evacuation] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Group E
Server or Data Room} Q4 -- Yes --> P4[Aspirating Smoke plus
Gaseous Suppression Interface] Q4 -- No --> Q5{Group G
Industrial Harsh Environment} Q5 -- Yes --> P5[Heat Detector or Flame Detector
plus Manual Call Points] Q5 -- No --> Q6{Group H
Warehouse Storage} Q6 -- Yes --> P6[Beam Smoke or ASD
plus Linear Heat Cable] Q6 -- No --> Q7{Group J
Hazardous or Explosive} Q7 -- Yes --> P7[Flame Detector IR UV plus
Gas Detector Intrinsically Safe] Q7 -- No --> P8[Default Smoke plus Heat
per IS 2189] P1 --> Out[Notification Layer
Sounder VES Strobe] P2 --> Out P3 --> Out P4 --> Out P5 --> Out P6 --> Out P7 --> Out P8 --> Out ``` ## 4.3 FDAS Decision Sequence — Time-Critical Action Chain ```mermaid graph LR S1[Fire Initiation
T equals 0] --> S2[Detection by Sensor
T equals 30 to 60 sec] S2 --> S3[FACP Signal Processing
T equals 1 to 5 sec] S3 --> S4[Alarm Activation
T equals 5 to 10 sec] S4 --> S5[Occupant Notification
VES and Sounders] S4 --> S6[Fire Brigade Auto Dial] S4 --> S7[Suppression Trigger if applicable] S5 --> S8[Evacuation Begins
T approx 1 to 3 min] S6 --> S9[Fire Brigade Arrival
T approx 8 to 15 min] S7 --> S10[Suppression Discharge] S8 --> S11[Safe Assembly Point] S9 --> S12[Fire Attack and Rescue] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Layered Defence Model ```mermaid graph TD L1[Layer 1 Detection
Sensors] --> L2[Layer 2 Decision
FACP and Logic] L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Notification
Sounders Strobes VES] L2 --> L4[Layer 4 Suppression
Sprinkler Gas Systems] L2 --> L5[Layer 5 Emergency Response
Fire Brigade Interface] L1 --> P1[Smoke Detectors] L1 --> P2[Heat Detectors] L1 --> P3[Flame Detectors] L1 --> P4[Gas Detectors] L1 --> P5[Aspirating Systems] L1 --> P6[Beam Detectors] L1 --> P7[Manual Call Points] L2 --> C1[Addressable Loop] L2 --> C2[Cause and Effect Matrix] L2 --> C3[Event Logging] L3 --> N1[Audible Alarms] L3 --> N2[Visual Alarms] L3 --> N3[Voice Messages] L4 --> SP1[Water Based Systems] L4 --> SP2[Gas Based Systems] L4 --> SP3[Foam Systems] L4 --> SP4[Chemical Suppression] L5 --> ER1[Auto Dialer] L5 --> ER2[Fire Pump Start] L5 --> ER3[AHU Shut Down] L5 --> ER4[Smoke Management] L5 --> ER5[Pressurization of Stairwells] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *Define the term "Fire Detection and Alarm System" and list any four detectors commonly used in such systems.* **Model Answer:** A Fire Detection and Alarm System (FDAS) is an integrated arrangement of sensors, control equipment, and notification devices designed to automatically detect the incipient stage of fire and alert occupants and emergency responders. Four common detectors are: 1. **Ionization smoke detector** — detects invisible combustion products. 2. **Photoelectric smoke detector** — detects visible smoke particles via light scattering. 3. **Heat detector (rate-of-rise / fixed temperature)** — detects temperature rise. 4. **Flame detector (IR / UV)** — detects radiant energy from flames. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]** *Why are aspirating smoke detectors preferred in hospital intensive care units (ICUs)?* **Model Answer:** Aspirating Smoke Detectors (ASD) are preferred in ICUs because: 1. They provide **very early warning** (detect smoke at 0.001% obscuration/m), critical for **defend-in-place** strategy. 2. They use **piped sampling**, avoiding ceiling-mounted detectors that may collect dust or interfere with sterile environments. 3. They allow **multiple sensitivity levels** and remote display, enabling nursing staff to monitor continuously without disturbing patients. 4. They integrate with **gaseous suppression systems** for ICU equipment rooms. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Examination]** **(a)** Explain in detail the requirements of a Fire Detection and Alarm System for **Hospital Buildings (Group C Institutional Occupancy)** as per IS 2189 and NBC 2016. Discuss detector selection, alarm strategy, and occupant notification in critical care areas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hotel floor has dimensions 60 m × 25 m with a smooth ceiling at 3 m height. Calculate: (i) The number of smoke detectors required as per IS 2189 spacing. (ii) The minimum number of sounders required if each sounder delivers 75 dB(A) at 3 m and a minimum of 65 dB(A) is required at the bedhead. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — Hospital FDAS Requirements **Detector Selection by Area:** | Area | Detector Type | Justification | |:---|:---|:---| | Patient rooms | Addressable photoelectric smoke with sounder base | Sleeping occupants; per-room localization | | ICU / OT | Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) | Sterile environment, very early warning | | Corridors | Smoke detectors at 7.5 m spacing | Early smoke spread detection | | Service areas / kitchens | Heat detector (fixed temperature) | False alarm immunity from fumes | | Medical gas storage | Intrinsically safe flame / gas detector | Hazardous zone | | Stairwells / lobbies | Smoke detectors + manual call points | Evacuation route protection | **Alarm Strategy:** - **Voice Evacuation System (VES)** is mandatory throughout — pre-recorded evacuation messages in local language. - **Phased evacuation** — alarms activate floor-by-floor to avoid panic. - **Defend-in-place** in patient care areas — alarm alerts staff to initiate horizontal evacuation. **Notification:** - 65 dB(A) at bedhead; 75 dB(A) in corridors. - Visual alarms (strobes) in patient areas where hearing aids may mask sounders. - Auto-dialer to fire brigade within 60 seconds of confirmation. **Cause-and-Effect Matrix (key actions):** 1. Smoke in patient room → room sounder + nurse station annunciator. 2. Smoke on corridor → floor alarm + adjacent floor pre-alarm. 3. ASD Alert (ICU) → staff investigate; ASD Fire → full evacuation initiated. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(i) Smoke Detector Count:** For a smooth ceiling at $H = 3$ m, IS 2189 specifies maximum spacing $S \leq 7.5$ m and maximum coverage area $A_c = 50$ m² per smoke detector. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total floor area} &= 60 \times 25 = 1500 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Maximum detectors} &= \dfrac{1500}{50} = 30 \text{ detectors} \end{aligned} $$ In practice, placed on a $7.5 \text{ m} \times 7.5 \text{ m}$ grid: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rows} &= \lceil 60 / 7.5 \rceil = 8 \\ \text{Columns} &= \lceil 25 / 7.5 \rceil = 4 \\ n_{\text{detectors}} &= 8 \times 4 = 32 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Sounder Count:** From Section 3.2 derivation, $r_{\text{eff}} \approx 9.49$ m, giving $A_{\text{sounder}} \approx 283$ m². $$ \begin{aligned} n &= \lceil 1500 / 283 \rceil \\ n &= \lceil 5.30 \rceil = 6 \text{ sounders} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students often forget that detectors must be placed **within 7.5 m of any wall corner** and **not within 500 mm of any wall**. Grid calculation alone is insufficient — explicit mention of wall offsets is required for full marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the FDAS requirements for **High-Rise Residential Buildings (Group A)** with reference to NBC 2016 and IS 2189. Explain the role of sounder bases, common area detection, and stairwell pressurization interfaces. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An office building has a 30 m × 20 m floor with a 3.5 m smooth ceiling. Calculate: (i) The number of photoelectric smoke detectors required as per IS 2189. (ii) The battery backup capacity (in Ah) required for the FACP, assuming the panel draws 1.5 A in standby and 4.5 A in alarm, with a 15-minute alarm duration. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — High-Rise Residential FDAS **Detector Layout:** - **Each dwelling unit:** Photoelectric smoke detector in the living room and one in each bedroom; sounder base to alert occupants directly within the unit. - **Kitchen:** Heat detector (fixed temperature, 57 °C) to avoid cooking aerosol false alarms. - **Common corridors:** Smoke detectors at 7.5 m spacing. - **Lift lobbies:** Smoke detectors to recall elevators to ground floor. - **Stairwells:** Smoke detectors at every landing. - **Car parks (basement):** Heat detectors (smoke detectors fail in vehicle exhaust environment). **Alarm Architecture:** - **Addressable sounder bases** in each dwelling unit. - **Common area sounders** in corridors (75 dB(A) at 3 m). - **Voice Evacuation System (VES)** mandatory for buildings > 15 m height. **Stairwell Pressurization Interface:** - The FACP must trigger the pressurization fans on **any smoke detection on a fire floor** or in the **stairwell itself**. - Signal transmission through a **dedicated fireman's lift interface**. **Phase Evacuation Logic:** - Alarm on fire floor → activate that floor + one floor above + one floor below. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(i) Smoke Detector Count:** For smooth ceiling at $H = 3.5$ m, $S \leq 7.5$ m, $A_c = 50$ m². $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Floor area} &= 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Detectors by area} &= 600 / 50 = 12 \\ \text{Detectors by grid} &= \lceil 30/7.5 \rceil \times \lceil 20/7.5 \rceil = 4 \times 3 = 12 \\ n_{\text{detectors}} &= 12 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Battery Backup Calculation:** Per IS 2189: **24 hours standby + 15 minutes alarm** at end of standby period. $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text{standby}} &= 1.5 \text{ A for 24 h} = 36 \text{ Ah} \\ I_{\text{alarm}} &= 4.5 \text{ A for 0.25 h} = 1.125 \text{ Ah} \\ I_{\text{total}} &= 36 + 1.125 = 37.125 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned} $$ Applying a **derating factor of 1.25** for battery ageing: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text{design}} &= 37.125 \times 1.25 \\ &= 46.41 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned} $$ Select a standard **48 Ah, 24 V DC** sealed lead-acid battery. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students frequently apply the derating factor **before** summing standby and alarm currents. The correct KTU-accepted sequence is: (1) calculate total Ah, (2) apply derating, (3) round up to next standard rating. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Occupancy classification (NBC 2016 Groups A–J)** is the foundation of all FDAS design decisions. - ✅ **Sleeping occupants** demand the highest detection sensitivity — photoelectric smoke + sounder base. - ✅ **Captive occupants** (hospitals, prisons) require defend-in-place with VES and ASD. - ✅ **Industrial occupancies** use heat/flame/gas detectors due to harsh environments and explosion risks. - ✅ **Alarm audibility thresholds:** **65 dB(A)** for sleeping areas, **75 dB(A)** for non-sleeping. - ✅ **Spacing formula:** $S \leq 4.5\sqrt{H}$ for heat detectors (engineering derivation), capped at 9 m practically. - ✅ **Smoke detector coverage:** 50 m² max on smooth ceilings, 7.5 m max spacing. - ✅ **Voice Evacuation Systems (VES)** are mandatory for high-rises, hospitals, and assemblies with occupant load > 1000. - ✅ **Battery backup:** 24 h standby + 15 min alarm at end of standby, per IS 2189. - ✅ **Derating factor of 1.25** must be applied to calculated Ah before selecting standard battery rating. - ✅ **Beam detectors** for high ceilings (> 6 m) where point detectors are ineffective due to smoke stratification. - ✅ **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)** is the gold standard for clean rooms, data centres, and ICUs. - ✅ **Intrinsically safe detectors** are mandatory in Group J (hazardous) occupancies per IS/IEC 60079. - ✅ **Addressable systems** allow per-device identification; **conventional systems** are zone-based. - ✅ **Cause-and-Effect Matrix** ties detection → decision → notification → suppression → emergency response. - ✅ **Always mention NBC 2016 + IS 2189** in KTU answers to demonstrate code awareness — examiners reward this.

topics.title: Firefighting Shaft, Fireman's Lift, Fire Protection Measures in Buildings as per NBC: Requirements for Portable and Fixed Fire Fighting Installations in Buildings of Different Occupancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firefighting Shaft, Fireman's Lift, Fire Protection Measures in Buildings as per NBC: Requirements for Portable and Fixed Fire Fighting Installations in Buildings of Different Occupancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Firefighting Shaft — The Backbone of Vertical Firefighting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NBC Part 4, Clause 3.1.32):** A **firefighting shaft** is a *protected enclosure (shaft)* that vertically connects the floors of a high-rise building and is purpose-built to enable fire service personnel to perform search, rescue, and firefighting operations safely. It is a *mandatory* vertical infrastructure element for buildings exceeding **15 m in height** as per the National Building Code of India (NBC) 2016.

A firefighting shaft is **NOT a normal staircase or lift shaft**. It is a dedicated, protected void that integrates three life-safety elements:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fireman's Lift** | Vertical transport of fire crew + equipment |
| **Fire Lobby (Vestibule)** | A pressurized refuge area to enter/exit safely |
| **Firefighting Staircase** | Emergency egress & manual hose deployment route |

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vertical Highway for Firefighters"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a building as a **vertical city**. During a fire, the regular lifts are switched off (or become dangerous due to shaft fire spread). The firefighting shaft acts like a **dedicated, armoured, pressurized emergency highway** — only firefighters have the key (Fireman's Switch). It allows them to climb up *next to* the fire, enter a *safe pressurized lobby*, and then attack the fire from a protected location. Without it, firefighters would be forced to climb stairs with all their heavy gear in smoke-filled conditions, drastically slowing response time.

### 1.2 Why is it Mandatory Above 15 m?

Above 15 m height:
- External rescue via ladders becomes **impossible** (most fire service ladders max out at 18 m effective rescue height).
- Internal staircase travel time becomes **excessive** for a fully geared firefighter carrying ~25 kg of equipment.
- Smoke logging in staircases renders them untenable without **pressurization**.

---

## 1.3 Fireman's Lift — The Specialist Vertical Rescue Vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **fireman's lift** is a passenger/goods lift installed in a firefighting shaft with special design features that allow it to be **recalled and operated exclusively by authorized fire service personnel** during a fire emergency via a dedicated **Fireman's Switch** located at the designated landing (usually Ground Floor Lobby).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Emergency Room on Rails"

Think of a fireman's lift as a **hospital ambulance on rails**:
- It has **independent power backup** (a dedicated secondary supply from a generator/DG set),
- It **ignores all normal floor calls** (you cannot call it from upper floors during emergency),
- It **returns automatically** to the designated floor when the fireman's switch is activated (Phase I recall),
- Then **operates only on the firefighter's manual command** from inside the car (Phase II in-car operation).

### Key Distinguishing Features (vs. Normal Lift)

| Parameter | Normal Lift | Fireman's Lift |
|---|---|---|
| Power Supply | Single source | **Dual** (main + emergency DG) |
| Control during fire | Recalled and parked | Recalled then manually controlled |
| Switch | Normal landing call | Dedicated **Fireman's Switch** (key-operated) |
| Car Size | Standard (e.g., 8-person) | **Larger** (typically 13–26 persons) |
| Rated Load | 544–680 kg | **Min. 544 kg, ideally 1020–1500 kg** |
| Fire Resistance | 2 hr shaft | **2 hr fire-rated shaft + lobby** |
| Lobby | Open corridor | **Pressurized fire lobby** |

---

## 1.4 NBC Fire Protection Philosophy — Two-Tier Defense

The National Building Code (NBC 2016, Part 4) prescribes a **defense-in-depth** strategy:

> **Tier 1 — Portable Installations:** Hand-held/mobile extinguishers (operated by trained occupants *before* fire becomes uncontrollable).

> **Tier 2 — Fixed Installations:** Permanently plumbed systems (operated by occupants *and* fire service — automatic detection, manual activation, full-scale suppression).

### Real-World Analogy — "Spray Bottle vs. Sprinkler System"

A **portable extinguisher** is like a **hand-held spray bottle** you use to douse a small kitchen flame. A **fixed installation** (sprinkler/hydrant) is like the **building's full plumbing network** that comes alive automatically when heat breaks a glass bulb — a far more capable, code-compliant solution for larger fires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key NBC Clause to Remember:** Portable extinguishers are required **in ALL** occupancies (Group A to J). Fixed installations (sprinklers, hydrants) are required **based on height, area, and occupancy type** — typically mandatory above **15 m (residential) or 24 m (commercial)** and in all **Hazardous (Group G/H/I)** occupancies.

---

## 1.5 NBC Building Occupancy Classification (Group A–J)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NBC Clause 3.1.3):** All buildings are classified into **10 occupancy groups (A through J)** for the purpose of prescribing life safety and fire protection requirements. The **type, number, and capacity** of fire protection systems depend on the occupancy group, height, and floor area.

| Group | Occupancy Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Residential | Apartments, Hostels, Hotels |
| **B** | Educational | Schools, Colleges, Coaching centres |
| **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, Jails, Mental healthcare |
| **D** | Assembly | Theatres, Stadiums, Auditoriums |
| **E** | Business | Offices, Banks, IT Parks |
| **F** | Mercantile | Shops, Malls, Showrooms |
| **G** | Industrial | Manufacturing, Workshops |
| **H** | Storage | Warehouses, Godowns, Cold storage |
| **I** | Hazardous | Petroleum, Chemical, Explosives storage |
| **J** | Mixed/Miscellaneous | Multi-occupancy mixed-use buildings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Group **A (Residential)** and Group **D (Assembly)** are the **most frequently asked occupancies** in KTU exams because they have the most stringent occupant-related requirements (sleeping occupants in A, large public gatherings in D).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of a Firefighting Shaft

A firefighting shaft must include **all** of the following components as per NBC Clause 3.4.7.2:

### Component Breakdown

1. **Fireman's Lift Shaft** — A dedicated, fire-rated (2 hour) vertical shaft. Walls of **REI 120** rating (Resistance, Integrity, Insulation for 120 minutes).
2. **Fire Lobby (Vestibule)** — A protected area at **every floor** where the lift door opens. Acts as a **refuge area** and a **buffer zone** between the fire floor and the lift shaft.
3. **Firefighting Staircase** — A dedicated staircase (separate from the normal egress staircase in many cases) located *within reach* of the fire lobby.
4. **Fire Service Inlet** — A **siamese connection** at ground level for the fire brigade to pump water into the building's internal hydrant system.
5. **Dry Riser / Wet Riser** — Vertical piping within the shaft for water supply to landing valves on every floor.
6. **Pressurization System** — Mechanical supply of fresh air to maintain positive pressure in the lobby (≥ 25 Pa) to prevent smoke ingress.

### Why Each Element Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Why a separate lift?** Normal lifts are NOT reliable during fires. Power may fail, cars may stop at the fire floor with doors opening onto the fire.
- **Why a lobby?** Even a fire-rated lift door can leak smoke. The pressurized lobby provides a *safe haven* to wait for rescue and a *protected base* to attack the fire.
- **Why pressurization?** Staircases become **chimneys** in a fire, drawing smoke upward. Pressurized lobbies and stairs keep the firefighter's path smoke-free.
- **Why a separate staircase?** Having **two independent staircases** (one for occupant evacuation, one for fire service approach) prevents *head-on collision* between fleeing occupants and approaching firefighters — a known cause of evacuation gridlock.

---

## 2.2 Fireman's Lift — Operating Logic in Two Phases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC Reference: Clause 3.4.7.3 & IS 14665 (Lift Code).**

### Phase I — Automatic Recall (Fireman's Switch Activated)

When the fireman's switch is turned "ON" by the fire service at the designated landing:

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | All car calls & landing calls are **cancelled** |
| 2 | The lift car **ignores upper floor calls** and returns directly to the designated floor (usually Ground or Fire Service Access Level) |
| 3 | Doors open and **remain open** for 30 seconds (configurable) |
| 4 | Lift becomes **inoperative** to normal users — only the firefighter can use it |

### Phase II — Manual In-Car Operation (Firefighter Enters Car)

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Firefighter uses a **special key** to enable manual control inside the car |
| 2 | Each floor is selected **manually** (typically by holding the floor button) |
| 3 | Doors are closed by **holding the door-close button** (overriding door safety for rescue operations) |
| 4 | The car operates on **emergency power** from the DG set |
| 5 | On reaching the fire floor, the firefighter parks, exits into the **pressurized lobby**, and proceeds to attack the fire |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Insight:** The "**two key switches**" — one at the **designated landing** (Phase I) and one **inside the car** (Phase II) — are a very common KTU short-answer question. Memorize this distinction.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Matrix of Fire Protection Installations (NBC Part 4, Table 3)

The NBC prescribes installations based on **occupancy group, height, and area**. The following table is a **synthesized high-yield reference** for KTU exam preparation.

### 2.3.1 Portable Fire Extinguishers — When & Where

| Occupancy | Min. Requirement (NBC) | Type Recommended |
|---|---|---|
| **A (Residential)** | 1 per floor per 200 m² | ABC powder (2 kg) + CO₂ (for electrical) |
| **B (Educational)** | 1 per 200 m² + 1 per lab | ABC + CO₂ |
| **C (Institutional)** | 1 per 100 m² | ABC + CO₂ + Water (mist) |
| **D (Assembly)** | 1 per 100 m² + 2 per stage | ABC + CO₂ + Water |
| **E (Business)** | 1 per 150 m² | ABC + CO₂ |
| **F (Mercantile)** | 1 per 100 m² | ABC + CO₂ + Foam (for storage) |
| **G (Industrial)** | Based on hazard class | Class-specific (Foam/DCP/CO₂) |
| **H (Storage)** | 1 per 200 m² | Foam + DCP |
| **I (Hazardous)** | Class A/B/C risk dependent | Specialized media only |

### 2.3.2 Fixed Installations — Requirements

| Installation | Trigger Condition (NBC) | Common Occupancies |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Riser** | Building > **45 m** height (or > 24 m per some local bylaws) | A, C, D, E, F |
| **Dry Riser** | Building **15 m to 45 m** height | A, B, D, E |
| **Sprinkler System** | > **15 m residential**, > **24 m commercial**, > **1000 m² mercantile** | A, D, E, F, H, I |
| **Hose Reel** | All buildings > **15 m** | All except small residential |
| **Fire Hydrant (External)** | All buildings > **15 m** | All |
| **Automatic Detection & Alarm** | > **24 m** all occupancies; mandatory in C, I | All high-rise |
| **Gas-based Suppression** | Server rooms, Data centres, Archives | E (specific areas) |
| **Foam Pourer System** | Flammable liquid storage (petrol pumps, tanks) | I |
| **Water Spray (Drencher)** | Vertical openings, exposed glazing | D (atrium), industrial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical NBC Threshold Numbers to Memorize:**
> - **15 m** — Firefighting shaft becomes mandatory
> - **24 m** — Sprinklers become mandatory in commercial
> - **45 m** — Wet riser becomes mandatory (vs. dry riser below)
> - **60 m** — Refuge areas every floor
> - **Higher than 70 m** (some bylaws) — Atrium smoke control mandatory

---

## 2.4 Detailed Study of Major Fixed Installations

### 2.4.1 Wet Riser System

A **permanently water-charged** vertical piping system with landing valves on every floor. Connected to a **dedicated fire pump (electric + diesel jockey)** with a **static water storage tank** at the bottom.

- **Pressure at topmost landing valve:** Min. **3.5 bar (≈ 35 m head)**
- **Flow rate:** Min. **900 LPM (litres per minute) per riser**
- **Capacity of static tank:** Min. **1 hour flow × 2 risers** = ~108,000 L for 2 risers
- **Pump capacity:** Min. **2850 LPM at 70 m head** (electric) + **2850 LPM at 70 m head** (diesel standby)

### 2.4.2 Dry Riser System

An **empty (air-charged)** vertical piping with landing valves on every floor. Water is supplied by the **fire brigade via a siamese inlet** at ground level when needed.

- **Charging pressure (air):** ~7 bar
- **Test pressure (water):** 21 bar
- **Advantage:** No risk of water damage in case of pipe burst; cheaper for shorter buildings

### 2.4.3 Sprinkler System

Automatic fire suppression — sprinkler heads (glass bulb or fusible link) **individually activate** when their activation temperature is reached, releasing water directly over the fire.

- **Design density:** Typically **4–12.5 mm/min** (depending on hazard classification — LIH, OH1, OH2, OH3, OH4, HHP per IS 15105 / NFPA 13)
- **Coverage per head:** **9–12 m²** (light hazard) to **7–9 m²** (high hazard)
- **Spacing:** **3–4 m** between heads
- **Spacing from wall:** Max **½ the spacing between heads**
- **Activation temperature (glass bulb):** **57°C (orange), 68°C (red), 79°C (yellow), 93°C (green)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the heat-sensing bulb?** It ensures **only the sprinklers directly above the fire activate** — typically 5–10 heads, conserving water and limiting water damage. This is why 90% of fires in sprinklered buildings are controlled by **2–4 sprinklers** (NFPA statistics).

### 2.4.4 Fire Hydrant System (External + Internal)

- **External Hydrant:** Distance from building **15–30 m**; spacing **every 30–45 m** along access roads
- **Internal Hydrant:** Landing valves on **every floor and at staircase landings**
- **Hose Diameter:** **63 mm (2.5")** for internal, **80–100 mm** for external
- **Hose Length:** Usually **15 m × 2 = 30 m** total

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas, coefficients, and threshold values are **essential for numerical problems** in KTU 2024 exams on this topic. These are tested as 3-mark direct questions and 7-mark problem statements.

### 2.5.1 Fire Pump Sizing Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow rate at landing valve | $Q_{LV} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | L/s | $C_d \approx 0.9$ for nozzle |
| Static tank capacity | $V_{tank} = Q_{pump} \times t_{design} \times N_{risers}$ | Litres | $t_{design} = 3600$ s (1 hour minimum) |
| Pump head (total) | $H_{pump} = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{nozzle}$ | m | Friction loss via Hazen-Williams |
| Friction head loss (Hazen-Williams) | $h_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | m | $Q$ in L/s, $d$ in m, $L$ in m |
| Sprinkler density | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{Q_{sprinkler}}{A_{coverage}}$ | mm/min | Typical: 4.1, 5.0, 12.5 mm/min |
| Number of sprinklers per design area | $n = \dfrac{A_{design}}{A_{per\,head}}$ | — | For LIH: design area 72 m², OH: 144 m² |

### 2.5.2 NBC Threshold Values (Memorize These)

| Parameter | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Firefighting shaft mandatory | $H \geq 15$ m |
| Refuge area mandatory (residential) | $H \geq 60$ m |
| Sprinkler mandatory (commercial) | $H \geq 24$ m or Area $\geq 1000$ m² |
| Wet riser (vs. dry riser) | $H \geq 45$ m |
| Travel distance to nearest exit (unsprinklered) | $\leq 22.5$ m (NBC Table 4) |
| Travel distance (sprinklered) | $\leq 30$ m |
| Dead-end travel | $\leq 6$ m (residential), $\leq 12$ m (others) |
| Fire lift rated speed (min.) | $\geq 1.0$ m/s |
| Fire lift rated load (min.) | $\geq 544$ kg |
| Lobby pressure (min.) | $\geq 25$ Pa (door-closed), $\leq 80$ Pa |

### 2.5.3 Discharge Through Nozzle (Hose Reel / Branch Pipe)

For a fire nozzle discharging water:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.95–0.98 for smooth nozzle, 0.7 for ring nozzle)
- $A$ = nozzle outlet area (m²)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $H$ = effective head at nozzle (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is used by fire engineers to **size pumps and tanks** for hydrant/sprinkler systems. Real-world projects in Kerala (e.g., Infopark buildings, Lulu Mall, KSUM towers) use these exact calculations in their NBC Section 4 (Fire Safety) submission to the local fire service.

### 2.5.4 Pressure-Volume Relationship for Dry Riser Charging

For pneumatic (air) charging of dry riser:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

Where $P$ is absolute pressure and $V$ is volume of trapped air. This is tested as a **small numerical problem** in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Application

In a real KTU-engineered building project, the fire protection design is integrated in this order:

1. **Occupancy classification** → determines applicable code provisions
2. **Height & area** → determines whether shaft/lift/sprinkler is needed
3. **Hydraulic calculation** → sizes pumps, tanks, and pipe diameters
4. **Layout design** → positions risers, sprinklers, hydrants per NBC spacing
5. **Power & control design** → DG sizing for fire pump (typically 100% standby), ATS for fireman's lift
6. **Commissioning tests** → flow test at topmost landing valve, sprinkler activation test, lift recall test

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Used in Practice:** Most Kerala projects use **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) guidelines** for fire insurance ratings, which often prescribe **stricter** requirements than the NBC minimum. The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** standards frequently referenced are **IS 15105 (Sprinklers), IS 15301 (Hydrants), IS 14665 (Lifts), IS 2190 (Extinguishers)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Sizing a Fire Pump for a 50 m Wet Riser

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question pattern** (numerical problem in Part B). The following step-by-step derivation is the model answer. Learn the *methodology* — you will be given different numbers in the exam.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (Sample)

> *"Design the fire pump and storage tank for a wet riser system serving a residential building of height **50 m** with **3 risers**. Each landing valve must deliver **900 LPM** at a minimum pressure of **3.5 bar**. Calculate the total pump capacity, head, and storage tank volume."*

### 3.1.2 Given Data (Extract from Problem)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $H$ | Building height | $50$ m |
| $N_r$ | Number of risers | $3$ |
| $Q_{LV}$ | Flow per landing valve | $900$ LPM = $15$ L/s |
| $P_{LV}$ | Min. pressure at topmost LV | $3.5$ bar = $35$ m head |
| $N_{active}$ | Number of risers assumed in use | $2$ (NBC standard assumption) |
| $t_{design}$ | Design duration | $1$ hour = $3600$ s |

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine Total Flow Rate**

The NBC assumes that during a fire, **2 risers** are in simultaneous use (worst-case scenario). Each riser delivers $Q_{LV}$ at the topmost landing valve. Therefore:

$$Q_{total} = N_{active} \times Q_{LV} = 2 \times 900 = 1800 \text{ LPM}$$

Converting to L/s:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to m³/s (SI Units)**

$$Q_{total} = 30 \times 10^{-3} = 0.030 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Pumping Head**

The total head required by the pump is the sum of:

$$H_{pump} = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{nozzle} + H_{velocity}$$

**Step 3a — Static Head**

$$H_{static} = \text{Height of topmost LV} = 50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3b — Pressure Head at Topmost Landing Valve (Required Residual)**

$$H_{residual} = 35 \text{ m (equivalent head of 3.5 bar)}$$

**Step 3c — Friction Head Loss (Friction in Pipes)**

Using Hazen-Williams formula:

$$h_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$$

Assume for a typical MS pipe:
- $C = 120$ (Hazen-Williams coefficient for mild steel)
- $d = 150$ mm = $0.15$ m (assumed riser diameter)
- $L = 50$ m (riser length)
- $Q = 0.030$ m³/s = $30$ L/s (per riser)

Plugging in:

$$h_f = \frac{4.52 \cdot (30)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \cdot (0.15)^{4.87}} \cdot 50$$

Computing $(30)^{1.85}$:

$$(30)^{1.85} = 30^{1.85} \approx 30^2 \times 30^{-0.15} \approx 900 \times 0.56 \approx 504$$

Computing $(120)^{1.85}$:

$$(120)^{1.85} \approx 120^2 \times 120^{-0.15} \approx 14400 \times 0.408 \approx 5875$$

Computing $(0.15)^{4.87}$:

$$(0.15)^{4.87} \approx 0.0006 \text{ (approximately)}$$

Substituting:

$$h_f = \frac{4.52 \times 504}{5875 \times 0.0006} \times 50$$

$$h_f = \frac{2278}{3.525} \times 50$$

$$h_f \approx 646 \times 50 / 1000 \approx 32.3 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Exam:** You do **NOT** need to memorize the Hazen-Williams constant 4.52. It will be provided in the question if needed. The step-by-step substitution method above is what scores full marks.

**Step 3d — Velocity Head (typically small, but include for completeness)**

For a 150 mm diameter pipe with Q = 0.030 m³/s:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.030}{0.01767} \approx 1.70 \text{ m/s}$$

$$H_{velocity} = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.70)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{2.89}{19.62} \approx 0.15 \text{ m}$$

Negligible — can be ignored for fire pump sizing.

**Step 3e — Total Pump Head**

$$H_{pump} = H_{static} + H_{residual} + H_{friction} + H_{velocity}$$

$$H_{pump} = 50 + 35 + 32.3 + 0.15$$

$$H_{pump} \approx 117.5 \text{ m}$$

We round up to provide a **safety margin** of 10%:

$$H_{pump,design} = 117.5 \times 1.10 \approx 130 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Pump Head: ~130 m** (rounded to standard pump head of 140 m available in market).

**Step 4 — Convert Pump Head to Pressure**

$$P_{pump} = \rho g H_{pump} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 130$$

$$P_{pump} \approx 1.275 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 12.75 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Static Storage Tank Capacity**

For a 1-hour design duration with 2 risers active:

$$V_{tank} = Q_{total} \times t_{design}$$

$$V_{tank} = 30 \text{ L/s} \times 3600 \text{ s}$$

$$V_{tank} = 108{,}000 \text{ litres} = 108 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Storage Tank: 108 m³ (108,000 L)**. Real projects add 20% margin → design for **130 m³** to account for the **jockey pump** keeping the system primed and the **main pump** being slightly oversized.

**Step 6 — Pump Specification Summary**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Pump Type** | Centrifugal, end-suction, split case |
| **Flow Rate** | 1800 LPM (30 L/s) |
| **Head** | 130 m |
| **Power (approx.)** | $\frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 130}{0.7} \approx 54.6$ kW |
| **Motor** | **75 kW** (next standard size) |
| **Diesel Engine (Standby)** | **75 kW** with separate fuel tank (4 hr runtime) |
| **Jockey Pump** | 180 LPM @ 130 m head (5% flow, 100% head) |
| **Pressure Switch Settings** | Jockey ON at 8 kg/cm², OFF at 10 kg/cm²; Main ON at 6 kg/cm² |

### 3.1.4 Verification & Reasoning

- **Pump power (kW)** formula:
$$P = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$$
- With $Q = 0.030$ m³/s, $H = 130$ m, $\eta = 0.7$ (pump + motor combined efficiency):
$$P = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 130}{0.7} = 54{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 55 \text{ kW}$$

Selecting the next standard motor rating → **75 kW** (with safety margin for pump wear, voltage fluctuations, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two pumps (electric + diesel)?** Kerala has grid power interruptions (especially in monsoon). During a fire, if the electric pump fails because power went out, the **diesel pump MUST start automatically within 30 seconds** and take over. This is a critical NBC and TAC requirement.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Number of Sprinklers in a Design Area

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> *"A sprinkler system is designed for a **Light Hazard** (LIH) occupancy. The design density is **4.1 mm/min** and each sprinkler head covers an area of **9 m²**. The design area per NBC/IS 15105 is **72 m²**. Determine (a) the number of sprinklers in the design area, (b) the total flow rate required, and (c) the required static water storage."*

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Number of Sprinklers**

$$n = \frac{A_{design}}{A_{per\,head}}$$

$$n = \frac{72}{9} = 8 \text{ sprinklers}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The design assumes the **worst-case** of 8 sprinklers opening simultaneously, even though in practice only 2–4 open in a real fire (NFPA statistics).

**Step 2 — Flow Per Sprinkler**

$$Q_{head} = \text{Density} \times A_{per\,head}$$

$$Q_{head} = 4.1 \text{ mm/min} \times 9 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{head} = 36.9 \text{ mm·m}^2/\text{min}$$

Converting to litres (1 mm·m² = 1 L):

$$Q_{head} = 36.9 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3 — Total Flow Rate for the Design Area**

$$Q_{total} = n \times Q_{head}$$

$$Q_{total} = 8 \times 36.9 = 295.2 \text{ L/min}$$

Converting:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{295.2}{60} \approx 4.92 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4 — Static Water Storage (30-minute design duration for LIH)**

$$V_{tank} = Q_{total} \times t_{design} = 4.92 \text{ L/s} \times 1800 \text{ s}$$

$$V_{tank} = 8{,}856 \text{ litres} \approx 9 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Sprinkler systems need **much less water** than hydrant systems because they apply water **directly at the fire's seat** with high efficiency. This is why sprinklers are **the most effective** fire protection system in terms of water usage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What System

The following decision matrix is a **study aid** to help you answer any "Which fire protection system is required for...?" type question in KTU exams.

| Building Type / Scenario | Height / Area | NBC-Mandated System |
|---|---|---|
| Bungalow (< 15 m) | Any area | Portable extinguishers only |
| Apartment (15–24 m) | Up to 1000 m²/flat | Extinguishers + Dry Riser + Hose Reel |
| Apartment (24–45 m) | Any | + Wet Riser, **Sprinklers**, Fireman's Lift |
| Apartment (> 45 m) | Any | + Refuge areas, full PA, voice evacuation |
| Hospital (Group C) | > 15 m | All above + **100% sprinkler coverage** + voice comm. |
| Office (Group E) | > 24 m | All above + sprinklers |
| Mall (Group F) | > 1000 m² | All above + sprinklers + atrium smoke control |
| Petrol Pump (Group I) | Any | Foam pourer, DCP, water spray, no lifts in hazardous zone |
| Data Centre (Group E) | Any | **Gas-based suppression** (FM-200 / Novec 1230) |
| Underground Parking | Any | CO₂, foam, sprinklers + CO detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazardous Group I (Petroleum, Chemicals)** has the **strictest** requirements — these buildings often need **explosion-proof electrical fittings, foam systems, gas detection, and a minimum 9 m setback** from property lines. KTU has asked this in the 2022 and 2023 ESE papers.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Fireman's Lift Phase I and Phase II Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Normal Operation]) --> detect{"Fire Detected OR<br/>Fireman's Switch ON?"}
    detect -- "NO" --> start
    detect -- "YES" --> recall["PHASE I: AUTOMATIC RECALL<br/>All car calls cancelled<br/>Doors close"]
    recall --> return["Lift returns to<br/>Designated Landing<br/>usually Ground Floor"]
    return --> open["Doors open for<br/>30 seconds<br/>then close"]
    open --> wait["Lift AWAITS<br/>firefighter entry"]
    wait --> enter{"Firefighter<br/>enters car?"}
    enter -- "NO, timeout" --> park["Lift parks at<br/>designated landing<br/>inoperable to others"]
    enter -- "YES" --> phase2["PHASE II: IN-CAR MANUAL<br/>Firefighter uses key switch<br/>inside car"]
    phase2 --> manual["Manual floor selection<br/>Hold door-close button<br/>Override door safety"]
    manual --> arrive["Lift arrives at<br/>target floor<br/>e.g. fire floor"]
    arrive --> exit["Firefighter exits into<br/>PRESSURIZED FIRE LOBBY<br/>and attacks fire"]
    exit --> end([End of Operation])
    park --> end

    style start fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style detect fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style recall fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style phase2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style arrive fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style exit fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style end fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flowchart shows the **two distinct phases** of fireman's lift operation.
- **Phase I (Recall)** is automatic; **Phase II (Manual)** is firefighter-controlled.
- The lift **does NOT automatically take the firefighter to the fire floor** — the firefighter must manually select it. This prevents the lift from opening on the fire floor without human control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

**Interpretation:**
- This is a **decision tree** that a fire protection consultant uses when designing a building.
- Start at the top with the building parameters → follow the "YES/NO" branches → end with the required system list.
- **Heights of 15, 24, and 45 m are the "magic numbers"** that trigger additional requirements.

```mermaid
graph TB

    cat3 --> sys3c["Foam Monitor<br/>Tank Farms"]

    cat4 --> sys4a["DCP System<br/>LPG Bottling Plants"]

    cat5 --> sys5a["Smoke Detector<br/>Ionization and Photoelectric"]
    cat5 --> sys5b["Heat Detector<br/>Fixed and Rate of Rise"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Water-based** systems are the most common and economical.
- **Gas-based** systems are used where water damage is unacceptable (data centres, museums).
- **Foam** is the go-to for flammable liquid (Class B) fires.
- **Detection & Alarm** is the *pre-requisite* for all other systems — you must *know* about the fire before you can fight it.
