## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the NBC 2016 clause reference** (e.g., "Clause 4.4.3") – 1 mark is reserved for citing the standard. **Memorize clause numbers!**
> 2. **Wrong OLF used** – e.g., applying 8 m² for hospitals. Always check NBC 2016, Table 5.
> 3. **Not cross-checking staircase width** against the minimum width requirement (150 cm residential / 200 cm commercial).
> 4. **Confusing active and passive systems** – they are distinct categories in part (a) questions.
> 5. **Missing the safety margin** (10–15%) in water tank capacity calculations.
> 6. **No units in final answer** – always state **m, m², m³, L, bar, or Pa**.
> 7. **Sketching a diagram in part (a)** – even a simple block diagram carries 1–2 marks.

### ✅ Active Systems (MEMORIZE)
- [ ] Sprinklers – NFPA 13 / IS 15105.
- [ ] Hydrants – IS 3844.
- [ ] Detection – IS 2189 / IS 14675.
- [ ] Fire dampers – close at **74°C**.

### ✅ Passive Systems (MEMORIZE)
- [ ] Compartmentation: **2 hr floor**, **4 hr basement**.
- [ ] Fire doors: **self-closing + intumescent seals**.
- [ ] Fire stops at all service penetrations.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Remember
- [ ] OLF (Office) = **8 m²/person**.
- [ ] OLF (Residential) = **12.5 m²/person**.
- [ ] Travel distance ≤ **22.5 m** (unsprinklered) / **30 m** (sprinklered).
- [ ] Staircase width = **0.5 cm per person** (per staircase).
- [ ] Minimum staircase width = **150 cm** (residential) / **200 cm** (commercial).
- [ ] Fire lift mandatory above **24 m**.

### ✅ The 5 Pillars of High-Rise Fire Safety
- [ ] Life Safety, Property Protection, Mission Continuity, Environmental Protection, First Responder Support.

### ✅ Engineering Reality
- [ ] High-rise fire safety is **multi-disciplinary** (Architecture + Structural + MEP + Fire Protection + Management).
- [ ] Every calculation must be **cross-checked** against NBC 2016 minimums.

> **🎯 Final Tip:** Before writing the KTU exam, **draw a labelled compartmentation diagram** for one high-rise floor and a simple evacuation flowchart. Both are commonly asked as **diagram questions** worth 4–6 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Protection in Underground Structures and in Buildings Under Construction, Sitting of Detectors as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Protection in Underground Structures and in Buildings Under Construction, Sitting of Detectors as per Relevant Indian Standard Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fire Protection in Underground Structures

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Underground structures (UGS) include subways, metro rail tunnels, underground parking, service tunnels, basements, and utility corridors. Fire protection in such structures refers to the integrated package of **passive fire protection (PFP)**, **active fire suppression systems**, **smoke management**, **pressurization**, and **means of egress** designed to mitigate the unique hazards of fire in confined, semi-enclosed or fully enclosed subterranean environments where heat and smoke accumulation is rapid, ventilation is restricted, and occupant evacuation is severely delayed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key UGS Hazards (Tacoma Tunnel Fire, 1990 & King's Cross, 1987 lessons):**
> 1. **Heat Trap Effect:** Hot smoke rises and forms a stratified layer; in tunnels this layer may extend hundreds of metres.
> 2. **Backlayering:** Smoke travelling against ventilation flow direction.
> 3. **Radiant Heat Flux:** A burning vehicle in a tunnel can produce **> 50 kW/m²** at adjacent vehicles, causing cascading fire spread.
> 4. **Limited Access:** Rescue operations are physically constrained.
> 5. **Communication Blackout:** Radio signals attenuate underground; this affects emergency response.

---

### 1.2 Fire Protection in Buildings Under Construction (BUC)

**Formal Definition:**
A building under construction is a transient occupancy with **partially installed fire safety systems**, exposed structural elements, combustible scaffolding, temporary electrical wiring, and ongoing hot work (welding, cutting, soldering). Fire protection during construction refers to the **interim measures** mandated by codes (NBC 2016 Part 4, IS 1646, NFPA 241) to safeguard workers, the partially completed structure, and neighbouring properties until the permanent fire protection systems become operational.

---

### 1.3 Siting (Sitting) of Fire Detectors as per Indian Standards

**Formal Definition:**
"Sitting" (technically **siting**) refers to the **spatial positioning, spacing, and zoning** of automatic fire detection components (heat detectors, smoke detectors, multi-criteria detectors, aspirating systems, flame detectors, linear heat detection cables) in accordance with the prescriptive geometry laid down in **IS 2189:2008** *Code of Practice for Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm System* and **IS 11360:1985** for general applications, with supplemental guidance from **IS 14604:1999** (Aspirating smoke detectors) and **TAC/NBC Part 4:2016** guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Three Scenarios:**
> Imagine fire safety like a **three-act safety net**:
> - **Act I (UGS):** You are inside a sealed glass box — smoke has nowhere to go. Solution = forced ventilation + suppression at source.
> - **Act II (BUC):** Your house is half-built; the roof is missing and wires are exposed. Solution = housekeeping, hot-work permits, temporary sprinklers.
> - **Act III (Detector Sitting):** The "smell sensors" of a building must be placed where smoke actually *arrives*, not where you assume it does. Solution = code-prescribed geometry.

### 1.4 Indian Standard Specifications — Snapshot

| IS Code | Title | Relevance |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| **IS 2189:2008** | Automatic Fire Detection & Alarm — Code of Practice | Detector sitting, spacing, zoning |
| **IS 11360:1985** | Specification for Heat Sensitive Fire Detectors | Heat detector ratings |
| **IS 14604:1999** | Aspirating Smoke Detectors | VESDA-type systems |
| **IS 1646:2015** | Fire Safety of Buildings (General) | Construction phase safety |
| **NBC 2016 Part 4** | Fire and Life Safety | Overall framework |
| **TAC Schedule (Tariff Advisory Committee)** | Fire Insurance Requirements | India-specific zoning |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fire Protection in Underground Structures — Layered Design Philosophy

#### A. Passive Fire Protection (PFP)

| Element | Specification | Purpose |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| Tunnel lining | **Concrete (min 4 hours fire rating)** | Structural integrity under hydrocarbon fire curve |
| Cable trunking | Intumescent coatings | Prevent cable fire propagation |
| Fire-rated doors | **2-hour fire-rated with intumescent seals** at cross-passages | Smoke compartmentalization |
| Penetrations | Fire-stop pillows / mortars | Maintain fire barrier rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrocarbon (HC) Fire Curve vs. Cellulosic Curve:**
> $$
> T_{HC}(t) = 1100 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right) + T_0
> $$
> versus the standard cellulosic ASTM E119 curve which peaks at **~1010°C at 4 hours**. HC curves reach **1100°C within 5 minutes** — hence tunnel structures need PFP rated to HC, not just cellulosic.

#### B. Active Fire Suppression

For UGS, the typical active systems are:

1. **Water-Based:**
   - **Deluge Systems:** Pre-action, dry-pipe sprinklers suited for tunnels (NFPA 13, NFPA 502).
   - **Water Mist Systems:** Lower water consumption, high cooling, ideal for metro tunnels.
   - **Foam-Water Systems:** For hydrocarbon risks (vehicle fires in UGS).

2. **Gas-Based:**
   - **Inert Gas (IG-541, IG-01):** Suitable for electrical rooms, sub-station chambers.

3. **Special:**
   - **Compressed Air Foam Systems (CAFS)** for road tunnels.

#### C. Smoke Management — Critical Ventilation Equations

The **critical velocity** needed to prevent backlayering in a tunnel is given by:

$$
V_c = K \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{Q}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, A \cdot T}
$$

Wait — let me use the canonical Kennedy/Williams formulation:

$$
V_c = K_1 \cdot \left(\frac{g H Q}{\rho_\infty c_p A T_\infty}\right)^{1/3}
$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = critical ventilation velocity (m/s)
- $K_1$ = constant ≈ **0.606** (Kennedy's correlation)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $H$ = tunnel height (m)
- $Q$ = heat release rate of fire (kW)
- $\rho_\infty$ = ambient air density ≈ **1.2 kg/m³**
- $c_p$ = specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $A$ = tunnel cross-sectional area (m²)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Used in **transverse** and **semi-transverse ventilation design** for metros. The Indian Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC/L & T Metro) design uses this for tunnel bore sizing.

#### D. Egress from Underground Structures

- **Max travel distance:** **30 m** (NBC 2016) to a refuge / cross-passage in metro tunnels.
- **Refuge area:** Min **1 m² per 5 persons** (NBC).
- **Cross-passages:** Every **240–500 m** in twin-bore tunnels.
- **Emergency lighting:** **≥ 30 lux** for 90 minutes minimum.
- **Directional signage:** Photoluminescent, every **15 m** at platform edge.

---

### 2.2 Fire Protection in Buildings Under Construction

#### A. Risk Profile

| Hazard | Description | Mitigation |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Hot work** | Welding, cutting, grinding | Hot Work Permit System |
| **Combustible debris** | Timber, packaging, paint | Daily housekeeping; NFPA 241: max 30 m³ accumulation |
| **Temporary electrical** | Exposed wiring, overloaded panels | RCD-protected, IS-marked cabling only |
| **Scaffolding fires** | Wood/bamboo planks | Substitute with metal / fire-retardant treated |
| **LPG / acetylene** | Cylinders on site | Cylinder storage cage, segregation from heat |
| **Site security** | Arson by outsiders | 24x7 guard, perimeter lighting, CCTV |

#### B. Active Protection During Construction

- **Temporary standpipes:** Installed progressively as building rises. Hydrants at every floor.
- **Portable extinguishers:** **ABC type (IS 13849)** at every floor, every staircase landing.
- **Construction elevators:** Fire-rated, with emergency communication.
- **Water supply:** Min **4 kg/cm²** pressure at highest point; **45,000 L static storage** (NBC 2016, occupancy dependent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NFPA 241 (adopted in India for high-rise construction):**
> *"Fire safety during construction shall be planned from project inception. A Fire Prevention Program Manager (FPPM) shall be appointed for projects > 40 m height."*

#### C. Pre-Commissioning Audit

Before any floor is occupied, the following must be operational:
1. Sprinklers on that floor fed by zone valves.
2. Detection & alarm on that floor.
3. Pressurized staircase serving that floor.
4. Means of egress cleared of debris.
5. Fire-fighting lift operational (if applicable).

---

### 2.3 Siting (Sitting) of Detectors — IS 2189:2008 Prescriptive Geometry

#### A. Heat Detectors (IS 11360 / IS 2189)

**Point-type heat detectors:**

$$
S_{max} = 7.5 \text{ m radius (low fire load)} \quad \text{to} \quad 5.3 \text{ m radius (high fire load)}
$$

**Linear heat detection cable:** Spaced such that any **1 m** of cable is within the detector's sensing radius.

#### B. Smoke Detectors — Spacing Rules

| Detector Type | Max Spacing (Slab) | Max Wall Distance | Min from Air Vent |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Point ionisation / photoelectric** | **10.6 m** (10 m nominal) | **5.3 m** from any wall | **1 m** |
| **Aspirating (VESDA-type)** | **>10.6 m** with class-A sampling | Per design | **0.5 m** |
| **Beam (linear optical)** | **15 m** between transmitter/receiver | **7.5 m** from side wall | As per design |
| **Flame (IR/UV)** | Line-of-sight cone, **< 30 m** | Height ≥ 1.5× obstruction | – |

#### C. Siting Geometry — The "0.7H" Rule

For a ceiling of height $H$ (m):

- **Smooth ceiling:** Detector spacing $= 0.7 \times H$ (typical for smoke detectors)
- For $H \le 4$ m, full spacing (10.6 m) allowed.
- For $4 < H \le 6$ m, reduce spacing by **20%**.
- For $H > 6$ m, use **aspirating systems** or **beam detectors** instead of point smoke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stratification Effect:** If $H > 8$ m, smoke cools and stratifies below the ceiling. Point smoke detectors may NOT respond. **Use beam or aspirating systems.**

#### D. Detector Coverage Under Sloped Ceilings

For sloped ceilings (atriums, staircase skylights):

- Treat as flat if slope ≤ **20°**.
- For slope > 20°, place detectors within **0.6 m of apex**.
- For peaked ceilings > 45°, treat each half as a separate compartment.

#### E. Detector Zoning (TAC/NBC)

$$
\text{One zone} \le 2000 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (NBC 2016)}
$$
$$
\text{One zone} \le 40 \text{ detectors (TAC for insurance)}
$$
$$
\text{One zone} \le 1 \text{ floor per fire compartment (mandatory)}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Unit / Value |
|---|----------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $T_{HC}(t)$ tunnel fire curve | Hydrocarbon tunnel lining | °C, time in min |
| 2 | $V_c = K_1 (gHQ / \rho c_p A T)^{1/3}$ | Critical tunnel vent velocity | m/s |
| 3 | Detector spacing = $0.7H$ | Smoke detector layout | metres |
| 4 | Max zone area $\le 2000$ m² | Detector zoning | m² |
| 5 | Max zone detectors $\le 40$ | Detector zoning | count |
| 6 | Detector wall distance $\le 5.3$ m | Point detector sitting | m |
| 7 | Travel distance in UGS $\le 30$ m | Egress in metro | m |
| 8 | Refuge area = $\frac{N}{5}$ m² | Metro evacuation | m² |
| 9 | HRR for tunnel vehicle | Design fire | **5 MW (car), 20 MW (coach)** |
| 10 | PFP rating for tunnel | Concrete liner | **4 h HC** |
| 11 | Pressure differential for stairwell | Pressurization | **12.5 Pa (NBC)** |
| 12 | Floor area per detector (smoke) | Max 50 m² for Class A | m² |
| 13 | Floor area per detector (heat) | Max 30 m² for Class A | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - Designers of **Delhi Metro** Phase-IV use the Kennedy critical velocity correlation for tunnel ventilation.
> - **NBCC Part 4 Fire** and **TAC** require all detector zones to be plotted in **AutoCAD/MEP drawings** with **symbol legend per IS 2189**.
> - Construction-stage fire plans are **mandatory documents for occupancy certificates** in Bangalore (BBMP) and Mumbai (MCGM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Ventilation Velocity in a Tunnel Fire

**Step 1 — The Physical Setup**

Consider a longitudinal ventilated tunnel of height $H$ and cross-section $A$. A fire of HRR $Q$ burns at the floor. Smoke rises and forms a stratified layer of depth $y$ below ceiling. The ventilation flow $V$ opposes upstream smoke spread (backlayering).

**Step 2 — Buoyancy Flux**

The buoyancy flux generated by the fire is:

$$
\dot{Q}_b = g \cdot \frac{Q}{\rho_\infty \, c_p \, T_\infty}
$$

This is the "driving force" for natural smoke spread against the ventilation.

**Step 3 — Froude Number Argument**

Define the Froude number:

$$
Fr = \frac{V^2}{gH} \cdot \frac{\rho_\infty T_\infty}{(\Delta T / T_\infty)}
$$

Backlayering stops when $Fr$ exceeds a critical value empirically determined.

**Step 4 — Empirical Correlation (Kennedy, 1996)**

After dimensional analysis and experimental correlation in tests at Memorial Tunnel (USA) and EUREKA projects:

$$
V_c = K_1 \cdot \left( \frac{g \cdot H \cdot Q}{\rho_\infty \cdot c_p \cdot A \cdot T_\infty} \right)^{1/3}
$$

with $K_1 \approx 0.606$ for a fire near the tunnel walls.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

A metro tunnel has:
- $H = 5.4$ m
- $A = 30$ m²
- $Q = 5$ MW (car fire)
- $T_\infty = 300$ K
- $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³

Substituting:

$$
V_c = 0.606 \cdot \left( \frac{9.81 \times 5.4 \times 5000}{1.2 \times 1.005 \times 30 \times 300} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
V_c = 0.606 \cdot \left( \frac{264,870}{10,854} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
V_c = 0.606 \cdot (24.40)^{1/3}
$$

$$
V_c = 0.606 \times 2.904
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 1.76 \text{ m/s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Designers typically apply a **safety factor of 1.4–1.6** to $V_c$. Thus, design velocity $V_d \approx 1.76 \times 1.5 \approx 2.65$ m/s. Fans are rated at this value with redundancy (N+1).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Detector Sitting Decision Tree

Use the following matrix to determine which detector type and spacing applies, per **IS 2189:2008** combined with **NBC 2016 Part 4**.

| Room Parameter | Recommended Detector | Spacing Rule | Reason |
|----------------|----------------------|--------------|--------|
| Height $H \le 4$ m, low fire load | **Point smoke (ionisation + photoelectric dual)** | 10.6 m centres, 5.3 m from wall | Standard class A coverage |
| Height $4 < H \le 6$ m, normal | **Point smoke with 20% derating** | 8.5 m centres | Smoke cools during rise |
| Height $6 < H \le 10$ m, atrium/industrial | **Beam detector OR aspirating (VESDA)** | Beam: 15 m; Aspirating: per design | Avoid stratification loss |
| Height $H > 10$ m | **Aspirating ONLY** | Per pipe network design | Stratification severe |
| Hot process areas (boiler room) | **Heat detector (ROR/FT)** | 5.3 m centres | Avoid false alarms from smoke |
| Cable tunnels / UGS service tunnels | **Linear Heat Detection (LHDC)** | Cable on tray, 1 m sensing per run | Cable fires produce slow heat |
| Electrical switchrooms | **Aspirating (very early warning)** | Class A sampling | Cannot use water-based suppression |
| Kitchens (commercial) | **Heat (FT, 90°C)** | 5.3 m centres | Avoid false alarms from cooking |
| Atrium, glass roof, sloped | **Beam + flame** | As per 0.6 m from apex | Smoke accumulation complex |
| Vehicle parking (closed) | **Heat (ROR) + CO detectors** | 5.3 m centres | Cars emit CO before flame |
| Underground metro platform | **Aspirating (VESDA, Class A) + linear heat** | Per platform length | Critical infrastructure |

---

### 3.3 Sizing of Refuge Area in Metro (NBC 2016)

**Step 1 — Passenger Load Calculation**

For a 6-coach metro train, peak capacity = **~1800 passengers**.

**Step 2 — Refuge Provision**

Per NBC 2016: **1 m² per 5 persons**

$$
A_{refuge} = \frac{N}{5} = \frac{1800}{5} = 360 \text{ m²}
$$

**Step 3 — Distribution**

If tunnel has 2 cross-passages in 1.2 km stretch:

$$
A_{each\ cross-passage} = 180 \text{ m²}
$$

Each cross-passage can serve **900 persons** (half the train), confirming the design.

---

### 3.4 Construction Phase — Sprinkler Progressive Installation

**Step 1 — Identify "Construction Floor"**

The floor where vertical riser terminates = "highest completed floor".

**Step 2 — Temporary Feed**

$$
P_{req} = P_{hose} + P_{sprinkler\ head} + P_{friction\ loss}
$$

$$
P_{req} = 2.5 \text{ bar} + 1.0 \text{ bar} + 1.0 \text{ bar (friction)} = 4.5 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Booster Pump Sizing**

$$
Q_{pump} = N \times 5 \text{ L/s} = 20 \text{ sprinklers} \times 5 = 100 \text{ L/s}
$$

(Conservatively, 20 heads × 5 L/s per head, per IS 15105).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the formula works:** The 5 L/s/head is the empirical K-factor 80 head discharge at 2.5 bar residual pressure, derived from IS 15105 / NFPA 13 default. The 20-head design area is the typical hazard density for commercial BUC sites.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Protection Layering for Underground Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Underground Structure]:::ugs --> B[Layer 1: Passive Fire Protection]
    A --> C[Layer 2: Detection & Alarm]
    A --> D[Layer 3: Active Suppression]
    A --> E[Layer 4: Smoke Management]
    A --> F[Layer 5: Egress and Refuge]

    B --> B1[4h HC-rated Concrete Liner]
    B --> B2[Intumescent Coatings on Cables]
    B --> B3[2h Fire-rated Cross-passage Doors]
    B --> B4[Fire-stop Penetrations]

    C --> C1[Aspirating Smoke VESDA]
    C --> C2[Linear Heat Detection Cable]
    C --> C3[Flame Detectors IR UV]
    C --> C4[Manual Call Points every 60m]

    D --> D1[Deluge Sprinkler System]
    D --> D2[Water Mist System]
    D --> D3[Foam Water System for HC Risks]
    D --> D4[IG-541 for Substations]

    E --> E1[Longitudinal Ventilation]
    E --> E2[Transverse Ventilation]
    E --> E3[Smoke Exhaust Shafts every 200m]
    E --> E4[Critical Velocity Control Vc]

    F --> F1[Cross-passage every 240-500m]
    F --> F2[Refuge Area 1 sq.m per 5 persons]
    F --> F3[Emergency Lighting 30 lux for 90min]
    F --> F4[Photoluminescent Signage every 15m]

    classDef ugs fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Fire Protection Strategy for Buildings Under Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Construction Site Risk Assessment]:::root --> B[Hot Work Permit System]
    A --> C[Temporary Standpipes per Floor]
    A --> D[Scaffold Material Fire Retardant]
    A --> E[Daily Housekeeping NFPA 241]
    A --> F[Temp Fire Watch for Hot Work]
    A --> G[Pre-Commissioning Audit per Floor]

    B --> B1[Ignition Source Control]
    C --> C1[Hydrants on Every Floor]
    D --> D1[Metal or FR-treated Wood]
    E --> E1[Dumpster Removal Daily]
    F --> F1[2 hr Post-Watch]
    G --> G1[Sprinkler Operational]
    G --> G2[Detection Operational]
    G --> G3[Pressurized Staircase Operational]

    H[Temporary Water Storage]:::root --> I[Static Tank 45000 L]
    I --> J[Booster Pump 450 Lpm]
    J --> K[4.5 bar at Highest Point]

    classDef root fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Detector Siting Flowchart per IS 2189:2008

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Room to be protected]:::start --> B{Ceiling Height H?}
    B -->|H ≤ 4 m| C{Fire Load?}
    B -->|4 < H ≤ 6 m| D[Use Point Smoke with 20% derating]
    B -->|6 < H ≤ 10 m| E[Use Beam OR Aspirating]
    B -->|H > 10 m| F[Use Aspirating ONLY]

    C -->|Low| G[Point Smoke 10.6m centres]
    C -->|Moderate| H[Point Smoke OR Heat per risk]
    C -->|High Temp| I[Heat Detector 5.3m centres]

    G --> J{Adjacent Wall Distance?}
    J -->|≤ 5.3 m| K[Acceptable]
    J -->|> 5.3 m| L[Add Detector]

    D --> M[Spacing 8.5m centres]
    E --> N[Beam: 15m OR VESDA]
    F --> O[VESDA Pipe Network]
    I --> P[Spacing 5.3m centres]

    M --> Q[Zone Check: ≤ 2000 m², ≤ 40 detectors]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    K --> Q

    Q --> R{Zone Compliant?}
    R -->|Yes| S[Mark on Layout Drawing]
    R -->|No| T[Subdivide Zone]

    classDef start fill:#16a085,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fire Alarm & Detection Panel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]:::panel --> Z1[Zone 1: ≤ 2000 m², ≤ 40 detectors]
    P --> Z2[Zone 2: Stairwell]
    P --> Z3[Zone 3: Electrical Room]
    P --> Z4[Zone 4: Basement]
    P --> Z5[Zone 5: Refuge Area]

    Z1 --> SD1[Smoke Detectors]
    Z1 --> HD1[Heat Detectors]
    Z1 --> MCP1[Manual Call Points]

    Z2 --> PA[Public Address System]
    Z3 --> ASD[Aspirating VESDA]
    Z4 --> LHDC[Linear Heat Cable]
    Z5 --> SOS[SOS Call Points every 250 m]

    P --> RL1[Relay to Sprinkler Solenoid]
    P --> RL2[Relay to AHU Shutdown]
    P --> RL3[Relay to Fire Pump Start]
    P --> RL4[Relay to Pressurization Fan Start]
    P --> RL5[Relay to Fire Lift Recall]
    P --> RL6[Relay to BMS via Modbus]

    P --> REP[Repeater Panel]
    P --> FDAP[Fire Display and Annunciation Panel]
    P --> BAT[Battery Backup 24 hr + 30 min alarm]

    classDef panel fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (model)]
**List any three critical hazards unique to fire in underground structures.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Heat and Smoke Stratification:** Smoke rises and forms a horizontal layer several metres thick, hampering both visibility and rescue.
2. **Backlayering:** If ventilation is inadequate, smoke spreads upstream against the airflow, endangering occupants far from the fire source.
3. **Limited Access and Egress:** Vertical evacuation is often impossible; horizontal egress requires refuge areas and cross-passages.
4. **High Radiant Heat Flux:** Vehicle fires in tunnels can produce 50 kW/m² to adjacent vehicles, causing cascading fire spread.
5. **Communication Attenuation:** Radio signals attenuate underground, hampering emergency response coordination.

*(Any three — full 3 marks)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (model)]
**As per IS 2189:2008, what is the maximum spacing and minimum wall distance for a point-type smoke detector installed below a flat ceiling of 4 m height?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Maximum spacing between detectors:** **10.6 m** (centre-to-centre).
- **Maximum distance from any wall:** **5.3 m** (i.e., half the spacing).
- **Minimum distance from an air diffuser / supply vent:** **1 m** (to avoid false alarms from dilution).

*(3 marks)*

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper PESFT416]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with sketches the **passive and active fire protection systems** required for an underground metro rail tunnel of 1.2 km length, twin-bore type. Discuss the design fire size and the critical velocity correlation.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A tunnel fire scenario has the following parameters:
- $H = 5.4$ m, $A = 30$ m², $Q = 10$ MW, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³.

Compute the **critical ventilation velocity** to prevent backlayering. Recommend the design velocity with a safety factor of 1.5 and explain the fan redundancy philosophy.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Passive & Active Fire Protection for Underground Metro Tunnel (7 marks)**

**Passive Fire Protection (3 marks):**
1. **Tunnel Lining:** Reinforced concrete liner rated for **4-hour hydrocarbon (HC) fire curve** because metro fires typically involve vehicle upholstery, electrical insulation, and wheel grease.
2. **Cross-passage Doors:** **2-hour fire-rated** with intumescent seals, located every **240–500 m** between the two bores.
3. **Cable Trays:** Coated with **intumescent paint** or enclosed in **fire-rated conduit** to prevent spread.
4. **Penetrations Sealed:** All cable and pipe penetrations through fire barriers sealed with **fire-stop pillows or mortars** to maintain compartment rating.

**Active Fire Protection (3 marks):**
1. **Water Mist System** in running tunnel: Delivers fine water droplets that cool smoke and suppress fire with minimal water damage to electronics.
2. **Deluge System** at station end (incident management zone).
3. **Aspirating Smoke Detection (VESDA)** in tunnel and station ceiling for very early warning.
4. **Linear Heat Detection Cable** on cable trays.
5. **Manual Call Points** every **60 m** in tunnel and every **30 m** on platforms.

**Design Fire & Critical Velocity (1 mark):**
- Design fire for a metro coach is **HRR = 20 MW** (5 MW for car, 20 MW for coach).
- Critical velocity is computed using Kennedy's correlation (see part b).

**Sketch (Mermaid / descriptive):**

```
Twin-bore Tunnel Cross-section:
[ Bore-1 Up ] | [Cross-passage 2h FD] | [ Bore-2 Down ]
              | (with refuge 360 m²)   |
```

---

**(b) Critical Velocity Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the formula**

$$
V_c = K_1 \left( \frac{g H Q}{\rho_\infty c_p A T_\infty} \right)^{1/3}
$$

with $K_1 = 0.606$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
V_c = 0.606 \left( \frac{9.81 \times 5.4 \times 10000}{1.2 \times 1.005 \times 30 \times 300} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator**

$$
\text{Num} = 9.81 \times 5.4 \times 10000 = 529,740
$$

$$
\text{Den} = 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 30 \times 300 = 10,854
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio**

$$
\frac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}} = \frac{529,740}{10,854} = 48.81
$$

**Step 5 — Cube root and final multiplication**

$$
V_c = 0.606 \times (48.81)^{1/3} = 0.606 \times 3.661 = 2.219 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor**

$$
V_d = 1.5 \times V_c = 1.5 \times 2.219 = 3.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Fan Redundancy (1 mark)**

- Each jet fan rated at $V_d = 3.33$ m/s with **N+1 redundancy** (i.e., if 4 fans are required for normal operation, install 5).
- Fans distributed every **80–120 m** along the tunnel.
- Power supply from **Essential Supply (DG + UPS)** with **30 sec automatic change-over**.
- Fans conform to **ISO 13347** for acoustics and **EN 12101-3** for smoke and heat exhaust.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often forget to cube-root correctly or drop the $K_1$ factor. Marks are awarded for **showing the substitution step-by-step** and for explicitly stating the **unit** (m/s). Do not present just the final answer. Each step is approximately **1 mark**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper PESFT416]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **fire protection measures that must be in place during the construction phase of a high-rise building** (> 40 m). Refer to NBC 2016 Part 4 and NFPA 241.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* As per **IS 2189:2008**, state and explain the rules for sitting **point-type smoke detectors, beam-type smoke detectors, and aspirating smoke detectors** in a commercial office floor of area 1500 m² with ceiling height 7 m. Show how you would subdivide the floor into detection zones.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Construction Phase Fire Protection (7 marks)**

1. **Fire Prevention Program Manager (FPPM):** Per NFPA 241, appoint an FPPM for buildings > 40 m; responsible for daily inspection, permit issuance, training.
2. **Hot Work Permit System:** Welding, gas-cutting, soldering allowed only with a written permit; **2-hour fire watch** post-completion; portable extinguisher at the work location.
3. **Site Security:** 24×7 watchmen, perimeter fencing ≥ 2 m, CCTV at entry/exit and storage.
4. **Combustible Material Control:** Daily removal of debris; no more than **30 m³** of combustible waste accumulation (NFPA 241); dedicated **non-combustible dumpsters**.
5. **Scaffolding:** Use metal scaffolding or **fire-retardant treated wood**; avoid bamboo.
6. **Temporary Standpipes:** Install progressively; **fire hydrant on every floor**; min **4.5 bar** at the highest point; **45,000 L static tank** (NBC 2016).
7. **Temporary Power:** RCD-protected, IS-marked cables; no open wiring; proper earthing; LPG storage in separate cage.
8. **Pre-Commissioning Audit:** Before a floor is occupied, sprinklers, detection, alarms, and pressurised staircase serving that floor must be operational.
9. **Construction Hoists:** Fire-rated doors, emergency communication.
10. **Means of Egress:** Staircases kept clear, illuminated at all times, signposted.

*(5 marks for 5 measures + 2 marks for NBC / NFPA reference)*

---

**(b) Detector Siting — IS 2189:2008 (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Floor area: 1500 m²
- Ceiling height: 7 m
- Type: Commercial office (moderate fire load)

**Step 1 — Detector Selection (1 mark)**

Since $H = 7$ m falls in the band $6 < H \le 10$ m, point smoke detectors are not ideal due to **stratification**. Per IS 2189, use **Beam Detectors** (preferred for large open areas) or **Aspirating Smoke Detectors (VESDA, Class A)**.

**Step 2 — Beam Detector Rules (2 marks)**

- **Spacing between transmitter and receiver:** **Max 15 m** (IS 2189:2008, Table 4).
- **Distance from side wall:** **Max 7.5 m** (i.e., 50% of spacing).
- **Distance below ceiling:** **0.3 m to 0.6 m** for $H = 7$ m.
- **If obstructed by partitions:** The beam should clear obstructions by at least **0.5 m**.
- **Coverage area per beam:** ~150 m² (15 m × 10 m grid).

**Step 3 — Aspirating Detector Rules (2 marks)**

- **Sample hole spacing:** Class A = max **10.6 m centres**, each sampling ≤ **0.5 m²** of equivalent coverage.
- **Pipe diameter:** 25 mm (1") typical; **capillary tubes** for small areas.
- **Sensitivity:** Class A = 0.5 % obscuration/m; Class B = 1.5 % obs/m; Class C = 3 % obs/m.
- For 7 m height, use **aspirating** with return-air sampling or sample holes on return-air duct.

**Step 4 — Zoning Rules (1 mark)**

Per NBC 2016:
- One zone $\le$ **2000 m²** (we have 1500 m² → fits in one zone)
- One zone $\le$ **40 detectors**
- One zone $\le$ **one fire compartment per floor**
- Detector zone indicator (LED) at FACP and Repeater.

**Step 5 — Layout Sketch (1 mark)**

```
+------------------- 1500 m² Office -------------------+
|                                                      |
|    [Beam1]    [Beam2]    [Beam3]    [Beam4]         |
|                                                      |
|    [Beam5]    [Beam6]    [Beam7]    [Beam8]         |
|                                                      |
|   (15m × 10m grid; 7.5m from walls)                  |
+------------------------------------------------------+
|     MCP MCP                        MCP MCP           |
|                          FACP                        |
+------------------------------------------------------+
        [ZONE 1 = entire floor = 1500 m²]
        [8 Beam detectors + 4 MCPs]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Common errors:
> 1. Using point smoke detector at 7 m height (loses **2 marks**).
> 2. Confusing **beam spacing** (15 m) with **point smoke spacing** (10.6 m).
> 3. Forgetting to apply **zone area** rule (≤ 2000 m²) — examiner deducts 1 mark if the zoning isn't shown.
> 4. Confusing **"spacing"** (centre-to-centre) with **"wall distance"** (half spacing).
> 5. Not referencing **IS 2189:2008** specifically — deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Underground structures** demand a **layered defence**: PFP + Detection + Suppression + Smoke Management + Egress.
- **HC fire curve** (1100°C in 5 min) — not cellulosic — governs tunnel lining rating.
- **Critical velocity** $V_c = 0.606 \cdot (gHQ/\rho c_p A T)^{1/3}$ is the design driver for tunnel longitudinal ventilation.
- **Refuge area** in metros = 1 m² per 5 persons; **cross-passage** every 240–500 m; **travel distance** ≤ 30 m.
- **Construction phase** = FPPM, hot-work permit, daily housekeeping (≤ 30 m³ debris), temporary standpipes, ABC extinguishers per floor.
- **Detector sitting (IS 2189:2008):**
  - Point smoke spacing = **10.6 m**; wall distance ≤ **5.3 m**; vent distance ≥ **1 m**.
  - Spacing = **0.7H** for smooth ceilings.
  - For $H > 6$ m → **beam** or **aspirating**; for $H > 10$ m → **aspirating** only.
  - **Linear heat cable** for cable trays / tunnels.
- **Zoning rules (NBC 2016 / TAC):** ≤ 2000 m², ≤ 40 detectors, one floor per zone, one fire compartment per zone.
- **Beam detector** max 15 m spacing; **0.3–0.6 m below ceiling**.
- **Aspirating (VESDA)** classified A/B/C; Class A is most sensitive (0.5 % obs/m).
- **Sloped ceiling** > 20° → place detector within 0.6 m of apex; treat each slope as separate compartment.
- **Heat detector** for kitchens, boilers, parking — avoids false alarms from smoke.
- **CO detector** in enclosed parking (cars emit CO before flame).
- **Stratification** at $H > 8$ m → point smoke fails; choose beam/VESDA.
- **Construction pre-commissioning audit** is mandatory before any floor is occupied.
- **NBC 2016 Part 4** + **IS 2189:2008** + **TAC** = the **Indian triumvirate** for detector sitting and fire safety.
- **IS 11360** governs heat detector ratings; **IS 14604** governs aspirating systems.
- **In UGS, pressurize staircases at 12.5 Pa** to prevent smoke ingress (NBC 2016).
- **Emergency lighting** in UGS = **30 lux for 90 minutes minimum**.
- **Scaffolding fires** are the **#1 cause** of construction site fires in India; substitute with metal where possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Planning of Alarm System as per Relevant Standards (BIS), General Requirements and Guidelines for the Installation of Fire Detection and Alarm System in Buildings of Different Occupancy Classification, Selection and Distribution of Portable Extinguishers (for Class A and B Fires) and Other Fire Protection Equipments and Systems for Different Occupancy Classification as per NBC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection and Planning of Alarm System as per Relevant Standards (BIS), General Requirements and Guidelines for the Installation of Fire Detection and Alarm System in Buildings of Different Occupancy Classification, Selection and Distribution of Portable Extinguishers (for Class A and B Fires) and Other Fire Protection Equipments and Systems for Different Occupancy Classification as per NBC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Detection and Alarm System (FDAS) – Formal KTU Definition

A **Fire Detection and Alarm System (FDAS)** is an integrated network of initiating devices (detectors, manual call points), notification appliances (hooters, bells, strobes), control and indicating equipment (fire alarm panel), and power supplies, designed in accordance with **IS 2189:2008 (Reaffirmed 2019)** – *Code of Practice for Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm System (Code of Practice)*, **IS 14612:1999**, and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) – Part 4: Fire and Life Safety**, to detect the unwanted presence of fire by monitoring one or more of the physical/chemical phenomena associated with it (smoke, heat, flame, gas), and to alert the occupants, fire brigade, and emergency responders for safe evacuation and intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly mandates familiarity with **BIS standards (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – particularly the **IS 2189** family of standards and **NBC 2016 Part 4**. Examiner questions are routinely framed around clauses of these standards.

## 1.2 Occupancy Classification as Per NBC 2016 – The Bedrock of FDAS Design

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) Part 4** classifies all buildings into **10 major occupancy groups** based on the nature of use, occupant load, fire load, and life-safety risk. This classification directly governs the type, density, and coverage of fire alarm devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – Why Occupancy Classification Matters:**
> Think of FDAS design like writing a prescription. A doctor does not prescribe the same medicine to a child, an adult, and a pregnant woman. Similarly, a fire engineer cannot prescribe the *same* alarm system to a **hospital (Group C – Institutional)**, an **office (Group E – Business)**, and a **petrol pump (Group I – Hazardous)**. The "patient" (the building) has different vulnerabilities, and the "prescription" (FDAS) must be tailored to it.

### Table 1.1: NBC 2016 Occupancy Groups

| Group | Occupancy Type | Typical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Residential | Apartments, hostels, hotels, dormitories |
| **B** | Educational | Schools, colleges, universities, coaching centres |
| **C** | Institutional | Hospitals, nursing homes, prisons, mental health facilities |
| **D** | Assembly | Theatres, cinemas, auditoriums, malls, stadiums |
| **E** | Business | Offices, banks, IT parks, call centres |
| **F** | Mercantile | Shops, showrooms, supermarkets, restaurants |
| **G** | Industrial | Factories, workshops, manufacturing plants |
| **H** | Storage | Warehouses, godowns, cold storage |
| **I** | Hazardous | Petrol pumps, LPG storage, chemical plants, paint factories |
| **J** | Mixed/Assembly-Others | Buildings with mixed occupancy |

## 1.3 Fire Classes – The Foundation of Extinguisher Selection

The selection of portable fire extinguishers is governed by the **class of fire** they are designed to suppress. The internationally accepted classification (also adopted by **IS 2190:2010** and **IS 6234:2003** family) is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Classes of Fire (Kerns of Extinguisher Selection):**
> - **Class A** – Ordinary combustibles: **wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics, trash**.
> - **Class B** – Flammable liquids: **petrol, diesel, oil, paint, solvents, kerosene**.
> - **Class C** – Flammable gases: **LPG, methane, propane, acetylene, hydrogen**.
> - **Class D** – Combustible metals: **magnesium, sodium, potassium, lithium, aluminium powder**.
> - **Class E (or Class C in Indian convention)** – Energised electrical: **live wiring, control panels, transformers**.
> - **Class F** – Cooking oils and fats: **vegetable oil, animal fat, kitchen fryers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – Class A vs Class B:**
> Imagine a bonfire (Class A – the wood is the *fuel*) versus a puddle of petrol (Class B – the liquid is the fuel). For the bonfire, you can **smother it with water** (cooling). For the petrol, water is **useless and dangerous** – it spreads the fire. You must **blanket the liquid** with foam or **starve the oxygen** with CO₂. This is why the **type of extinguisher must match the fire class**.

## 1.4 Governing BIS Standards – The KTU "Must-Know" List

| Standard | Title | Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IS 2189:2008** | Selection, Installation & Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm System | **Primary** code for FDAS |
| **IS 14612:1999** | General Requirements of Fire Alarm System | Components and testing |
| **IS 2190:2010** | Selection, Installation & Maintenance of Portable First-Aid Fire Extinguishers | Extinguisher placement |
| **IS 940:1988** | Portable Fire Extinguisher, Water Type (Gas Cartridge) | Class A fires |
| **IS 2546:1994** | Portable Fire Extiguisher, Foam Type | Class A and B fires |
| **IS 6234:2003** | Portable Fire Extinguisher, Dry Powder Type | Class B, C, E fires |
| **IS 13849:1993** | Portable Fire Extinguisher, Carbon Dioxide Type | Class B and E fires |
| **IS 15683:2006** | Portable Fire Extinguishers – Performance & Construction | General spec |
| **NBC 2016 Part 4** | Fire and Life Safety | Overarching regulatory code |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Fire Detection and Alarm System (As Per IS 2189)

A compliant FDAS under **IS 2189:2008** consists of **four logical sub-systems**:

### 2.1.1 Initiating Devices (Input Section)
These are the "senses" of the system – they detect the fire.

**(a) Automatic Detectors:**
- **Heat Detectors (Fixed Temperature / Rate-of-Rise):**
  - *Fixed Temperature (FT)*: Triggers when ambient temperature exceeds a **threshold (typically 57°C, 68°C, 93°C, 141°C)**. The bulb or bimetallic strip breaks/snaps. Selection depends on the **normal ambient temperature** of the protected area – use a rating **20°C above the maximum expected ambient**.
  - *Rate-of-Rise (ROR)*: Triggers when temperature rises faster than **8.3°C per minute** (1°F per minute).
  - *Combination (FT + ROR)*: Dual criterion, used in critical areas.
  - **Application**: Kitchens, garages, boiler rooms (where smoke detectors give false alarms from cooking fumes or exhaust).

- **Smoke Detectors:**
  - *Ionisation Chamber Detectors*: Use Americium-241 to ionise air; smoke disrupts the ion current. **Better for flaming fires producing small smoke particles.**
  - *Photoelectric (Optical) Detectors*: Use a light beam and a photodiode; smoke scatters light onto the sensor. **Better for smouldering fires producing large smoke particles.**
  - *Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)*: Actively draws air through a pipe network to a highly sensitive laser detector. Used in **clean rooms, data centres, heritage buildings**.

- **Flame Detectors:**
  - *Infrared (IR)* and *Ultraviolet (UV)* detectors that sense the radiation signature of flames. Used in **Group I (Hazardous)** and **Group G (Industrial)** – petroleum refineries, chemical plants, paint stores.

- **Gas Detectors:**
  - Sense combustible or toxic gases (LPG, methane, CO). Mandatory in **LPG storage rooms, battery charging rooms, underground car parks**.

**(b) Manual Call Points (MCPs) / Break-Glass Units:**
- Wall-mounted devices that allow a human to manually trigger the alarm by breaking a glass / pressing a button.
- **KTU High-Yield Clause:** MCPs must be installed at **every staircase landing, every exit door, and at every floor exit** at a height of **1.4 m to 1.6 m** above floor level.

### 2.1.2 Notification Appliances (Output Section)
The "voice" of the system.

- **Hooters / Bells** – audible alarms, **minimum 65 dB(A) at 3 m distance**, or **5 dB(A) above ambient noise** (whichever is higher) per IS 2189.
- **Strobes** – visual alarms for hearing-impaired occupants; mandatory in **Group C (Institutional – Hospitals)** and **Group D (Assembly)**.
- **Voice Evacuation Systems (EVAC)** – pre-recorded or live voice messages; mandatory in high-rise buildings and assembly occupancies.

### 2.1.3 Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)
The "brain" of the system. Receives signals from initiating devices, processes logic, and activates notification appliances. May be:
- **Conventional (zonal)** – each zone is a wire loop with multiple detectors; the panel shows which *zone* is in alarm, not the exact detector.
- **Addressable** – each detector has a unique address; the panel pinpoints the *exact* device in alarm. **Preferred for large buildings and per NBC 2016 for high-rise.**

### 2.1.4 Power Supply
- **Primary**: 230 V AC mains.
- **Secondary (Standby)**: Sealed Maintenance-Free (SMF) batteries capable of **24 hours of quiescent operation + 15 minutes of full alarm** as per IS 2189.
- **Tertiary (Optional)**: DG set for high-rise buildings.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Detector Selection Logic (As Per IS 2189 + NBC 2016)

The selection follows a structured decision tree. Here is the analytical reasoning:

> **Step 1: Identify the Occupancy Group** (per Table 1.1).
> **Step 2: Assess the Fire Load & Risk Profile.**
>   - *Low Fire Load (offices, schools)* → Smoke detectors preferred.
>   - *High Ambient Heat / Fumes (kitchens, garages)* → Heat detectors.
>   - *Flammable Liquids/Gases (petrol pumps, LPG stores)* → Flame + Gas detectors.
> **Step 3: Calculate Coverage Area per Detector.**
>   - **Smoke detector**: **100 m²** (general), reduced to **50 m²** in high-risk or high-ceiling spaces.
>   - **Heat detector**: **50 m²** (general), **30 m²** for high-risk.
> **Step 4: Apply Spacing Rule.**
>   - Detector spacing **S** satisfies: **S² ≤ A**, where A = coverage area.
>   - For square coverage: S = √A. For a 100 m² smoke detector: S = **10 m** on a square grid.
> **Step 5: Check Distance from Walls.**
>   - No detector should be more than **half the spacing distance** from any wall (i.e., ≤ 5 m from wall for a 10 m spacing).
> **Step 6: Account for Ceiling Geometry.**
>   - For ceilings **> 4 m** high (heat) or **> 6 m** high (smoke), use the **"0.3 × H" rule** – mount detectors at **0.3 × ceiling height** below the ceiling for optimal smoke/heat layer detection.
> **Step 7: Verify Ceiling Obstructions.**
>   - Beams deeper than **10% of ceiling height** are treated as walls, requiring additional detector rows.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Unit | Source |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Coverage of Smoke Detector (general) | $A_{s} = 100$ | m² | IS 2189 |
| 2 | Coverage of Heat Detector (general) | $A_{h} = 50$ | m² | IS 2189 |
| 3 | Detector Spacing (square grid) | $S = \sqrt{A}$ | m | IS 2189 |
| 4 | Distance from wall to detector | $\leq 0.5 \times S$ | m | IS 2189 |
| 5 | Mounting height below ceiling (sloped) | $d = 0.3 \times H$ | m | NBC 2016 |
| 6 | Number of detectors required | $N = \lceil A_{\text{floor}} / A_{\text{detector}} \rceil$ | – | Derived |
| 7 | Hooter sound level | $L_{hoot} \geq \max(65,\ L_{ambient} + 5)$ | dB(A) | IS 2189 |
| 8 | Battery standby duration | $T_{stby} = 24\ \text{h quiescent} + 15\ \text{min alarm}$ | h | IS 2189 |
| 9 | Travel distance to extinguisher (Class A) | $\leq 15$ | m | IS 2190 |
| 10 | Travel distance to extinguisher (Class B) | $\leq 10$ | m | IS 2190 |
| 11 | Extinguisher coverage (Class A) | $A_{ext,A} = 200$ | m² per unit | IS 2190 |
| 12 | Extinguisher coverage (Class B) | $A_{ext,B} = \text{floor area of room (max)}$ | m² | IS 2190 |
| 13 | Water flow per hydrant | $Q_{h} \geq 450$ | LPM | NBC 2016 |
| 14 | Hydrant pressure | $P_{h} \geq 3.5$ | bar | NBC 2016 |
| 15 | Sprinkler spacing (light hazard) | $S_{sp} \leq 4.5$ | m | IS 15105 |
| 16 | Sprinkler design density (light hazard) | $D_{sp} = 5.0$ | L/min/m² | IS 15105 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of These Formulas:**
> In a real building project, the **fire protection consultant** uses these formulas to produce a **Fire Protection Report (FPR)** – a statutory document submitted to the **Fire Department (Kerala Fire and Rescue Services)** for the **No-Objection Certificate (NOC)**. The design is sealed by a registered fire engineer, and any deviation from these formulas is a non-compliance that can stop building occupancy.

## 2.4 Portable Fire Extinguisher Selection – The Engineering Decision Tree

> **Step 1: Identify the Class of Fire** likely in the area (A, B, C, D, E, F).
> **Step 2: Select the extinguishing medium** based on compatibility:
>   - **Water (IS 940)** → Class A only. **NEVER** on Class B, E, or F (water + oil = explosion; water + electricity = electrocution).
>   - **Foam (AFFF – Aqueous Film-Forming Foam, IS 2546)** → Class A and B.
>   - **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC type, IS 6234)** → Class A, B, C, E. **The most versatile.**
>   - **CO₂ (IS 13849)** → Class B and E. **Clean agent – leaves no residue** – ideal for server rooms, electrical panels.
>   - **Clean Agents (FM-200, Novec 1230)** → Class E in critical electronic areas.
>   - **Wet Chemical** → Class F (commercial kitchens).
>   - **Special Dry Powder (M28, Copper powder, etc.)** → Class D (metal fires).
> **Step 3: Determine extinguisher size (capacity).**
>   - Water: 9 L (typical).
>   - Foam: 9 L / 50 L.
>   - DCP (ABC): 4 kg / 6 kg / 25 kg / 75 kg.
>   - CO₂: 4.5 kg / 22.5 kg.
> **Step 4: Apply travel-distance rule (IS 2190:2010).**
>   - Class A hazard: **travel distance ≤ 15 m**.
>   - Class B hazard: **travel distance ≤ 10 m** (or **≤ 15 m** in some NBC clauses for light hazard).
> **Step 5: Verify visibility and signage.**
>   - Extinguishers must be **clearly visible, unobstructed, and marked with a red rectangle sign** per **IS 9457**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Number of Smoke Detectors Required for a Floor

**Problem Statement:** A **Group E (Business) office floor** measures **40 m × 25 m** (1000 m²). Design requires **addressable photoelectric smoke detectors** with a coverage of **100 m²** each. Determine:
(i) The number of detectors required.
(ii) The grid spacing.
(iii) Maximum permissible distance from any wall.

> **Step 1: Identify the total floor area.**
> $$A_{\text{floor}} = L \times B = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \ \text{m}^2$$
> *(multiplication of length and breadth in metres)*

> **Step 2: Recall the coverage rule per IS 2189.**
> $$A_{\text{detector}} = 100 \ \text{m}^2 \quad \text{(for smoke detector, general occupancy)}$$

> **Step 3: Calculate the number of detectors needed.**
> $$N = \left\lceil \frac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_{\text{detector}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{100} \right\rceil = \lceil 10 \rceil = 10$$
> *(ceiling function: round up to the next whole number because you cannot have a fractional detector)*

> **Step 4: Calculate the grid spacing on a square layout.**
> $$S = \sqrt{A_{\text{detector}}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \ \text{m}$$
> *(square root of 100 m², expressed in metres)*

> **Step 5: Verify against the floor dimensions.**
> $$\text{Detectors along length} = \left\lceil \frac{40}{10} \right\rceil = 4$$
> $$\text{Detectors along breadth} = \left\lceil \frac{25}{10} \right\rceil = 3$$
> $$\text{Total detectors (actual grid)} = 4 \times 3 = 12$$
> *(we add 2 extra detectors because 25 m is not perfectly divisible by 10 m; the ceiling function rounds up)*

> **Step 6: Compute the maximum wall-to-detector distance.**
> $$d_{\text{wall}} = 0.5 \times S = 0.5 \times 10 = 5 \ \text{m}$$
> *(half the spacing distance, per IS 2189, to ensure no point in the room is more than 5 m from a detector)*

### Final Answer Box:
- Theoretical detectors: **10**
- Actual grid-based detectors: **12**
- Grid spacing: **10 m × 10 m**
- Max wall distance: **≤ 5 m**

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Number of CO₂ Extinguishers for a Server Room (Class E Risk)

**Problem Statement:** A **Group C (Institutional) hospital server room** is 15 m × 10 m (150 m²). Per IS 2190, **CO₂ extinguishers of 4.5 kg capacity** are to be provided, with **one extinguisher per 50 m²** for Class B/Class E risk. Determine the number of CO₂ extinguishers.

> **Step 1: Identify the area.**
> $$A_{\text{room}} = 15 \times 10 = 150 \ \text{m}^2$$

> **Step 2: Apply the Class B / E distribution rule.**
> $$A_{\text{ext}} = 50 \ \text{m}^2 \ \text{per CO}_2 \text{ extinguisher}$$
> *(rule of thumb in IS 2190 for Class B high-hazard; conservatively applied to Class E)*

> **Step 3: Calculate the number of extinguishers.**
> $$N_{CO_2} = \left\lceil \frac{150}{50} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.0 \rceil = 3$$
> *(ceiling of 3.0 is exactly 3)*

> **Step 4: Verify the travel distance.**
> $$\text{Room diagonal} = \sqrt{15^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{225 + 100} = \sqrt{325} \approx 18.03 \ \text{m}$$
> $$\text{Approx. max travel} \approx 18.03 / 2 \approx 9.0 \ \text{m} \leq 10 \ \text{m (Class B limit)}$$
> *(the room diagonal divided by 2 represents the maximum distance an occupant would travel to reach a corner; this must be ≤ 10 m for Class B risk)*

### Final Answer Box:
- **3 CO₂ extinguishers (4.5 kg capacity)** are required, and the travel distance constraint is satisfied.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Sizing the Standby Battery for an FDAS

**Problem Statement:** A **Group A (Residential) high-rise apartment** has an FDAS with a **quiescent (standby) load of 2.5 A** and an **alarm load of 5.5 A** at **24 V DC**. Per IS 2189, the battery must support **24 hours of quiescent operation + 15 minutes of full alarm**. Calculate the **battery capacity in Ampere-hours (Ah)**.

> **Step 1: Compute the energy required during the 24-hour standby phase.**
> $$Q_{stby} = I_{stby} \times T_{stby} = 2.5 \ \text{A} \times 24 \ \text{h} = 60 \ \text{Ah}$$
> *(current multiplied by time, units: ampere-hours)*

> **Step 2: Compute the energy required during the 15-minute alarm phase.**
> $$T_{alarm} = 15 \ \text{min} = 0.25 \ \text{h}$$
> $$Q_{alarm} = I_{alarm} \times T_{alarm} = 5.5 \ \text{A} \times 0.25 \ \text{h} = 1.375 \ \text{Ah}$$

> **Step 3: Add the two phases and apply a safety derating factor.**
> $$Q_{total} = Q_{stby} + Q_{alarm} = 60 + 1.375 = 61.375 \ \text{Ah}$$

> **Step 4: Apply the 1.25× derating (industry standard, accounts for battery ageing).**
> $$Q_{design} = 1.25 \times Q_{total} = 1.25 \times 61.375 = 76.72 \ \text{Ah}$$
> *(factor of safety ensures the battery still meets spec after years of degradation)*

> **Step 5: Round up to the next standard battery size.**
> $$Q_{selected} = 80 \ \text{Ah},\ 24\ \text{V DC SMF battery}$$

### Final Answer Box:
- **80 Ah, 24 V DC** Sealed Maintenance-Free (SMF) battery is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detector & Extinguisher Selection Across NBC 2016 Occupancy Groups

| Occupancy | Detector Type | Detector Coverage | Manual Call Point? | Hooter Level | Extinguisher Type | Travel Dist. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A – Residential** | Smoke (Photo) | 100 m² | Yes (every staircase) | 65 dB(A) | ABC DCP (4 kg) | ≤ 15 m |
| **B – Educational** | Smoke (Photo) | 100 m² | Yes (every floor) | 65 dB(A) | ABC DCP + Water (9 L) | ≤ 15 m |
| **C – Institutional** | Smoke (Addressable) | 80 m² (lower density for phased evac) | Yes (bed-head + staircase) | 65 dB(A) + Voice | CO₂ (electrical) + ABC | ≤ 10 m |
| **D – Assembly** | Smoke + Flame (where needed) | 80 m² | Yes (every exit) | 75 dB(A) + Voice + Strobes | ABC + CO₂ (Stage lighting) | ≤ 15 m |
| **E – Business** | Smoke (Addressable) | 100 m² | Yes (every staircase) | 65 dB(A) | CO₂ (server rooms) + ABC | ≤ 15 m |
| **F – Mercantile** | Smoke (Aspirating in malls) | 80–100 m² | Yes (every exit) | 65 dB(A) | ABC DCP + CO₂ | ≤ 15 m |
| **G – Industrial** | Heat / Flame / Gas (risk-based) | 50 m² | Yes (every exit) | 75–85 dB(A) | Foam / DCP (size per risk) | ≤ 10 m |
| **H – Storage** | Heat (High temp) | 30–50 m² | Yes | 75 dB(A) | DCP (large, 25–75 kg) | ≤ 10 m |
| **I – Hazardous** | Flame + Gas + Heat (redundant) | 25–30 m² | Yes (intrinsically safe) | 85 dB(A) + Voice | DCP / CO₂ / Special | ≤ 10 m |
| **J – Mixed** | Per dominant risk | Per dominant risk | Yes | Per dominant risk | Per dominant risk | Per dominant risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Detector for a Given Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START: IDENTIFY OCCUPANCY AND ROOM USE] --> q1{IS FLAME / SMOKE EXPECTED IN NORMAL OPERATION?}
    q1 -- YES SMOKE --> qsmoke{WHAT IS THE CEILING HEIGHT?}
    qsmoke -- BELOW 6 M --> SMOKE1[USE PHOTOELECTRIC SMOKE DETECTOR]
    qsmoke -- ABOVE 6 M --> SMOKE2[USE ASPIRATING SMOKE DETECTION ASD OR BEAM DETECTOR]
    q1 -- YES HEAT FUMES --> qheat{IS THE AREA PRONE TO HEAT BUILDS UP}
    qheat -- YES --> HEAT1[USE HEAT DETECTOR FIXED TEMP 68 TO 93 C]
    q1 -- YES FLAME --> FLAME1[USE IR UV FLAME DETECTOR]
    q1 -- YES GAS --> GAS1[USE GAS DETECTOR LPG METHANE CO]
    q1 -- NO CLEAN AREA --> qclean{IS CLEANLINESS CRITICAL}
    qclean -- YES --> CLEAN1[USE ASD OR BEAM SMOKE DETECTOR]
    qclean -- NO --> CLEAN2[USE STANDARD PHOTOELECTRIC SMOKE DETECTOR]
    SMOKE1 --> endnode[APPLY COVERAGE 100 M2 GRID SPACING 10 M]
    SMOKE2 --> endnode
    HEAT1 --> endnode
    FLAME1 --> endnode
    GAS1 --> endnode
    CLEAN1 --> endnode
    CLEAN2 --> endnode
```
