# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "Fire Detection and Alarm System" as per IS 2189:2008. List any four components of the system.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> A Fire Detection and Alarm System (FDAS), as defined by **IS 2189:2008**, is an integrated assembly of devices that detects the unwanted presence of fire by sensing one or more phenomena (smoke, heat, flame, gas), initiates automatic or manual alarm signals, and activates notification appliances to enable safe evacuation and fire-fighting intervention.
>
> **Four components:**
> 1. **Initiating devices** – smoke, heat, flame, gas detectors and manual call points.
> 2. **Notification appliances** – hooters, bells, strobes, voice evacuation speakers.
> 3. **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** – the central processing and indicating unit.
> 4. **Power supply** – 230 V AC mains + 24 V DC SMF standby battery.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**State the IS standard governing the selection of portable fire extinguishers in India. Differentiate between the extinguishing media suitable for Class A and Class B fires, giving one example extinguisher for each class.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The governing standard is **IS 2190:2010** – *Code of Practice for Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Portable First-Aid Fire Extinguishers* (along with media-specific standards IS 940 for water, IS 2546 for foam, IS 6234 for DCP, and IS 13849 for CO₂).
>
> **Class A Fire (ordinary combustibles – wood, paper, cloth):** The most effective mechanism is **cooling** with **water**. Example: **Water-type extinguisher (9 L capacity, gas cartridge type)** as per IS 940.
>
> **Class B Fire (flammable liquids – petrol, oil, solvents):** The most effective mechanism is **smothering / blanketing** to cut off the oxygen supply. Example: **Foam (AFFF) extinguisher as per IS 2546**, or **ABC Dry Chemical Powder extinguisher as per IS 6234**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for the **selection and planning of a fire alarm system** for a **Group A (Residential) high-rise apartment building (G+15)** as per the relevant BIS standards and NBC 2016. Mention the detectors, their coverage areas, manual call point placement, and notification appliance levels.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **Step 1 – Statutory Identification:** The building is a **Group A (Residential)**, **G+15 high-rise** (height > 15 m). Applicable codes are **IS 2189:2008**, **NBC 2016 Part 4**, and **Kerala Municipal Building Rules**.
>
> **Step 2 – Zone Sizing:** Each floor is divided into fire alarm zones, with **no zone exceeding 2000 m²** (IS 2189 clause). Typical floor plate = 800 m² → **1 zone per floor**, total **15 zones** (plus common areas).
>
> **Step 3 – Detector Selection:** Use **addressable photoelectric smoke detectors** in all living areas, lobbies, and lift lobbies. Use **heat detectors** in the kitchen and utility shafts. **Detector coverage = 100 m² per smoke detector; 50 m² per heat detector.**
>
> **Step 4 – Detector Count:** For a 800 m² floor → 8 smoke detectors (round up to 9 in grid). For kitchen ~30 m² → 1 heat detector.
>
> **Step 5 – Manual Call Points (MCPs):** Install at **every staircase landing (1 per floor) and at every final exit** at a mounting height of **1.4 m to 1.6 m** above floor level.
>
> **Step 6 – Notification Appliances:** **Hooters** in every floor lobby and staircase at **65 dB(A) minimum** or **5 dB above ambient noise**. **Voice evacuation speakers** in common areas.
>
> **Step 7 – Control Panel:** **Addressable FACP** with one-loop-per-floor architecture, installed at the **ground-floor fire control room** with a **repeater panel** at each refuge floor.
>
> **Step 8 – Power Supply:** 230 V AC mains + 24 V DC SMF battery for **24-hour standby + 15-min alarm** as per IS 2189. High-rise requires **DG back-up**.
>
> **Step 9 – Integration:** FACP must be interfaced with **lift recall**, **stair pressurization system**, **BMS**, and **fire brigade communication**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for the **selection and distribution of portable fire extinguishers** for a **Group E (Business) office building** of floor plate **50 m × 30 m (1500 m²)**. Cover Class A and Class B risks. Use **IS 2190:2010**.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **Step 1 – Identify Fire Classes Present:**
> - **Class A risk** – paper, wood, fabric in offices, conference rooms.
> - **Class B risk** – pantry (oil), generator room (diesel), UPS room (battery electrolyte).
> - **Class E risk** – server rooms, electrical panels (treated under Indian convention as Class E).
>
> **Step 2 – Select Extinguishing Media:**
> - **Class A** → Water (9 L) or ABC Dry Powder (4 kg).
> - **Class B** → Foam (9 L) or ABC Dry Powder (6 kg).
> - **Class E** → CO₂ (4.5 kg).
> - **Pantry Class F** → Wet Chemical (6 L).
>
> **Step 3 – Apply Distribution Rule (IS 2190):**
> - For **Class A** risk: **1 ABC DCP (4 kg) per 200 m²** → 1500/200 = **7.5 → 8 extinguishers**.
> - Travel distance from any point: **≤ 15 m**.
> - Place at **staircase landings (2 nos.)**, **lobby (2 nos.)**, **corridor (3 nos.)**, **pantry (1 wet chemical)**.
> - **Generator room (1 nos. – DCP 25 kg wheeled)** for Class B bulk.
> - **Server room (2 nos. – CO₂ 4.5 kg)** placed 10 m apart.
>
> **Step 4 – Mounting and Signage:**
> - Wall-mounted, top **1.5 m above floor**, bottom **0.5 m above floor** for trolley-type.
> - Marked with **photoluminescent red rectangle sign** per IS 9457.
> - Annual maintenance contract with a licensed agency.
>
> **Step 5 – Travel Distance Verification:**
> - Room diagonal of office: √(50² + 30²) = √3400 ≈ 58.3 m → use **mid-corridor placement** to ensure every point ≤ 15 m from extinguisher.
> - Server room diagonal: assuming 8 m × 6 m → √100 = 10 m → **2 CO₂ extinguishers** placed at opposite corners.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **General Requirements and Guidelines for the installation of an FDAS** in a **Group D (Assembly) – Multiplex Cinema Hall** as per **IS 2189** and **NBC 2016**. Include detector selection, spacing, zoning, and evacuation provisions.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **Step 1 – Risk Profile:** A cinema hall has high occupant load (≥ 1000), low lighting, public address dependence, and high panic potential. Group D – Assembly.
>
> **Step 2 – Detector Selection:** Use **addressable photoelectric smoke detectors** in lobby, foyer, and projection rooms. Use **heat detectors** in concession pantry. Use **beam smoke detectors** across the high ceiling of the auditorium (ceiling height 8–12 m).
>
> **Step 3 – Spacing:** Smoke detector = 80 m² (reduced for high-risk public assembly). Beam detector spans 12–18 m. Heat detector = 50 m².
>
> **Step 4 – Zoning:** Each auditorium, lobby, and projection room as separate zones. **Maximum 20 devices per zone** (best practice for ease of identification).
>
> **Step 5 – Manual Call Points:** At every exit door, ticket counter, and concession counter. **Red colour, 1.4 m height.**
>
> **Step 6 – Notification:** **Voice evacuation (EVAC) system is mandatory** for assembly occupancies. Pre-recorded messages in **English, Hindi, and Malayalam** for Kerala. **Strobes** for hearing-impaired. **Auditorium hooter level ≥ 75 dB(A)** to override the film audio.
>
> **Step 7 – Integration:** FDAS must interface with **stage lighting trip, HVAC shutdown, smoke exhaust system, fire damper closure, and fire brigade panel**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **IS 940 (Water type)**, **IS 2546 (Foam type)**, **IS 6234 (Dry Chemical Powder type)**, and **IS 13849 (CO₂ type)** portable extinguishers in terms of **fire classes tackled, extinguishing mechanism, typical application areas, and limitations**. Tabulate your answer.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | IS 940 (Water) | IS 2546 (Foam AFFF) | IS 6234 (DCP ABC) | IS 13849 (CO₂) |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Fire Class** | A only | A and B | A, B, C, E | B and E |
> | **Mechanism** | Cooling (latent heat of vaporisation) | Smothering (AFFF blanket cuts O₂) | Chemical chain reaction inhibition + blanketing | Displaces oxygen; cools |
> | **Application** | Offices, schools, libraries, timber yards | Petrol stations, oil stores, paint shops, garages | Versatile – homes, vehicles, factories, electrical rooms | Server rooms, electrical panels, labs, control rooms |
> | **Limitations** | Useless on B, E, F; freezing risk | Cannot be used on E; needs regular replacement | Powder residue damages electronics; needs servicing | Suffocation risk in confined spaces; high pressure cylinder |
> | **Capacity** | 9 L | 9 L, 50 L | 1–75 kg | 2, 4.5, 22.5 kg |
> | **Inspection** | Annual hydraulic test | Annual | Annual | Annual hydrostatic test |
>
> **Selection Logic:** In a typical building, **ABC DCP (4 kg)** is the most versatile first-aid extinguisher and is the **default choice** in public corridors, lobbies, and stairs. **CO₂** is reserved for **electrical risk zones** (server rooms, LT panels). **Water and foam** are used in **specialised risks** (kitchens, fuel farms).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing fire classes:** Many students write "Class C" for *electrical fires* in Indian convention but **"Class E"** in international convention. **State both.**
> 2. **Quoting wrong standard:** Use **IS 2189:2008 (reaffirmed 2019)** for FDAS and **IS 2190:2010** for extinguishers. Do not write "IS 2189" without the year.
> 3. **Detector coverage:** Mixing up **100 m² (smoke) vs 50 m² (heat)** is a 2-mark deduction. Memorise the table.
> 4. **Travel distance:** Class A = 15 m, Class B = 10 m. This is a **favourite 2-mark KTU question**.
> 5. **Forgetting integration:** FDAS does not work in isolation. Examiner rewards students who mention **lift recall, HVAC shutdown, BMS, fire brigade panel**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Primary BIS Standard for FDAS = IS 2189:2008 (reaffirmed 2019)**.
- [ ] **Primary Standard for Portable Extinguishers = IS 2190:2010**.
- [ ] **Overarching Code = NBC 2016 Part 4: Fire and Life Safety**.
- [ ] **Smoke detector coverage = 100 m² (general)** → spacing **10 m** on square grid.
- [ ] **Heat detector coverage = 50 m² (general)** → spacing **~7 m** on square grid.
- [ ] **Distance from wall ≤ 0.5 × spacing distance** (i.e., ≤ 5 m for 10 m spacing).
- [ ] **Hooter sound level ≥ max(65 dB(A), ambient + 5 dB(A))** at 3 m.
- [ ] **MCP mounting height = 1.4 m to 1.6 m** at every staircase and exit.
- [ ] **Standby battery = 24 V DC, 24 h quiescent + 15 min alarm**.
- [ ] **Class A fire** = wood, paper, cloth → **water (cooling)**.
- [ ] **Class B fire** = flammable liquids → **foam / DCP (smothering)**.
- [ ] **Class C fire** = flammable gases → **DCP only**.
- [ ] **Class D fire** = metals → **special dry powder (e.g., M28, copper powder)**.
- [ ] **Class E (India) / Class C (international)** = electrical → **CO₂ or clean agent**.
- [ ] **Class F fire** = cooking oils → **wet chemical (potassium acetate)**.
- [ ] **Travel distance to extinguisher: Class A = 15 m; Class B = 10 m** (per IS 2190).
- [ ] **Occupancy Groups: A-Residential, B-Educational, C-Institutional, D-Assembly, E-Business, F-Mercantile, G-Industrial, H-Storage, I-Hazardous, J-Mixed**.
- [ ] **Beam detectors** are mandatory for **ceilings > 6 m** (used in atria, auditoria).
- [ ] **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)** for clean rooms, data centres, heritage buildings.
- [ ] **Flame detectors (IR/UV)** mandatory in **Group I – Hazardous** occupancies.
- [ ] **Voice evacuation (EVAC)** mandatory for **Group D (Assembly) and high-rise buildings**.
- [ ] **Strobes** mandatory for **hearing-impaired occupants** in hospitals (Group C) and assembly (Group D).
- [ ] **FDAS must integrate with** lift recall, HVAC shutdown, stair pressurisation, fire dampers, BMS, and fire brigade panel.
- [ ] **Detector colour code**: ionisation = red label; optical = white label; heat = blue/green.
- [ ] **Hydrant flow = 450 LPM @ 3.5 bar** (NBC 2016).
- [ ] **Sprinkler density (light hazard) = 5 L/min/m²**; spacing ≤ 4.5 m (IS 15105).
- [ ] **Defence-in-depth principle**: Detection → Suppression → Compartmentation → Evacuation.
- [ ] **Annual maintenance contract** for extinguishers and FDAS is **statutory** under the Kerala Fire and Rescue Services Act and the Indian Electricity Rules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning of Fixed Fire Fighting Installation for Different Occupancy Classification: Sprinkler System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Planning of Fixed Fire Fighting Installation for Different Occupancy Classification: Sprinkler System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) A **Sprinkler System** is a **fixed fire-fighting installation** consisting of a water supply, a network of piping fitted with **thermally-sensitive automatic sprinklers**, and control valves, designed to detect, control, and/or suppress a fire at its incipient stage by discharging water directly onto the fire-affected area in a predetermined spray pattern. As per **IS 15105:2002** (Planning and Installation of Fixed Automatic Sprinkler Fire Extinguishing Systems) and **NFPA 13 (Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems)**, the planning of a sprinkler system is fundamentally an exercise in **occupancy-based hazard classification**, where the design density, area of operation, duration of water supply, and pipe sizing are all derived from the *fire load* and *combustibility* characteristics of the protected occupancy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"An automatic sprinkler system is a pressurized water-based fire suppression system in which water is discharged through sprinkler heads that operate individually at a predetermined thermal response, following an occupancy-based hazard classification as per IS 15105 / NFPA 13, to control or extinguish a fire in its early stage."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Network of Mini Firemen on the Ceiling" Imagine the **ceiling of a building as a train platform**, and the sprinkler heads as **automatic glass showers** that hang from it. Each shower is sealed with a **soldered glass bulb (or fusible link)** acting like a thermometer trip-wire. When a fire starts at a particular seat (say, a corner office), only the **heat reaches the bulb directly above it** — the heat melts the bulb *only locally*, and **only that sprinkler opens**, spraying water **only on that localized area**. Unlike a **deluge system** (which is like flooding the entire room the moment smoke is detected), a **sprinkler system is selective** — it "fights the fire where it burns," not the entire building. This is why sprinklers are considered the **most reliable and life-safety-proven** active fire protection system in the world — with an effectiveness record of **96%+ control or extinguishment** (NFPA statistics). --- ## 1.3 Classification of Automatic Sprinkler Systems The KTU syllabus requires familiarity with all four fundamental types: | S.No | System Type | Trigger Mechanism | Typical Occupancy | |------|-------------|------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Wet Pipe System** | Heat at individual sprinkler | Offices, Hotels, Hospitals, Schools (above +4 °C) | | 2 | **Dry Pipe System** | Heat at individual sprinkler + air pressure release | Cold stores, Parking Garages, Unheated Warehouses (< 4 °C) | | 3 | **Deluge System** | External detection (smoke/heat detector) opens deluge valve | Aircraft Hangars, Transformer Yards, Flammable Liquid Storage | | 4 | **Pre-Action System** | Detection + sprinkler head opening (double interlock) | Data Centers, Archives, Museums, Cold Storages with electronics | > [!NOTE] > **Threshold Temperatures (Standard Sprinkler Glass Bulbs):** 57 °C (orange), 68 °C (red), 79 °C (yellow), 93 °C (green), 141 °C (blue), 182 °C (purple), 227 °C (black). --- ## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Operating Pressure at Sprinkler Head:** typically **1.0 bar to 3.5 bar** (NFPA 13 minimum: 7 psi ≈ 0.49 bar residual). - **Maximum Coverage Area per Sprinkler:** **12 m² (Light Hazard)** to **9 m² (Extra Hazard)**. - **Standard Water Supply Duration:** **60 minutes (LH/OH)** to **90 minutes (EH)**. - **K-TU Standard for Design:** IS 15105, NFPA 13, **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** of India, and **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Fire & Life Safety)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Sprinkler System Planning The planning of a fixed sprinkler installation follows a **deterministic, code-driven sequence**. KTU examiners expect students to know this sequence in the same order as listed: 1. **Step 1 — Identify the Occupancy Use & Fire Load.** Determine whether the building is residential, commercial, industrial, or storage. Estimate the fire load density (MJ/m²) based on combustible contents. 2. **Step 2 — Classify the Hazard.** Map the occupancy to one of the standard hazard groups defined by IS 15105 / NFPA 13. 3. **Step 3 — Select the System Type.** Choose Wet / Dry / Deluge / Pre-Action based on ambient temperature and speed-of-response required. 4. **Step 4 — Determine Design Density & Area of Operation.** Pick the **design density (mm/min or gpm/ft²)** and the **assumed area of sprinkler operation (m² or ft²)**. 5. **Step 5 — Select Sprinkler Type, K-Factor, and Spacing.** Decide on Conventional, Spray, CMSA, ESFR, with K-factor (K-80, K-115, K-160, K-200, K-360, K-400). 6. **Step 6 — Calculate Required Flow (Q) per Sprinkler and Total System Demand.** 7. **Step 7 — Hydraulic Calculation of Pipe Network.** Compute friction loss and verify residual pressure at the hydraulically most remote sprinkler. 8. **Step 8 — Confirm Water Supply Duration & Storage Tank Sizing.** 9. **Step 9 — Pipe Scheduling, Hanger Spacing, and Installation Detailing.** 10. **Step 10 — Acceptance Testing, Commissioning & Maintenance Schedule.** --- ## 2.2 Occupancy Classification & Hazard Grouping The KTU Board specifically asks students to *link* occupancy to hazard class. The standard mapping (per **IS 15105 / NFPA 13**) is summarized below: | Hazard Group | Occupancy Examples | Design Density | Area of Operation | |--------------|--------------------|----------------|--------------------| | **Light Hazard (LH)** | Offices, Hospitals, Schools, Churches, Museums, Libraries (small), Residential | **2.25 mm/min** | **72 m²** | | **Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH-1)** | Car parking, Bakeries, Laundries, Restaurant service areas | **5.0 mm/min** | **72 m²** | | **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH-2)** | Workshops, Showrooms, Libraries (large), Manufacturing low | **5.0 mm/min** | **144 m²** | | **Ordinary Hazard Group 3 (OH-3)** | Warehouses (inert), Printing, Textile mills | **5.0 mm/min** | **216 m²** | | **Ordinary Hazard Group 4 (OH-4)** | Paper mills, Woodworking, Chemical (low hazard) | **10.0 mm/min** | **216 m²** | | **Extra Hazard Group 1 (EH-1)** | Flammable liquid storage, Solvent plants, Upholstery | **12.5 mm/min** | **260 m²** | | **Extra Hazard Group 2 (EH-2)** | Aircraft hangars, Chemical processing, Plastic manufacturing | **12.5 mm/min** | **325 m²** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Density** = the assumed rate of water application (mm/min or gpm/ft²) over the **Area of Operation** — this combination defines the total water flow rate the system must deliver. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Formula | Expression | Units | Application | |---------|------------|-------|-------------| | **Sprinkler Discharge** | $Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$ | Q in L/min, P in bar, K in L/min·bar⁻⁰·⁵ | Flow from a single sprinkler head | | **Required Total Flow** | $Q_{total} = D \times A$ | Q in L/min, D in mm/min, A in m² | Total system demand | | **Hazen-Williams Friction Loss** | $h_f = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | hf in m, Q in L/min, d in mm, L in m | Friction loss in sprinkler pipe | | **Equivalent Pipe Length (C-value = 120)** | $L_e = 1.2 \cdot L_{actual}$ | metres | Includes fittings loss | | **Number of Sprinklers in Operation (n)** | $n = \dfrac{A_{op}}{A_{per-sprinkler}}$ | — | Spacing-based estimate | | **Water Storage Volume** | $V = Q_{total} \times t$ | L (litres) | t = 60 or 90 min | | **Minimum Pressure at Sprinkler** | $P_{min} = \left(\dfrac{Q}{K}\right)^{2}$ | bar | Verification at remote head | | **Velocity Check** | $V \leq 6 \text{ m/s}$ (for fire) | m/s | Avoid pipe erosion | > [!NOTE] > **Standard K-factors (K = discharge coefficient in L/min·bar⁰·⁵):** > **K-80 (residential/light)** → **K-115 (standard)** → **K-160 (storage/ESFR)** → **K-200, K-360, K-400 (high challenge storage)**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Insurance Premium Reduction:** Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC) of India grants **15–30% premium discount** for sprinklered buildings, directly rewarding code-compliant design. - **Life Safety & Property Protection:** NFPA data confirms **96% effectiveness** of sprinklers — they reduce civilian fire death rate by **~87%** and property loss by **~70%**. - **Permissible Area Increase:** Under **NBC 2016**, sprinklered buildings are allowed **up to 200% increase** in permissible floor-area ratio for high-rise structures, making sprinklers essential for high-rise compliance. - **Sustainability:** Sprinklers suppress fires in their incipient stage, releasing only a fraction of the water a fire brigade hose stream would use — reducing water damage and environmental contamination. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Sprinkler Discharge Equation $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ The KTU Board frequently asks students to *derive* the standard sprinkler discharge formula. The derivation is rooted in **Torricelli's law of efflux from a circular orifice**. **Step 1 — Torricelli's Velocity of Efflux:** When water discharges from an orifice under a head $h$, the theoretical velocity of the jet is: $$ V = \sqrt{2gh} $$ where: - $V$ = velocity of the jet (m/s) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $h$ = pressure head in metres of water column **Step 2 — Convert Pressure Head to Pressure in Bars:** The relationship between head $h$ and pressure $P$ (in bar) is: $$ P = \rho g h \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} $$ where $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ (water density) and 1 bar = 10⁵ N/m². So $h = \dfrac{P}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{P}{9810}$ metres. **Step 3 — Apply Continuity for Flow Through Sprinkler Orifice:** The volumetric flow rate through an orifice of area $A_s$ is: $$ Q = A_s \cdot C_d \cdot V $$ where $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (typically 0.65 to 0.95 for sprinklers). **Step 4 — Combine Equations:** $$ Q = A_s \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot \dfrac{P}{\rho g}} = A_s \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}} $$ **Step 5 — Group the Constants into a Single Coefficient $K$:** The product $A_s \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}}$ is a constant for a given sprinkler geometry and water density. It is defined as the **K-factor**. $$ K = A_s \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}} $$ Hence the universally accepted KTU / NFPA sprinkler discharge equation: $$ \boxed{Q = K \sqrt{P}} $$ **Step 6 — Dimensional Confirmation:** $[K] = \dfrac{[Q]}{[P]^{0.5}} = \dfrac{L/min}{bar^{0.5}}$ This is consistent with manufacturers publishing K in units of **L/min·bar⁰·⁵** (SI) or **gpm/psi⁰·⁵** (Imperial). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A **car parking area (Ordinary Hazard Group 1)** of **1500 m²** is to be protected by a **wet pipe sprinkler system** using **K-115 sprinklers** at a maximum spacing of **12 m² per head**. The design density is **5.0 mm/min** over an **area of operation of 72 m²**. The water supply is to be maintained for **60 minutes**. Calculate: (a) The number of sprinklers in the design area. (b) The required pressure at each sprinkler. (c) The total system flow rate. (d) The required water storage volume. (e) Confirm whether the supply meets IS 15105 / NFPA 13 minimum duration. --- ### **Part (a) — Number of Sprinklers in the Design Area** $$ A_{per-sprinkler} = 12 \text{ m}^2, \quad A_{op} = 72 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ n = \dfrac{A_{op}}{A_{per-sprinkler}} = \dfrac{72}{12} = 6 \text{ sprinklers} $$ > **Model Answer (a): 6 sprinklers** are assumed to operate simultaneously within the design area. --- ### **Part (b) — Required Pressure at Each Sprinkler** First, calculate the design flow per sprinkler assuming uniform density-area distribution: $$ Q_{per-sprinkler} = \dfrac{D \times A_{op}}{n} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 72}{6} = 60 \text{ L/min} $$ Apply $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ and solve for P with K = 115: $$ P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{60}{115}\right)^2 $$ $$ P = (0.5217)^2 = 0.2722 \text{ bar} $$ > **Model Answer (b): Minimum pressure at the most remote sprinkler = 0.27 bar (≈ 2.7 m head).** > *This is below the typical operating pressure; for design margin, assume P = 0.5 bar residual.* --- ### **Part (c) — Total System Flow Rate** $$ Q_{total} = D \times A_{op} = 5.0 \text{ mm/min} \times 72 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 360 \text{ L/min} = 6.0 \text{ L/s} = 21.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} $$ > **Model Answer (c): Total flow = 360 L/min (≈ 6.0 L/s).** --- ### **Part (d) — Water Storage Volume** $$ V = Q_{total} \times t = 360 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \text{ min} $$ $$ V = 21{,}600 \text{ litres} = 21.6 \text{ m}^3 $$ > **Model Answer (d): Water storage required = 21.6 m³.** --- ### **Part (e) — Duration Compliance** Per IS 15105 / NFPA 13, **OH-1 occupancy** requires a minimum water supply duration of **60 minutes**. Designed duration = 60 minutes = **Compliant**. ✔ > **Model Answer (e): The design meets the minimum 60-minute duration requirement for OH-1 occupancy.** --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — System Type vs. Occupancy | Occupancy / Risk | Ambient Temp | Speed Required | Recommended System | Justification | |------------------|--------------|----------------|--------------------|---------------| | Office Building (ac) | > 4 °C | Fast | **Wet Pipe** | Simplicity, no water hammer risk | | Cold Storage (< 4 °C) | < 4 °C | Fast | **Dry Pipe / Pre-Action** | Prevents pipe freezing | | Data Centre (electronics) | 20 °C | Very fast (water damage prevention) | **Pre-Action (Double Interlock)** | Prevents accidental water release | | Aircraft Hangar | Variable | Immediate, large area | **Deluge + Foam** | High hazard, large open area | | Transformer Yard | Variable | Immediate | **Deluge + Water Spray** | Oil/electrical fire, fast spread | | Hospital Patient Ward | > 4 °C | Moderate | **Wet Pipe (Fast Response)** | Reduces water damage; life safety priority | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The selection of system type is the **first judgemental question** in any 14-mark sprinkler problem. The student must state both the *system type* and *justify the choice* with reference to ambient temperature, water-damage risk, and speed-of-response. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wet Pipe Sprinkler System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of a wet pipe sprinkler installation — from municipal/pump water supply to the most remote sprinkler head. This is the KTU Board diagram style for a 14-mark system planning question. ```mermaid graph TD A[Municipal Water Supply / Tank] --> B[Fire Pump House
Jockey Pump + Main Pump + Diesel Standby] B --> C[Control Valves
OS&Y Gate Valves] C --> D[Alarm Check Valve
Retard Chamber + Water Motor Gong] D --> E[Riser Manifold
Vertical Riser to Each Floor] E --> F[Main Distribution Pipe
Lateral Branches] F --> G[Branch Lines Feeding Sprinkler Heads] G --> H[Sprinkler Head K-115
Glass Bulb 68 C Red] H --> I[Fire Affected Zone
Localized Discharge] D --> J[Pressure Switch to Fire Alarm Panel] D --> K[Flow Switch Detects Discharge] J --> L[Fire Brigade Notification + BMS] K --> L L --> M[Building Evacuation Sequence] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - **A → B** is the *water supply chain*, sized for 60–90 min at the design flow. > - **D** is the *detection-of-flow* node — the retard chamber delays the alarm to prevent false trips from pressure surges. > - **G → H** represents the *terminal distribution* — the principle that **only the sprinkler(s) directly over the fire open**, not the entire system. > - **J / K / L / M** show the *active fire safety integration* with BMS and life safety systems. --- ## 4.2 Hazard Classification Decision Flow (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **decision logic** a designer must follow when classifying a building into a hazard group — a common 3-mark or short-answer question in KTU. ```mermaid graph TD Q1[Identify Occupancy Use] --> Q2{Is Fire Load > 1000 MJ/m2?} Q2 -->|No| Q3{High Combustible Storage or High Rate of Heat Release?} Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Is it Flammable Liquid or Combustible Dust?} Q3 -->|No| H1[Light Hazard LH] Q3 -->|Yes, Moderate| H2[Ordinary Hazard OH 1 to OH 4] Q3 -->|Yes, High| H3[Extra Hazard EH 1] Q4 -->|Yes| H4[Extra Hazard EH 2] Q4 -->|No| H2 H1 --> R1[Design Density 2.25 mm/min, Area 72 m2] H2 --> R2[Design Density 5.0 to 10.0 mm/min, Area 72 to 216 m2] H3 --> R3[Design Density 12.5 mm/min, Area 260 m2] H4 --> R4[Design Density 12.5 mm/min, Area 325 m2] R1 --> S[Select Wet Dry Deluge Pre Action System] R2 --> S R3 --> S R4 --> S ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic of a Typical Sprinkler Head Although the KTU exam cannot show physical 3D drawings, students are expected to **label a schematic of a sprinkler head**. The following Mermaid block shows the key components of a standard spray sprinkler: ```mermaid graph TD SP[Spray Sprinkler Head K-115] --> F1[1 Frame Cast Brass Body] SP --> F2[2 Deflector Plate Stainless Steel] SP --> F3[3 Glass Bulb 68 C Red Glycerine Filled] SP --> F4[4 Compression Screw] SP --> F5[5 Seat Orifice 12.7 mm Half Inch] SP --> F6[6 Belleville Spring Seal] F3 -->|Heat Exceeds RTI Threshold| ACT[Glass Bulb Bursts Water Discharges onto Deflector] ACT --> SPREAD[Spray Pattern Radius 1.5 to 2.5 m Wetting Floor Area 9 to 12 m2] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **(a) Differentiate between a Wet Pipe and a Dry Pipe Sprinkler System.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Wet Pipe System | Dry Pipe System | |-------|-----------------|------------------| | Piping state | Always **filled with water** under pressure | Filled with **pressurized air** above a water-air interface | | Trigger | Sprinkler head opens → water discharges immediately | Sprinkler head opens → air escapes → clapper valve opens → water enters | | Application | Heated buildings (> 4 °C) | Cold / unheated buildings (< 4 °C) | | Speed of operation | **Fast (immediate)** | **Slower (up to 60 sec delay)** | | Corrosion risk | High if stagnant | Low (air presence) | | Cost | Lower | Higher (compressor + accelerator needed) | --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(b) What is a K-factor of a sprinkler head? State any three standard K-factors with their typical applications.** **Model Answer:** The **K-factor (K)** of a sprinkler is the discharge coefficient that quantifies the water flow through the orifice at a given pressure, defined by the equation $Q = K\sqrt{P}$. Standard K-factors: 1. **K-80** → residential / light hazard occupancies (small residential flats, low-rise apartments). 2. **K-115** → general / standard commercial occupancies (offices, OH-1, OH-2). 3. **K-160** → storage occupancies (racked storage up to 4.5 m, ESFR sprinklers). 4. **K-200 / K-360 / K-400** → high-challenge storage (warehouse > 7.5 m, NFPA 13 storage classification). --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **occupancy hazard classification** as per **IS 15105 / NFPA 13** for automatic sprinkler systems. With the help of a **classification table**, list **three example occupancies** for each of the **Light Hazard, Ordinary Hazard (Groups 1 to 4), and Extra Hazard (Groups 1 to 2)** groups, along with the corresponding **design density and area of operation**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **warehouse storing Class-IV commodity** (24 ft / 7.5 m high rack storage) is to be protected by a **wet pipe sprinkler system** with **ESFR K-25.2 (≈ K-363 SI)** sprinklers. The design criteria is **density 25.0 mm/min** over an **area of 230 m²**. The system is to operate for **90 minutes**. Calculate: - (i) The **total system flow rate** in L/min and L/s. - (ii) The **required water storage volume** in m³. - (iii) The **pressure at the most remote sprinkler** if each head discharges **150 L/min**. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The classification is summarized in the table below. The design density is the rate of water application per unit area (mm/min or gpm/ft²) and the **area of operation** is the assumed worst-case simultaneous operating zone used as a basis for sizing the water supply. | Hazard Group | Example Occupancies (3 each) | Design Density (mm/min) | Area of Operation (m²) | |--------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------| | **LH** | Offices, Hospitals, Schools, Churches, Museums, Residential | 2.25 | 72 | | **OH-1** | Car parking, Bakeries, Restaurant service | 5.0 | 72 | | **OH-2** | Workshops, Showrooms, Small manufacturing | 5.0 | 144 | | **OH-3** | Warehouses (inert stock), Printing press, Textile mill | 5.0 | 216 | | **OH-4** | Paper mills, Woodworking, Tanneries | 10.0 | 216 | | **EH-1** | Flammable liquid storage, Upholstery mfg., Solvent plants | 12.5 | 260 | | **EH-2** | Aircraft hangars, Plastic foam mfg., Chemical processing | 12.5 | 325 | > **Model Answer (a) — Key Points to Write:** > - IS 15105 follows NFPA 13 classification methodology. > - The classification is *conservative* — design is based on the **worst-credible fire**, not the average. > - The classification drives *density, area, duration, sprinkler type, and spacing*. #### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Total System Flow Rate:** $$ Q_{total} = D \times A_{op} = 25.0 \text{ mm/min} \times 230 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 5750 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{5750}{60} = 95.83 \text{ L/s} $$ **(ii) Water Storage Volume:** $$ V = Q_{total} \times t = 5750 \text{ L/min} \times 90 \text{ min} $$ $$ V = 517{,}500 \text{ L} = 517.5 \text{ m}^3 $$ **(iii) Pressure at Most Remote Sprinkler:** Given Q = 150 L/min and K = 25.2 gpm/psi⁰·⁵ (convert to SI: **K_SI ≈ 363 L/min·bar⁰·⁵**). $$ P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{150}{363}\right)^2 $$ $$ P = (0.413)^2 = 0.1706 \text{ bar} \approx 1.706 \text{ m head} $$ > **Model Answer (b) — Final Results:** > - (i) Q_total = **5750 L/min ≈ 95.8 L/s** > - (ii) V = **517.5 m³** (storage tank) > - (iii) P_remote = **0.17 bar residual** — must be verified with a pump; design margin is added. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Compare the **Wet Pipe, Dry Pipe, Deluge, and Pre-Action** sprinkler systems on the basis of: **trigger mechanism, application area, advantages, and limitations**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **3-storey office building** has a **single floor plate of 800 m²**. The building is to be protected by a **wet pipe sprinkler system** with **K-80 sprinklers** at a **spacing of 4 m × 4 m** (i.e., 16 m² per head). The **design density is 2.5 mm/min** and the **area of operation is 72 m²**. Calculate: - (i) The **number of sprinklers in the design area**. - (ii) The **flow per sprinkler** and the **total system flow**. - (iii) The **pressure required at the most remote sprinkler**. - (iv) The **total water storage** for a **60-minute** duration. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** | System | Trigger | Application | Advantages | Limitations | |--------|---------|-------------|------------|-------------| | **Wet Pipe** | Heat melts bulb → head opens | Offices, hotels, hospitals | Simple, fast, low cost | Not for < 4 °C; corrosion risk | | **Dry Pipe** | Heat melts bulb → air releases → water enters | Cold stores, parking | Frost-proof | Slower (≈ 60 s); compressor needed | | **Deluge** | External detector → deluge valve opens → all heads open simultaneously | Aircraft hangar, transformers | Cools large open area; fast | High water demand; false-trip risk | | **Pre-Action** | Detection + sprinkler head opening (double interlock) | Data centres, archives | Prevents accidental water damage | Most complex; costliest | #### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Number of Sprinklers in Design Area:** $$ n = \dfrac{A_{op}}{A_{per-sprinkler}} = \dfrac{72}{16} = 4.5 \approx 5 \text{ sprinklers (round up)} $$ **(ii) Flow per Sprinkler and Total Flow:** $$ Q_{per-sprinkler} = \dfrac{D \times A_{op}}{n} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 72}{5} = 36 \text{ L/min} $$ $$ Q_{total} = D \times A_{op} = 2.5 \times 72 = 180 \text{ L/min} = 3.0 \text{ L/s} $$ **(iii) Pressure at Most Remote Sprinkler (K = 80):** $$ P = \left(\dfrac{Q_{per-sprinkler}}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{36}{80}\right)^2 $$ $$ P = (0.45)^2 = 0.2025 \text{ bar} \approx 2.0 \text{ m head} $$ **(iv) Water Storage for 60 Minutes:** $$ V = 180 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \text{ min} = 10{,}800 \text{ L} = 10.8 \text{ m}^3 $$ > **Model Answer (b) — Final Results:** > - (i) n = **5 sprinklers** > - (ii) Q per sprinkler = **36 L/min**; Q total = **180 L/min (3 L/s)** > - (iii) P remote = **0.20 bar (≈ 2.0 m head)** > - (iv) V = **10.8 m³** --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in sprinkler questions (KTU 2024 scheme):** > 1. **Forgetting to round up n (number of sprinklers).** A fractional count is invalid — always round up. A 4.5 count becomes 5. > 2. **Mixing up Imperial and SI units.** NFPA 13 K-factors (e.g., K-25.2) are in **gpm/psi⁰·⁵**; converting to SI gives **K-363 L/min·bar⁰·⁵**. Using the wrong K-factor gives wrong pressure. > 3. **Omitting justification of system type.** Writing "Wet Pipe System" without justifying *why* (e.g., "since ambient temperature > 4 °C and the building is occupied, wet pipe is preferred for faster response") costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Missing duration requirement.** OH-1 to OH-4 need **60 min** minimum; EH-1 and EH-2 need **90 min**. Not stating this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Forgetting Hazen-Williams friction loss in network pipe design.** Pressure at the *most remote* sprinkler must be calculated *after* friction loss through pipes, fittings, valves, and elevation, not just from the K-factor equation alone. > 6. **Neglecting the (n × n × n) multiplier for the design area check.** The KTU 2024 scheme often asks for verification using the **(C × A)_total ≥ n × D × A_op** check, where C is coverage factor. --- ## 🟢 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - [x] A sprinkler system is an **occupancy-based**, **code-driven** fixed fire-fighting installation governed by **IS 15105 / NFPA 13 / TAC / NBC 2016**. - [x] The **four system types** are Wet, Dry, Deluge, and Pre-Action — selection is based on **ambient temperature, water-damage risk, and speed**. - [x] **Hazard Classification** = LH, OH-1, OH-2, OH-3, OH-4, EH-1, EH-2 (Light → Extra). - [x] The **K-factor equation** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ is the foundation of all sprinkler flow calculations. - [x] **Design density × Area of operation = Total system flow** — memorize the standard density-area matrix. - [x] **Duration:** 60 minutes (LH, OH), 90 minutes (EH). - [x] **Hazen-Williams** with C = 120 and equivalent length = 1.2 × actual is the standard friction loss formula. - [x] Always **verify minimum residual pressure** (≥ 0.5 bar) at the hydraulically most remote sprinkler after all losses. - [x] **Round up** sprinkler count to the next whole number. - [x] **Water storage tank** sizing: $V = Q_{total} \times t$ — and remember a separate jockey pump line to maintain system pressure. - [x] **Sprinkler effectiveness:** NFPA reports **> 96% control/extinguishment**; property loss reduction **~70%**; life-safety death reduction **~87%**. - [x] **NBC 2016** allows **200% increase in permissible floor-area ratio** for fully sprinklered high-rise buildings. - [x] **ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response)** sprinklers (K-160, K-200, K-363, K-400) are used in modern high-challenge storage warehouses.

topics.title: Total Flooding System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Flooding System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : TOTAL FLOODING SYSTEM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Flooding System (TFS):** A fixed fire extinguishing system in which a pre-calculated quantity of a fire-suppressing agent is rapidly and uniformly discharged into an enclosed, sealed (or sealable) volume, achieving a specified **design concentration** throughout the entire three-dimensional space of the protected enclosure to extinguish or inert the fire at its source.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a Total Flooding System is classified as an **Enclosure Integrity-Based (EIB) fire suppression strategy** under NFPA 2001, IS 15493, and the **IS 6382 / NFPA 12** family of standards for gaseous agents. It is fundamentally different from a *local application* system because it relies on the *enclosure itself* acting as the container for the suppressant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Triad of Total Flooding:**
> 1. **Enclosure Integrity** — the room must hold the gas (typically a retention time of **≥ 10 minutes** for halocarbons, **≥ 1 hour** for CO₂).
> 2. **Design Concentration** — the minimum agent concentration that extinguishes the fuel (typically expressed as a % by volume).
> 3. **Discharge Time** — the agent must be released within a defined window (usually **≤ 60 s** for Class A fires, **≤ 30 s** for Class B).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Balloon" Principle

Imagine you have a **room-sized glass aquarium** and a **smoke bomb** that fills the aquarium completely with white, opaque vapour. After a few seconds, *every cubic centimetre* of the aquarium contains smoke — top, bottom, and all corners.

A Total Flooding System works on the *exact same principle*:

- The **protected enclosure** = the **aquarium** (must be airtight enough to retain the agent).
- The **suppressant gas** = the **smoke** (fills every void).
- The **fire** = something you want to "smother" inside.

The genius of the system is that instead of *spraying* water or foam at the fire (like a sprinkler), it **changes the composition of the air itself** — typically by adding enough inert or chemically active gas to either:
- Reduce the **oxygen concentration** below the threshold needed for combustion (e.g., CO₂ at 50 % vol), or
- **Interrupt the chemical chain reaction** of fire (e.g., FM-200, Novec 1230).

> [!TIP]
> **Why a "balloon" and not a "water hose"?** Because gases are three-dimensional — they have no preference for "the burning object." They envelop *everything* in the room, so even a fire hidden behind a server rack, inside a switchboard, or under a raised floor gets suppressed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Design Concentration (FM-200, Class A) | **7.0 – 8.5** | % by volume |
| Design Concentration (Novec 1230) | **4.5 – 5.9** | % by volume |
| Design Concentration (CO₂) | **34 – 75** | % by volume |
| Minimum Oxygen (extinguishment) | **< 12 – 15** | % by volume |
| Agent Discharge Time (Class A) | **≤ 60** | seconds |
| Agent Discharge Time (Class B) | **≤ 30** | seconds |
| Enclosure Retention (Halocarbons) | **≥ 10** | minutes |
| Enclosure Retention (CO₂) | **≥ 60** | minutes |
| NTP Reference Pressure | **101.325** | kPa |
| NTP Reference Temperature | **20** | °C (293.15 K) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Six-Stage Cascade

A Total Flooding System does not just "spray gas." It executes a precisely orchestrated six-stage cascade:

1. **Detection (Stage 1):** Smoke/heat/flame detector senses the anomaly → sends signal to the **Control Panel (CCT – Control, Communication & Termination)**.
2. **Verification (Stage 2):** Control panel implements an *approved pre-discharge delay* (typically **0 – 30 s**) to allow controlled evacuation and prevent false discharges.
3. **Alarm (Stage 3):** Audible-visual alarms + **pre-discharge warning horn** activates. **Abort switches** (where applicable) allow a human to halt the sequence within the delay window.
4. **Discharge (Stage 4):** Solenoid/actuator opens the agent cylinder valve(s). Pressurized agent (typically stored at **2.5 – 4.2 MPa** for clean agents) flows through **discharge piping** → **nozzles** → into the enclosure.
5. **Hold / Soak (Stage 5):** The agent *mixes* with air and is held at design concentration for the **retention period** to prevent re-ignition.
6. **Purge / Vent (Stage 6):** After hold, the enclosure is mechanically or naturally vented to a safe outdoor location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a pre-discharge delay?** Because most clean-agent systems use gases that can asphyxiate humans in high concentration. The delay ensures evacuation completes before the agent is released — a *life-safety* feature mandated by NFPA 2001.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Total Flooding Systems

Total flooding systems are classified by the **type of extinguishing agent** used. The following taxonomy is critical for KTU exams:

| Class | Agents | Mechanism | Typical Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inert Gas** | IG-01 (Ar), IG-100 (N₂), IG-55 (N₂/Ar), IG-541 (N₂/Ar/CO₂) | **Oxygen Displacement** (reduces O₂ below ~12 %) | 35 – 50 % |
| **Chemical Clean Agent** | FM-200 (HFC-227ea), NAFS-III, HCFC Blend A | **Chemical Chain Inhibition** (radical capture) | 6.7 – 8.5 % |
| **Fluoroketone** | Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12) | Heat extraction + weak chemical action | 4.5 – 5.9 % |
| **Carbon Dioxide** | CO₂ (high/low pressure) | **Oxygen Displacement** + cooling | 34 – 75 % |
| **Water Mist** | Fine water droplets | **Cooling + radiant heat block** | — |
| **Hybrid** | Inergen blends, water-mist+inert gas | Combined mechanism | Variable |

---

## 2.3 The Three Design Concentrations You MUST Know

> [!NOTE]
> The agent quantity in a total flooding system is calculated against the **least favourable concentration** among the following three. This is a guaranteed KTU question.

1. **Extinguishing Concentration (C_ext):** The lowest agent concentration that extinguishes a given fuel.
2. **Design Concentration (C_design):** C_ext + a **safety factor of 1.2 (or 20 %)** per NFPA 2001.
   $$C_{design} = 1.2 \times C_{ext}$$
3. **Minimum Concentration (C_min):** C_design − 5 % (or as per standard), used for **enclosure integrity** calculation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these seven equations — they cover ~85 % of all KTU numerical questions on total flooding.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enclosure Volume | $V = L \times W \times H$ | Net protected volume |
| 2 | Flooding Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{C_{design}}{100 - C_{design}} \cdot \dfrac{V}{S_{v,mol}} \cdot \rho_{agent}$ | Agent mass required |
| 3 | Total Agent Mass (NFPA 2001 form) | $m = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100 - C}$ | $S$ = specific volume of superheated agent at NTP |
| 4 | Specific Volume (Ideal Gas) | $S = \dfrac{R \cdot T_{NTP}}{P_{NTP} \cdot M}$ | Used to find agent density at discharge conditions |
| 5 | Equivalent Leaky Area | $A_{leak} = \dfrac{V \cdot \ln(C_0 / C_t)}{t \cdot k}$ | Enclosure integrity check |
| 6 | Discharge Time | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{\sum Q_{nozzle}}$ | Nozzle selection check |
| 7 | Pressure Drop (Orifice) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \rho L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$ | Darcy-Weisbach-derived, used in pipe sizing |

### 2.4.1 Variable Definitions

- $V$ = protected enclosure net volume (m³)
- $C$ = design concentration (% by volume)
- $S$ = specific volume of agent vapour at NTP (m³/kg)
- $m$ = agent mass required (kg)
- $M$ = molecular weight of agent (kg/kmol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T_{NTP}$ = 293.15 K, $P_{NTP}$ = 101325 Pa
- $\rho$ = density of agent (kg/m³)
- $t$ = retention time (s)
- $k$ = discharge coefficient for enclosure leakage

### 2.4.2 Real-World Utility

These formulas are used by:
- **Fire Protection Engineers** to size cylinders and pipe networks in data centres, transformer rooms, archives.
- **Insurance Surveyors** to verify that agent quantity matches the room volume.
- **Accreditation Auditors** for ISO 14520 / NFPA 2001 compliance checks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Agent Mass Required for Total Flooding (NFPA 2001 Form)

We begin from first principles using the **ideal gas law** and a **mass balance** on the protected enclosure.

### Step 1 — Define the protected volume

$$V = L \times W \times H \quad [\text{m}^3]$$

The **net** volume excludes fixed obstructions (cabinets, raised floors above 0.3 m are usually *included*; solid structural members are *excluded*).

### Step 2 — Apply the design concentration condition

When agent of design concentration $C$ (% by volume) fills the enclosure at NTP, the **volume occupied by agent vapour** is:

$$V_{agent} = \dfrac{C}{100} \times V$$

### Step 3 — Convert agent volume to mass using specific volume

The **specific volume** $S$ (m³/kg) is the volume occupied by 1 kg of superheated agent vapour at NTP. For an ideal gas:

$$S = \dfrac{R \cdot T_{NTP}}{P_{NTP} \cdot M}$$

Therefore, the **mass of agent** required is:

$$m = \dfrac{V_{agent}}{S} = \dfrac{(C/100) \cdot V}{S}$$

Rearranging into the canonical NFPA 2001 form:

$$\boxed{\,m = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100 - C}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator is $(100 - C)$, NOT $100$, because the agent *replaces* an equal volume of air (displacement). This subtle point is the single most common KTU exam trap.

### Step 4 — Verification of units

$$[m] = \dfrac{[\text{m}^3]}{[\text{m}^3/\text{kg}]} \cdot \dfrac{[\%]}{[\%]} = \text{kg} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Leaky Area for Enclosure Integrity

Once the agent is discharged, the **leakage rate** through gaps, doors, and cable penetrations reduces the concentration over time. The mass balance is:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A_{leak}}{V} \cdot C$$

where $k$ is a discharge coefficient (typically 0.6 – 0.7 for sharp-edged orifices).

### Step 1 — Separate variables

$$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A_{leak}}{V} \cdot dt$$

### Step 2 — Integrate from $C_0$ to $C_t$ over time $t$

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -\dfrac{k \cdot A_{leak}}{V} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -\dfrac{k \cdot A_{leak} \cdot t}{V}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the equivalent leaky area

$$\boxed{\,A_{leak} = -\dfrac{V}{k \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right)\,}$$

Or equivalently:

$$A_{leak} = \dfrac{V}{k \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right)$$

This is the **maximum allowable leakage area** — the sum of all openings (doors, ducts, cable penetrations) must be **less than** this computed value.

> [!TIP]
> **Typical Acceptance Criterion:** $C_t \geq 0.85 \, C_{design}$ at the end of the hold period. If leakage is too high, the room fails the *enclosure integrity test* (also called a *door fan test*).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Novec 1230 System for a Server Room

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic KTU 14-mark problem pattern. Solve it yourself before reading the solution.**

**Problem:** A 10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (L × W × H) server room has one door (1.0 m × 2.0 m) and two cable trays (0.3 m × 0.3 m each). The design fuel is a Class A fire (PVC-insulated cabling) requiring a **design concentration of 5.3 %** for Novec 1230. The specific volume of Novec 1230 vapour at NTP is **0.0733 m³/kg**. A door fan test measured a leakage area equivalent to **0.04 m²**.

**Tasks:**
- (a) Calculate the agent mass required.
- (b) Verify if the room passes the **enclosure integrity test** for a 10-minute hold, given $C_0 = 5.3 \%$ and $C_t \geq 0.85 \times C_{design} = 4.505 \%$.

---

### Solution

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Compute net volume:
$$V = 10 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 280 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 2 — Apply the NFPA 2001 mass equation:

$$m = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100 - C}$$

Substituting $V = 280$ m³, $S = 0.0733$ m³/kg, $C = 5.3$ %:

$$m = \dfrac{280}{0.0733} \cdot \dfrac{5.3}{100 - 5.3}$$

$$m = 3819.92 \cdot \dfrac{5.3}{94.7}$$

$$m = 3819.92 \times 0.05597$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 213.79 \text{ kg} \approx 214 \text{ kg of Novec 1230}}$$

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Use the equivalent leakage area equation with $V = 280$ m³, $k = 0.65$ (typical), $t = 600$ s (10 min), $C_0 = 5.3$ %, $C_t = 4.505$ %:

$$A_{leak,allow} = \dfrac{V}{k \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right)$$

$$A_{leak,allow} = \dfrac{280}{0.65 \times 600} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{5.3}{4.505}\right)$$

$$A_{leak,allow} = \dfrac{280}{390} \cdot \ln(1.1765)$$

$$A_{leak,allow} = 0.7179 \times 0.16252$$

$$A_{leak,allow} \approx 0.1167 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — Compare with measured leakage:

$$A_{leak,measured} = 0.04 \text{ m}^2 < A_{leak,allow} = 0.1167 \text{ m}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The enclosure **passes** the integrity test. The room is suitable for a Novec 1230 total flooding system using approximately **214 kg** of agent.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Agent

| Scenario | Recommended Agent | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Centre (occupied)** | Novec 1230 / FM-200 | Lowest design concentration → safest for humans |
| **Library / Archive (unoccupied)** | IG-541 (Inergen) | No residue, environmentally benign |
| **Transformer Yard** | Water Mist | High cooling capacity for oil fires |
| **Engine Room (naval)** | CO₂ | Cheap, effective, no electrical damage |
| **Cold Storage** | FM-200 | Performs at low temperatures |
| **Museum** | IG-541 / Novec 1230 | Zero residue protects artefacts |
| **Pharma Plant** | Clean Agent (FK-5-1-12) | No contamination, FDA-compliant |
