# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Total Flooding System. List any four clean agents used in total flooding systems. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A *Total Flooding System* is a fixed fire suppression arrangement in which a pre-calculated quantity of a fire-extinguishing agent is discharged into a sealed or sealable enclosure to achieve a uniform design concentration throughout the entire three-dimensional volume, thereby extinguishing the fire.

**Four clean agents:**
1. FM-200 (HFC-227ea)
2. Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)
3. Inergen (IG-541)
4. Argonite (IG-01)

---

### Question 2: State the significance of enclosure integrity in a total flooding system. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

Enclosure integrity is critical because:
1. The agent must be **retained** long enough to suppress and prevent re-ignition (typically ≥ 10 minutes for halocarbons, ≥ 60 minutes for CO₂).
2. If leakage is excessive, the agent concentration falls below the design value, allowing **re-flashover**.
3. A **door fan test** (per NFPA 2001) is performed to measure equivalent leakage area, which must be below the calculated maximum.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a total flooding system using **Novec 1230** for a server room protection. List any **three advantages** of Novec 1230 over FM-200. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A transformer room measuring 9 m × 6 m × 4.5 m is to be protected by an **Inergen (IG-541)** total flooding system. The design concentration is **43.3 %** and the specific volume of Inergen at NTP is **0.699 m³/kg**. Calculate the mass of agent required. Also verify that the actual charge required should account for a safety factor of 1.2. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working Principle of Novec 1230 Total Flooding System:**

1. **Detection:** Smoke detectors (ionization + optical) installed in the server room monitor air quality continuously.
2. **Signal & Verification:** A confirmed signal (two detectors in cross-zoned configuration) is sent to the **Control Panel** (CCT). A **30-second pre-discharge delay** is initiated.
3. **Alarm:** Audible-visual alarms (horn + strobe) and a *pre-discharge horn* operate to warn personnel to evacuate.
4. **Discharge:** After the delay, the solenoid actuator opens the **Novec 1230 cylinder valve** (stored at 4.2 MPa / 25 °C). Agent flows through discharge piping and **180°-pattern nozzles** into the room.
5. **Flooding & Hold:** The Novec 1230 vapour mixes with air to reach the **design concentration of 5.3 %** within 60 s. It is held for **10 minutes** to prevent re-ignition.
6. **Reset:** After the fire is verified extinguished, the system is manually vented, re-armed, and cylinders are recharged.

**Three Advantages of Novec 1230 over FM-200:**
1. **Lower design concentration** (4.5 – 5.9 % vs 7 – 8.5 %), giving a larger safety margin for occupied spaces.
2. **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** and a GWP of **< 1** (vs 3220 for FM-200), making it environmentally compliant with **Kigali Amendment / F-Gas Regulation**.
3. **Higher boiling point (49 °C)** — stored as a liquid at lower pressure (≈ 4.2 bar vs 25 bar for FM-200), giving **safer handling** and longer hold time.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L \times W \times H = 9 \times 6 \times 4.5$ m
- $C = 43.3$ %
- $S = 0.699$ m³/kg
- Safety Factor $SF = 1.2$

**Step 1 — Volume:**
$$V = 9 \times 6 \times 4.5 = 243 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Base mass required:**
$$m_{base} = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100 - C}$$

$$m_{base} = \dfrac{243}{0.699} \cdot \dfrac{43.3}{56.7}$$

$$m_{base} = 347.64 \times 0.76367 = 265.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor:**
$$m_{actual} = SF \times m_{base} = 1.2 \times 265.5 = \boxed{318.6 \text{ kg}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare **CO₂ Total Flooding** and **Novec 1230 Total Flooding** systems under the following heads: extinguishing mechanism, design concentration, safety for human occupancy, environmental impact, and typical application. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A library archive room of dimensions 12 m × 10 m × 4 m is protected by an **FM-200** system. The extinguishing concentration for the paper fire is **5.8 %** and the specific volume at NTP is **0.137 m³/kg**. Compute: (i) the design concentration using a 1.2 safety factor, (ii) the total FM-200 mass required, and (iii) the maximum allowable equivalent leakage area if a 10-minute hold is required with $C_0 = 6.96$ % and $C_t = 5.92$ % (assume $k = 0.65$). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CO₂ Total Flooding | Novec 1230 Total Flooding |
|---|---|---|
| **Extinguishing Mechanism** | Oxygen displacement (reduces O₂ to ~12–14 %) | Heat extraction + weak chemical action |
| **Design Concentration** | 34 – 75 % | 4.5 – 5.9 % |
| **Human Safety** | Lethal at > 10 %; **NOT** for occupied spaces | Safe up to 10 %; designed for occupied rooms |
| **Environmental Impact** | GWP = 1; no ODP | GWP < 1; no ODP; zero VOC |
| **Typical Application** | Engine rooms, paint shops, unoccupied industrial | Data centres, museums, control rooms |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 12 \times 10 \times 4 = 480$ m³, $C_{ext} = 5.8$ %, $S = 0.137$ m³/kg, $SF = 1.2$, $t = 600$ s, $k = 0.65$.

**(i) Design concentration:**
$$C_{design} = SF \times C_{ext} = 1.2 \times 5.8 = \boxed{6.96 \text{ \%}}$$

**(ii) Total FM-200 mass required:**
$$m = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C_{design}}{100 - C_{design}}$$

$$m = \dfrac{480}{0.137} \cdot \dfrac{6.96}{93.04}$$

$$m = 3503.65 \times 0.07481$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 262.1 \text{ kg}}$$

**(iii) Maximum allowable leakage area:**
$$A_{leak,max} = \dfrac{V}{k \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right)$$

$$A_{leak,max} = \dfrac{480}{0.65 \times 600} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{6.96}{5.92}\right)$$

$$A_{leak,max} = \dfrac{480}{390} \cdot \ln(1.1757)$$

$$A_{leak,max} = 1.2308 \times 0.16188$$

$$\boxed{A_{leak,max} \approx 0.1993 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.2 safety factor** in Part B(b)(i) — directly using 5.8 % as design concentration. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using (100) instead of (100 − C)** in the mass formula — this gives a mass ~6 % too low for halocarbons. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing retention time** (10 min for halocarbons, 60 min for CO₂) — write the standard value explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Not labelling sketches** in part (a) — KTU evaluators mark component labels. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — always quote mass in **kg**, volume in **m³**, concentration in **%** — mixing lb, ft³, etc. loses full credit in numericals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Total Flooding System (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A fixed suppression system that fills an *enclosed* space uniformly with a gaseous/semi-gaseous agent.
- ✅ **Three Critical Concentrations to Memorize:** $C_{ext}$, $C_{design} = 1.2 \times C_{ext}$, $C_{min} = C_{design} - 5\%$.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $m = \dfrac{V}{S} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100 - C}$ — *the denominator is (100 − C), NOT 100.*
- ✅ **Leakage Formula:** $A_{leak} = \dfrac{V}{k \cdot t} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right)$.
- ✅ **Standard Specific Volumes (NTP):** FM-200 ≈ 0.137 m³/kg; Novec 1230 ≈ 0.0733 m³/kg; Inergen ≈ 0.699 m³/kg.
- ✅ **Discharge Time:** ≤ 60 s for Class A; ≤ 30 s for Class B (NFPA 2001).
- ✅ **Retention Time:** Halocarbons ≥ 10 min; CO₂ ≥ 60 min.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** **NFPA 2001** (clean agents), **IS 15493**, **ISO 14520**, **NFPA 12** (CO₂), **IS 6382**.
- ✅ **Agent Classification:** Inert gases (IG-01/100/55/541), Chemical clean agents (FM-200, NAFS-III), Fluoroketones (Novec 1230), CO₂, Water Mist.
- ✅ **Enclosure Integrity Test:** Door fan test per **NFPA 2001 Annex C** is mandatory.
- ✅ **Occupied vs Unoccupied:** CO₂ is *NEVER* for occupied spaces; Novec 1230 is safe up to 10 %.
- ✅ **Environmentally Preferred Agents (2024):** Novec 1230 (GWP < 1), Inergen (GWP ≈ 0) — preferred over FM-200 (GWP = 3220).
- ✅ **Six-Stage Cascade to Draw:** Detect → Verify → Alarm → Discharge → Hold → Vent.
- ✅ **Three Critical Abort Conditions:** Power failure, manual abort, single-detector signal (cross-zoning must confirm).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Total Flooding System**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CO2 System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CO2 System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide) Fire Suppression System** is a *clean-agent gaseous fire extinguishing system* classified under the **NFPA 12 — Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems**. It uses **liquefied carbon dioxide (CO₂)** stored under high pressure (typically **5.73 MPa ≈ 850 psi at 21 °C**) as the fire-extinguishing medium. Upon discharge, the liquid CO₂ rapidly expands into a gas, achieving fire suppression primarily through **oxygen displacement (smothering effect)**, supplemented by a modest **cooling effect** as the gas absorbs latent heat during expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO₂ System):**
> The CO₂ system is taught in Module 4 as part of **special hazard fire suppression systems**. Students must master:
> - The **mechanism of extinguishment** (oxygen depletion, cooling)
> - **Total flooding** vs. **local application** design philosophies
> - **Design concentration** tables and the **quantity / cylinder** calculation methodology
> - **Discharge time** requirements per NFPA 12
> - **Hazards to personnel** and life-safety interlocks (since CO₂ is a *life-threatening* asphyxiant at extinguishing concentrations)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Invisible Blanket That Starves the Fire"**
>
> Imagine a small kitchen fire. If you place a heavy metal **lid** over the pan, the flames vanish — not because the lid cooled the fire, but because it **cut off the oxygen** (the fire's "food"). A CO₂ system does the **same job**, but for an *entire room* and in **less than 60 seconds**. Cylinders filled with liquid CO₂ are released; the liquid flashes into a cold, heavy gas that **pours across the floor** (CO₂ is **1.5× heavier than air**) and **fills the room from the bottom up**, pushing breathable air — and oxygen — *out* of the room. Once the oxygen level drops below roughly **15 %**, combustion can no longer be sustained, and the fire self-extinguishes.
>
> **Critical life-safety caveat:** This same "oxygen-starving blanket" that kills fire also **kills humans**. That is why CO₂ systems are restricted to *normally unoccupied* spaces (server rooms, transformer rooms, paint spray booths) and require **pre-discharge alarms and time delays** before release.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Molecular weight of CO₂** | **44.01 g/mol** |
| **Specific gravity of liquid CO₂ (at 0 °C)** | **0.914** |
| **Vapor density of CO₂ (relative to air)** | **1.527** (heavier than air) |
| **Latent heat of vaporization** | **≈ 571 kJ/kg** at 0 °C |
| **Critical temperature of CO₂** | **31.04 °C** |
| **Critical pressure of CO₂** | **7.38 MPa (73.8 bar)** |
| **Filling ratio (NFPA 12)** | **0.50 to 0.75 kg/L** of cylinder water volume |
| **Minimum design concentration — Class B liquids** | **34 %** by volume |
| **Minimum design concentration — Electrical (Class C)** | **50 %** by volume (NFPA 12A legacy; per NFPA 12 current) |
| **Maximum allowable discharge time** | **≤ 60 s** for total flooding |
| **Maximum exposure limit (OSHA PEL)** | **0.5 % (5 000 ppm)** for 8 h TWA |
| **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous)** | **4 %** (40 000 ppm) |
| **Minimum oxygen for human life** | **≈ 19.5 %** |
| **Oxygen level at CO₂ 34 % flood** | **≈ 14 %** — below human tolerance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Mechanism of Fire Extinguishment — Why CO₂ Works

The CO₂ system extinguishes fire by **three concurrent physical processes**:

1. **Oxygen Displacement (Primary Mechanism — ≈ 80–90 % effectiveness)**
   - CO₂ gas displaces atmospheric air in the protected enclosure.
   - Combustion requires **O₂ ≥ 15 %** by volume. NFPA 12 design concentrations reduce O₂ below this threshold.

2. **Cooling / Heat Absorption (Secondary Mechanism — ≈ 10–20 % effectiveness)**
   - Stored liquid CO₂ at ≈ −18 °C (5.73 MPa) vaporizes upon discharge.
   - Latent heat of vaporization (≈ **571 kJ/kg**) is drawn from the flames and surrounding air.

3. **Dilution / Thermal Mass Effect**
   - The high specific heat capacity of CO₂ (≈ **0.839 kJ/kg·K**) and its molar heat capacity help absorb radiated heat from the fire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CO₂ is electrically non-conductive:** Pure gaseous CO₂ contains no ions and is an excellent dielectric. It leaves **zero residue** (a "clean agent"), making it ideal for protecting live electrical equipment, control panels, and switchgear.

### 2.2 Types of CO₂ Systems (KTU Classification)

| Type | Description | Typical Application | Discharge Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Flooding** | Releases CO₂ into an enclosed volume to achieve a uniform design concentration | Generator rooms, cable vaults, server rooms, paint booths | **≤ 60 s** (NFPA 12) |
| **Local Application** | CO₂ discharged directly onto a localized hazard (open surface or 3-D object) | Printing presses, oil quenching tanks, dip tanks | **≥ 30 s** and not exceeding **manufacturer-listed max** |
| **Hand-Hose Line System** | Operator-directed hose for spot/incipient fire fighting | Industrial plants, mechanical rooms | Manual, continuous |
| **Standpipe / Mobile System** | Pre-piped but discharged via mobile units on wheels | Marine, large warehouses | Manual |

### 2.3 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (per V10 protocol):** All absolute-value / dividing symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve markdown-table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V_{room} \times C}{100 - C}$ | **Ideal gas volume** of CO₂ required at standard conditions (m³), where $V_{room}$ = protected volume (m³) and $C$ = design concentration (%) |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{V_{CO_2} \times \rho_{v}}{S_{v}}$ | **Mass of CO₂** (kg) required, where $\rho_{v}$ = density of CO₂ vapor at 21 °C and 1 atm ≈ **1.838 kg/m³**, and $S_{v}$ = specific volume of CO₂ at NTP ≈ **0.5457 m³/kg** |
| 3 | $S_{v} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{v}} = \dfrac{RT}{PM}$ | **Specific volume** of CO₂ gas from ideal gas law; $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $P$ = pressure, $M$ = molar mass |
| 4 | $W_{actual} = W \times F_{T} \times F_{S} \times F_{L}$ | **Final CO₂ quantity** with temperature, safety, and leakage factors |
| 5 | $N_{cyl} = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{cyl}}$ | **Number of cylinders**, where $W_{cyl}$ = usable mass of CO₂ per cylinder (typically 22.7 kg or 45.4 kg) |
| 6 | $t_{d} \le 60~\text{s}$ | **Discharge time** constraint (total flooding, NFPA 12) |
| 7 | $\%O_{2,residual} = (1 - C/100) \times 20.9$ | Residual oxygen concentration in protected space |
| 8 | $A_{opening} = 0.0046 \times A_{room}$ | **Maximum unclosable opening** (m²) per NFPA 12 (≈ 1 % of room area) |
| 9 | $Q_{vessel} = V_{w} \times \eta$ | **Cylinder water capacity** (L) × **filling ratio** $\eta$ (0.50 to 0.75) → liquid mass of CO₂ per cylinder |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \rho L Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} D^{5}} f$ | **Pressure drop in pipe** (Darcy–Weisbach form) for pipe-sizing verification |

### 2.4 Step-by-Step CO₂ System Design Logic (Total Flooding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Five-step KTU-board-friendly design procedure:**

1. **Determine the protected volume** $V_{room} = L \times W \times H$ in m³.
2. **Select the design concentration** $C$ from NFPA 12 Tables (e.g., **34 %** for gasoline, **50 %** for electrical).
3. **Compute the CO₂ vapor volume** $V_{CO_2}$ using Formula 1.
4. **Apply the basic specific-volume conversion** $W = V_{CO_2} \cdot \rho_{v}$ using Formula 2 to get the mass in kg.
5. **Apply real-world correction factors** for temperature (ambient extremes), leakage, enclosure geometry (high ceilings), and a **1.2 safety factor** (typical).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers / Server Rooms:** CO₂ preserves the IT hardware (no residue, non-conductive) and rapidly extinguishes without water damage.
- **Power Generation:** Protects turbine enclosures, transformer bays, and switchgear.
- **Marine Industry:** Engine rooms on ships (high-value, high-risk machinery spaces).
- **Industrial Processes:** Paint spray booths, hydraulic press pits, flammable liquid storage.
- **Heritage / Archives:** Where water damage is unacceptable but *only with extreme life-safety controls* (CO₂ is increasingly replaced by inert-gas blends for occupied spaces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life-Safety Reality Check:** Because CO₂ is **odorless, colorless, and lethal at extinguishing concentrations**, NFPA 12 mandates:
> - **Pre-discharge alarm** (audible + visual) of at least **30 s** before release.
> - **Time delay** to permit evacuation.
> - **Lock-out** to prevent entry during discharge.
> - **NO PERSONNEL** in the protected space during discharge in *unoccupied* designs.
> This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** — be prepared to discuss the human-hazard mitigation strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Design Concentration to Cylinder Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**
> Design a **CO₂ total flooding system** for an electrical control room of internal dimensions:
> - Length $L = 10$ m, Width $W = 5$ m, Height $H = 4$ m.
> - Hazard: Energized electrical switchgear (Class C).
> - Minimum ambient temperature: **15 °C**.
> - Available cylinder size: **45.4 kg** liquid CO₂ capacity.

**Step 1 — Compute the protected gross volume**

$$
V_{room} = L \times W \times H = 10 \times 5 \times 4 = 200~\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Select the design concentration**

For Class C (electrical) hazards, NFPA 12 specifies a minimum design concentration of **C = 50 %**.

**Step 3 — Compute the ideal CO₂ vapor volume required**

$$
V_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{room} \times C}{100 - C} = \frac{200 \times 50}{100 - 50} = \frac{10000}{50} = 200~\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert the vapor volume to mass using the specific volume of CO₂**

At 21 °C and 1 atm, the specific volume of CO₂ gas is **$S_v \approx 0.5457~\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$**.

$$
W = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{S_v} = \frac{200}{0.5457} \approx 366.5~\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply correction factors**

- **Temperature factor** $F_T$ (for 15 °C): 1.0 (reference temperature)
- **Leakage / enclosure factor** $F_S$: 1.05
- **Loading safety factor** $F_L$ (NFPA 12): 1.20

$$
W_{actual} = W \times F_T \times F_S \times F_L = 366.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.05 \times 1.20 \approx 461.8~\text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Determine the number of cylinders**

$$
N_{cyl} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{cyl}} = \frac{461.8}{45.4} \approx 10.17 \Rightarrow \text{11 cylinders (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify discharge time**

If a single cylinder at full discharge releases its 45.4 kg in roughly 30 s through a properly sized orifice, the total system (11 cylinders manifolded) discharges in **≈ 30 s** ≤ 60 s → **NFPA 12 compliant**. ✔

**Step 8 — Verify the maximum unclosable opening**

$$
A_{opening, \max} = 0.0046 \times A_{room} = 0.0046 \times (10 \times 5) = 0.23~\text{m}^{2}
$$

If the door underfloor cable trench exceeds this, add a **mechanical damper / automatic drop-out shutter** to seal during discharge.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Volume of CO₂ from First Principles

Starting from the **ideal gas equation of state**:

$$
PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{nRT}{P}
$$

Since specific volume is the volume per unit mass:

$$
S_v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{nRT}{mP}
$$

But $n = m/M$ (mass / molar mass), therefore:

$$
S_v = \frac{mRT}{mPM} = \frac{RT}{PM}
$$

Substituting the numerical values for CO₂ at $T = 294.15$ K and $P = 101325$ Pa:

$$
S_v = \frac{8.314 \times 294.15}{101325 \times 0.04401}
$$

$$
S_v = \frac{2445.46}{4458.90} \approx 0.5484~\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}
$$

> **Comparison with empirical value (0.5457 m³/kg):** Agreement within 0.5 %. The minor discrepancy arises from CO₂'s non-ideal behaviour (compressibility factor $Z \approx 0.994$ at 1 atm, 21 °C).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use CO₂ vs. Alternative Clean Agents

| Decision Criterion | CO₂ | Inert Gas (IG-541, IG-55, IG-100) | Halocarbon (FM-200, Novec 1230) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Toxicity at design dose** | **HIGH (lethal)** — unoccupied only | Safe for occupied | Safe for occupied |
| **O₂ reduction in room** | Yes (to ≈ 14 %) | Yes (to ≈ 12 %) | No (chemical agent) |
| **Residue / cleanup** | None | None | None |
| **Storage pressure** | 5.73 MPa | 15–30 MPa | 2.5–4.2 MPa |
| **Cost per kg** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Suitable for occupied spaces?** | **NO** | YES | YES |
| **Environmental (GWP)** | GWP = 1 | GWP = 0 | GWP 0–3 500 |
| **Electrical hazard** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The most common 14-mark question contrasts CO₂ with **inert-gas** and **halocarbon** systems on the axes of *life safety*, *environmental impact*, and *cost*. Memorize the trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CO₂ Total Flooding System Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a CO₂ total-flooding installation, from the detection signal to the discharge nozzle. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase text (per V10 safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FireDetectorSmokeHeat] --> B1[ControlPanel]
    A2[ManualPullStation] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{ConfirmFireCondition}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[PreDischargeAlarm30s]
    D1 --> E1[AutomaticTimeDelayRelay]
    E1 --> F1[ActuateSolenoidValve]
    F1 --> G1[OpenPilotCylinderValve]
    G1 --> H1[OpenMasterCylindersN11]
    H1 --> I1[CO2FlowsThroughManifold]
    I1 --> J1[DistributionPipingNetwork]
    J1 --> K1[DischargeNozzlesCeilingMounted]
    K1 --> L1[ProtectedEnclosureVolumeFloods]
    L1 --> M1[O2FallsBelow15pct]
    M1 --> N1[FireSuppressed]
    B1 --> O1[AbortSwitchHoldToPrevent]
    B1 --> P1[VentilationAutoShutoff]
    P1 --> L1
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Detection-to-Discharge Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage1Detection] --> S2[Stage2AlarmAndEvacuation]
    S2 --> S3[Stage3TimeDelay30s]
    S3 --> S4[Stage4SolenoidActuation]
    S4 --> S5[Stage5DischargeLessThan60s]
    S5 --> S6[Stage6HoldAndSoakMin20min]
```

### 4.3 Component Schematic — Functional Description

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CYL1[CO2StorageCylindersHighPressure] --> MAN1[ManifoldHeader]
    MAN1 --> CHK1[CheckValveNonReturn]
    CHK1 --> PVT1[PressureRegulator]
    PVT1 --> PV1[PrimaryControlValve]
    PV1 --> PIPE1[DistributionPiping]
    PIPE1 --> N1[NozzleZoneA]
    PIPE1 --> N2[NozzleZoneB]
    PIPE1 --> N3[NozzleZoneC]
    PVT1 --> SV1[SafetyReliefValve]
    CYLDET[PressureSwitchCylinder] --> CYL1
    ACT1[PneumaticActuator] --> PV1
```

### 4.4 System Hierarchy — KTU Schematic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Det[DetectionLayer]
        D1[SmokeDetector]
        D2[HeatDetector]
        D3[FlameDetector]
    end
    subgraph Ctrl[ControlLayer]
        CP1[FireAlarmControlPanelFACP]
        TD1[TimeDelayModule30s]
        AS1[AbortStationManualHold]
    end
    subgraph Sup[SuppressionLayer]
        SV1[SolenoidValve]
        PV1[PneumaticActuator]
        CY1[CO2CylinderBank]
    end
    subgraph Dis[DischargeLayer]
        PI1[PipingManifold]
        NO1[DischargeNozzles]
        EN1[ProtectedEnclosure]
    end
    Det --> Ctrl --> Sup --> Dis
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Diagrams:**
> - The **redundancy** in detection (smoke + heat + flame) minimizes *false-positive* releases — critical for CO₂ where a false release is both expensive and life-threatening.
> - The **30-second time delay** is not optional — it is a **life-safety code requirement** under NFPA 12, Clause 4.3.1.
> - The **abort station** must be of the *hold-to-abort* type; a *momentary-release* abort is prohibited.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*List the three mechanisms by which a CO₂ fire suppression system extinguishes a fire. Which mechanism is dominant, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Oxygen displacement (smothering)** — dominant, because CO₂ is 1.527× heavier than air and pours into the enclosure, lowering O₂ below the 15 % combustion threshold.
2. **Cooling** — liquid CO₂ vaporization absorbs ≈ 571 kJ/kg of latent heat.
3. **Thermal-mass / dilution** — high specific heat of CO₂ absorbs radiated heat.

The **smothering effect** contributes ≈ 80–90 % of the extinguishing effect and is the reason NFPA 12 specifies concentrations (e.g., 34 %, 50 %) directly tied to O₂ reduction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State any three life-safety requirements that must be complied with when installing a CO₂ total-flooding system in an industrial enclosure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. A **pre-discharge alarm** (audible + visual) must sound for **at least 30 s** before CO₂ release to allow evacuation.
2. The protected space must be **treated as unoccupied** during discharge; entry interlocks (locks, signage) are required.
3. An **abort switch** of the *hold-to-disable* type must be provided outside the room.
4. **Ventilation must auto-shut down** within the discharge period.
5. The room must display **warning placards** as per NFPA 12.

(Any three carry 1 mark each.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain with a neat schematic the **working principle, components, and types** of a CO₂ fire suppression system. Compare **total flooding** and **local application** modes.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

- **Working Principle:** Liquid CO₂ stored at 5.73 MPa at 21 °C is released through a control valve into the protected space. Upon depressurization, the liquid flashes to gas, expands ≈ 450×, displaces O₂, and extinguishes the fire.
- **Major Components (each 0.5 mark):**
  1. CO₂ storage cylinders with safety rupture discs
  2. Manifold header and check valves
  3. Pneumatic / solenoid actuators
  4. Master control valves
  5. Distribution piping (schedule 40 / 80 steel)
  6. Discharge nozzles (180°, 360°, or directional)
  7. Detection & control panel
  8. Time-delay module
  9. Abort station
  10. Warning alarms and signage

- **Types of CO₂ Systems:**
  - **Total Flooding** — releases CO₂ into an enclosed volume to achieve a uniform design concentration; discharge ≤ 60 s.
  - **Local Application** — directs CO₂ onto a localized hazard (open tanks, 3-D objects); discharge ≥ 30 s.
  - **Hand Hose Line** — operator-directed mobile discharge.
  - **Standpipe/Mobile** — pre-piped with wheeled CO₂ carts.

- **Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Total Flooding | Local Application |
|---|---|---|
| Protected geometry | Enclosed room | Open surface / localized object |
| Design concentration | % by volume (e.g., 34 %) | Mass per unit area (kg/m²) |
| Discharge time | ≤ 60 s | ≥ 30 s (varies) |
| Enclosure integrity | Must be tight (≤ 1 % opening) | Open or partially open allowed |
| Typical applications | Server rooms, switchgear | Printing presses, quench tanks |

---

> **(b)** [7 marks] A paint-bulk-storage room of internal dimensions **8 m × 4 m × 3.5 m** is to be protected by a CO₂ total-flooding system. Hazard: Class B (flammable liquid — paint thinner, flash point < 32 °C). Ambient temperature: **20 °C**. Using **45.4-kg cylinders**, determine the **number of cylinders** required. Use a design concentration of **C = 34 %**, $S_v$ = 0.5457 m³/kg, $F_T$ = 1.00, $F_S$ = 1.05, $F_L$ = 1.20.

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Volume of room (1 mark)**
$$
V_{room} = 8 \times 4 \times 3.5 = 112~\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ vapor volume required (1.5 marks)**
$$
V_{CO_2} = \frac{112 \times 34}{100 - 34} = \frac{3808}{66} = 57.70~\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂ at standard conditions (1.5 marks)**
$$
W = \frac{57.70}{0.5457} = 105.7~\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply correction factors (1.5 marks)**
$$
W_{actual} = 105.7 \times 1.00 \times 1.05 \times 1.20 = 133.2~\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of 45.4-kg cylinders (1.5 marks)**
$$
N_{cyl} = \frac{133.2}{45.4} = 2.93 \Rightarrow 3~\text{cylinders}
$$

**Final Answer: 3 cylinders of 45.4 kg each** (round up; never round down — partial-cylinder over-runs are not acceptable in fire protection).

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **life-safety hazards** of CO₂ exposure and the **mandatory design safeguards** required by NFPA 12 for a CO₂ total-flooding system installed in a substation.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Hazard summary (3 marks):**

- CO₂ is **odorless, colorless, and tasteless** — humans cannot detect a leak.
- **0.5 %** = OSHA 8-h TWA — slight respiratory effect.
- **3 %** = breathing becomes labored; 30-min exposure.
- **4 %** = IDLH — immediate life threat.
- **10 %** = unconsciousness within minutes; **> 10 %** = death within minutes.
- At 34 % design concentration the residual O₂ is only **≈ 13.8 %** — unconsciousness in 1–2 breaths.

**Mandatory safeguards (4 marks):**

1. **Pre-discharge alarm** (audible horn ≥ 90 dB at 3 m + visual strobe) for minimum **30 s**.
2. **Time delay** of 30 s (NFPA 12) before solenoid actuation — adjustable to 60 s for large enclosures.
3. **Lock-out valves** on entry doors; **mechanical / electrical interlock** to prevent entry during armed state.
4. **Hold-to-abort** station located outside, with supervised wiring.
5. **Ventilation auto-shut-off** to prevent re-entry of fresh air during discharge.
6. **Warning signs** at every entry: "DANGER — CO₂ — DO NOT ENTER WHEN ALARM SOUNDS".
7. **Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** stations for emergency personnel within 30 m of the room.
8. **Manual release** at the door for emergency override plus **manual abort**.

---

> **(b)** [7 marks] A **45.4-kg CO₂ cylinder** has a **water volume of 50 L**. If the **filling ratio is 0.68 kg/L**, calculate: (i) the **liquid mass** of CO₂ stored, (ii) the **vapor volume** this would occupy at 21 °C, 1 atm, and (iii) whether a single such cylinder is sufficient for a **3 m × 3 m × 3 m** electrical room (design concentration 50 %, $S_v$ = 0.5457 m³/kg).

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Liquid mass stored (2 marks)**
$$
m = V_w \times \eta = 50 \times 0.68 = 34.0~\text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Vapor volume at NTP (2.5 marks)**
$$
V_{gas} = m \times S_v = 34.0 \times 0.5457 = 18.55~\text{m}^{3}
$$

**(iii) Is one cylinder sufficient? (2.5 marks)**

Room volume:
$$
V_{room} = 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27~\text{m}^{3}
$$

Required CO₂ mass at 50 % concentration:
$$
W = \frac{V_{room} \times C \times \rho_v}{100 - C} = \frac{27 \times 50 \times 1.838}{50} = 49.6~\text{kg}
$$

(Equivalently: $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{27 \times 50}{50} = 27$ m³ → $W = 27/0.5457 = 49.5$ kg.)

**Comparison: 49.5 kg required vs. 34.0 kg available → ONE CYLINDER IS NOT SUFFICIENT.**
**Number required: $\lceil 49.5 / 34.0 \rceil = 2$ cylinders (after applying 1.05 × 1.20 safety factors, recommend 3 cylinders).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in CO₂ System questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting correction factors** $F_T$, $F_S$, $F_L$ — losing 1.5–2 marks in 14-mark numericals.
> 2. **Rounding DOWN the cylinder count** — always round **up**; an undersized suppression system is a code violation.
> 3. **Confusing** the *flooding concentration* (e.g., 34 %) with the *oxygen concentration* (≈ 14 %) — these are NOT the same.
> 4. **Omitting life-safety interlocks** (alarm, time delay, abort, ventilation cut-off) — examiners expect 2–3 marks of life-safety discussion in any 14-mark answer.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversions**: m³ ↔ ft³ (1 m³ = 35.31 ft³), kg ↔ lb (1 kg = 2.205 lb). Use SI throughout.
> 6. **Confusing Total Flooding discharge time (60 s max)** with **Local Application (≥ 30 s)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CO₂ System**

- ☐ **CO₂ is stored as a liquid** under ≈ 5.73 MPa (≈ 850 psi) at 21 °C.
- ☐ Vapor density of CO₂ is **1.527 × air** → heavier-than-air, pools at floor.
- ☐ **Extinguishing mechanism:** Primarily **O₂ displacement (smothering)**, secondarily **cooling**.
- ☐ **Design concentrations:** Class B = **34 %**, Electrical = **50 %** (per NFPA 12).
- ☐ **Discharge time:** ≤ **60 seconds** for total flooding.
- ☐ **Maximum unclosable opening:** ≈ **0.46 % of room floor area** (per NFPA 12 simplified factor 0.0046).
- ☐ **Specific volume of CO₂** at 21 °C, 1 atm: **$S_v$ ≈ 0.5457 m³/kg**.
- ☐ **Filling ratio** of cylinders: **0.50 to 0.75 kg/L** (typically ≈ 0.68).
- ☐ **Residual O₂** at 34 % concentration ≈ **13.8 %** → **unconsciousness in 1–2 breaths** → human life-safety hazard.
- ☐ **OSHA IDLH** for CO₂ = **4 %**; extinguishing concentration exceeds this → system must remain unoccupied.
- ☐ **Life-safety interlock chain:** Detection → Alarm (30 s) → Time Delay → Solenoid → Discharge → Hold.
- ☐ **Clean agent** — leaves **zero residue** and is **electrically non-conductive**.
- ☐ **Always round UP** cylinder count.
- ☐ **Mandatory safety accessories:** Pre-discharge alarm, time delay, abort station, ventilation auto-shut-off, warning placards, SCBA station.
- ☐ **CO₂ GWP = 1** (low) but **ODP = 0**; suitable where the *unoccupied-only* constraint is acceptable.
- ☐ **Compare with:** Inert-gas blends (safe for occupied) and Halocarbons (FM-200, Novec 1230) — know the trade-off matrix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foam System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foam System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foam System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A fire suppression system that uses a homogeneous mass of small, gas-filled bubbles (formed mechanically or chemically from a foam concentrate, water, and air) having a lower density than the burning liquid it is designed to float upon, to achieve vapor suppression, oxygen exclusion, and cooling of the fuel surface.

In the context of KTU PESFT416 (Life Safety in Building Fire), a **Foam System** is classified as a special hazard fire protection system, primarily engineered for **Class A (ordinary combustibles)** and **Class B (flammable liquids / hydrocarbons / polar solvents)** fire scenarios. Unlike water sprinklers, foam systems are deployed where water alone is either ineffective (e.g., pool fires of gasoline) or hazardous (e.g., aircraft hangars, chemical storage, transformer yards).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blanket on a Lake" Analogy**
> Imagine you throw a match into a pool of gasoline. Water sprayed on it will sink (because water is denser than gasoline), allowing the fuel to keep burning. Now imagine pouring a giant, floating, bubble-filled **blanket** on top of the gasoline — it sits *on top* of the fuel, smothers the flame, stops oxygen from reaching the vapor, and gently cools the surface. **That blanket is foam.** It is essentially a **lighter-than-water fire extinguisher** engineered to suppress vapors.

### 1.3 Classification of Foam (KTU Syllabus Taxonomy)

Foams are classified based on **expansion ratio** and **chemical composition**.

| Expansion Ratio Category | Expansion Range | Bubble Density | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Expansion Foam** | 2:1 to 20:1 | 0.8 – 0.1 g/mL | Hydrocarbon pool fires, bunds, tanks |
| **Medium Expansion Foam** | 20:1 to 200:1 | 0.1 – 0.01 g/mL | Aviation hangars, LNG spills |
| **High Expansion Foam** | 200:1 to 1000:1 | 0.01 – 0.002 g/mL | Warehouses, cable tunnels, enclosed volumes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Standard:** All expansion ratios are referenced against the **NFPA 11 (Standard for Low-, Medium-, and High-Expansion Foam)** as adopted in KTU 2024 syllabus for PESFT416.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of Finished Foam (Low Expansion):** **0.8 – 1.0 g/cm³** (typically lighter than most hydrocarbon fuels)
- **Surface Tension of Foam Solution:** **30 – 50 mN/m** (lower than water's 72 mN/m, allowing fuel wetting)
- **Standard Application Density (Hydrocarbon):** **4.1 L/min·m² (0.10 gpm/ft²)**
- **Standard Application Density (Polar Solvents):** **6.5 L/min·m² (0.16 gpm/ft²)**
- **Critical Fire Suppression Time:** **≤ 65 seconds** for Class B hydrocarbon fires (NFPA 11 metric)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Three-Sided" Mechanism of Foam Action (Theoretical Pillars)

Foam suppresses fire through three simultaneous physical-chemical pathways:

1. **Vapor Suppression (Smothering Effect):**
   - Foam floats atop the fuel, forming a continuous, heat-resistant barrier.
   - The film of foam blocks the **mass transfer of flammable vapors** from the liquid surface to the combustion zone.
   - **Why it works:** The lamellae (foam bubble walls) are impermeable to hydrocarbon vapors, reducing the fuel-air mixture concentration below the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**.

2. **Oxygen Exclusion (Inerting/Exclusion Effect):**
   - The foam mass displaces atmospheric oxygen from the fuel surface.
   - The water content within foam bubbles is partially converted to **steam at ≥ 100°C**, which dilutes combustion air and absorbs latent heat (**~2260 kJ/kg** of water vaporized).

3. **Cooling Effect (Heat Sink Mechanism):**
   - Water released from collapsing foam absorbs heat via evaporation.
   - Specific heat capacity of water: **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
   - Latent heat of vaporization: **2260 kJ/kg**
   - Combined cooling potential per kg of water: **~2600 kJ**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow (Logic Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner expects students to articulate the **operational sequence** in fire engineering problems.

- **Step 1 — Detection:** Heat/smoke detector (or manual actuator) senses fire.
- **Step 2 — Actuation:** Control valve (deluge or pre-action) opens; water flows into the proportioner.
- **Step 3 — Proportioning:** Foam concentrate (typically **1%, 3%, or 6%** of water flow) is inducted into the water stream via a proportioner (e.g., bladder tank, balanced pressure, around-the-pump).
- **Step 4 — Aeration:** The foam solution is mixed with air through an **aspirating nozzle / foam maker** to generate the final foam.
- **Step 5 — Delivery:** Foam travels through piping to discharge devices (foam chambers, pourers, monitors).
- **Step 6 — Suppression:** Foam spreads across the fuel surface, achieving vapor suppression within the **critical 65-second** window.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Symbol | Name / Description | Equation | Units | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_{foam}$ | Foam Solution Flow Rate | $Q_{foam} = A \times q_{app}$ | L/min | NFPA 11 |
| $Q_{concentrate}$ | Foam Concentrate Quantity | $Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times \frac{C}{100}$ | L/min | NFPA 11 |
| $Q_{water}$ | Water Demand | $Q_{water} = Q_{foam} \times \left(1 - \frac{C}{100}\right)$ | L/min | NFPA 11 |
| $V_{storage}$ | Foam Concentrate Storage Volume | $V_{storage} = Q_{concentrate} \times T_{discharge}$ | Litres | NFPA 11 |
| $E_R$ | Expansion Ratio | $E_R = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}$ | Dimensionless | NFPA 11 |
| $T_{25}$ | 25% Drainage Time | Time for 25% of foam solution to separate | minutes (≥ 5 min) | EN 1568 |
| $A_{protected}$ | Protected Area | $A = \frac{Q_{foam}}{q_{app}}$ | m² | NFPA 11 |
| $F_{factor}$ | Foam F-Factor (Fuel) | $F = 1.0$ (heptane), $F = 1.15$ (gasoline), $F = 1.65$ (kerosene) | dimensionless | NFPA 11 |

Where:
- $A$ = Area of the protected surface (m²)
- $q_{app}$ = Application density (L/min·m²)
- $C$ = Foam concentration percentage (e.g., 3%)
- $T_{discharge}$ = Required discharge duration (typically 10, 15, 30, 55, 65 min)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Foam systems are mandated (per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Fire and Life Safety**, adopted by KTU 2024) for:
- **Petrochemical tank farms** (cone roof, floating roof tanks)
- **Aviation hangars** (typically medium expansion)
- **Transformer bays** (Class B dielectric fluid fires)
- **Marine engine rooms** (high expansion)
- **Hazardous chemical warehouses** (high expansion flooding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Linkage:** Foam is the **only water-based suppression medium effective on Class B polar solvent fires** (e.g., methanol, acetone) when AFFF-AR (Alcohol-Resistant) foam is used, because polar solvents dissolve standard AFFF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Derivation: Foam Concentrate Storage for a Hydrocarbon Tank

**Problem Scenario (Typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
A fixed-roof petroleum storage tank has a **diameter of 20 m** and stores **gasoline**. It is protected by a **low-expansion foam chamber system** using **3% AFFF concentrate**. The application density is **4.1 L/min·m²** and the discharge duration is **55 minutes** (per NFPA 11 for hydrocarbon tanks > 18 m diameter). Calculate:
- (a) Foam solution flow rate
- (b) Foam concentrate consumption rate
- (c) Total foam concentrate storage required
- (d) Total water demand
- (e) Number of foam chambers required (if each delivers 200 L/min of foam solution)

---

#### Step 1: Calculate the Protected Surface Area

The fuel surface inside a cylindrical tank of diameter $D$ has area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting $D = 20$ m:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4}
$$

$$
A = 100\pi \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A \approx 314.159 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Logic:** We compute the area of a circle because a foam system protects the **horizontal fuel surface** of a fixed-roof tank.

---

#### Step 2: Calculate the Foam Solution Flow Rate (Part a)

The foam solution flow rate is the application density multiplied by the area:

$$
Q_{foam} = A \times q_{app}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Q_{foam} = 314.159 \text{ m}^2 \times 4.1 \frac{\text{L}}{\text{min} \cdot \text{m}^2}
$$

$$
Q_{foam} = 1288.05 \text{ L/min}
$$

> **Logic:** Application density specifies how many litres of foam solution must be applied per square metre per minute.

---

#### Step 3: Calculate the Foam Concentrate Consumption Rate (Part b)

The concentrate is a fraction $C$ of the foam solution:

$$
Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times \frac{C}{100}
$$

Substituting $C = 3$:

$$
Q_{concentrate} = 1288.05 \times \frac{3}{100}
$$

$$
Q_{concentrate} = 38.64 \text{ L/min}
$$

> **Logic:** Only 3% of the foam solution is the active chemical agent; the remaining 97% is water.

---

#### Step 4: Calculate Total Foam Concentrate Storage (Part c)

The total concentrate volume is consumption rate × discharge duration:

$$
V_{concentrate} = Q_{concentrate} \times T_{discharge}
$$

Substituting $T_{discharge} = 55$ min:

$$
V_{concentrate} = 38.64 \times 55
$$

$$
V_{concentrate} = 2125.24 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{concentrate} \approx 2.125 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (cubic metres)}
$$

> **Logic:** The concentrate must be stored in bulk drums/tanks sized to last the **entire 55-minute discharge** without resupply.

---

#### Step 5: Calculate the Total Water Demand (Part d)

Water is the remainder of the foam solution after concentrate is removed:

$$
Q_{water} = Q_{foam} \times \left(1 - \frac{C}{100}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{water} = 1288.05 \times \left(1 - \frac{3}{100}\right)
$$

$$
Q_{water} = 1288.05 \times 0.97
$$

$$
Q_{water} = 1249.41 \text{ L/min}
$$

For the entire 55-minute duration, total water:

$$
V_{water} = 1249.41 \times 55 = 68{,}717.6 \text{ L} \approx 68.72 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** The water supply (hydrant, tank) must sustain this flow rate for 55 minutes.

---

#### Step 6: Number of Foam Chambers Required (Part e)

Each foam chamber delivers a fixed capacity. The number is:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{Q_{foam}}{Q_{chamber}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1288.05}{200} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil 6.44 \right\rceil = 7 \text{ chambers}
$$

> **Logic:** You cannot have a fraction of a chamber; round **up** to ensure adequate coverage.

---

### 3.2 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protected Area | $A$ | 314.16 | m² |
| Foam Solution Flow | $Q_{foam}$ | 1288.05 | L/min |
| Concentrate Flow | $Q_{concentrate}$ | 38.64 | L/min |
| Concentrate Storage | $V_{concentrate}$ | 2125.24 | L |
| Water Flow | $Q_{water}$ | 1249.41 | L/min |
| Total Water (55 min) | $V_{water}$ | 68,717.6 | L |
| Foam Chambers | $N$ | 7 | nos. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Foam System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Water Supply - Hydrant / Tank] --> B[Main Control Valve - Deluge]
    B --> C[Foam Proportioner - Bladder Tank or Venturi]
    D[Foam Concentrate Storage Drum] --> C
    C --> E[Proportioning Skid - Mixes 3% or 6%]
    E --> F[Aspirating Nozzle / Foam Maker]
    F --> G[Distribution Piping Network]
    G --> H[Discharge Devices - Foam Chambers or Monitors]
    H --> I[Fire Hazard Surface - Fuel]
    I --> J[Vapor Suppression - Smothering]
    I --> K[Oxygen Exclusion - Cooling]
    J --> L[Fire Suppression Achieved]
    K --> L
    M[Fire Detection Panel] --> B
    N[Manual Push Button] --> B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Water enters from the left, is metered with foam concentrate at the proportioner, aerated at the foam maker, and distributed via piping to discharge devices that deliver foam onto the fire hazard.

### 4.2 Foam Proportioning Methods (Comparative Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Foam Proportioning Methods] --> P1[Around the Pump - Simple, low cost]
    P0 --> P2[Bladder Tank - Pre-mixed stored energy]
    P0 --> P3[Balanced Pressure - High accuracy]
    P0 --> P4[In-line Venturi - Compact installation]
    P0 --> P5[Inline Balanced Pressure Proportioner - NFPA 11 Premium]
    P1 --> X1[Accuracy +/- 30 percent]
    P2 --> X2[Accuracy +/- 10 percent]
    P3 --> X3[Accuracy +/- 5 percent]
    P4 --> X4[Accuracy +/- 15 percent]
    P5 --> X5[Accuracy +/- 1 percent]
```

> **Engineering Note:** Higher accuracy ensures the foam mixture remains in its **optimal suppression range** despite fluctuations in water pressure.

### 4.3 Foam Expansion Categories (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Finished Foam by Expansion] --> S1[Low Expansion 2 to 20 times]
    S0 --> S2[Medium Expansion 20 to 200 times]
    S0 --> S3[High Expansion 200 to 1000 times]
    S1 --> S1a[Use - Hydrocarbon pool tanks]
    S2 --> S2a[Use - Aviation hangars and LNG]
    S3 --> S3a[Use - Enclosed warehouses and tunnels]
```

### 4.4 Process Logic — Detection to Suppression

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Fire Initiation on Fuel] --> stepB[Heat or Smoke Detector Triggers]
    stepB --> stepC[Control Panel Sends Signal]
    stepC --> stepD[Deluge Valve Opens]
    stepD --> stepE[Water Flows into Proportioner]
    stepE --> stepF[Concentrate Inducted at 3 percent]
    stepF --> stepG[Mixing with Air at Foam Maker]
    stepG --> stepH[Finished Foam Discharged]
    stepH --> stepI[Fuel Surface Sealed within 65 seconds]
    stepI --> stepJ[Fire Suppressed and Held]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define foam and explain its three mechanisms of fire suppression. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

Foam is a homogeneous aggregation of gas-filled bubbles formed by mechanically mixing a foam concentrate with water and air, possessing a lower density than most flammable liquids.

**Three mechanisms:**

1. **Vapor suppression** — Foam blankets the fuel, blocking the escape of flammable vapors into the combustion zone.
2. **Oxygen exclusion** — Foam displaces air above the fuel and the water component produces steam, diluting oxygen below 16%.
3. **Cooling** — Water in the foam absorbs heat via evaporation (latent heat **2260 kJ/kg**), reducing fuel temperature below the fire point.

---

#### Question 2: Differentiate between Low, Medium, and High Expansion Foam. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low Expansion | Medium Expansion | High Expansion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expansion Ratio | 2:1 to 20:1 | 20:1 to 200:1 | 200:1 to 1000:1 |
| Bubble Size | Large | Medium | Very small |
| Application | Hydrocarbon tanks | Aviation hangars | Enclosed volumes |
| Distance of Throw | High (≈ 30 m) | Moderate (≈ 10 m) | Low (≈ 5 m) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [Optional Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of foam concentrates used in fire protection with their chemical composition and application areas. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Protein Foam (P):** Derived from hydrolyzed protein (animal hoof, horn). Forms a thick, heat-resistant blanket. Best for **hydrocarbon pool fires** (tank farms, refineries).

2. **Fluoroprotein Foam (FP):** Protein foam with fluorinated surfactants added. Provides fuel-shedding properties and **fast knockdown**. Used in **aviation fuel fires**.

3. **Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF):** Contains fluorocarbon surfactants and synthetic foaming agents. Forms a **thin aqueous film** on the fuel that rapidly spreads and seals vapors. Best for **fast knockdown of hydrocarbon fires**.

4. **Film Forming Fluoroprotein (FFFP):** Combines protein and AFFF chemistry. Provides **dual film + blanket** action. Used where rapid suppression is critical (military, marine).

5. **Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AFFF-AR):** Contains a polymer that precipitates on contact with polar solvents (alcohols, ketones), forming a protective barrier. Essential for **methanol, ethanol, acetone** storage.

6. **Class A Foam:** Specifically for **ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, vegetation)**. Reduces water surface tension from **72 mN/m to ~30 mN/m**, improving penetration.

---

**(b)** A fixed roof hydrocarbon storage tank of **diameter 18 m** is protected by a top-mounted foam chamber system using **3% AFFF concentrate**. The application density is **4.1 L/min·m²** and discharge time is **55 minutes**. Each foam chamber delivers **200 L/min** of foam solution. Calculate:
- (i) Foam solution flow rate
- (ii) Concentrate consumption rate
- (iii) Total concentrate storage
- (iv) Number of foam chambers required **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 18^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 324}{4} = 81\pi \approx 254.47 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Foam solution flow (i)**

$$
Q_{foam} = 254.47 \times 4.1 = 1043.33 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 3: Concentrate rate (ii)**

$$
Q_{concentrate} = 1043.33 \times \frac{3}{100} = 31.30 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 4: Concentrate storage (iii)**

$$
V_{concentrate} = 31.30 \times 55 = 1721.5 \text{ L} \approx 1.72 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Number of chambers (iv)**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1043.33}{200} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5.22 \right\rceil = 6 \text{ chambers}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Optional Choice 2]**

**(a)** Describe the working of a **Balanced Pressure Foam Proportioning System** with a labeled schematic. State two advantages and two disadvantages. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A balanced pressure proportioner maintains a **constant pressure differential** between the water and the foam concentrate lines, regardless of fluctuations in water flow. A **ratio controller** uses a diaphragm to keep the pressure on both sides of the proportioning valve balanced, so the concentrate flow automatically tracks the water flow at the correct percentage (e.g., 3% or 6%).

**Schematic block flow:**

```
Water supply --> Pressure regulator --> Proportioning valve
Foam concentrate --> Pressure regulator --> Proportioning valve
Proportioning valve --> Foam solution line --> Foam maker
```

**Advantages:**
1. **High accuracy** (±1%) across a wide flow range.
2. Suitable for **large, variable-flow** systems like tank farms.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Complex** with multiple regulators and sensing lines.
2. **Higher cost** and requires skilled maintenance.

---

**(b)** An aviation hangar of **30 m × 20 m** is protected by a **medium expansion foam system** with an application density of **5.0 L/min·m²** and **discharge time of 25 minutes**. The foam concentrate is **6% FFFP**. Compute:
- (i) Foam solution flow rate
- (ii) Concentrate consumption rate
- (iii) Total concentrate storage **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Area**

$$
A = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Foam solution flow (i)**

$$
Q_{foam} = 600 \times 5.0 = 3000 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 3: Concentrate rate (ii)**

$$
Q_{concentrate} = 3000 \times \frac{6}{100} = 180 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 4: Concentrate storage (iii)**

$$
V_{concentrate} = 180 \times 25 = 4500 \text{ L} = 4.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never use $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 2. **Always convert discharge time to the same units** as the flow rate (e.g., L/min × min = L). A common error is mismatching **gpm** with **L/min**.
> 3. **Round UP** the number of discharge devices — never round down. Partial coverage is a failure to meet NFPA 11.
> 4. **Polar solvents (methanol, ethanol) require AFFF-AR** — using regular AFFF will result in foam destruction. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **High-expansion foam is for enclosed spaces only** — applying it in open areas is wasteful as wind disperses the foam blanket.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Foam** is a **gas-in-liquid emulsion** with three fire-suppression mechanisms: **vapor suppression, oxygen exclusion, and cooling**.
- **Expansion ratio** is the key classifier: **Low (2–20×)**, **Medium (20–200×)**, **High (200–1000×)**.
- **Concentrate types to memorize:** **Protein (P)**, **Fluoroprotein (FP)**, **AFFF**, **FFFP**, **AFFF-AR (for polar solvents)**, **Class A**.
- **Core equation trio:**
  - $Q_{foam} = A \times q_{app}$
  - $Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times (C/100)$
  - $V_{storage} = Q_{concentrate} \times T_{discharge}$
- **Standard application densities:** **4.1 L/min·m²** (hydrocarbons) and **6.5 L/min·m²** (polar solvents).
- **Critical performance metric:** Foam must achieve **vapor suppression within 65 seconds** of discharge (NFPA 11).
- **Proportioning accuracy hierarchy:** Around-pump < Venturi < Bladder < Balanced Pressure < Inline Balanced Pressure.
- **Polar solvent rule:** Always use **AFFF-AR** for alcohols, ketones, and esters.
- **Engineering design sources:** **NFPA 11**, **IS 15525**, **NBC 2016 Part 4**, and **OISD 117** (for petroleum sector).
- **Master the rounding rule:** Always **round up** when computing the number of chambers/nozzles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Investigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT416 - LIFE SAFETY IN BUILDING FIRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Investigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fire Investigation** is the systematic, scientific, and legal process of determining the **origin**, **cause**, and **circumstances** of a fire or explosion. It involves the careful examination of a fire scene, collection and preservation of physical evidence, analysis of burn patterns, witness statements, and documentary records to establish an accurate account of the fire's inception, propagation, and resulting losses.

According to **NFPA 921: Guide for Fire and Explosion Investigations (2024 Edition)**, fire investigation is defined as:

> *"The process of determining the origin, cause, and development of a fire or explosion, including the identification and evaluation of physical evidence, witness statements, and other relevant information."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, fire investigation is treated as a multidisciplinary applied science that combines:
- **Fire Science & Thermodynamics** (heat transfer, combustion chemistry)
- **Forensic Engineering** (failure analysis)
- **Legal Procedure** (rules of evidence, chain of custody)
- **Behavioral Science** (fire pattern analysis, human behavior in fire)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fire Investigation is the terminal module of PESFT416 and integrates all previous learning (fire dynamics, suppression, building services, evacuation). Expect application-based and analytical questions in ESE, often linked to case studies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fire Detective" Approach

Think of a **fire investigation** like a **medical post-mortem for a building**:

| Medical Post-Mortem | Fire Investigation |
|---------------------|-------------------|
| Patient (deceased) | Fire scene (burned building) |
| Doctor/Coroner | Fire Investigator |
| Symptoms & wounds | Burn patterns, charring, soot deposition |
| Medical history | Building plans, occupancy records |
| Autopsy report | Investigation report |
| Cause of death | Cause of fire (accidental/incendiary/natural/undetermined) |

Just as a doctor traces symptoms back to a root cause, a fire investigator traces **fire patterns backward from the most damaged area to the least damaged area** — working from the **"cold zone" (least damage) toward the "hot zone" (origin)**. The fire did not start where the most damage is; rather, the **origin** is where the damage pattern radiates *outward from*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Origin of Fire** = *Where* the fire started (a physical location in space)
> - **Cause of Fire** = *Why* and *how* the fire started (the ignition source, fuel, and circumstances)
> These are two independent determinations and must never be confused.

## 1.3 Objectives of Fire Investigation

The primary objectives, as standardized under **NFPA 921** and adopted by Indian forensic practice, are:

1. **Identification of the Origin** — pinpoint the exact geometric location of fire ignition
2. **Determination of the Cause** — classify as accidental, natural, incendiary (arson), or undetermined
3. **Documentation of Fire Development** — map the spread pattern (path, rate, direction)
4. **Identification of Victims & Property Loss** — cause and manner of injuries, extent of property damage
5. **Detection of Code Violations** — identify failures in fire protection, suppression, or alarm systems
6. **Collection of Evidential Material** — for potential criminal prosecution or insurance claims
7. **Prevention of Future Incidents** — generate data for improved building codes and public safety

## 1.4 Types of Fire Investigation

```
                    FIRE INVESTIGATION
                          |
       ___________________|___________________
      |                   |                   |
   CRIMINAL             CIVIL              ADMINISTRATIVE
   (Arson/Criminal      (Insurance,        (Code compliance,
    negligence)         tort claims)       safety audits)
```

- **Criminal Investigation** — Conducted by police/fire authorities when arson, homicide, or negligence is suspected
- **Civil Investigation** — Conducted for insurance claims, product liability, or wrongful death suits
- **Administrative Investigation** — Internal review by fire departments or building owners for SOP improvement
- **Insurance/Private Investigation** — Hired by insurance companies to verify claims

## 1.5 Legal Framework in India

In India, fire investigation operates under the intersection of multiple statutes:

| Statute | Relevance |
|---------|-----------|
| **Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860** — Sec 285, 286, 435, 436 | Negligence with fire, mischief by fire |
| **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023** — Sec 268, 324, 351 | Replaced IPC for fire-related offences |
| **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023) | Rules on admissibility of fire scene evidence |
| **National Building Code (NBC) 2016, Part 4** | Fire safety standards whose violation = negligence |
| **State Fire Services Acts** (e.g., Kerala State Fire and Rescue Services Act) | Powers of fire officers, investigation authority |
| **Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority (IRDA)** | Guidelines on fire insurance claim settlement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 304-A IPC / Sec 106 BNS (Causing Death by Negligence)** is the most common criminal charge arising from faulty fire investigations — making rigorous methodology essential.

## 1.6 The Investigator — Roles & Qualifications

A fire investigator is typically:
- A **Fire Service Officer** (in India, a Station Officer or above)
- A **Forensic Scientist** (from FSL — Forensic Science Laboratory)
- A **Certified Fire Investigator (CFI)** — credential from International Association of Arson Investigators (IAAI)
- An **Insurance Loss Adjuster**

**Required Competencies (per NFPA 1033: Standard for Professional Qualifications for Fire Investigator):**
- Knowledge of fire science, building construction, electricity, chemistry
- Understanding of legal procedures and evidence rules
- Skill in interview techniques and report writing
- Ability to use scientific methodology without bias

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scientific Method in Fire Investigation

Fire investigation must follow the **scientific method** to avoid cognitive bias (a major criticism of traditional "folklore" fire investigation). The steps are:

1. **Recognize the Need** — A fire has occurred; an answer is required
2. **Define the Problem** — What specifically must be determined? (origin? cause?)
3. **Collect Data** — Scene examination, witness interviews, records
4. **Analyze Data** — Apply fire dynamics, chemistry, electricity principles
5. **Develop Hypotheses** — Multiple competing hypotheses
6. **Test Hypotheses** — Eliminate through physical evidence
7. **Select Final Hypothesis** — Only the hypothesis *consistent with all data*
8. **Document & Report** — Final investigation report

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"List the steps of the scientific method as applied to fire investigation."* Memorize the above sequence.

## 2.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Foundation of Cause Analysis

Every fire requires four components simultaneously. **Removal of any one extinguishes the fire.** Cause analysis determines which component was *introduced* to initiate combustion:

$$
\text{Fire} = f(\text{Fuel}, \text{Oxygen}, \text{Heat}, \text{Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction})
$$

| Component | Investigative Clue |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Fuel** | First material ignited, fuel load calculations |
| **Oxygen** | Ventilation patterns, V-pattern analysis |
| **Heat (Ignition Source)** | Electrical faults, open flames, hot surfaces, chemical reactions |
| **Sustained Reaction** | Self-propagation indicators (flashover evidence) |

## 2.3 Fire Cause Classification

Per **NFPA 921**, fire causes are classified into **four mutually exclusive categories**:

$$
\text{Cause} \in \{\text{Accidental}, \text{Natural}, \text{Incendiary}, \text{Undetermined}\}
$$

| Category | Definition | Typical Indicators |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| **Accidental** | Fire resulted from unintended action or mechanical failure | Electrical short circuits, cooking mishaps, smoking, children playing with fire |
| **Natural** | Fire caused by natural phenomena without human action | Lightning strike, spontaneous combustion, wildfire spread |
| **Incendiary (Arson)** | Fire intentionally set with malicious intent | Multiple origins, trailers, liquid accelerants, delayed ignition devices, suspicious circumstances |
| **Undetermined** | Evidence insufficient to classify definitively | Destroyed scene, conflicting witness statements, no physical evidence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "Incendiary" is **not synonymous** with "arson" in all jurisdictions. Incendiary = intentional ignition; Arson = incendiary fire that meets the legal statutory definition (which varies by jurisdiction).

## 2.4 Burn Pattern Analysis — The Core Visual Skill

Burn patterns are the **visual fingerprints** of a fire's behavior. They are categorized as:

### A. Damage Patterns (Depth/Intensity of Burning)
- **Local Burn Patterns** — Confined to area of origin
- **Total Burn Patterns** — All combustibles consumed (often hides the origin)
- **Trailers (Arson Indicators)** — Lines of burning leading to/from origin
- **V-Patterns** — Vertical char pattern indicating upward fire spread
- **Hourglass Patterns** — Indicate floor joist burning above origin
- **Inverted Cone Patterns** — Plume-driven ceiling damage

### B. Soot/Staining Patterns
- **Soot on Walls** — Indicates smoke movement
- **Clean V** — Areas cleaned by hot gases rising
- **Crazed Glass** — Thermal fracture patterns on windows indicating fire side

### C. Arc Mapping
Electrical arc mapping uses **beads and spatter** from short circuits to trace electrical faults and sometimes the fire's origin.

## 2.5 Heat Release Rate and Origin Determination

The **Fire Origin** correlates with the location of **maximum heat release rate (HRR) history** and **deepest char**. Several engineering relationships guide this:

### 2.5.1 Char Depth Equation (Time-Temperature Relationship)

For wood char depth estimation:

$$
d = k \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = depth of char (mm)
- $k$ = char rate coefficient (mm/min$^{1/2}$); for typical softwood $k \approx 0.6\text{ to } 0.8$ mm/min$^{1/2}$
- $t$ = duration of heating (min)

### 2.5.2 V-Pattern Angle Analysis

The angle of a V-pattern indicates the **rate of fire growth**:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{L}{H}
$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = angle of V from vertical (degrees)
- $L$ = horizontal spread at base of V
- $H$ = height of V apex

A **wide V** ($\theta > 30°$) = fast, fuel-rich fire
A **narrow V** ($\theta < 20°$) = slow, ventilation-controlled fire

### 2.5.3 Plume Velocity at Ceiling

For origin location beneath a ceiling:

$$
V_p = 0.20 \cdot \left( \frac{g \cdot \dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot T_\infty} \right)^{1/3} \cdot (z)^{1/3}
$$

Where:
- $V_p$ = plume velocity (m/s)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat release rate (kW)
- $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) for air
- $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $T_\infty = 293$ K
- $z$ = height above fire (m)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Char Depth** | $d = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | $d$ (mm), $k$ (mm/min$^{1/2}$), $t$ (min) | Estimating fire duration at origin |
| 2 | **V-Pattern Angle** | $\tan(\theta) = L/H$ | $\theta$ (°), $L$ (m), $H$ (m) | Classifying fire growth rate |
| 3 | **Ceiling Jet Velocity** | $V_p = 0.20 \cdot \left( \frac{g \dot{Q}}{c_p \rho_\infty T_\infty} \right)^{1/3} z^{1/3}$ | $V_p$ (m/s), $\dot{Q}$ (kW), $z$ (m) | Locating fire origin from plume |
| 4 | **Plume Temperature** | $\Delta T_p = 9.1 \cdot (Q_c^{2/3}/z^{5/3})$ | $\Delta T$ (K), $Q_c$ (kW) | Origin confirmation via heat flux |
| 5 | **Spalling Threshold** | $T_{spall} \approx 300°C$ | Temperature (°C) | Concrete spalling indicates localized heat |
| 6 | **Ignition Temperature Range** | $T_{ig} = 200$–$600°C$ | Temperature (°C) | Material-specific ignition analysis |
| 7 | **Flashover Criterion** | $\dot{Q}_{fo} \approx 1000$ kW | Heat Release Rate (kW) | Marks transition to fully developed fire |
| 8 | **Fire Tetrahedron** | Fuel + O₂ + Heat + Chain Reaction | — | Cause analysis framework |
| 9 | **Cause Classification** | 4 categories | — | NFPA 921 standard |
| 10 | **Chain of Custody** | 1 person = 1 link | — | Legal admissibility of evidence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Warning:** Always state units explicitly in numerical problems. Marks are frequently lost for unit omissions.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fire investigation principles are applied in:
- **Building Code Development** — Post-fire reports inform NFPA, NBC revisions
- **Product Liability Litigation** — e.g., identifying defective electrical products
- **Insurance Fraud Detection** — Recognizing "red flags" of staged fires
- **Wildland-Urban Interface Fires** — Determining cause to prevent recurrence
- **Industrial Fire Safety Audits** — Tracing fire in petrochemical plants
- **Heritage Building Preservation** — Understanding historical fire causes
- **Court Testimony** — Expert witness presentation of scientific findings
